In July 2012, the ATLAS and CMS collaborations announced the discovery of a new resonance [1] during their search for the Standard Model (SM) Higgs boson. The primary experimental focus now is to validate all of its properties, as predicted by the SM, particularly its spin and parity [3]. This validation must be conducted independently across all of its decay channels.

For a spin-1 boson, the helicity amplitudes can be expressed as 

$M_{X_{1}}=\left(\begin{array}[]{ccccc}0&0&0&0\\
0&-V_{gg1}^{++}{V_{\gamma\gamma 1}^{++}}^{*}\cos\theta&-V_{gg1}^{++}{V_{\gamma\gamma 1}^{--}}^{*}\cos\theta&0\\
0&\,\,-V_{gg1}^{--}{V_{\gamma\gamma 1}^{++}}^{*}\cos\theta&\,\,-V_{gg1}^{--}{V_{\gamma\gamma 1}^{--}}^{*}\cos\theta&0\\
0&0&0&0\\
\end{array}\right),$  (15) 

which exhibits the property $M_{X_{1}}(\theta) = -M_{X_{1}}(\theta + \pi)$ under the interchange of either initial or final state particles. However, for identical particles in either the initial or final state, the condition $M_{X_{1}}(\theta) = M_{X_{1}}(\theta + \pi)$ must hold. This necessitates that $V_{1}^{++} = V_{1}^{--} = 0$, thereby forbidding this partonic channel, consistent with the Landau-Yang theorem [21]. In the case of non-identical particles, the behavior of a spin-1 resonance aligns with that of a spin-0 boson, though with a characteristic $\cos^{2}\theta$ dependence in the cross-section.

We develop a spectral representation for the baryon-to-meson transition distribution amplitudes (TDAs), which are matrix elements involving three-quark correlators that emerge in the description of baryon-to-meson transitions within the factorization framework for hard exclusive reactions. Building on the concept of double distributions, originally introduced in the context of generalized parton distributions, we extend this notion to baryon-to-meson TDAs. Furthermore, we introduce a generalized version of A. Radyushkin's factorized Ansatz tailored for these TDAs. This construction provides a foundation for modeling baryon-to-meson TDAs across their full domain of definition and enables quantitative predictions of cross-sections for various hard exclusive reactions.

Nucleon-to-meson transition distribution amplitudes, referred to in the literature as skewed DAs (5) and superskewed parton distributions (6), generalize the concept of usual parton distributions and find applications, for example, in the description of meson electroproduction on a nucleon target (13). In the following discussion, we focus on nucleon-to-pion transition distribution amplitudes (denoted as $\pi N$ TDAs) while noting that the analysis is sufficiently general to apply to other baryon-meson and baryon-to-photon TDAs. $\pi N$ TDAs are relevant in the context of backward pion electroproduction:

$\displaystyle\gamma^{*}(q)\,+\,N(p_{1})\,\rightarrow N^{\prime}(p_{2})\,+\,\pi(p_{\pi})\,,$  (1)

within the generalized Bjorken regime ($-q^{2}$ large; $q^{2}/(2p_{1}\cdot q)$ fixed; $-q^{2}\gg-u$). The factorization theorem for this process was established in (5) (as illustrated in Fig. 1). The kinematics are defined as follows (15):

$\displaystyle P=\frac{1}{2}(p_{1}+p_{\pi})\,;\ \ \ \Delta=p_{\pi}-p_{1}\,;$

$\displaystyle u=\Delta^{2}\,;\ \ \ \xi=-\frac{\Delta\cdot n}{2P\cdot n}\,,$  (2)

where $u$ represents the squared momentum transfer between the meson and the nucleon, and $\xi$ is the skewness parameter. Here, $n$ and $p$ are light-cone vectors used in the Sudakov decomposition of momenta ($n^{2}=p^{2}=0$; $n\cdot p=1$). A vector $v^{\mu}$ can be decomposed in the light-cone basis as $v^{\mu}=v^{+}p^{\mu}+v^{-}n^{\mu}+v_{T}^{\mu}$.

The idea of generalized parton distributions (GPDs), which are non-diagonal matrix elements of quark-antiquark or gluon-gluon non-local operators on the light cone in their simplest (leading twist) form, has been expanded to include baryon-to-meson (and baryon-to-photon) transition distribution amplitudes (TDAs). These TDAs represent non-diagonal matrix elements of three-quark operators connecting hadronic states with different baryon numbers (or a baryon state and a photon). Nucleon-to-meson TDAs are closely related to meson-nucleon generalized distribution amplitudes, as they share the same non-local operators. These quantities are essential for describing exclusive processes involving baryonic exchange, such as backward electroproduction of mesons or proton-antiproton hard exclusive annihilation processes. Additionally, nucleon-to-meson TDAs are considered valuable for quantifying the pion cloud in baryons.

In this paper, we introduced the concept of quadruple distributions and established the spectral representation for transition distribution amplitudes involving three-parton correlators, which are relevant to baryon-to-meson transitions. We extended Radyushkin’s factorized Ansatz to accommodate quadruple distributions and derived an explicit expression for the associated profile function. Similar to the case of generalized parton distributions (GPDs), the profile function’s shape is expected to be determined by the nucleon distribution amplitude’s asymptotic form. Our model necessitates the nucleon-to-meson transition distribution amplitudes (TDAs) in the forward limit as input. Unlike GPDs, nucleon-to-meson TDAs lack a well-defined forward limit, presenting a challenge that requires further study. Tentatively, we propose employing a simplified form for nucleon-to-meson TDAs in the forward limit, based on their behavior at the domain boundaries. This framework enables quantitative modeling of baryon-meson and baryon-photon TDAs across their entire domain of definition.

In a manner analogous to how nucleon-to-meson transverse density amplitudes (TDAs) exhibit factorization in the context of backward meson electroproduction, nucleon-to-photon TDAs may similarly factorize in backward virtual Compton scattering (30). The analytical framework developed in our study is largely applicable to the nucleon-to-photon TDA scenario. However, the modeling of the quadruple distribution necessitates careful consideration of the photon's anomalous characteristics. As demonstrated by investigations into the photon's anomalous structure functions (31) and its generalized parton distributions (GPDs) (32), addressing this anomalous nature presents a nontrivial challenge that warrants dedicated research efforts.

To summarize the essential aspects, we initially considered the spectral representation of \( H(x_{1},\,x_{2}=2\xi-x_{1},\xi) \), which depends on the skewness parameter \(\xi\) and the longitudinal momentum fractions \(x_{1}\) and \(x_{2}\), with the constraint \(x_{1} + x_{2} = 2\xi\). This representation inherently satisfied the necessary support properties for \(x_{1}\) and \(x_{2}\), as well as the polynomiality condition for the associated Mellin moments. Notably, the spectral density in this framework was a quadruple rather than a double distribution.  

To address the constraint \(x_{1} + x_{2} = 2\xi\), we introduced two \(\delta\)-functions that confined the integration domain within the spectral variable space. These \(\delta\)-functions also facilitated the elimination of two redundant integrations, a process requiring careful consideration of the integration limits in the spectral parameter space. Simplifying the problem by adopting a set of natural variables in both the spectral and \(x_{1},\,x_{2}\) spaces proved particularly effective.  

In this simplified context, lifting the two integrations did not impose additional constraints on the remaining spectral parameters \(\alpha_{-}\) and \(\beta_{-}\), ultimately reproducing the standard Radyushkin double distribution formalism for GPDs. Furthermore, we identified the symmetric spectral representation (Equation 26) with respect to \(x_{1}\) and \(x_{2}\) as a suitable foundation for extending the analysis to multiparton scenarios. The subsequent subsection delves into the application of this methodology to \(\pi N\) TDAs.

Let us underscore that, at this stage, we have not incorporated any $D$-term-like contributions into the spectral representation of nucleon-to-meson TDAs expressed in terms of quadruple distributions. The findings from equation (36) and Chapter 3.8 of reference (27) provide confidence that such $D$-term-like contributions can be accommodated by augmenting the spectral density in equation (39) with additional terms proportional to powers of $\xi$. The subsequent analysis would then follow the same methodology.

The support properties (9) remain unchanged under the permutation of the longitudinal momentum fractions \(x_i\). As \(\xi \rightarrow 1\), the support of the \(\pi N\) TDA is confined to an ERBL-like domain (an equilateral triangle, as shown in Fig. 4) and aligns with the nucleon distribution amplitude (DA). This correspondence is expected, as \(\xi = 1\) signifies the soft pion limit, where the \(\pi N\) TDA transitions to the nucleon DA (15).

We now proceed to apply the concepts outlined in the previous section to the context of $\pi N$ transverse density amplitudes (TDAs). Specifically, we examine the $\pi N$ TDA $H(x_{1},\,x_{2},\,x_{3}=2\xi-x_{1}-x_{2},\,\xi)$ as a function of the light-cone momentum fractions $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$ associated with three quarks. These momentum fractions satisfy the constraint $x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}=2\xi$. The support properties in $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$ are defined as:

\[
\displaystyle-1+\xi\leq x_{1}\leq 1+\xi\,;\ \ \ -1+\xi\leq x_{2}\leq 1+\xi\,;\ \ \ -1+\xi\leq x_{3}\leq 1+\xi\,.\ \ \ (36)
\]

The ERBL-like and DGLAP-like domains for the $\pi N$ transversity distribution amplitude (TDA) in quark-diquark coordinates are illustrated in Figure 5. In this coordinate system, the ERBL-like region is represented by a central isosceles triangular domain, bounded by specific lines.

A key aspect of constructing theoretical models is ensuring compliance with fundamental principles of field theory, such as general Lorentz covariance. This requirement imposes the polynomiality property on the Mellin moments of $\pi N$ twist distribution amplitudes (TDAs) in terms of the light-cone momentum fractions $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$:

\[
\int dx_{1}dx_{2}dx_{3}\delta(2\xi-x_{1}-x_{2}-x_{3})x_{1}^{n_{1}}x_{2}^{n_{2}}x_{3}^{n_{3}}H(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},\xi,u)
\]

\[
\sim\left.\left(i\frac{d}{dz_{1}^{-}}\right)^{n_{1}}\left(i\frac{d}{dz_{2}^{-}}\right)^{n_{2}}\left(i\frac{d}{dz_{3}^{-}}\right)^{n_{3}}\left[\langle\pi(P+\Delta/2)|\psi(z_{1})\psi(z_{2})\psi(z_{3})|N(P-\Delta/2)\rangle\right]\right|_{z_{i}=0}\,.
\]

(7)

The Mellin moments of $\pi N$ TDAs with respect to $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$ correspond to the form factors of local twist-3 three-quark operators connecting nucleon and pion states. Consequently, the right-hand side of equation (7) exhibits polynomials in $\xi$. While a straightforward analysis suggests that the polynomial order is $n_{1}+n_{2}+n_{3}$, the determination of the maximum achievable power of $\xi$ in (7) remains incomplete. This is a critical issue, as resolving it would provide insights into whether $D$-term contributions (33) must be incorporated into the spectral representation of $\pi N$ TDAs (as discussed in Sec. 7).

The central achievement of our work is the spectral representation (58) for $\pi N$ time-dependent angular distributions (TDAs) formulated in terms of quadruple distributions. While this result is significant, its current form remains impractical for direct application. In the subsequent section, we demonstrate that this spectral representation (58) adheres to the support properties of $\pi N$ TDAs, as previously established in Section 3. Additionally, we provide explicit expressions for $\pi N$ TDAs within the ERBL-like and DGLAP-like domains, specifically addressing types I and II.

The support properties (9) remain unchanged under the permutation of the longitudinal momentum fractions $x_i$. As $\xi$ approaches 1, the support of the $\pi N$ TDA narrows to the ERBL-like domain, forming an equilateral triangle (as shown in Fig. 4), and aligns with the support of the nucleon distribution amplitude (DA). This convergence is expected, as $\xi=1$ signifies the soft pion limit, where the $\pi N$ TDA simplifies to the corresponding nucleon DA (15).

The normalization of transversity distribution amplitudes (TDAs) for the nucleon-pion system can be established through several approaches. One method involves leveraging the soft pion limit or conducting lattice computations of the initial Mellin moments of πN TDAs, or alternatively, by cross-referencing with findings from prior studies (e.g., those cited in reference 18). Another avenue entails calculating the contribution of light baryon exchanges to the Mellin moments of πN TDAs, employing empirical values such as the coupling constants \( g_{\pi NN} \) and \( g_{\pi N\Delta} \). Additionally, in principle, the normalization could be directly determined from experimental cross-section measurements once a satisfactory scaling behavior is observed.

This study focuses on the development of a spectral representation for baryon-to-meson transition distribution amplitudes. A novel framework involving quadruple distributions is introduced, building upon Radyushkin’s factorized approach. This innovation enables comprehensive modeling of these amplitudes across their entire domain of definition and facilitates precise quantitative analysis of rates in various hard exclusive processes.

The double distribution representation (21) was identified as a sophisticated approach to integrate the polynomiality property of Mellin moments with the support characteristics of generalized parton distributions (GPDs). Within this formalism, the GPD \( H \) is expressed as a one-dimensional cross-section of the double distribution (DD) \( f(\alpha, \beta) \):

\[
H(x, \xi) = \int_{-1}^{1} d\beta \int_{-1+|\beta|}^{1-|\beta|} d\alpha \, \delta(x - \beta - \alpha \xi) f(\beta, \alpha). \quad (23)
\]

The spectral representation (23) was initially derived through diagrammatic analysis utilizing \(\alpha\)-representation techniques (19). The spectral constraints \( |\beta| \leq 1 \) and \( |\alpha| \leq 1 - |\beta| \) guarantee the support property of the GPD \( |x| \leq 1 \) for any \( |\xi| \leq 1 \).

We propose an alternative approach based on the inversion of the original logic. From a purely mathematical standpoint, expressing GPD as the Radon transform of a specific spectral density is the most straightforward method to guarantee the polynomiality property. By assuming the polynomiality property of GPD along with the support condition $|x|\leq 1$, one can derive the spectral representation (23) and uniquely determine the spectral constraints $|\beta|\leq 1$ and $|\alpha|\leq 1-|\beta|$. It is important to note that this approach does not offer an independent derivation of (23), as the support property $|x|\leq 1$ of GPD cannot be established separately. Nonetheless, we believe this reasoning supports the utilization of the Radon transform (23) as a general framework for functions with polynomiality and restricted support in $x$, serving as the foundation for the spectral representation of multipartonic extensions of GPDs, particularly for $\pi N$ TDAs. To establish the form of the spectral representation for $\pi N$ TDA, we will begin by examining the simpler case of ordinary GPDs.

At this juncture, we introduce the factorized Ansatz (88) utilizing the profile function (96) as a conceptual framework. It is prudent to now consider a potential model for the function $f(\sigma,\rho)$, which constitutes the second component of the factorized Ansatz (88). In the limit where $\xi\rightarrow 0$, the $\pi N$ TDA simplifies to this function:

$\displaystyle H(w,v,\xi=0)=f(w,v)\,.$  (102)

This leads to the following spectral representation for $\pi N$ time-dependent angular distributions (TDAs):

\[
H(x_{1},\,x_{2},\,x_{3}=2\xi-x_{1}-x_{2},\,\xi)
\]
\[
= \left[\prod_{i=1}^{3}\int_{\Omega_{i}}d\beta_{i}d\alpha_{i}\right]\delta(x_{1}-\xi-\beta_{1}-\alpha_{1}\xi)\,\delta(x_{2}-\xi-\beta_{2}-\alpha_{2}\xi)\,
\]
\[
\times \delta(\beta_{1}+\beta_{2}+\beta_{3})\delta(\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\alpha_{3}+1)F(\beta_{1},\,\beta_{2},\,\beta_{3},\,\alpha_{1},\,\alpha_{2},\alpha_{3})\,.
\]
By $\Omega_{i},\,i=\left\{1,\,2,\,3\right\}$, we denote the usual domains in the parameter space:
\[
\Omega_{i}=\{|\beta_{i}|\leq 1\,;\ \ |\alpha_{i}|\leq 1-|\beta_{i}|\}\,;
\]
and $F(\beta_{1},\,\beta_{2},\,\beta_{3},\,\alpha_{1},\,\alpha_{2},\alpha_{3})$ is a sextuple distribution. The spectral conditions (40) ensure the support properties (36). The $(n_{1},\,n_{2},\,n_{3})$-th Mellin moment in $(x_{1},\,x_{2},\,x_{3})$ of $\pi N$ TDA is a polynomial of order $n_{1}+n_{2}+n_{3}$ of $\xi$:
\[
\left[\prod_{i=1}^{3}\int_{-1+\xi}^{1+\xi}dx_{i}\right]\,\delta(x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}-2\xi)x_{1}^{n_{1}}x_{2}^{n_{2}}x_{3}^{n_{3}}H(x_{1},\,x_{2},\,x_{3}=2\xi-x_{1}-x_{2},\,\xi)
\]
\[
=P_{n_{1}+n_{2}+n_{3}}(\xi)\,.
\]

In the natural spectral parameters (46), (52), and quark-diquark coordinates (54), we restored the spectral representation of $\pi N$ TDAs in terms of quadruple distributions:

$\displaystyle H(w_{3},\,v_{3},\,\xi)$

$\displaystyle=\int_{-1}^{1}d\beta_{1}d\beta_{2}d\beta_{3}\,\delta(\beta_{1}+\beta_{2}+\beta_{3})\int_{-1+|\beta_{1}|}^{1-|\beta_{1}|}d\alpha_{1}\int_{-1+|\beta_{2}|}^{1-|\beta_{2}|}d\alpha_{2}\int_{-1+|\beta_{3}|}^{1-|\beta_{3}|}d\alpha_{3}\delta(\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\alpha_{3}+1)$

$\displaystyle\delta(x_{1}-\xi-\beta_{1}-\alpha_{1}\xi)\,\delta(x_{2}-\xi-\beta_{2}-\alpha_{2}\xi)\,F(\beta_{1},\,\beta_{2},\,\beta_{3},\,\alpha_{1},\,\alpha_{2},\alpha_{3})$

$\displaystyle=\int_{-1}^{1}d\sigma_{3}\int_{-1+\frac{|\sigma_{3}|}{2}}^{1-\frac{|\sigma_{3}|}{2}}d\rho_{3}\int_{-1+|\sigma_{3}|}^{1-|\rho_{3}-\frac{\sigma_{3}}{2}|-\rho_{3}+\frac{\sigma_{3}}{2}|}d\omega_{3}\int_{-\frac{1}{2}+|\rho_{3}-\frac{\sigma_{3}}{2}|+\frac{\omega_{3}}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}-|\rho_{3}+\frac{\sigma_{3}}{2}|-\frac{\omega_{3}}{2}}d\nu_{3}\delta(w_{3}-\sigma_{3}-\omega_{3}\xi)$

$\displaystyle\times\delta(v_{3}-\rho_{3}-\nu_{3}\xi)\,F_{3}(\sigma_{3},\,\rho_{3},\,\omega_{3},\,\nu_{3})\,,$  (56)

where

$\displaystyle F_{3}(\sigma_{3},\,\rho_{3},\,\omega_{3},\,\nu_{3})\equiv F(\rho_{3}-\frac{\sigma_{3}}{2},\,-\rho_{3}-\frac{\sigma_{3}}{2},\,\sigma_{3},\,\nu_{3}-\frac{1+\omega_{3}}{2},\,-\nu_{3}-\frac{1+\omega_{3}}{2},\omega_{3})\,.$  (57)

One may draw an analogy with the case of standard GPDs to posit that the structure of $F(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)$ in $(\sigma,\,\rho)$ space is governed by the form of the function $f(\sigma,\,\rho)$, which represents the reduction of $\pi N$ TDA in the limit $\xi\rightarrow 0$. At this stage, we refrain from addressing the intricate and compelling question of the precise physical interpretation of this limit, as it will be explored in a separate discussion. Therefore, we propose adopting the following factorized ansatz for quadruple distributions:

$\displaystyle F(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)=f(\sigma,\,\rho)\,h(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)\,,$  (88)

where $h(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)$ is a normalized profile function, satisfying:

$\displaystyle\int_{-1+|\sigma|}^{1-|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}|-|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}|}d\omega\int_{-1+|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}|+\frac{1+\omega}{2}}^{1-|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}|-\frac{1+\omega}{2}}d\nu\,h(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)=1\,.$  (89)

The results of the calculation for the contribution $H(w_{3},v_{3},\xi)\equiv H(w,v,\xi)$ as a function of $w$ and $v$, utilizing the factorized Ansatz (88) with the profile (96) and $f(\sigma,\rho)$ defined by the toy model (103), are presented in Figure 7 for various values of $\xi$.

A potential model involves extending the analogy with the standard Radyushkin Ansatz for double distributions (24) and assuming that the $(\omega,\,\nu)$ dependence of $h(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)$ is governed by the shape of the nucleon distribution amplitude's asymptotic form:

$\displaystyle\Phi^{\rm as}(y_{1},\,y_{2},\,y_{3})=\frac{15}{4}\,y_{1}y_{2}y_{3}\,.$ (90) 

This distribution amplitude (90) is defined for $y_{1,\,2,\,3}\in[0;\,2]$ under the condition that $y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}=2$.

Consider the scenario where $\xi=1$; in this case, the TDA $H(w,v,\xi)$ does not approach zero at the boundaries of its domain. This situation is concerning because it could disrupt the factorization property inherent to the hard exclusive process under examination. However, this issue arises from an oversimplified model (104) used to describe the forward limit of $\pi N$ TDA. By selecting a function $f(\sigma,\rho)$ that diminishes quadratically at the edges of its domain,

$$
\left.f(\beta_{1},\,\beta_{2},\,\beta_{3})\right|_{\sum_{i}\beta_{i}=0}=\frac{4410}{3167}\,\prod_{i=1}^{3}\theta(|\beta_{i}|\leq 1)(1-\beta_{i}^{2})^{2}\big{|}_{\sum_{i}\beta_{i}=0},
$$

the $\pi N$ TDA exhibits vanishing behavior at the domain's corners when $\xi=1$, thereby resolving the issue.

To characterize $\pi N$ TDA, we employ quark-diquark coordinates as an alternative to the conventional variables $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$ that satisfy the condition 

$\displaystyle x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}=2\xi\,,$  (10) 

instead of the standard $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$. This substitution is motivated by the desire to simplify the description and does not imply any specific dynamical significance to the term "diquark." The choice of diquark system can vary, leading to three distinct sets of coordinates:

$\displaystyle v_{1}=\frac{x_{2}-x_{3}}{2}\,;\ \ \ w_{1}=\frac{x_{1}-x_{2}-x_{3}}{2}\,;$ 

$\displaystyle v_{2}=\frac{x_{3}-x_{1}}{2}\,;\ \ \ w_{2}=\frac{x_{2}-x_{3}-x_{1}}{2}\,;$ 

$\displaystyle v_{3}=\frac{x_{1}-x_{2}}{2}\,;\ \ \ w_{3}=\frac{x_{3}-x_{1}-x_{2}}{2}\,.$  (11) 

To denote the longitudinal momentum fraction carried by the diquark system, we introduce the symbols $\xi^{\prime}_{1}$, $\xi^{\prime}_{2}$, and $\xi^{\prime}_{3}$:

$\displaystyle\frac{x_{2}+x_{3}}{2}=\frac{\xi-w_{1}}{2}\equiv\xi^{\prime}_{1}\,;$ 

$\displaystyle\frac{x_{1}+x_{3}}{2}=\frac{\xi-w_{2}}{2}\equiv\xi^{\prime}_{2}\,;$ 

$\displaystyle\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}=\frac{\xi-w_{3}}{2}\equiv\xi^{\prime}_{3}\,.$  (12)

Now, let us combine the elements and express the spectral representation for $\pi N$ TDAs in terms of quadruple distributions. A key insight is that after selecting the variables $\sigma_{3}$, $\rho_{3}$, $\omega_{3}$, and $\nu_{3}$ to perform the constrained integration over $\beta_{1}$, $\beta_{2}$, $\beta_{3}$, and $\alpha_{1}$, $\alpha_{2}$, $\alpha_{3}$, the natural variables on which $\pi N$ TDAs depend are

$\displaystyle w_{3}=\frac{x_{3}-x_{1}-x_{2}}{2},\ \ \ v_{3}=\frac{x_{1}-x_{2}}{2}\,.$  (54)

By expressing $\beta_{i}$ and $\alpha_{i}$ in terms of $\sigma_{3}$, $\rho_{3}$, $\omega_{3}$, and $\nu_{3}$, the two delta functions in the definition (39) can be rewritten as

$\displaystyle\left.\delta(x_{1}-\xi-\beta_{1}-\alpha_{1}\xi)\,\delta(x_{2}-\xi-\beta_{2}-\alpha_{2}\xi)\right|_{x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}=2\xi}$

$\displaystyle=\delta(w_{3}-\sigma_{3}-\omega_{3}\xi)\,\delta(v_{3}-\rho_{3}-\nu_{3}\xi)\,.$  (55)

This demonstrates that, at the level of delta functions, the dependencies of $w_{3}$ and $v_{3}$ on the spectral parameters are effectively factorized.

To analyze the structure of $\pi N$ TDA in quark-diquark coordinates, we focus on the ERBL-like and DGLAP-like domains. For the remainder of this section, we utilize the specific quark-diquark coordinate system defined as:

$$
v \equiv v_3 = \frac{x_1 - x_2}{2}, \quad w \equiv w_3 = \frac{x_3 - x_1 - x_2}{2}, \quad \xi' \equiv \xi'_3 = \frac{\xi - w_3}{2},
$$

where the extension to other scenarios follows directly.

The Mellin moments of $\pi N$ transition distribution amplitudes (TDAs) in variables $x_1$, $x_2$, $x_3$, evaluated using the weighting function 

$$
\int dx_1 dx_2 dx_3 \, \delta(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 - 2\xi),
$$ 

are of significant theoretical relevance. In the quark-diquark coordinate system (14), these integrals can be transformed into the expression

$$
\int_{-1+\xi}^{1+\xi} dx_1 dx_2 dx_3 \, \delta(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 - 2\xi) \, x_1^{n_1} x_2^{n_2} x_3^{n_3} H(x_1, x_2, x_3 = 2\xi - x_1 - x_2)
$$

$$
= \int_{-1}^{1} dw \int_{-1+|\xi - \xi'|}^{1 - |\xi - \xi'|} dv \, (v + \xi')^{n_1} (-v + \xi')^{n_2} (w + \xi)^{n_3} H(w, v, \xi),
$$

where $\xi'$ is an auxiliary variable introduced to simplify the integration limits.

In terms of quark-diquark variables $\tilde{\omega}=1-y_{1}-y_{2}$ and $\tilde{\nu}=\frac{y_{1}-y_{2}}{2}$, the function $\Phi^{\rm as}$ is expressed as:

\[
\Phi^{\rm as}(\tilde{\omega},\,\tilde{\nu})=\frac{15}{4}(\tilde{\omega}+1)(\tilde{\nu}+\frac{1-\tilde{\omega}}{2})(-\tilde{\nu}+\frac{1-\tilde{\omega}}{2})\,.\tag{91}
\]

It is important to note that:

\[
\int_{0}^{2}dy_{1}dy_{2}dy_{3}\,\delta(2-y_{1}-y_{2}-y_{3})\Phi^{\rm as}(y_{1},\,y_{2},\,y_{3})\equiv\int_{-1}^{1}d\tilde{\omega}\int_{-\frac{1-\tilde{\omega}}{2}}^{\frac{1-\tilde{\omega}}{2}}d\tilde{\nu}\Phi^{\rm as}(\tilde{\omega},\,\tilde{\nu})=1\tag{92}.
\]

In this appendix, we provide the result for the $\pi N$ TDA $H(w,v,\xi)$ in the ERBL-like and DGLAP-like type I and II domains for the case where $-1\leq\xi<0$, which is relevant, for example, to the process $\bar{N}N\rightarrow\pi\gamma^{*}$ in the forward region (16).
The Mellin moments of the $\pi N$ transition distribution amplitudes (TDAs) in the variables $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$ are calculated utilizing a specified weighting function.
The quantities of major theoretical importance can be expressed in terms of quark-diquark coordinates, where the corresponding integrals can be reformulated as

For $\xi\geq 0$, the validity of the following inequalities can be confirmed:

\[
\begin{cases}w<-\xi\\ \xi^{\prime}>\xi\end{cases};\ \ \ \text{and}\ \ \ \begin{cases}w>-\xi\\ \xi^{\prime}<\xi\end{cases}.\ (19)
\]

The equality $\xi^{\prime}=\xi$ holds on the line $w=-\xi$. Consequently, the entire domain of definition of $\pi N$ TDA in quark-diquark coordinates, as illustrated in Figure 5, can be expressed as:

\[
-1\leq w\leq 1;\ \ \ \ -1+|\xi-\xi^{\prime}|\leq v\leq 1-|\xi-\xi^{\prime}|.\ (20)
\]

The eight remaining cases (74) can be addressed using the same pattern as previously described. By evaluating the quadruple integral (58) for $H(w, v, \xi)$ with $\xi \geq 0$, we find the following expressions:

- When $w$ and $v$ lie outside the domain defined by $w \in [-1, 1]$ and $v \in [-1 + |\xi - \xi'|, 1 - |\xi - \xi'|]$, the integral equals zero.

- For $w \in [-1, -\xi]$ and $v \in [\xi', 1 - \xi' + \xi]$ (DGLAP-like type I domain):

  $\displaystyle H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{2(v\xi - \xi')}{1 - \xi^2}}^{\frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi}} d\sigma \int_{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 - \xi}}^{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 + \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}) \,.$ (81)

- For $w \in [-1, -\xi]$ and $v \in [-\xi', \xi']$ (DGLAP-like type II domain):

  $\displaystyle H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w - \xi}{1 + \xi}}^{\frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi}} d\sigma \int_{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 + \xi}}^{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 + \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}) \,.$ (82)

- For $w \in [-1, -\xi]$ and $v \in [-1 + \xi' - \xi, -\xi']$ (DGLAP-like type I domain):

  $\displaystyle H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{-\frac{2(v\xi + \xi')}{1 - \xi^2}}^{\frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi}} d\sigma \int_{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 + \xi}}^{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 - \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}) \,.$ (83)

- For $w \in [-\xi, \xi]$ and $v \in [\xi', 1 - \xi + \xi']$ (DGLAP-like type II domain):

  $\displaystyle H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{2(v\xi - \xi')}{1 - \xi^2}}^{\frac{w + \xi}{1 + \xi}} d\sigma \int_{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 - \xi}}^{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 + \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}) \,.$ (84)

- For $w \in [-\xi, \xi]$ and $v \in [-\xi', \xi']$ (ERBL-like domain):

  $\displaystyle H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w - \xi}{1 + \xi}}^{\frac{w + \xi}{1 + \xi}} d\sigma \int_{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 + \xi}}^{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 + \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}) \,.$ (85)

- For $w \in [-\xi, \xi]$ and $v \in [-1 + \xi - \xi', -\xi']$ (DGLAP-like type II domain):

  $\displaystyle H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{-\frac{2(v\xi + \xi')}{1 - \xi^2}}^{\frac{w + \xi}{1 + \xi}} d\sigma \int_{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 + \xi}}^{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 - \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}) \,.$ (86)

- For $w \in [\xi, 1]$ and $v \in [-\xi', 1 - \xi + \xi']$, the result matches (84) as it should since this is part of the same DGLAP type II domain. This ensures $H(w, v, \xi)$ is smooth at $w = \xi$, as there is no change in evolution properties at this line ($w_i = \xi \Leftrightarrow x_i = 2\xi$).

- For $w \in [\xi, 1]$ and $v \in [\xi', -\xi']$ (DGLAP-like type I domain):

  $\displaystyle H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w - \xi}{1 - \xi}}^{\frac{w + \xi}{1 + \xi}} d\sigma \int_{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 - \xi}}^{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 - \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}) \,.$ (87)

- For $w \in [\xi, 1]$ and $v \in [-1 + \xi - \xi', \xi']$, the result coincides with (86) because this is part of the same DGLAP-like type II domain.

For values of $w$ and $v$ outside the specified domain defined by $-1 \leq w \leq 1$ and $-1 + |\xi - \xi'| \leq v \leq 1 - |\xi - \xi'|$, the integral evaluates to zero. Within the DGLAP-like type II domain where $w \in [-1, \xi]$ and $v \in [\xi', 1 - \xi' + \xi]$, the function $H(w, v, \xi)$ is expressed as:

$$
H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi}}^{\frac{2(v\xi - \xi')}{1 - \xi^2}} d\sigma \int_{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 + \xi}}^{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 - \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}).
$$

For the DGLAP-like type I domain with $w \in [-1, \xi]$ and $v \in [-\xi', \xi']$, the function is given by:

$$
H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi}}^{\frac{w - \xi}{1 + \xi}} d\sigma \int_{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 + \xi}}^{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 + \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}).
$$

In the DGLAP-like type II domain where $w \in [-1, \xi]$ and $v \in [-1 + \xi' - \xi, -\xi']$, the expression for $H(w, v, \xi)$ becomes:

$$
H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi}}^{-\frac{2(v\xi + \xi')}{1 - \xi^2}} d\sigma \int_{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 - \xi}}^{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 + \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}).
$$

For $w \in [\xi, -\xi]$ and $v \in [-\xi', 1 - \xi' + \xi]$, the result aligns with equation (B16). In the ERBL-like domain where $w \in [\xi, -\xi]$ and $v \in [\xi', -\xi']$, the function is:

$$
H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi}}^{\frac{w - \xi}{1 - \xi}} d\sigma \int_{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 - \xi}}^{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 - \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}).
$$

For $w \in [\xi, -\xi]$ and $v \in [-1 + \xi' - \xi, \xi']$, the result coincides with equation (B18). In the DGLAP-like type I domain where $w \in [-\xi, 1]$ and $v \in [-\xi', 1 - \xi + \xi']$, the function is:

$$
H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w + \xi}{1 + \xi}}^{\frac{2(v\xi - \xi')}{1 - \xi^2}} d\sigma \int_{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 + \xi}}^{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 - \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}).
$$

For $w \in [-\xi, 1]$ and $v \in [\xi', -\xi']$ within the DGLAP-like type II domain, the expression is:

$$
H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w + \xi}{1 + \xi}}^{-\frac{2(v\xi + \xi')}{1 - \xi^2}} d\sigma \int_{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 - \xi}}^{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 - \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}).
$$

Finally, in the DGLAP-like type I domain where $w \in [\xi, 1]$ and $v \in [-1 + \xi - \xi', \xi']$, the function is:

$$
H(w, v, \xi) = \frac{1}{\xi^2} \int_{\frac{w + \xi}{1 + \xi}}^{\frac{w - \xi}{1 - \xi}} d\sigma \int_{\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 - \xi}}^{-\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 + \xi}} d\rho \, F(\sigma, \rho, \frac{w - \sigma}{\xi}, \frac{v - \rho}{\xi}).
$$
The domain defined by $w \in [\xi, 1]$ with $v \in [-\xi^{\prime}, 1 - \xi + \xi^{\prime}]$ or $v \in [-1 + \xi - \xi^{\prime}, \xi^{\prime}]$ corresponds to the DGLAP-like type II domain.

We will now demonstrate that the spectral representation (56) exhibits the required support characteristics for any $\xi \in (0, 1]$. A detailed result for $\xi \in [-1, 0)$ is provided in Appendix B.

The abscissa of the apex of this triangular domain is given by 

$$\sigma = \frac{2(v\xi - \xi')} {1 - \xi^2} \,.$$ (78)

It can be verified that when $v = \xi'$, the abscissa of the apex aligns with the left boundary of the strip defined by (77):

$$\left. \frac{2(v\xi - \xi')}{1 - \xi^2} \right|_{v = \xi'} = \frac{w - \xi}{1 + \xi} \,,$$ (79)

whereas for $v = 1 - \xi' + \xi$, it aligns with the right boundary of the strip (77):

$$\left. \frac{2(v\xi - \xi')}{1 - \xi^2} \right|_{v = 1 - \xi' + \xi} = \frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi} \,.$$ (80)

When $v > 1 - \xi' + \xi$, the apex of the triangular domain lies to the right of the strip (77), resulting in no overlap with it. Consequently, the double integral (67) vanishes for $v \geq 1 - \xi' + \xi$, thereby establishing the desired support property of $H(w, v, \xi)$.

The same calculation at $\xi=1$ with $h(\kappa)=1$ is presented in Ref. [ 41 ]. For the current purpose, let's just note that the intercept is universal and that the slope $\mathcal{A}$ is proportional to the profile function $h$ at the saturation scale.

To express the spectral representation for \( H(x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}=2\xi - x_{1} - x_{2}, \xi) \), we introduce two sets of spectral parameters, \(\beta_{1,2,3}\) and \(\alpha_{1,2,3}\). The momentum fractions \(x_{1,2,3}\) are decomposed in terms of these spectral parameters as follows:

\[
x_{1} = \xi + \beta_{1} + \alpha_{1}\xi, \quad x_{2} = \xi + \beta_{2} + \alpha_{2}\xi, \quad x_{3} = \xi + \beta_{3} + \alpha_{3}\xi. \quad (37)
\]

To satisfy these constraints, we impose the conditions:

\[
\beta_{1} + \beta_{2} + \beta_{3} = 0, \quad \alpha_{1} + \alpha_{2} + \alpha_{3} = -1. \quad (38)
\]

Figure 8 presents the $\pi N$ TDA $H(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},\xi)$ for $\xi=0.5$, plotted as a function of the three dependent light-cone momentum fractions $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$ (where $x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}=2\xi$) in barycentric coordinates. Thick solid lines are used to depict the continuation of the edges of the equilateral triangle, forming the ERBL-like domain, as shown in Figure 3.

To begin with, we determine the analog of the ERBL region, where the three longitudinal momentum fractions carried by the quarks are all positive. In the barycentric coordinate system, the ERBL-like region corresponds to the interior of an equilateral triangle with a height of $2\xi$ (as shown in Figure 3). For the DGLAP-like domains, the boundaries are defined by the lines:

$\displaystyle x_{1}=-1+\xi\,;\ \ x_{1}=0\,;\ \ x_{1}=1+\xi\,;$

$\displaystyle x_{2}=-1+\xi\,;\ \ x_{2}=0\,;\ \ x_{2}=1+\xi\,;$

$\displaystyle x_{3}=-1+\xi\,;\ \ x_{3}=0\,;\ \ x_{3}=1+\xi\,.$  (9)

These definitions are guided by the following considerations:

- The domain of definition for $\pi N$ TDA should be symmetric in $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$.

- When $\xi=1$, the domain should coincide with the ERBL-like domain where the nucleon DA is defined, represented by an equilateral triangle in barycentric coordinates.

- Setting any $x_{i}$ to zero should recover the usual domain of definition for GPDs in terms of the remaining two variables.

By utilizing three distinct sets of natural spectral parameters, one can express three equivalent spectral representations in terms of three corresponding sets of quark-diquark coordinates \(w_{i}\), \(v_{i}\) with \(i=1,\,2,\,3\) as defined in (11):

\[
H(w_{i},\,v_{i},\,\xi) = \int_{-1}^{1}d\sigma_{i}\int_{-1+\frac{|\sigma_{i}|}{2}}^{1-\frac{|\sigma_{i}|}{2}}d\rho_{i}\int_{-1+|\sigma_{i}|}^{1-|\rho_{i}-\frac{\sigma_{i}}{2}|-|\rho_{i}+\frac{\sigma_{i}}{2}|}d\omega_{i}\int_{-\frac{1}{2}+|\rho_{i}-\frac{\sigma_{i}}{2}|+\frac{\omega_{i}}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}-|\rho_{i}+\frac{\sigma_{i}}{2}|-\frac{\omega_{i}}{2}}d\nu_{i}\delta(w_{i}-\sigma_{i}-\omega_{i}\xi)
\]

\[
\times\delta(v_{i}-\rho_{i}-\nu_{i}\xi)\,F_{i}(\sigma_{i},\,\rho_{i},\,\omega_{i},\,\nu_{i})\,,
\]

where \(F_{3}(\sigma_{3},\,\rho_{3},\,\omega_{3},\,\nu_{3})\) is defined in (57) and

\[
F_{1}(\sigma_{1},\,\rho_{1},\,\omega_{1},\,\nu_{1}) \equiv F(\sigma_{1},\,\rho_{1}-\frac{\sigma_{1}}{2}\,,-\rho_{1}-\frac{\sigma_{1}}{2},\,\omega_{1},\,\nu_{1}-\frac{1+\omega_{1}}{2},\,-\nu_{1}-\frac{1+\omega_{1}}{2})\,;
\]

\[
F_{2}(\sigma_{2},\,\rho_{2},\,\omega_{2},\,\nu_{2}) \equiv F(-\rho_{2}-\frac{\sigma_{2}}{2},\,\sigma_{2},\,\rho_{2}-\frac{\sigma_{2}}{2},\,-\nu_{2}-\frac{1+\omega_{2}}{2},\,\omega_{2},\,\nu_{2}-\frac{1+\omega_{2}}{2})\,.
\]
We propose the introduction of the notations $\xi^{\prime}_{1}$, $\xi^{\prime}_{2}$, and $\xi^{\prime}_{3}$ to represent the fraction of longitudinal momentum associated with the diquark.

In the following, we examine the scenario of $\pi N$ TDAs. Consider the light-cone momentum fractions $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$, and $x_{3}$, which satisfy the constraint $x_{1} + x_{2} + x_{3} = 2\xi$, where $\xi \geq 0$. These fractions represent the momentum carried by the three quarks relative to the average hadron momentum $P = \frac{p_{1} + p_{\pi}}{2}$. It is advantageous to illustrate the support characteristics of $\pi N$ TDAs using barycentric coordinates (Mandelstam plane).

The inverse transformation, as given in equation (95), is expressed as:

\[
\displaystyle\omega=\tilde{\omega}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\left(\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+|\sigma|\right)\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|-|\sigma|\right)\,;
\]

\[
\displaystyle\nu=\tilde{\nu}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\left(\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+|\sigma|\right)\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|-\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|\right)\,.
\]

This transformation facilitates the computation of the integrals involved in determining the $(N-n,\,n)$-th Mellin moments (where $N\geq n\geq 0$) in the $(w,\,v)$ plane of $\pi N$ TDAs:

\[
\displaystyle\int_{-1+|\sigma|}^{1-|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}|-|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}|}d\omega\int_{-1+|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}|+\frac{1+\omega}{2}}^{1-|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}|-\frac{1+\omega}{2}}d\nu\,\omega^{N-n}\nu^{n}h(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)\,.
\]

Let us examine the scenario where \( w \) lies within the interval \([-1, -\xi]\) and \( v \) spans from \(\xi'\) to infinity, as described in equation (75). The integration region in the \((\sigma, \rho)\) plane is established by the overlap of the domains defined by inequalities (68), (71), (72), and (73):

\[
\rho \geq -\frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v - \xi'}{1 - \xi}, \quad \rho \leq \frac{\sigma}{2} + \frac{v + \xi'}{1 + \xi}, \quad \rho \leq \frac{\sigma}{2\xi} + \frac{\xi'}{\xi}, \quad |\rho| \leq 1 - \frac{|\sigma|}{2}, \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{w - \xi}{1 + \xi} \leq \sigma \leq \frac{w + \xi}{1 - \xi}.
\]

This intersection is further confined by the strip specified in equation (77). The resulting domain is illustrated in Figure 6. The thick solid lines denote the boundaries imposed by the first two inequalities of equation (76). The thin solid line represents the constraint \(\rho \leq 1 - \frac{\sigma}{2}\), while the dashed line corresponds to the boundary set by the third inequality in equation (76). The shaded region signifies the final integration domain for the ranges \(-1 \leq w \leq -\xi\) and \(\xi' \leq v \leq 1 - \xi' + \xi\), as outlined in equation (67).

To characterize the support properties of $\pi N$ TDAs, we begin by examining the behavior of GPDs (as illustrated in Fig. 2a). Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ denote the fractions (measured with respect to the average nucleon momentum $P = \frac{p_1 + p_2}{2}$) of the light-cone momentum carried by a quark and antiquark within the nucleon, respectively, with the condition $x_1 + x_2 = 2\xi$. In the ERBL region, both $x_1$ and $x_2$ are positive. The variable $x$ is defined as 

\[
x = \frac{x_1 - x_2}{2}.
\]

Within the ERBL region, $x_1$ and $x_2$ are confined to the interval $[0, 2\xi]$, leading to $x \in [-\xi, \xi]$. In contrast, the DGLAP region is characterized by either $x_1 \in [2\xi, 1+\xi]$ and $x_2 \in [-1+\xi, 0]$ or the opposite, resulting in $x \in [\xi, 1]$ and $x \in [-1, -\xi]$, respectively.

The non-perturbative aspects of hard processes involving hadrons are captured by universal partonic distributions, including parton distribution functions, fragmentation functions, distribution amplitudes, and their extensions. In the absence of a complete theoretical understanding of quark and gluon confinement dynamics, these distributions must be modeled in accordance with the fundamental principles of the underlying field theory, such as Lorentz invariance and causality. Spectral representation of hadronic matrix elements provides a sophisticated framework for achieving this objective. The double distribution approach for generalized parton distributions (GPDs) has served as the foundation for numerous successful phenomenological GPD models.

In this study, we analyze the dependence of the invariant functions \( V_{i} \), \( A_{i} \), and \( T_{i} \), which appear as coefficients of the independent spin-flavor structures in equation (5), on the variables \( x_{1} \), \( x_{2} \), \( x_{2} \), and the skewness parameter \( \xi \). It is important to note that our subsequent analysis is entirely general, treating all invariant functions uniformly. To simplify the discussion, we adopt the same notation for all invariant functions, denoted as

\[
\displaystyle H(x_{1},\,x_{2},\,x_{3},\,\xi,\,t)\equiv\left\{V_{i},\,A_{i},\,T_{i}\right\}(x_{1},\,x_{2},\,x_{3},\,\xi,\,u)\,.
\]

(6)

To date, no satisfactory phenomenological parametrization has been available for TDAs involving states with unequal baryon numbers across their entire domain of definition, as evidenced in the quark model framework outlined in reference (18). A comprehensive parametrization must inherently satisfy the fundamental condition of Lorentz covariance, which is reflected in the polynomial property of Mellin moments concerning the relevant light-cone momentum fraction throughout their complete domain of applicability. In the context of GPDs, a particularly effective method to address this requirement involves the use of spectral representation. The corresponding spectral characteristics were derived using alpha-representation techniques (19). Radyushkin’s factorized Ansatz, rooted in the double distribution formalism for GPDs (21), has served as the foundational basis for numerous successful phenomenological GPD models, as demonstrated in (25).

The ERBL-like and DGLAP-like domains for $\pi N$ TDA in quark-diquark coordinates (14) are illustrated in Figure 5. In this coordinate system, the ERBL-like region is represented by a central isosceles triangular domain, bounded by the lines:

$\displaystyle v=-\xi^{\prime}\ \ (x_{1}=0)\,;\ \ \ v=\xi^{\prime}\ \ (x_{2}=0)\,;\ \ \ w=-\xi\ \ (x_{3}=0)\,.$ (15)

The DGLAP-like type I regions are characterized by three smaller isosceles triangular domains, while the DGLAP-like type II region corresponds to three trapezoidal domains.
Our method can also be extended to the case of $4$-quark correlators, which are significant for describing higher twist contributions.

The formal expression of $\pi N$ TDAs can be represented symbolically by equation (5):

\[
\int\left[\prod_{i=1}^{3}\frac{dz^{-}_{i}}{2\pi}\right]e^{ix_{1}(P\cdot z_{1})+ix_{2}(P\cdot z_{2})+ix_{3}(P\cdot z_{3})}\times
\]

\[
\left.\langle\pi(P+\Delta/2)|\epsilon_{abc}\psi_{j_{1}}^{a}(z_{1})\psi_{j_{2}}^{b}(z_{2})\psi_{j_{3}}^{c}(z_{3})|N(P-\Delta/2)\rangle\right|_{z_{i}^{+}=z_{i}^{\bot}=0}
\]

\[
\sim\delta(2\xi-x_{1}-x_{2}-x_{3})H_{j_{1}\,j_{2}\,j_{3}}(x_{1},\,x_{2},\,x_{3},\,\xi,\,u)\,.
\]

In this equation, $j_{1,2,3}$ denote spin-flavor indices, while $a$, $b$, and $c$ represent color indices. The Fourier transform of the matrix element of the three-point light-cone quark operator is decomposed into a set of independent spin-flavor structures, each multiplied by corresponding invariant functions, which are referred to as $\pi N$ TDAs.

The function $\Phi^{\rm as}(\tilde{\omega},\,\tilde{\nu})$ is valid within the domain 

$\displaystyle-1\leq\tilde{\omega}\leq 1\,;\ \ \ \text{and }\ \ \ -\frac{1-\tilde{\omega}}{2}\leq\tilde{\nu}\leq\frac{1-\tilde{\omega}}{2}\,,$ (93) 

whereas $h(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)$ is defined over the region 

$\displaystyle-1+|\sigma|\leq\omega\leq 1-|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}|-|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}|\,;$ 

$\displaystyle-\frac{1-\omega}{2}+|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}|\leq\nu\leq\frac{1-\omega}{2}-|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}|\,.$ (94) 

To bridge these definitions, the following substitution is proposed: 

$\displaystyle\tilde{\omega}=\frac{\omega+\frac{1}{2}\left(\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|-|\sigma|\right)}{1-\frac{1}{2}\left(\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+|\sigma|\right)}\,;$ 

$\displaystyle\tilde{\nu}=\frac{(1-\tilde{\omega})}{2}\frac{2\nu-\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|}{1-\omega-\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|-\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|}\,.$ (95) 

Substituting these expressions leads to the profile function $h(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)$ being expressed as 

$\displaystyle h(\sigma,\,\rho,\,\omega,\,\nu)=\frac{15}{16}\frac{\left(1+2\nu-\omega-2\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|\right)\left(1-2\nu-\omega-2\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|\right)(1-|\sigma|+\omega)}{\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\left(\left|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+\left|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}\right|+|\sigma|\right)\right)^{5}}\,.$ (96) 

Verification confirms that this profile function satisfies the normalization condition (89). Notably, when expressed in terms of the original spectral parameters $\alpha_{1}$, $\alpha_{2}$, $\alpha_{3}$, and $\beta_{1}$, $\beta_{2}$, $\beta_{3}$, which satisfy $\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\alpha_{3}=-1$ and $\beta_{1}+\beta_{2}+\beta_{3}=0$, the profile function (96) assumes a highly symmetric form: 

$\displaystyle\left.h(\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\beta_{3}\,;\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2},\alpha_{3})\right|_{\sum_{i}\beta_{i}=0\atop\sum_{i}\alpha_{i}=-1}=\frac{15}{4}\frac{\prod_{i=1}^{3}(1+\alpha_{i}-|\beta_{i}|)}{(1-\frac{1}{2}(|\beta_{1}|+|\beta_{2}|+|\beta_{3}|))^{5}}\Big{|}_{\sum_{i}\beta_{i}=0\atop\sum_{i}\alpha_{i}=-1}\,.$

We analyze the structure of the ERBL-like and DGLAP-like domains for $\pi N$ TDA in quark-diquark coordinates. For the remainder of this section, we utilize the specific quark-diquark coordinate system defined in (11):

The parameter $w$ lies within the interval $[-\xi, \xi]$, while $v$ is confined to either $[\xi^{\prime}, 1-\xi+\xi^{\prime}]$ or $[-1+\xi-\xi^{\prime}, -\xi^{\prime}]$, thereby defining the DGLAP-like type II domain.

Analogously, the inequality (70) for $\xi \geq 0$ is equivalent to

$\displaystyle \rho \leq \frac{\sigma}{2\xi} + \frac{\xi'}{\xi} \quad \text{for} \quad v \geq |\xi'|; \quad \rho \geq -\frac{\sigma}{2\xi} - \frac{\xi'}{\xi} \quad \text{for} \quad v \leq -|\xi'|;$

$\displaystyle \sigma \geq \frac{w - \xi}{1 + \xi} \quad \text{for} \quad \begin{cases} v \geq -\xi' \\ v \leq \xi' \end{cases}; \quad \sigma \geq \frac{w - \xi}{1 - \xi} \quad \text{for} \quad \begin{cases} v \leq -\xi' \\ v \geq \xi' \end{cases}.$ (73)

To enhance the conciseness of our formulations moving forward, we shall omit the subscript $i$ associated with the quark-diquark coordinates $w_{i}$ and $v_{i}$, as well as the spectral parameters $\sigma_{i}$, $\rho_{i}$, $\omega_{i}$, $\nu_{i}$, and the spectral densities $F_{i}$. The analysis that follows remains applicable to all three cases where $i=1,\,2,\,3$.

We are now proceeding to perform the integration over $\nu$ by utilizing the remaining $\delta$-function. The domain of integration in $\sigma$ and $\rho$ is specified by the inequalities:

$\displaystyle-1+\frac{|\sigma|}{2}\leq\rho\leq 1-\frac{|\sigma|}{2}\,;$  (68)

$\displaystyle-\frac{1}{2}+|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}|+\frac{w-\sigma}{2\xi}\leq\frac{v-\rho}{\xi}\leq\frac{1}{2}-|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}|-\frac{w-\sigma}{2\xi}\,;$  (69)

$\displaystyle 1-|\rho-\frac{\sigma}{2}|-|\rho+\frac{\sigma}{2}|\geq\frac{w-\sigma}{\xi}\,;$  (70)

as well as the integration limits in $\sigma$ which depend on the value of $w$ (see (67)).

In summary, the results can be categorized as follows:
- For \( w \in [-1; -\xi] \), the domain corresponds to DGLAP-like type I when \( v \in [\xi'; 1 - \xi' + \xi] \) or \( v \in [-1 + \xi' - \xi; -\xi'] \).
- The DGLAP-like type II domain is characterized by \( w \in [-1; -\xi] \) and \( v \in [-\xi'; \xi'] \).
- The ERBL-like domain is defined by \( w \in [-\xi; \xi] \) and \( v \in [-\xi'; \xi'] \).
- For \( w \in [-\xi; \xi] \), the DGLAP-like type II domain applies when \( v \in [\xi'; 1 - \xi + \xi'] \) or \( v \in [-1 + \xi - \xi'; -\xi'] \).
- The DGLAP-like type II domain also includes \( w \in [\xi; 1] \) with \( v \in [-\xi'; 1 - \xi + \xi'] \) or \( v \in [-1 + \xi - \xi'; \xi'] \).
- Finally, \( w \in [\xi; 1] \) with \( v \in [\xi'; -\xi'] \) corresponds to the DGLAP-like type I domain.

The convergence requirements for integrals (81)–(87) in the context of $\pi N$ TDA impose constraints on the behavior of the function $f(\sigma,\rho)$ at the boundaries of its domain. Specifically, $f(\sigma,\rho)$ must vanish at least as a certain power of the relevant variables near these boundaries. For simplicity, we propose the following functional form for $f(\sigma,\rho)$:

\[
f(\sigma,\rho)=\theta(-1\leq\sigma\leq 1)\,\theta(-1+\frac{|\sigma|}{2}\leq\rho\leq 1-\frac{|\sigma|}{2})\,
\]
\[

\]

In terms of the original spectral parameters $\beta_{i}$, subject to the condition $\sum_{i}\beta_{i}=0$, equation (103) can be expressed as:

\[
\left.f(\beta_{1},\,\beta_{2},\,\beta_{3})\right|_{\sum_{i}\beta_{i}=0}=\frac{40}{47}\,\prod_{i=1}^{3}\theta(|\beta_{i}|\leq 1)(1-\beta_{i}^{2})\big{|}_{\sum_{i}\beta_{i}=0}\,.\quad (104)
\]

The function $f(\sigma,\rho)$ vanishes on the boundary of its domain and is normalized according to:

\[
\int_{-1}^{1}d\sigma\int_{-1+\frac{|\sigma|}{2}}^{1-\frac{|\sigma|}{2}}f(\sigma,\,\rho)=1\,.\quad (105)
\]

It is important to note that this normalization (105) is specific to our toy model. More sophisticated modeling of $\pi N$ TDAs, aimed at accurately describing physical observables, would necessitate a more complex form for $f(\sigma,\rho)$.
