Our chosen backbone for feature extraction is the ResNet34 architecture, whose intricate configuration is delineated in Figure 7. It is noteworthy that the feature maps resulting from up-convolution may exhibit disparate spatial dimensions compared to those intended for concatenation, thereby necessitating a resizing procedure to ensure dimensional congruence, which we achieve through the application of bilinear interpolation techniques.

The efficacy of BARF in jointly optimizing NeRF with imperfect camera poses can be substantially ascribed to its innovative coarse-to-fine training paradigm, which adeptly mitigates the discrepancy in gradients between low-frequency and high-frequency components. By initially focusing on the low-frequency aspects while suppressing the high-frequency elements, the model establishes a stable foundation; subsequently, it proceeds to refine the high-frequency components, thereby precluding the dominance of gradients derived from the latter. This strategic approach is necessitated by the inherent properties of positional encodings, which, as evidenced by the formula $\frac{\partial\gamma_{k}(\mathbf{P})}{\partial\mathbf{P}}=2^{k}\pi\cdot[-\sin(2^{k}\pi\mathbf{P}),\ \cos(2^{k}\pi\mathbf{P})]$ - where $\gamma_{k}(\mathbf{P})$ is defined as $[\cos(2^{k}\pi\mathbf{P}),\ \sin(2^{k}\pi\mathbf{P})]$ - tend to prioritize high-frequency signals, denoted by large $k$ values, due to their exaggerated gradient magnitudes.

Our evaluation also encompasses seven scenes from the ScanNet dataset, with qualitative outcomes of the rendered images and corresponding geometries post-fine-tuning presented in Figures 13 and 14, and quantitative results summarized in Table 4. Notably, our approach demonstrates superior performance compared to IBRNet, with a significant margin. The suboptimal performance of IBRNet on this dataset can be attributed to its reliance on inaccurate camera poses, whereas our method circumvents this limitation by not requiring precomputed camera poses, instead utilizing accurately regressed poses to facilitate high-quality image rendering.

GeNeRFs initiate the process by extracting features from 2D images through the projection of a point onto a feature map, utilizing a differentiable bilinear interpolation function and a reprojection function that maps points from a world frame to an image plane, taking into account the camera poses of the reference and nearby view images, as well as the 3D point coordinates and depth information derived from camera normalized pixel coordinates.

Our methodology and IBRNet undergo pretraining on a comprehensive dataset comprising 63 scenes from IBRNet, 33 real-world scenes captured using a handheld device from LLFF with camera poses derived from COLMAP, and 20 indoor scenes from ScanNet with poses obtained through BundleFusion, noting that while ground truth camera poses are available, they are not utilized in our approach.

To facilitate a more seamless joint training process, our approach draws inspiration from BANet and utilizes a Feature Pyramid Network as the foundation for our feature extraction. When provided with a cost feature map, as defined in equation 11, our primary goal is to refine the relative positioning of cameras, denoted as $\mathbf{P}_{ij}$, and generate an accurate depth map, represented as $\mathbf{D}_{i}$.

Our evaluation results on the ScanNet dataset, comprising seven scenes, are presented, with qualitative assessments of rendered images and corresponding geometries after fine-tuning illustrated in Figures 13 and 14, and quantitative results summarized in Table 4. Notably, our approach significantly surpasses IBRNet, whose performance is hindered by inaccurate camera pose estimations. In contrast, our method achieves high-quality image rendering without relying on precomputed camera poses, instead utilizing accurately regressed camera poses to outperform IBRNet by a substantial margin.

The evaluation of our novel view synthesis method is based on metrics including PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS, with quantitative results presented in Table 1, demonstrating superior rendering quality compared to BARF, GARF, and even IBRNet in certain scenes, despite IBRNet's advantage of known camera poses. Qualitative results on the LLFF dataset, illustrated in Figure 8, show per-scene finetuned visual outcomes for IBRNet and our method, with our approach yielding higher-quality images in scenes such as horns and orchids, and avoiding noticeable artifacts present in IBRNet's rendering of the room scene. Additionally, Figure 5 presents a comparison of rendering results on the ScanNet dataset, where our method outperforms IBRNet, and highlights discrepancies in camera pose accuracy, with further results available in the supplementary materials.

Our method's generalization capabilities are demonstrated through a comparison of its performance with and without fine-tuning, as presented in Tables 1 and 2. Notably, our approach outperforms BARF and GARF in novel view synthesis, even in the absence of per-scene fine-tuning. In terms of pose accuracy, our method achieves a rotation error of less than 13 degrees for most scenes in the LLFF dataset without fine-tuning, representing a significant reduction in computational cost compared to per-scene training from scratch. Furthermore, fine-tuning yields a rotation error of less than 1.5 degrees for all scenes except the leaves scene, underscoring the method's generalizability across scenes. Additionally, we contend that the camera poses generated by COLMAP should be considered pseudo ground truth, and our own camera poses are superior due to the higher rendering quality of DBARF compared to IBRNet on several scenes, including fern, flower, and fortress.

In various frequency estimation applications, the high signal frequencies often render data acquisition at the Nyquist rate impractical due to hardware constraints. This paper presents a innovative subspace-based approach to estimate frequencies using two sub-Nyquist sample sequences, assuming the under-sampling ratios are coprime integers. By examining the effects of under-sampling and addressing aliasing issues, the proposed method combines the estimates from both sequences to accurately identify the true frequency components present in the signals. Notably, this approach requires minimal hardware and computational resources, and numerical simulations demonstrate its efficacy and precision even at significantly reduced sampling rates.

The ESPRIT technique, renowned for its robustness to noise as a super-resolution subspace method, is employed as the inaugural step in our approach. This selection enables the transformation of the generalized eigenvalues $\gamma$ in equation (10) into $\gamma={e^{j2\pi{f_{k}}\cdot p/{f_{H}}}}$, where the product of $p$ and $f_{k}$ divided by $f_{H}$ exceeds unity for certain values of $k$. Consequently, multiple estimated frequencies $\tilde{f}_{k}$ emerge, all of which fulfill the condition ${e^{j2\pi{\tilde{f}_{k}}\cdot p/{f_{H}}}}=\gamma$. By constraining the under-sampling ratio $p$ to positive integer values, we derive the principal argument of $\gamma$, denoted as $Arg(\gamma)$, to estimate frequencies ${\hat{f}_{k}}$ from sub-Nyquist samples as ${\hat{f}_{k}}={Arg(\gamma)\cdot f_{H}}/{(2\pi p)}$ for $k=1,2,\cdots,K$. These estimated frequencies, $\hat{f}_{k}$, represent a subset of possible frequencies, specifically those situated at the lower end of the frequency spectrum. The entirety of potential frequencies, referred to as eligible frequencies, can be further expanded upon.
This can be traced back to the fact that the condition number of the matrix product is large, which leads to an ill-conditioned linear system, and thus a large error is introduced in the solution.

We employ a MUSIC-like algorithm to estimate the actual frequencies, eliminating the need for a matching process. The secondary sample sequence can be represented as a summation of complex exponentials, where the frequency components are expressed in terms of the estimated frequency ${{\hat{f}}_{k}}$ and the integer ambiguity ${{\bar{l}}_{k}}$. By defining $g_{k}={\hat{f}}_{k}/f_{H}$, the time series of the secondary sampling can be rewritten, and the subspace spanned by the vector $\bm{a}(g_{k},{\bar{l}_{k}})$ can be expanded, allowing for the augmentation of $\bm{a}(g_{k},{\bar{l}_{k}})$ for each $k$. This augmentation transforms the original equation, resulting in a modified expression that facilitates further analysis.

The methodology outlined herein bears a resemblance to the MUSIC algorithm. By subjecting the auto-covariance matrix of $\bm{x}^{\prime}$ to eigen-decomposition, one obtains a factorization of ${\bm{R}_{{\bm{x^{\prime}x^{\prime}}}}}$ into a product of orthogonal matrices and a diagonal matrix, specifically ${\bm{R}_{{\bm{x^{\prime}x^{\prime}}}}}=\left[{{\bm{U}_{s}},{\bm{U}_{e}}}\right]\left[{\begin{array}[]{*{20}{c}}\bm{\Lambda}&0\\ 0&{{{\sigma^{\prime}}^{2}}{\bm{I}}}\end{array}}\right]\left[{\begin{array}[]{*{20}{c}}{\bm{U}_{s}^{H}}\\ {\bm{U}_{e}^{H}}\end{array}}\right]$, where the column vectors of $\bm{U}_{s}$ and $\bm{U}_{e}$ constitute an orthogonal basis for the signal and noise subspaces of $\bm{R}_{\bm{x^{\prime}x^{\prime}}}$, with the corresponding eigenvalues populating the diagonals of $\bm{\Lambda}$ and ${\sigma^{\prime}}^{2}{\bm{I}}$. Consequently, the pseudo-spectrum corresponding to the frequencies $\tilde{f}_{k}=g_{k}\cdot f_{H}+l_{k}\cdot f_{H}/p$ can be determined, and the maximum $K$ frequencies associated with the most prominent spectral peaks or those exceeding a predetermined threshold are deemed the estimated frequencies $\bar{f}_{k}$. Notably, this approach entails a reduced computational burden relative to traditional MUSIC, as the number of candidate frequencies is typically less than the number of uniform grid points. Furthermore, when $m^{\prime}$ is an integral multiple of $p$, the mode vectors ${\bm{a}_{r}}({g_{k}})$ corresponding to the same ${\hat{f}}_{k}$ exhibit mutual orthogonality, thereby facilitating the distinction of actual frequencies. The stipulation that $p$ and $q$ be relatively prime ensures high discriminability in the screening process, thereby enhancing the overall efficacy of the algorithm.

A signal comprising $K$ frequency components with unspecified amplitudes and phases, corrupted by additive zero-mean stationary complex white Gaussian noise, can be represented by the discrete-time model $x(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} s_k e^{j(2\pi f_k n \Delta t)} + e(n)$, where $f_k$ and $s_k$ denote the $k$th frequency and complex amplitude, respectively, and $e(n)$ represents the additive noise. Given a known upper bound on the frequency range and limited by low-rate analog-to-digital converters with sampling rates significantly below the Nyquist threshold, we challenge the prevailing approach of utilizing multi-channel measurement systems for frequency estimation, instead proposing that a double-channel sub-Nyquist sampling strategy with judiciously selected sampling rates can provide sufficient information for accurate estimation.

The ESPRIT technique is utilized as the initial step in our approach due to its robustness to noise as a super-resolution subspace method. By applying ESPRIT, the generalized eigenvalues are transformed into a form that allows for the estimation of frequencies from sub-Nyquist samples. The estimated frequencies are derived from the principal argument of the eigenvalues and are given by $\hat{f}_{k} = \frac{Arg(\gamma) \cdot f_{H}}{2\pi p}$, which represents a subset of possible frequencies occupying the lower end of the frequency domain. To obtain all eligible frequencies, these initial estimates can be expanded using the formula $\tilde{f}_{k} = \hat{f}_{k} + \frac{l_{k} \cdot f_{H}}{p}$, where $l_{k}$ ranges from 0 to $p-1$, thereby generating all possible frequency candidates.

In our initial simulation, conducted under ideal noise-free conditions, we illustrate the operational methodology of our approach. With the number of frequency components fixed at $K=2$ and amplitudes set to 0.8, Figure 2 presents a comparative plot of the actual frequencies $f_{k}$, the frequencies estimated using ESPRIT denoted as $\hat{f}_{k}$, the eligible frequencies $\tilde{f}_{k}$, and the corresponding pseudo-spectrum. The actual frequencies utilized in this scenario are 25Hz and 50Hz, with the eligible frequencies totaling $p\cdot K=10$. Notably, the eligible frequencies exhibit a consistent deviation from the true frequencies by a factor of ${l}_{k}\cdot f_{H}/p$, demonstrating a predictable pattern in the frequency estimation process.

In the second simulation, we configured the parameter K to 3 and assigned frequencies of 10Hz, 25Hz, and 50Hz. White Gaussian noise was introduced at a signal-to-noise ratio of 10dB. We applied the initial step of the ESPRIT method to the sequence sampled at a rate of 100/p and the secondary step of the MUSIC-like algorithm to the sequence sampled at a rate of 100/q, generating a pseudo-spectrum. By reversing the sequence order, we produced a second pseudo-spectrum, which, as illustrated in Figure 3, yielded consistent results with the first.

This study presents a novel approach for estimating frequency components from undersampled data, leveraging two subsets of sub-Nyquist samples to reduce hardware requirements. Provided that specific sampling rate conditions are met, the proposed method enables accurate frequency estimation. The manuscript is structured to first introduce the problem formulation and review existing ESPRIT methodology, followed by a detailed presentation of the new approach and its theoretical underpinnings. The efficacy of the method is then demonstrated through simulated results, with concluding remarks summarizing the key findings and implications.

In the subsequent discussion, the commutator and gradient force term are frequently referenced. To streamline the notation, we introduce 

$U(t)\equiv[V(t),[T,V(t)]]\quad{\rm and}\quad G_{i}(t)\equiv\nabla_{i}(|{\bf F}(t)|^{2})$ 

as shorthand representations. The time-evolving form of the four FSI equations, as derived by Chin and Chen in 2002, is given by:

${\cal T}_{A}^{(4)}(\epsilon)\equiv{\rm e}^{{1\over 6}\epsilon V(t+\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{{2\over 3}\epsilon\widetilde{V}(t+\epsilon/2)}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{{1\over 6}\epsilon V(t)},$  (15)

where $\widetilde{V}(t)$ is defined as

$\widetilde{V}(t)=V(t)+{1\over 48}\epsilon^{2}U(t),$  (16)

and corresponds to a force modification expressed as

$\widetilde{\bf F}(t)={\bf F}(t)+{1\over 48}\epsilon^{2}{\bf G}(t).$  (17)

Starting with initial conditions ${\bf p}_{0}={\bf p}(t)$ and ${\bf q_{0}}={\bf q}(t)$, substituting each operator from equation (15) into (5) results in the explicit algorithm 4A. This algorithm advances the system from time $t$ to $t+\epsilon$ by computing:

$\displaystyle{\bf p}_{1}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf p}_{0}+{1\over 6}\epsilon\,{\bf F}({\bf q}_{0},t)$

$\displaystyle{\bf q}_{1}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf q}_{0}+{1\over 2}\epsilon\,{\bf p}_{1}$

$\displaystyle{\bf p}_{2}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf p}_{1}+{2\over 3}\epsilon\,\widetilde{\bf F}({\bf q}_{1},t+\epsilon/2)$  (18)

$\displaystyle{\bf q}_{2}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf q}_{1}+{1\over 2}\epsilon\,{\bf p}_{2}$

$\displaystyle{\bf p}_{3}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf p}_{2}+{1\over 6}\epsilon\,{\bf F}({\bf q}_{2},t+\epsilon).$

Here, the final ${\bf p}$ and ${\bf q}$ values represent the updated system variables at $t+\epsilon$, specifically ${\bf q}(t+\epsilon)={\bf q}_{2}$ and ${\bf p}(t+\epsilon)={\bf p}_{3}$. For long-term simulations where intermediate results are unnecessary, the first and last force evaluations are identical and can be computed just once. Consequently, algorithm 4A achieves fourth-order accuracy with only two force evaluations and one gradient evaluation. It is important to note that force calculations are performed at intermediate times corresponding to the cumulative time steps of all preceding $T$ operators, as discussed in Suzuki (1993).

The middle time step in equation (9) is negative, and this occurrence is not coincidental. Sheng (1989) and Suzuki (1991) independently demonstrated that any factorization of the form (7) beyond second order necessarily includes negative coefficients within the set $\{a_{i},b_{i}\}$. Building on this, Goldman and Kaper (1996) established that such factorizations must include at least one negative coefficient for both operators. Consequently, for any evolution equation involving an irreversible operator, such as the diffusion kernel $T = \frac{1}{2}\nabla^{2}$, factorizations of the form (7) are not feasible beyond second order. This limitation arises because $\langle{\bf r}^{\prime}|{\rm e}^{a_{i}\epsilon T}|{\bf r}\rangle \propto {\rm e}^{-({\bf r}^{\prime}-{\bf r})^{2}/(2a_{i}\epsilon)}$ represents the diffusion kernel only when $a_{i}$ is positive. If $a_{i}$ were negative, the kernel would become unbounded and non-normalizable, underscoring the irreversible nature of diffusion. Therefore, positive coefficients are indispensable for solving evolution equations with irreversible components. As demonstrated here and further elaborated below, even in time-reversible systems—such as quantum mechanics (Chin and Chen, 2002) or classical dynamics (Chin, 1997)—purely positive coefficients yield highly efficient algorithms with superior convergence properties.

The lack of progress in developing FSI algorithms beyond second order is puzzling, as highlighted by Wisdom and Holman in 1991. This stagnation persisted until recently, primarily due to the inherent time-reversible nature of classical dynamics, which eliminates the necessity for strictly positive time steps. The demand for positive time steps arises exclusively in the context of solving time-irreversible equations, such as diffusion equations (Auer et al., 2001), Fokker-Planck equations (Forbert and Chin, 2001), and Navier-Stokes equations. Nonetheless, once FSI algorithms are formulated, they prove versatile, capable of addressing both time-reversible and time-irreversible evolution equations. For time-irreversible systems, FSI algorithms represent the sole viable decomposition approach, while for time-reversible equations, they offer algorithms characterized by exceptional efficiency and stability.

$\displaystyle{\bf q}_{1}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf q}_{0}+t_{0}\epsilon\,{\bf p}_{0}$  
$\displaystyle{\bf p}_{1}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf p}_{0}+\frac{1}{2}\epsilon\,{\bf F}({\bf q}_{1},t+a_{1}\epsilon)$  
$\displaystyle{\bf q}_{2}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf q}_{1}+\frac{1}{2}t_{1}\epsilon\,{\bf p}_{1}$  
$\displaystyle{\bf p}_{2}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf p}_{1}+c_{0}\epsilon^{3}\,{\bf G}({\bf q}_{2},t+\frac{\epsilon}{2})$  (23)  
$\displaystyle{\bf q}_{3}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf q}_{2}+\frac{1}{2}t_{1}\epsilon\,{\bf p}_{2}$  
$\displaystyle{\bf p}_{3}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf p}_{2}+\frac{1}{2}\epsilon\,{\bf F}({\bf q}_{3},t+a_{2}\epsilon).$  
$\displaystyle{\bf q}_{4}$ $\displaystyle=$ $\displaystyle{\bf q}_{3}+t_{2}\epsilon\,{\bf p}_{3}.$  

The final state variables are given by ${\bf q}(t+\epsilon)={\bf q}_{4}$ and ${\bf p}(t+\epsilon)={\bf p}_{3}$. Unlike algorithm 4A, 4B$^{\prime}$ minimizes computational overhead by performing only two force evaluations and one gradient evaluation per iteration, while also allowing for the generation of continuous output data.

Suzuki (1996) retained the commutator [T, [V, T]] based on McLachlan’s work (1995) and introduced two fourth-order factorization schemes, 4A and 4B, which utilize purely positive coefficients. However, he did not provide classical or quantum mechanical implementations or demonstrate their practical utility. Independently of McLachlan’s work, one of us (Chin, 1997) derived schemes 4A and 4B using elementary methods and explicitly implemented them in a classical context, showcasing their effectiveness in solving the Kepler problem. Additionally, algorithm 4C (Chin, 1997), a direct extension of Ruth’s third-order algorithm (1983), consistently exhibits significantly smaller error coefficients compared to 4A and 4B in all tested scenarios. Prior to Chin’s contributions, there were no known implementations of fourth-order factorization schemes for addressing any specific problem.

Symplectic integration techniques are uniquely suited for solving evolution equations with time-irreversible kernels, as demonstrated by Forbert and Chin (2001). These forward symplectic integrators (FSI) have proven to be significantly more efficient than non-forward symplectic counterparts, even for reversible systems, as highlighted by Chin (1997). In this study, we extend their application to classical dynamical systems under time-dependent forces, achieving remarkable precision. Specifically, in the circularly restricted three-body problem, FSI exhibit error magnitudes far superior to non-symplectic or non-forward methods. While their utility spans various classical systems, including molecular dynamics as shown by Omelyan, Mryglod, and Folk (2002), FSI are particularly advantageous for long-term, high-precision simulations of gravitational few-body systems. Their computational efficiency is enhanced by the straightforward calculation of force gradients, which do not exceed the time cost of force evaluation. Additionally, FSI exhibit superior convergence properties during close encounters, a critical feature for gravitational dynamics. The availability of a parameterized family of algorithms further allows tailoring to specific applications, making this class of integrators a prime candidate for addressing complex astrophysical scenarios beyond idealized models.

Symplectic Integrators (SI), as introduced by Yoshida (1993), preserve all Poincaré invariants during the integration of classical trajectories. Unlike Runge-Kutta algorithms, where energy errors grow linearly with integration time (Gladman, Duncan, & Candy, 1991), SI exhibit bounded and periodic energy errors for periodic orbits. This property makes them superior for long-term astrophysical simulations, particularly for many-body problems (Wisdom & Holman, 1991), and has garnered significant attention in the literature (Candy & Rozmus, 1991). However, higher-order SI face two notable challenges: a rapid increase in the number of force evaluations compared to non-symplectic methods and a narrower stability range. For instance, a sixth-order Runge-Kutta-Nyström algorithm (Albrecht, 1955) requires only five force evaluations, whereas a sixth-order symplectic algorithm with negative intermediate time steps demands at least seven (Yoshida, 1990). Additionally, the use of negative time steps to mitigate lower-order errors may contribute to reduced stability. In this study, we explore a class of fourth-order Forward Symplectic Integrators (FSI) that exclusively employ positive time steps (Chin, 1997). While these FSI necessitate the computation of the force gradient in addition to the force itself, the incremental computational cost for gravitational few-body problems is minimal. The resulting enhancement in numerical efficiency and stability is substantial. As demonstrated below, these FSI can attain the accuracy of existing symplectic integrators using step sizes five to ten times larger. Given their remarkable performance, these algorithms should be seriously considered for long-term few-body simulations by researchers in the field.

To evaluate the convergence properties of the algorithms described, we analyze the energy convergence of the third body after one period as a function of the step size $\epsilon$, with results presented in Fig. 5. These data align exceptionally well with a single fourth-order monomial of the form $c_{i}\epsilon^{4}$, as indicated by the solid lines. The efficiency of each algorithm can be inferred from the flatness of its convergence curve, moving from left to right in the figure. Notably, RK and RKN exhibit similar convergence behavior, despite RK requiring an additional force evaluation. 

The algorithm Cor demonstrates exceptional efficiency, achieving results comparable to FR while employing only one force and gradient evaluation. A surprising observation is that the second-order kernel algorithm 2M exhibits fourth-order convergence without the aid of correctors, essentially matching the performance of the complete corrector algorithm Cor. This phenomenon can be explained by the properties of the modified force defined in equation (13). Specifically, the coefficients of the second-order error terms [T,[V,T]] and [V,[T,V]] are identical in magnitude but opposite in sign, leading to their mutual cancellation. 

This behavior is further clarified by the expansion:
\[
{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{\epsilon V^{\prime}}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon T} = {\rm e}^{\epsilon\left(T + V + {1\over 24}\epsilon^{2}\,[T,[V,T]] - {1\over 24}\epsilon^{2}\,[V,[T,V]] + \cdots\right)},
\]
which shows that the errors from these terms cancel out when their magnitudes are nearly equal. Observations confirm that the energy error's sign depends on whether the coefficient of the modified force is below or above 1/24, consistent with the theoretical prediction in equation (39).

To evaluate the effectiveness of these fluid-structure interaction methods, we analyze the planar circularly restricted three-body problem, which is governed by the equation:

\[
\frac{d^{2}{\bf r}}{dt^{2}} = {\bf a}({\bf r}, t) \equiv -\frac{1}{2}\left[{\bf a}_{1}({\bf r}, t) + {\bf a}_{2}({\bf r}, t)\right]
\]

Here, for \(i = 1, 2\):

\[
{\bf a}_{i}({\bf r}, t) = \frac{{\bf r} - {\bf r}_{i}(t)}{S_{i}^{3}}, \quad \text{where} \quad S_{i} = |{\bf r} - {\bf r}_{i}(t)|
\]

The two gravitational centers, \({\bf r}_{1}(t)\) and \({\bf r}_{2}(t)\), orbit the origin in circular paths with angular velocity \(\omega = 1\):

\[
{\bf r}_{1}(t) = -\frac{1}{2}(\cos(t), \sin(t)), \quad {\bf r}_{2}(t) = \frac{1}{2}(\cos(t), \sin(t))
\]

The gradient of the squared acceleration is expressed as:

\[
\nabla(|{\bf a}|^{2}) = -\frac{1}{2}\left[C_{1}{\bf a}_{1} + C_{2}{\bf a}_{2}\right]
\]

where:

\[
C_{1} = 2S_{1}^{-3} + 3S_{1}{\bf a}_{1} \cdot {\bf a}_{2} - S_{2}^{-3}
\]
\[
C_{2} = 2S_{2}^{-3} + 3S_{2}{\bf a}_{1} \cdot {\bf a}_{2} - S_{1}^{-3}
\]

The problem is solved in a space-fixed frame, where the acceleration field is explicitly time-dependent. The initial conditions, \({\bf r}_{0} = (0, 0.0580752367)\) and \({\bf v}_{0} = (0.489765446, 0)\), generate a complex "Chinese Coin" orbit, suitable for algorithm testing. The natural orbital period is \(18\pi\), but due to the symmetry of the two identical attracting centers, the orbit repeats after \(9\pi\). For this study, the orbital period is set to \(P = 9\pi\).

The accuracy of each algorithm can be quantitatively evaluated by analyzing the Jacobi constant. Typically, the Jacobi constant is expressed in the co-rotating frame, where the two attracting centers are stationary. However, it is equally straightforward to transform this constant into the space-fixed frame, where it assumes a more interpretable form. For the circularly restricted three-body problem defined by (31), the Jacobi constant \( J \) is given by

\[
J = {\bf v}^2 - \frac{1}{|{\bf r} - {\bf r}_1(t)|} - \frac{1}{|{\bf r} - {\bf r}_2(t)|} - 2{\bf r} \times {\bf v}, \quad (34)
\]

which represents twice the difference between the energy and angular momentum. For fourth-order algorithms, we expect \( J(t) = J_0 + \epsilon^4 J_4(t) \), where the step-size independent error coefficient \( J_4(t) \) can be obtained from

\[
J_4(t) = \lim_{\epsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{J - J_0}{\epsilon^4}.
\]

As the step size decreases, \( J_4(t) \) converges to a fixed characteristic shape of the algorithm. In Fig. 3, we present this coefficient function for the standard fourth-order Runge-Kutta algorithm at a step size of \( \epsilon = P/50000 \approx 0.00056 \). Over the course of one period, the third body experiences five close encounters with the two attracting centers, resulting in five error spikes at \( t/P = 1/10, 3/10, 5/10, 7/10, \) and \( 9/10 \). Between each close encounter, there are four minor encounters, producing barely noticeable error fluctuations. Fig. 3 highlights the distinction between symplectic and non-symplectic algorithms. For symplectic algorithms, the error resets to zero after each close encounter and remains bounded after each period. In contrast, for Runge-Kutta-type algorithms, each encounter introduces an irrecoverable error, leading to linear growth in time without bounds.

The observed irregular behavior might be attributed to selecting an endpoint that is overly "tame." After one period, the third body returns to a position far from both attractors, with minimal error in the Jacobi constant. To investigate this further, we recalculated the energy at mid-period, when the third body has a close encounter and the Jacobi constant error peaks. This is illustrated in Figure 6. Here, it becomes evident that the kernel algorithm 2M converges quadratically, as expected, differing from the fourth-order corrector algorithm Cor. Additionally, the convergence rates of 4C and 4D are now clearly fourth-order, outperformed by the optimized 4ACB′ algorithm. However, fitting fourth-order models to 4B′ and 4ACB′ produces unnatural results with very small coefficients. Both can be better represented by a single eighth-order curve, as shown. The inverse of the error coefficient is provided in Table 1. Despite some puzzling behaviors, Table 1 clearly indicates that forward symplectic integrators, as a group, can be orders of magnitude more efficient than non-forward methods.

The qualitative distinction between symplectic and non-symplectic algorithms is illustrated in Fig. 3, while Fig. 4 quantitatively compares forward and non-forward integrators by analyzing the detailed shape of the error spike at t/P=1/10. In Fig. 4a, the left panel, the error function \( J_{4}(t) \) generated by algorithms FR, M, and Cor is contrasted with that of 4A. Cor represents a processor or corrector algorithm defined as:

\[
{\cal T}_{Cor}^{(4)}(\epsilon)\equiv{\rm e}^{C(\epsilon)}{\cal T}_{2M}^{(2)}(\epsilon){\rm e}^{-C(\epsilon)},
\]
where the kernel is a second-order algorithm (2M):

\[
{\cal T}_{2M}^{(2)}(\epsilon)\equiv{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{\epsilon V^{\prime}(t+\epsilon/2)}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon T},
\]
with the same modified force \( V^{\prime} \) as defined in (13). This second-order kernel has been widely used in processor or corrector algorithms (Lopez-Marcos et al., 1996). However, a fourth-order corrector with analytical coefficients appears to have been overlooked in previous studies. This corrector, introduced by Chin (2002), is expressed as:

\[
{\rm e}^{C(\epsilon)} = {\rm e}^{v_{2}\epsilon V(t+[t_{1}+t_{2}]\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{v_{1}\epsilon V(t+t_{1}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{1}\epsilon T},
\]
\[
{\rm e}^{-C(\epsilon)} = {\rm e}^{-t_{1}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{-v_{1}\epsilon V(t-t_{2}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{-t_{2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{-v_{2}\epsilon V(t)},
\]
with coefficients:

\[
t_{1} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}, \quad t_{2} = -\frac{1}{2^{1/3}\sqrt{3}}, \quad v_{1} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} - \frac{1}{2^{4/3}\sqrt{3}}, \quad v_{2} = -\frac{1}{2^{4/3}\sqrt{3}}.
\]
At the conclusion of \( {\rm e}^{-C(\epsilon)} \), time is adjusted to \( t - (t_{1} + t_{2})\epsilon \). This corrector is notably concise, as two error terms with identical coefficients can be eliminated by a single corrector operator. It is the fastest fourth-order algorithm when intermediate results are not required, necessitating only one evaluation of the force and its gradient. However, for continuous output, it becomes the slowest algorithm, requiring five force and one gradient evaluations. Its error function is approximately half that of RF, comparable to M, but an order of magnitude larger than that of 4A. In Fig. 4b, the right panel, all FSI algorithms considered in this study are compared. Algorithm 4B\(^{\prime}\), which requires one fewer force gradient evaluation, exhibits only half the error of 4B. The one-parameter family algorithm ACB\(^{\prime}\) with \( t_{0} = 0.138 \) has only one-third the error of 4C. The error magnitude of ACB\(^{\prime}\) is two orders of magnitude smaller than that of non-forward symplectic integrators. In Table 1, the inverse error height of each algorithm, normalized to that of RF, is provided. Thus, ACB\(^{\prime}\)’s error height is approximately 300 times smaller than RF’s and 150 times smaller than M’s.

Similarly, Algorithm 4B in operator form is expressed as 

\[
{\cal T}_{B}^{(4)}(\epsilon)\equiv{\rm e}^{t_{2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon\bar{V}(t+a_{2}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{1}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon\bar{V}(t+a_{1}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{0}\epsilon T},
\]

where 

\[
t_{0}=t_{2}={1\over 2}(1-{1\over{\sqrt{3}}}),\quad t_{1}={1\over{\sqrt{3}}},\quad a_{1}={1\over 2}(1-{1\over{\sqrt{3}}}),\quad a_{2}={1\over 2}(1+{1\over{\sqrt{3}}}),
\]

and 

\[
\bar{V}(t)=V(t)+c_{0}\epsilon^{2}\,U(t),
\]

with \(c_{0}={1\over 24}(2-\sqrt{3})\). Similar to the 4A case, the explicit algorithm derivation is not repeated here. Algorithm 4B involves two force evaluations and two force gradient evaluations. Upon discovering the one-parameter family of algorithms, it was recognized that 4B could be optimized by reducing one gradient evaluation. This is achieved by centralizing the commutator term within the algorithm:

\[
{\cal T}_{B^{\prime}}^{(4)}(\epsilon)\equiv{\rm e}^{t_{2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon V(t+a_{2}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}t_{1}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{c_{0}\epsilon^{3}U(t+\epsilon/2)}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}t_{1}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon V(t+a_{1}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{0}\epsilon T}.
\]

To avoid confusion, Algorithm 4B\(^{\prime}\) is transcribed as follows.

In Figure 1, we present a comparison of the trajectory after three periods ($t=27\pi$) using the Forest-Ruth algorithm and our 4B$'$ algorithm. Figure 2 compares the same trajectory using algorithm M and algorithm 4C. Algorithm M, which corresponds to McLachlan’s fourth-order, four-force evaluation algorithm (McLachlan, 1995), is defined as:

$$
{\cal T}_{M}^{(4)}(\epsilon)\equiv{\rm e}^{t_{5}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{v_{1}\epsilon V(t+a_{4}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{4}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{v_{2}\epsilon V(t+a_{3}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{3}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{v_{2}\epsilon V(t+a_{2}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{2}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{v_{1}\epsilon V(t+a_{1}\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{t_{1}\epsilon T},
$$

where $v_{1}=6/11$, $v_{2}=1/2-v_{1}$, $a_{i}=\sum_{j=1}^{i}t_{j}$, and

$$
t_{1}=t_{5}=\frac{642+\sqrt{471}}{3924},\quad t_{2}=t_{4}=\frac{121}{3924}(12-\sqrt{471}),\quad t_{3}=1-2(t_{1}+t_{2}).
$$

Both algorithms RF and M are representative fourth-order symplectic algorithms that incorporate negative intermediate time steps. However, they are outperformed by 4B$'$, which requires only two force and one gradient evaluations. Additionally, we tested the standard four-force evaluation Runge-Kutta algorithm and the three-force evaluation Runge-Kutta-Nyström algorithm (Battin, 1999). At the same step size, both Runge-Kutta-type algorithms exhibit instability and fail to produce a bounded trajectory. Algorithms 4A and 4B are comparable to 4B$'$, with algorithm 4D performing better than 4C due to an additional force evaluation.

Suzuki’s proof offers a more insightful approach to circumventing the negative coefficient problem, despite Sheng’s proof being slightly more general. The core of Suzuki’s proof lies in recognizing the operator representation of the velocity form of the Verlet algorithm:

\[
{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon V}{\rm e}^{\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon V}={\rm e}^{\epsilon H^{\prime}},
\]

where

\[
H^{\prime}=T+V-{1\over 12}\epsilon^{2}[T,[V,T]]+{1\over 24}\epsilon^{2}[V,[T,V]]+O(\epsilon^{4}).
\]

To achieve a fourth-order algorithm, both double commutator error terms must be eliminated. However, with purely positive coefficients \(a_{i}\) and \(b_{i}\), only one of these terms can be eliminated. Therefore, retaining one of the double commutators is necessary for a fourth-order factorization with positive coefficients. Specifically, the commutator

\[
[V,[T,V]]=2F_{j}{{\partial F_{i}}\over{\partial q_{j}}}{{\partial}\over{\partial p_{i}}}=\nabla_{i}(|{\bf F}|^{2}){{\partial}\over{\partial p_{i}}}
\]

introduces a new force derived from the gradient of the square of the force modulus, which can be retained. Although Ruth (1983) initially derived a third-order derivative algorithm using canonical transformations, it was largely overlooked until Channell and Neri (1996), with no subsequent discussion. Later, Chin (1997) observed that Ruth’s algorithm corresponds to a time-asymmetric operator decomposition, where the force derivative aligns with the gradient defined by \([V,[T,V]]\). Suzuki’s proof is significant for elucidating the connection between the requirement for positive time steps and the necessity of retaining higher-order commutators like \([V,[T,V]]\).

All forward symplectic algorithms outperform their non-forward counterparts, similar to the Jacobi constant case. Notably, algorithm 4ACB′ demonstrates significantly better performance compared to 4A, though not to 4C. Algorithms 4C and 4D exhibit anomalous behavior, with their errors being fourth-order but characterized by exceptionally small coefficients \( c_i \). In Table 1, the inverse of the error coefficient \( 1/|c_i| \) is normalized relative to that of RF. This reveals that the error coefficient of RK or RKN is tenfold larger than that of RF, whereas the error coefficient of 4C is nearly 2200 times smaller than RF's. For a more precise comparison, the number of force and gradient evaluations must be considered. While computing the gradient may nominally require more effort than evaluating the force, when both are computed simultaneously, the additional effort, as shown by (32), is negligible. Comparing with RF and M, which require three and four force evaluations respectively, provides a reasonable measure of FSI's efficiency. By taking the fourth root of \( 1/|c_i| \), the effective step size relative to RF for achieving the same error level can be determined. For 4C, this results in a step size approximately 7 times larger than RF's, 4 times larger than M's, and 12 times larger than RF's.

When considering the retention of $[V,[T,V]]$, two distinct methods emerge for addressing the other double commutator $[T,[V,T]]$. Although Rowland (1991) did not employ an operator formalism, they observed that the velocity form of the Verlet algorithm exhibits error terms of the form (11). To eliminate the $[T,[V,T]]$ term, Rowland proposed an explicit transformation, interpreting the algorithm as fourth-order for a Hamiltonian with a modified potential:

$V^{\prime}=V+{1\over 24}\epsilon^{2}[V,[T,V]].$ (13)

While redefining the potential presents no difficulty, the challenge lies in ensuring that the transformation to remove $[T,[V,T]]$ is correctly reversed to maintain fourth-order accuracy for the original variables. This reversal often proves more complex than the original algorithm itself. This elimination of $[T,[V,T]]$ can be achieved by applying the operators:

${\rm e}^{C(\epsilon)}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon V}{\rm e}^{\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{{1\over 2}\epsilon V}{\rm e}^{-C(\epsilon)}$ (14)

where ${\rm e}^{-C(\epsilon)}$ and ${\rm e}^{C(\epsilon)}$ represent the initial and final transformations, respectively. These transformations can be further broken down into products of the form ${\rm e}^{C(\epsilon)}=\prod_{i}{\rm e}^{c_{i}\epsilon T}{\rm e}^{d_{i}\epsilon V}$. This class of "process" algorithms, as described by Lopez-Marcos et al. (1996) and "corrector" methods by Wisdom, Holman, and Touma (1996), offers a significant advantage: when iterating the algorithm, only the kernel algorithm itself needs to be repeated. If intermediate results need not be tracked, a fourth-order algorithm can be achieved with a single evaluation of the modified force, as demonstrated by Lopez-Marcos et al. (1996). However, due to their construction, these methods inevitably involve negative time steps in either ${\rm e}^{-C(\epsilon)}$ or ${\rm e}^{C(\epsilon)}$, rendering them inapplicable to systems with irreversible operators. Despite this limitation, they remain a valuable asset for solving reversible dynamical systems within the symplectic framework. Recent process algorithms have also adopted a modified potential, though only in the form of equation (13) and within the context of a second-order kernel algorithm, as shown by Blanes et al. (1999). Lopez-Marcos et al. (1997) introduced a two-fold Rowland kernel that could theoretically achieve fourth-order accuracy, yet the precise parameters required for this were never explored.

We present a novel class of fourth-order symplectic algorithms tailored for efficient long-term integration of gravitational few-body systems. These algorithms utilize exclusively positive time steps, with the added requirement of computing the force gradient alongside the force. Their effectiveness is showcased through solving the circularly restricted three-body problem in a space-fixed frame, where the force acting on the third body exhibits explicit time dependence. Notably, these Forward Symplectic Integrators achieve the same level of accuracy as Runge-Kutta, backward time step, and corrector symplectic algorithms, but with step sizes five to ten times larger, underscoring their computational efficiency.

A perfect $k$-coloring of a graph $G$ is defined by a surjective function $f$ mapping the vertex set to the color set $\{1, \ldots, k\}$. For each vertex $x$ assigned color $i$, it is connected to exactly $s_{i,j}$ vertices of color $j$, where $s_{i,j}$ is a constant independent of the choice of $x$. These constants $s_{i,j}$ represent the parameters of the perfect coloring and correspond to the degrees of the regular or biregular subgraphs formed by the color classes. The matrix $S = (s_{i,j})$ of size $k$ is referred to as the quotient matrix associated with the perfect coloring $f$.

Let $M^{1}, \dots, M^{q}$ be a partition of the vertex set of $H(m,q)$ into $q$ mutually disjoint distance-$2$ MDS codes. For a given integer $\tau$, where $0 \leq \tau \leq q$, define $G^{i}_{\tau}$ as the $2$-coloring induced by the $\tau$-fold MDS code $\bigcup\limits_{j=i}^{i+\tau-1}M^{j}$ (with indices $j$ taken cyclically modulo $q$) within $H(m,q)$, provided that $m \geq 1$. If $m=0$ (so that $H(0,q)$ consists of a single vertex), then $G^{i}_{\tau}$ assigns the first color to the vertex when $1 \leq i \leq \tau$, and the second color when $\tau + 1 \leq i \leq q$.

For every perfect coloring of \( H(n,q) \) with a quotient matrix \( S \) and for any positive integer \( u \), there exists a corresponding perfect coloring of \( H(n+u,q) \) with a quotient matrix \( S + u(q-1)I \). Specifically, if there exists a perfect \( (b,c) \)-coloring of \( H(n,q) \), then such a coloring also exists for \( H(n+u,q) \) for \( u = 1, 2, \ldots \).

This constraint represents a specific instance of the bounds established for the correlation immunity of Boolean functions, which is equivalent to the strength of simple binary orthogonal arrays, as demonstrated in [12]. This concept was later expanded to encompass non-simple orthogonal arrays in [24]. Expanding this particular bound to accommodate cases where \( q > 2 \) remains an unresolved challenge in ongoing research.

For any perfect coloring of $H(n,q)$ characterized by a quotient matrix $S$, and for any positive integer $u$, a corresponding perfect coloring exists for $H(n+u,q)$ with quotient matrix $S+u(q-1)I$. Consequently, if $H(n,q)$ admits a perfect $(b,c)$-coloring, then so does $H(n+u,q)$ for all $u=1,2,\ldots$.

(i) implies (ii) via Proposition 3.2. As observed in [41], any orthogonal array $(N,n,q,t)$ that meets the Bierbrauer–Friedman bound $N\geq q^{n}\left(1-\frac{(q-1)n}{q(t+1)}\right)$ corresponds to a simple independent set with a characteristic function that acts as a $(b,c)$-coloring, where $b=(q-1)n$ and $c=q(t+1)-(q-1)n$. Consequently, (i) is derived from (ii).

It is straightforward to demonstrate that in a perfect $(b,c)$-coloring of the hypergraph $H(n,q)$, the first color occupies $\frac{c}{b+c}\cdot q^{n}$ vertices, whereas the second color accounts for $\frac{b}{b+c}\cdot q^{n}$ vertices. Upon simplifying these expressions, it becomes evident that the respective color densities in the coloring $f$ are $\frac{c}{b+c}$ and $\frac{b}{b+c}$. These densities can alternatively be expressed as $\frac{c^{\prime}}{b^{\prime}+c^{\prime}}$ and $\frac{b^{\prime}}{b^{\prime}+c^{\prime}}$, where $b^{\prime}=\frac{b}{\gcd(b,c)}$ and $c^{\prime}=\frac{c}{\gcd(b,c)}$. As we will observe in subsequent discussions, the reduced parameters $b^{\prime}$ and $c^{\prime}$ not only dictate the proportion of colors in a perfect $(b,c)$-coloring but also serve as critical components in characterizing the permissible parameters for such colorings.

The invasion coloring \( h \) of \( H(n+m,q) \) is constructed by substituting each vertex of color \( i \) in the coloring \( f \) of \( H(n,q) \) with the graph \( H(m,q) \) colored using \( g_{i} \).

For any perfect coloring of the hypercube graph \( H(n,q) \) characterized by a quotient matrix \( S \), and for any positive integer \( p \), a perfect coloring of \( H(n,pq) \) exists with the quotient matrix \( pS + n(p-1)I \). Specifically, if a perfect \( (b,c) \)-coloring is admitted by \( H(n,q) \), then \( H(n,pq) \) admits a perfect \( (pb,pc) \)-coloring for \( p = 1,2,\ldots \).

The arguments for (2) and (3) closely mirror those of (1). Specifically, to establish (2), consider the group homomorphism defined by

$\varphi(x_{1},\ldots,x_{vn})=(x_{1}+\cdots+x_{v},\ldots,x_{v(n-1)+1}+\cdots+x_{vn}),$

where addition is performed in $\mathbb{Z}_{q}$. Similarly, for (3), the homomorphism

$\varphi(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})\bmod q;\quad x_{i}\in\mathbb{Z}_{pq}$

is employed. This completes the proof.

In this work, we investigate perfect $2$-colorings of the $q$-ary Hamming graph $H(n,q)$, with a focus on the non-binary scenario where $q>2$, as well as novel constructions for perfect $2$-colorings. A comprehensive characterization of completely regular codes, including perfect $2$-colorings, in $H(n,q)$ with the second largest eigenvalue $\theta_{1}=n(q-1)-q$ has been established in [38], which also addresses the same problem for Johnson and regular complete multipartite graphs. Additionally, [39] provides descriptions of perfect $2$-colorings of $H(n,q)$ with the third largest eigenvalue $\theta_{2}=n(q-1)-2q$. For the binary Hamming graph $H(n,2)$, or hypercube, the parameters of $2$-colorings were analyzed in [9]. [13] introduced general constructions for perfect $2$-colorings of the hypercube, noting that some of these results were initially presented without proof in the conference paper [9]. A significant non-feasibility test for parameters of perfect $2$-colorings of the hypercube was derived in [12] through a bound on the correlation immunity of Boolean functions. In [14], two specific parameter sets of perfect $2$-colorings of $H(n,2)$ were examined, resulting in constructions in one case and proofs of nonexistence in the other. The infeasibility of an infinite series of parameters for $2$-colorings in $H(n,2)$ was demonstrated in [34]. In this study, we generalize the constructions from [13] to the non-binary case $q>2$ and introduce new constructions for composite $q$. The connection between perfect colorings and orthogonal arrays is pivotal to our exploration, enabling the creation of new orthogonal arrays. Notably, perfect $2$-colorings correspond to a special class of completely regular codes, specifically those with a covering radius of $1$. In a recent survey [7] on completely regular codes, Borges, Rifà, and Zinoviev highlighted that perfect $2$-colorings remain poorly understood for $q>2$. Given the scarcity of answers in this domain, our research represents a meaningful advancement from a state of limited knowledge to one with slightly more understanding.

The Hamming graphs are members of the distance-regular graph class, and the study of their perfect colorings is of significant interest due to several factors. Many combinatorial configurations can be characterized as perfect colorings with specific parameters, including perfect codes, $t$-$(v,t+1,\lambda)$- and $t$-$(v,t+2,1)$-designs [36], Latin squares and hypercubes (equivalent to distance-2 MDS codes), MDS codes with distance 3 [25], and transversals in Latin squares. Optimal objects, defined by certain bounds, often correspond uniquely with perfect colorings of particular parameters. For instance, unbalanced Boolean functions achieving the correlation-immunity bound [12], almost perfect and optimal codes (e.g., [27], [29]), orthogonal arrays reaching the Bierbrauer–Friedman bound [4], induce perfect 2-colorings in Hamming graphs [40], [41]. Similarly, binary orthogonal arrays attaining the Bierbrauer–Gopalakrishnan–Stinson bound [5] yield perfect 3-colorings in hypercubes [31]. Potapov's result [41] establishes a bijection between perfect 2-colorings of the Hamming graph $H(n,q)$ and Boolean functions on $H(n,q)$ that meet a bound linking correlation immunity, density of ones, and average 0-1 contact number. Beyond Hamming graphs, perfect colorings have been explored in Johnson graphs [1], Latin-square graphs [2], halved hypercubes [32], Grassmann graphs $G_{q}(n,2)$ [10], [37], and finite geometries, where they are studied as intriguing sets [3]. Notably, [42] demonstrates how perfect colorings of non-distance-regular graphs can lead to profound and insightful results.

The characteristic function of $C$ within the space $(\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{s})^{n}$ serves as a perfect 2-coloring for the hypergraph $H(n,q)$, characterized by the quotient matrix $\begin{bmatrix}0 & b \\ c & b - c\end{bmatrix}$. Here, the parameters are defined as $b = (p^{s} - 1)n$ and $c = \frac{b}{p^{m} - 1}$.

Let us examine the parameters of perfect 2-colorings of \( H(n,q) \). The quotient matrix of a perfect 2-coloring \( f \) is typically expressed as

\[
S = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}.
\]

This matrix has two distinct eigenvalues: the trivial eigenvalue \( n(q-1) \) (corresponding to the graph degree) and the secondary eigenvalue \( \theta = n(q-1) - (b + c) = a - c \). Given that \( a + b = c + d = n(q-1) \), the parameters \( b \) and \( c \) uniquely determine the matrix. Consequently, we refer to \( f \) as a \( (b,c) \)-coloring if its quotient matrix is as defined above. Without loss of generality, we may assume \( b \geq c \) by appropriately rearranging the colors.

An equitable $k$-partition of a graph $G=(V,E)$ (allowing multigraphs, including loops and multiple edges) is a division of the vertex set $V$ into $k$ nonempty subsets $V_1, \dots, V_k$ such that each subset induces a regular subgraph, and the bipartite graph formed by the edges between any two distinct subsets is biregular. This type of partition is also referred to as a perfect $k$-coloring, which formally is a $k$-valued function on $V$ that defines an equitable partition, with each partition subset being the preimage of a specific value.

We organize their proofs into multiple lemmas. Initially, we focus on specific values of $q$ and demonstrate that for any perfect $(b,c)$-coloring of the graph $H(n,q)$, a corresponding perfect $2q$-coloring can be constructed.

For an arbitrary $n$-ary quasigroup $R'$ of order $q$ on the vertices of $H(n,q)$, define a coloring $g'$ of $H(qn,q)$ as

$$
g'(y) = g'(y^{1}, \ldots, y^{n}) = \begin{cases}
R'(j_{1}, \ldots, j_{n}) & \text{if } f(X_{y}) = 1, \text{ where } j_{i} = h(y^{i}) \text{ for } i \in I_{X_{y}} \text{ and } y^{i} \in L^{h(y^{i})}_{j_{i}}, \\
q+1 & \text{if } f(X_{y}) = 2,
\end{cases}
$$

and for an $(n-k)$-ary quasigroup $R''$ of order $q$, define a coloring $g''$ of $H(qn,q)$ as

$$
g''(y) = g''(y^{1}, \ldots, y^{n}) = \begin{cases}
R''(j_{i_{1}}, \ldots, j_{i_{n-k}}) & \text{if } f(X_{y}) = 1, \text{ where all } i_{l} \notin I_{X_{y}} \text{ and } y^{i_{l}} \in L^{h(y^{i_{l}})}_{j_{i_{l}}}, \\
q+1 & \text{if } f(X_{y}) = 2.
\end{cases}
$$

We now demonstrate that $g'$ and $g''$ are perfect $(q+1)$-colorings with quotient matrices $T'$ and $T''$ respectively.

The fundamental distance-invariance property states that the weight distributions of all colors in a perfect coloring are determined solely by the color of the starting vertex and the graph and coloring parameters. (The weight distribution refers to the multiset of distances from the starting vertex to vertices of a specified color.) This property applies to perfect colorings of any distance-regular graph and extends the well-established Shapiro–Slotnick–Lloyd theorem [35] on the distance invariance of perfect codes. Furthermore, it can be broadened to enable the calculation of the weight distribution of a coloring with respect to any completely regular code, not limited to a single vertex, as demonstrated in [28].

Let $q$ be a prime number. The parameters $b, c,$ and $q$ are considered admissible if and only if $b^{\prime}+c^{\prime}=q^{k}$ for some $k\in\mathbb{N}$. Furthermore, if $b^{\prime}+c^{\prime}=q^{k}$ for some $k\in\mathbb{N}$, then the threshold parameter $n_{0}$ for the existence of $(b,c)$-colorings satisfies the following inequalities:

$$
\max\left\{\frac{b}{q-1},\frac{b+c}{q}+k-1\right\}\leq n_{0}\leq\frac{b+c-\gcd(b,c)}{q-1}.
$$

In this lemma's proof, we adhere to the notations established in Lemma 4.11. Furthermore, for a vertex \( x \) in \( H(n,q) \) colored with the first color in the coloring \( f \), we define \( I_{x} = \{i_{1}, \ldots, i_{k}\} \) as the set of \( k \) free directions within the \( k \)-dimensional face \( \Gamma \) that contains vertex \( x \) in the required decomposition of this color into faces. Here, \( I_{x} \) is referred to as the set of special directions for vertex \( x \).

We note that all applicable results from our paper in the context of $q=2$ were originally established by Fon-Der-Flaass [13]. Additionally, certain binary findings (including bounds from [12] and [13], the ability to partition one color into lines in the primary construction of [13], and specific cases in [14]) either remain ungeneralized or have been partially generalized. Conversely, the novel results we present for composite $q$ lack counterparts in the binary scenario.
Each color class within a $(b,c)$-coloring of the hypergraph $H(n,q)$ corresponds to an orthogonal array of strength $\frac{b+c}{q}-1$.

The invasion coloring \( h \) of the hypergraph \( H(n+m, q) \) is constructed by substituting each vertex of color \( i \) in the coloring \( f \) of \( H(n, q) \) with the hypergraph \( H(m, q) \), which is then colored using the coloring \( g_{i} \).

In the following and subsequent sections, we present a series of constructions designed to facilitate the derivation of perfect $2$-colorings characterized by novel color distribution ratios. These constructions, which we refer to as splitting constructions, operate on the principle of initially scaling the parameters of an existing $2$-coloring by a factor $q$, as outlined in Theorem 4.6. Subsequently, leveraging the properties of a $1$-perfect code within the space $H(q+1,q)$, one color (designated as the second color) is subdivided into $q$ "equivalent" components. Following an intermediate adjustment step, $\tau$ of these subdivided components are merged with the first color, while the remaining $q-\tau$ components form the new second color. Consequently, the color ratio evolves as follows:

$c:b=qc:qb\to qc:b:\cdots:b\to qc+\tau b:(q-\tau)b.$

These constructions extend the primary methodology introduced in [13], originally confined to the case $q=2$, to a broader context where $q$ can assume any value for which a $1$-perfect code exists in $H(q+1,q)$.

For any perfect coloring of \( H(n,q) \) with a quotient matrix \( S \) and any positive integer \( v \), there exists a corresponding perfect coloring of \( H(vn,q) \) with a quotient matrix \( vS \). Specifically, if there is a perfect \( (b,c) \)-coloring of \( H(n,q) \), then there also exists a perfect \( (vb,vc) \)-coloring of \( H(vn,q) \) for \( v = 1,2,\ldots \).

In the proof of this lemma, we adopt the same notations as established in Lemma 4.11. Additionally, if a vertex \( x \) of \( H(n,q) \) is assigned the first color in the coloring \( f \), let \( I_{x} = \{i_{1}, \ldots, i_{k}\} \) denote the set of \( k \) free directions within the \( k \)-dimensional face \( \Gamma \) that contains the vertex \( x \) in the required decomposition of this color into faces. Here, \( I_{x} \) is referred to as the set of special directions for the vertex \( x \).

Let \( f \) be a perfect \((q+1)\)-coloring of \( H(n,q) \) with the quotient matrix 
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha' & \cdots & \alpha & \beta \\
\vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
\alpha & \cdots & \alpha' & \beta \\
\gamma & \cdots & \gamma & \delta
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
If \( 0 \leq \gamma - \alpha = m \), then for each \( \tau = 0, \ldots, q \) and \( l = 1, 2 \) such that \( \tau(l-1) + (q-\tau)(2-l) \neq 0 \), the invasions \( h_{l} = f \times (G_{\tau}^{1}, \ldots, G_{\tau}^{q}, \mathbf{l}) \), where \( \mathbf{l} \) is the solid coloring of \( H(m,q) \) into the color \( l \), are \((b_{l}, c_{l})\)-colorings of \( H(n+m,q) \) with \( b_{l} = \gamma(q-\tau) + \beta(l-1) \) and \( c_{l} = \gamma\tau + \beta(2-l) \).
If a $(b,c)$-coloring of $H(n,2)$ exists with $b \neq c$, then $n$ must satisfy $n \geq \frac{3}{4}(b + c)$.

For every perfect coloring of \( H(n,q) \) with quotient matrix \( S \) and for every positive integer \( p \), a perfect coloring of \( H(n,pq) \) exists with quotient matrix \( pS + n(p-1)I \). Specifically, if \( H(n,q) \) admits a perfect \( (b,c) \)-coloring, then \( H(n,pq) \) also admits a perfect \( (pb,pc) \)-coloring for \( p = 1, 2, \ldots \).

In this section, we explore highly significant properties of perfect colorings referred to as distance invariance and their extensions. Based on our experience, these invariants have not been effective in rejecting candidate quotient matrices for perfect 2-colorings of Hamming graphs in non-binary scenarios. However, their utility becomes evident when dealing with colorings involving more than two colors, necessitating their inclusion in the discussion. The method to validate a candidate quotient matrix using distance invariants involves verifying if the number of vertex groups with specific properties, computed using graph parameters and the quotient matrix, yields a non-negative and integral result. If such calculations produce negative or non-integer values, perfect colorings with that quotient matrix cannot exist.

We investigated the parameters of perfect $2$-colorings in Hamming graphs, examining various known and novel constructions while compiling tables of small parameters. In our conclusion, we emphasize a specific subcase of the broader problem: the existence of perfect $2$-colorings of $H(n,q)$ with a quotient matrix $\left(\begin{matrix}a&b\\ c&d\end{matrix}\right)$ where $a=0$, meaning the first color forms an independent set. Such colorings exist if and only if there is an orthogonal array OA $(\frac{q^{n}c}{b+c},n,q,\frac{b+c}{q}-1)$ that meets the Bierbrauer–Friedman bound. Notably, initial examples of these colorings are linked to $1$-perfect codes, and subsequent constructions involve scaling the quotient matrix (with corresponding orthogonal arrays derived from linear codes). In the binary scenario, the primary recursive method from [13] allows decrementing $a$ in the quotient matrix until $a=0$, yielding an infinite series of parameters, such as $(13,3)$-, $(29,3)$-, and $(59,5)$-colorings with $a=0$ (OA $(1536,13,2,7)$, OA $(3\cdot 2^{24},29,2,15)$, OA $(5\cdot 2^{53},59,2,31)$). However, in the non-binary case, the Fon-Der-Flaass construction analog (Splitting I) does not reduce $a$, limiting us to constructing perfect $2$-colorings with $a=0$ as additive codes. This approach introduces new parameters for nonprime prime-power $q$, but for odd primes $q$, the existence of $2$-colorings in $H(n,q)$ with $a=0$ beyond those from perfect codes remains unresolved. The only known nonexistence occurs for $(14,4)$-colorings in $H(7,3)$, corresponding to OA $(2\cdot 3^{5},7,3,5)$ [19]. Open questions in this area include the existence of $(25,7)$-colorings in $H(25,2)$, $(22,5)$-colorings in $H(11,3)$, and $(39,9)$-colorings in $H(13,4)$.

For every perfect coloring of $H(n,q)$ with quotient matrix $S$ and any positive integer $v$, there exists a corresponding perfect coloring of $H(vn,q)$ with quotient matrix $vS$. Specifically, if $H(n,q)$ admits a perfect $(b,c)$-coloring, then $H(vn,q)$ also admits a perfect $(vb,vc)$-coloring for any positive integer $v$.

In Section 5, we compile findings concerning the admissibility criteria for parameters of perfect colorings. Specifically, when $q$ assumes the form of a prime power, we establish sufficient conditions for the existence of $(b,c)$-colorings within the hypercube $H(n,q)$, where $n$ is greater than or equal to a threshold value $n_{0}$. Additionally, we provide an estimation for the minimal such $n_{0}$ that ensures the feasibility of these colorings.

To establish sufficiency, we must construct a $(b,c)$-coloring $f$ in $H(n,q)$ for a specific $n$. Let $m = \gcd(b,c)$, $b' = b/m$, and $c' = c/m$. We observe that $b' + c' = q^k$ and $b + c = mq^k$ for some integer $k$. As noted in Subsection 4.3, when $n' = \frac{q^k - 1}{q - 1}$, there exists a $c'$-fold $1$-perfect code in $H(n', q)$, which corresponds to a $(b', c')$-coloring $f'$. By applying Theorem 4.6, we amplify the perfect coloring $f'$ by a factor of $m$, resulting in a $(b, c)$-coloring for the graph $H(n, q)$ with 

$$n = mn' = m\frac{q^k - 1}{q - 1} = \frac{b + c - \gcd(b,c)}{q - 1}.$$

Let \( f(X_{y}) = 2 \). According to the definition of the coloring \( h \), there are \( q(p - \tau) \) vertices \( z \) adjacent to \( y \) such that \( y_{i} \equiv z_{i} \mod q \) for all \( i \) and \( h(z) = 2 \). For each vertex \( y \), there are precisely \( q^{2} - 1 \) sets \( \mathcal{Z}_{i}^{\alpha} \). If \( i = i_{X_{y}} \) is a special direction for the vertex \( X_{y} \) in \( H(q+1,q) \), then for all \( \alpha \not\equiv y_{i} \mod q \), all vertices \( z \) in the sets \( \mathcal{Z}_{i}^{\alpha} \) are colored with the first color by \( h \). Conversely, if \( i \neq i_{X_{y}} \), each of the \( q^{2} - q - 1 \) sets \( \mathcal{Z}_{i}^{\alpha} \) with \( f(X_{z}) = 2 \) contains exactly \( \tau \) vertices \( z \) such that \( h(z) = 1 \) and \( p - \tau \) vertices \( z \) for which \( h(z) = 2 \). The remaining set \( \mathcal{Z}_{i}^{\alpha} \) with \( z \) such that \( f(X_{z}) = 1 \) consists solely of vertices colored with the first color by \( h \). In conclusion, each vertex \( y \) with \( h(y) = 1 \) is adjacent to \( (q^{2} - 1)(p - \tau) \) vertices of the second color in the coloring \( h \).
All constructions of perfect colorings for the hypergraphs $H(n,q)$ presented in this section stem from a well-established and easily comprehensible principle.

If a function \( f \) serves as a perfect coloring for a multigraph \( G \) with an associated quotient matrix \( S \), then this function \( f \) also acts as a perfect coloring for the multigraph \( vG \), with the corresponding quotient matrix being \( vS \).

where \((x^{\prime}_{1}, \ldots, x^{\prime}_{q+1}) = X^{\prime}_{y}\). We aim to demonstrate that the coloring \( h \) is a perfect coloring of \( H(q+1, pq) \) with the desired parameters.

such that the vertices of the first color form \( k \)-faces. If a \( 1 \)-perfect code exists in \( H(q+1,q) \), then there are perfect colorings \( g' \) and \( g'' \) in \( q+1 \) colors in \( H(qn,q) \) with the quotient matrices

In this and the following sections, we present constructions that facilitate the derivation of perfect $2$-colorings with novel color ratios. These constructions are referred to as splitting constructions. The core concept involves initiating the process by scaling the parameters of a $2$-coloring by a factor $q$, as outlined in Theorem 4.6. Subsequently, utilizing a $1$-perfect code in $H(q+1,q)$, one of the colors (for instance, the second color) is subdivided into $q$ "equivalent" colors. Finally, after an intermediate step, $\tau$ of these subdivided colors are merged with the first color, while the remaining $q-\tau$ colors form the new second color. Consequently, the color ratio evolves as follows: $c:b = qc:qb \to qc:b:\cdots:b \to qc+\tau b:(q-\tau)b$. These constructions extend the primary construction in [13], originally developed for $q=2$, to accommodate an arbitrary $q$ provided that a $1$-perfect code exists in $H(q+1,q)$.

A function \( f \) serves as a perfect coloring for a multigraph \( G \) with a quotient matrix \( S \) if and only if \( f \) also functions as a perfect coloring for the multigraph \( G + uI \) with the corresponding quotient matrix \( S + uI \).

Proposition 2.1 allows us to utilize (either once or recursively) the construction from Theorem 4.14 (1) for all $(b,c)$-colorings in $H(n,2)$ with a non-positive second eigenvalue. Additionally, Theorem 4.14 (2) can be applied to construct perfect colorings from colorings obtained after employing Theorem 4.16, as this theorem produces colorings with a large second eigenvalue, where one of the colors can be split into faces.

To initiate the analysis, we impose a fundamental algebraic constraint on the parameters of $(b,c)$-colorings. A classical result from [17] establishes that the spectrum of the quotient matrix derived from a perfect coloring within a graph is encompassed by the graph's eigenvalues. Notably, the eigenvalues of the Hamming graph $H(n,q)$ are characterized by the formula $\theta_{i}=n(q-1)-qi$ for $i=0,\ldots,n$. For a $(b,c)$-coloring of $H(n,q)$, the second eigenvalue is determined to be $\theta=n(q-1)-(b+c)$, leading to the subsequent deductions.

By integrating the derived constructions of perfect $2$-colorings, we validate Conjecture 3 for specific instances and establish bounds on the critical threshold $n_{0}$. Our analysis begins with identifying necessary conditions for permissible parameters $(b,c)$, particularly when $q$ assumes the role of a prime power.

In the context of perfect colorings within the framework of $H(n,q)$, it is established that all sufficiently large faces—those exceeding a dimension threshold of $(n+\theta)/q$, where $\theta$ denotes the second largest eigenvalue of the quotient matrix—exhibit color densities consistent with the entire graph. This result, as referenced in [31], essentially aligns with the broader findings presented in [11]. Specifically, for $2$-colorings, the behavior is as follows.

Let $f$ be a perfect coloring in $H(n,q)$ with the quotient matrix $\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}a&b\\ c&d\end{array}\right)$. If a $1$-perfect code exists in $H(q+1,q)$, then there exists a perfect $2q$-coloring $g$ of $H(qn,q)$ with the quotient matrix $T=\left(\begin{array}[]{cccccc}a&\cdots&a&b&\cdots&b\\ \vdots&\ddots&\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\ a&\cdots&a&b&\cdots&b\\ c&\cdots&c&d&\cdots&d\\ \vdots&\ddots&\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\ c&\cdots&c&d&\cdots&d\\ \end{array}\right)$.
All perfect colorings of \( H(n,q) \) presented in this section are constructed using a well-known and elementary principle.

Let $f$ be a proper vertex coloring within the hypercube graph $H(n,q)$, characterized by the quotient matrix $\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}a&b\\ c&d\end{array}\right)$, where the first color class can be subdivided into $k$-dimensional faces. Assuming the existence of a $1$-perfect code in $H(q+1,q)$, there exist two perfect colorings $g^{\prime}$ and $g^{\prime\prime}$ in $H(qn,q)$ utilizing $q+1$ colors. These colorings are associated with the quotient matrices:

$T^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}[]{cccc}a - k(q - 1) & \cdots & a + k & qb \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ a + k & \cdots & a - k(q - 1) & qb \\ c & \cdots & c & qd \\ \end{array}\right);$

and

$T^{\prime\prime}=\left(\begin{array}[]{cccc}a + k(q - 1)^2 & \cdots & a - k(q - 1) & qb \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ a - k(q - 1) & \cdots & a + k(q - 1)^2 & qb \\ c & \cdots & c & qd \\ \end{array}\right).$

Let us consider an arbitrary $n$-ary quasigroup $R^{\prime}$ of order $q$ acting on the vertices of the hypercube $H(n,q)$. We define a coloring $g^{\prime}$ for the hypercube $H(qn,q)$ as follows: for any vertex $y = (y^{1}, \ldots, y^{n})$, set
$$
g^{\prime}(y) = 
\begin{cases}
R^{\prime}(j_{1}, \ldots, j_{n}) & \text{if } f(X_{y}) = 1, \\
q + 1 & \text{if } f(X_{y}) = 2,
\end{cases}
$$
where $j_{i} = h(y^{i})$ for $i \in I_{X_{y}}$ and $y^{i} \in L^{h(y^{i})}_{j_{i}}$ otherwise. Similarly, for a $(n - k)$-ary quasigroup $R^{\prime\prime}$ of order $q$, we define a coloring $g^{\prime\prime}$ of $H(qn,q)$. We aim to demonstrate that both $g^{\prime}$ and $g^{\prime\prime}$ constitute perfect $(q + 1)$-colorings with corresponding quotient matrices $T^{\prime}$ and $T^{\prime\prime}$, respectively.

Suppose that a perfect $(b,1)$-coloring exists for the hypergraph $H(n,q)$. In such a case, it can be established that $b$ must be a multiple of $q-1$. Furthermore, if $q$ is expressed as a prime power, specifically $q = p^s$ where $p$ is prime, then it follows that $b + 1$ is equal to $q^r$ for some natural number $r$.

To establish (1), let us consider the group homomorphism $\varphi:\mathbb{Z}_{q}^{n+u}\rightarrow\mathbb{Z}_{q}^{n}$ defined by $\varphi(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n},\ldots,x_{n+u})=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})$. Observing the following is straightforward: if $\mathcal{I}(n+u,q)$ denotes the set of all elements in $\mathbb{Z}_{q}^{n+u}$ at Hamming distance 1 from the identity element, then the multiset $B:=\varphi(\mathcal{I}(n+u,q))$ is precisely $\mathcal{I}(n,q)\cup\{u(q-1)\times\bar{0}\}$. The Cayley multigraph $\mathrm{Cay}(\mathbb{Z}_{q}^{n},B)$ corresponds exactly to $H(n,q)+u(q-1)I$. Therefore, by Lemma 4.1, $\varphi$ constitutes a covering of $H(n,q)+u(q-1)I$ by $H(n+u,q)$.

Furthermore, the methodology outlined in Section 4.2 demonstrates that constructing perfect colorings from additive codes can address the existence of $(b,c)$-colorings under specific conditions. Namely, when $q$ is a prime power $p$ and the sum $b^{\prime}+c^{\prime}$ also constitutes a power of $p$, even if it does not align with a power of $q$.

Each vertex \( y = (y_1, \ldots, y_{qn}) \) in \( H(qn, q) \) can be viewed as a tuple \( y = (y^1, \ldots, y^n) \), where each \( y^i = (y_{(i-1)q+1}, \ldots, y_{iq}) \) is a vertex in \( H(q, q) \). Let \( X_y = (x_1, \ldots, x_n) \) denote a vertex in \( H(n, q) \) such that \( x_i = h(y^i) \). Similarly, let \( J_y = (j_1, \ldots, j_n) \) represent another vertex in \( H(n, q) \), where each \( j_i \) corresponds to the index of the distance-3 MDS code containing \( y^i \), i.e., \( y^i \in L^{h(y^i)}_{j_i} \). For any \( n \)-ary quasigroup \( R \) of order \( q \) defined on the vertex set of \( H(n, q) \), we define a coloring \( g \) for \( H(qn, q) \) as \( g(y) = q(f(X_y) - 1) + R(J_y) \). Consequently, \( g \) represents a \( 2q \)-coloring of \( H(qn, q) \) using the color set \( \{1, \ldots, q, q+1, \ldots, 2q\} \).

In Section 4, we focus on the construction of perfect colorings. We begin with a series of constructions derived from coverings of Hamming graphs. Subsequently, we present three direct methods for constructing perfect colorings, utilizing additive codes (as detailed in Section 4.2, which includes the specific construction of an orthogonal array OA $(2048,7,4,5)$ ), MDS codes, and $1$-perfect codes (covered in Section 4.3). Finally, in Section 4.5, we explore more intricate constructions that blend previous techniques, thereby introducing novel admissible parameters for colorings.
