The realization that hadrons are not fundamental particles, but rather complex structures consisting of asymptotically free quarks and gluons, paved the way for the concept that hadronic matter subjected to extreme temperatures and pressures would transition into a "quark-gluon plasma" (2). In this condition, quarks and gluons could be conceptualized as quasi-free particles moving along trajectories, allowing the thermodynamic properties to be approximately equivalent to an ideal gas with non-interacting degrees of freedom. Specifically, quarks and gluons would no longer be bound within their parent hadron, instead wandering through larger volumes. This hypothesis provides 40 degrees of freedom for the quark and gluon states (including two light quarks, effectively 37 when calculating energy density due to the factor of 7/8 from Fermi-Dirac statistics) as opposed to the 3 pion degrees of freedom in a hadronic system at lower temperatures. This distinction is typically linked to the order of magnitude change in the entropy $s/T^{3}$ and energy density $\epsilon/T^{4}$ as observed near the transition temperature in lattice QCD calculations (3).

Although the physics field has segmented its primary focus into distinct $p_{T}$ regions, the scaling principle appears to be consistent across all $p_{T}$ values. To evaluate this mathematically, the data is adjusted to a reference curve, represented as $v_{2}/n_{q}=\alpha\tanh(\beta(m_{T}-m)/n_{q})$, with $\alpha$ and $\beta$ determined solely from charged kaon data (refer to Figure 2). The fitting process for kaon data is arbitrary, and consequently, dividing the data by the reference line provides a measurement of the relative deviation among various hadrons as demonstrated in Figure 3.

In the realm of specialized research, the transverse momentum spectrum is conventionally segmented into three distinct domains: The "lower transverse momentum" region, characterized by values below 1.5 GeV, in which hydrodynamic models align harmoniously with experimental findings regarding flow behavior; the "intermediate transverse momentum" region, encompassing momenta approximately within the range of 1.5 to 4.5 GeV/c, where hydrodynamic models tend to overestimate the elliptic flow coefficient $v_{2}$ ; and the "higher transverse momentum" region, exceeding 4.5 GeV, where it is generally posited that jet fragmentation during hadronization plays a predominant role. Within the intermediate transverse momentum region, anomalies such as the deviation from "hydrodynamic scaling" and the conspicuous enhancement of (anti) baryon yields relative to meson yields, a phenomenon often referred to as the "baryon anomaly" (33), have prompted some to entertain the idea of alternative hadronization mechanisms.

The widespread adoption of personal computing devices, including laptops and smartphones, has created a need for secure and convenient authentication methods, as these devices are increasingly used for various aspects of daily life, such as work, financial transactions, social interactions, and entertainment. Biometric authentication, particularly face recognition, has gained popularity due to its unique identification capabilities and user-friendly interface, as exemplified by technologies like Apple's FaceID. However, this has also led to a rise in sophisticated spoofing attempts, including print, replay, and mask attacks, which can deceive authentication systems. As a result, the development of effective face anti-spoofing (FAS) techniques has become a crucial challenge in the field of computer vision, necessitating innovative solutions to detect and prevent such attacks.

Contemporary methods leveraging convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have demonstrated exceptional performance in various computer vision applications, largely due to the robust representational capabilities of their learned features when trained on extensive datasets. However, significant challenges persist in the field of face anti-spoofing (FAS), notably the domain shift problem, which arises when a model trained on one dataset (source domain) is applied to a completely disparate dataset (target domain), a scenario referred to as cross-testing. In contrast to intra-testing, where training and testing occur on the same dataset, deep learning-based approaches exhibit substantial performance degradation under cross-testing conditions. This decline is primarily attributed to the disparity in feature distribution between source and target domains, stemming from dataset-specific factors such as variations in environmental conditions, spoofing mediums, and video capture device quality, resulting in models that fail to generalize effectively to unseen datasets with distinct cues.

By integrating the CCDD with a gradient reversal layer, the resultant architecture inherently embodies the coveted property of conditional invariance within the extracted feature representations, thereby fostering a domain-agnostic paradigm. This invariance is facilitated by the incorporation of class-conditional losses, which solely leverage source domain label information to render the representations of each class domain-invariant. A visualization using t-SNE (elaborated in § 4.4) illustrates the efficacy of the proposed CCDD in establishing a congruence between the live and spoof features of the target domain and those of the source domains. Furthermore, a comprehensive suite of quantitative experiments serves as a testament to the CCDD's efficacy, while a more nuanced exposition of the network's design architecture is provided in Appendix A.1.

A comparative analysis is conducted to evaluate the efficacy of our proposed face anti-spoofing approach against existing domain adaptation-based methods, as outlined in [36]. The experimental framework adheres to the conventional unsupervised domain adaptation paradigm, wherein the neural network is trained on a singular source domain with labeled examples, supplemented by unlabeled data from the target domain. To maintain consistency with this setup, the default domain discriminator, as described in Section 4.5 and illustrated in Figure 5, is utilized. The outcomes of this comparison are summarized in Table 9, revealing that our proposed framework surpasses the performance of [36] in three out of four domain adaptation test sets, while exhibiting comparable results in the remaining set. These findings underscore the versatility of our model, which can be effectively leveraged in both domain adaptation and domain generalization contexts. Notably, although this study focuses on the latter setting, the experimental results demonstrate that our model, with minimal modifications, can yield substantial improvements in the former setting as well, highlighting its adaptability and potential for broader applications.

In our experimental framework, each dataset is treated as a distinct domain, and our model is trained on domain-agnostic representations derived from three out of four datasets, following the approach outlined in [52]. Specifically, three datasets are randomly designated as source domains for training purposes, while the fourth, unseen domain is reserved for evaluation. The performance of our model is assessed using Half Total Error Rate (HTER) and Area Under Curve (AUC) as key evaluation metrics.

Our evaluation utilizes four domain generalization datasets derived from publicly available face anti-spoofing datasets, specifically Oulu-NPU, CASIA-MFSD, Idiap Replay-Attack, and MSU-MFSD, as referenced in [52]. The composition of the training, validation, and test sets for these datasets is summarized in Table 6. Notably, CASIA-MFSD and MSU-MFSD lack designated validation sets, prompting the use of a subset of their training data for validation purposes, consistent with established protocols. During inference, predictions from both image- and video-based live/spoof classifiers are considered, with the optimal performer on the validation set selected as the final output. The ResNet-50 backbone is initialized with pre-trained weights from ImageNet [32].

Traditional face anti-spoofing methods utilize manually extracted features, such as LBP, HoG, and SURF, to identify texture discrepancies between genuine and spoofed facial images, or rely on heuristic indicators like eyeblink, lip motion, and visual rhythms to detect anomalies characteristic of spoof attacks. Nevertheless, these approaches have limitations, as they may not be effective against all types of spoofing attacks, including print, replay, and mask attacks, and often fail to generalize across diverse datasets due to the specialized nature of the learned features, which can be heavily influenced by varying factors such as background, lighting, camera resolution, and spoof materials.

This section reports the outcomes of experiments utilizing a downsized backbone, specifically ResNet-18, as opposed to the ResNet-50 employed in the primary research. By integrating ResNet-18 into our proposed framework and conducting training, we observed that despite the utilization of a less robust backbone network, our approach consistently yields enhancements across four demanding domain generalization test sets, as illustrated in Table 7. Notably, the I&C&M $\to$ O dataset possesses the smallest training set among the four domain generalization datasets outlined in Table 6, and our framework achieves superior performance with the ResNet-18 backbone on this particular dataset.

This section outlines the detailed architectural framework of the proposed network, utilizing ResNet-50 as the foundational backbone and PyTorch for implementation. The layer-by-layer configuration of the Class-conditional Domain Discriminator (CCDD) and the live/spoof classifier (LSC) is presented in Table 5. The ResNet-50 backbone processes a 224x224 image, generating a 2048-dimensional feature vector that serves as input for both the CCDD and LSC. The CCDD's live and spoof heads, denoted as FC31 and FC32, produce three softmax probability scores corresponding to the three source domains, while the LSC's final layer, FC2, yields two softmax probability scores representing the "live" and "spoof" class labels.

This section presents a visual examination of class activation maps, utilizing the Grad-CAM technique, to garner insight into the network's decision-making process for specific predictions. The visualization of class activation maps for live, print, and replay attack test samples, as depicted in Figure 6, yields notable observations. The network prioritizes facial regions when identifying "live" classes, which is consistent with the fact that facial characteristics, such as skin texture and eye movement, are primary indicators of a live face. In contrast, the network focuses on the paper surface for print attacks and identifies distinctive features, including hands or screens, for video replay attacks, while considering both facial and background areas in the absence of such features.

This section provides supplemental class activation maps for "live", "print attack", and "replay attack" samples from four face anti-spoofing datasets, analogous to those presented in Section 4.6 of the primary manuscript. The maps, generated using Grad-CAM, reveal that the network exhibits high activation levels around facial regions for "live" samples. In contrast, "print attack" samples demonstrate high activation in background areas, except for the Oulu-NPU dataset, where the network focuses on both facial and background regions, likely due to the high-resolution print quality. For "video replay attacks", the network activates both facial and background areas, potentially leveraging cues such as moire patterns on CRT displays and the distinct texture of screen frames to classify these attacks.

The architectural specifics of our approach are as follows: we leverage the ResNet-50 model as the foundational network, with input images scaled to a uniform dimension of 224x224 pixels. Our training regimen employs the stochastic gradient descent optimizer, adopting an alternating training strategy to concurrently train both the Information Bottleneck (IB) and Variational Bottleneck (VB) networks, as illustrated in Figure 3. The optimization process is governed by a fixed learning rate of 0.0003, complemented by a momentum of 0.9 and a weight decay of 0.00001. Notably, the IB network is trained with a mini-batch size of 48, comprising 16 images from each of the three domains, whereas the VB network operates with a mini-batch size of 6, consisting of 2 video sequences from each domain, and an LSTM sequence length of 8. Furthermore, the LSTM architecture is characterized by an input dimension of 2048 and a hidden layer dimension of 256. To facilitate domain adaptation, we apply a constant Gradient Reversal Layer (GRL) adaptation factor of -0.2, while setting the lambda values for IB and VB to 1, as informed by existing research. A comprehensive overview of additional experimental parameters and settings can be found in Appendix A.2.

In the configuration of the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, the input tensor assumes a three-dimensional structure, with dimensions corresponding to the sequence length, the stochastic gradient descent (SGD) mini-batch size, and the feature dimensionality, yielding a tensor of size $[T \times B \times 2048]$. Specifically, the sequence length $T$ and the mini-batch size $B$ are set to $8$ and $2$, respectively. At each temporal iteration $t$, where $t$ ranges from $1$ to $8$, the LSTM generates a feature vector of $256$ dimensions. The resultant eight feature vectors, each corresponding to a distinct time step, are then concatenated, yielding a single, high-dimensional feature vector of $2048$ dimensions, which serves as the input to the subsequent processing stage, namely the Linear Support Vector Classifier (LSC).

Prior to the emergence of convolutional neural networks, conventional face anti-spoofing methodologies predominantly relied on the synergy of manually engineered features and shallow classification algorithms to discern textural discrepancies between genuine and spoofed images. The repertoire of hand-crafted features typically encompassed techniques such as Local Binary Patterns, Histogram of Oriented Gradients, Difference of Gaussians, Scale-Invariant Feature Transform, and Speeded-Up Robust Features. In terms of classification, these approaches predominantly utilized Support Vector Machines, with Linear Discriminant Analysis being employed to a lesser extent. Additionally, other traditional methodologies exploited heuristic cues indicative of "liveliness" that are inherently absent in spoof attempts, including ocular movements, labial motions, visual rhythmic patterns, Haralick texture features, auditory signals, dynamic textures, and other related indicators. Alternatively, researchers have explored the utilization of temporal cues in video sequences to combat face spoofing, as exemplified by the work of Bharadwaj et al., who leveraged motion magnification to accentuate facial expressions, while others have investigated the combination of motion and texture cues. To mitigate the impact of variability factors such as illumination and pose on comparative image analysis, various strategies have been investigated, including the deployment of diverse color spaces, image distortion analysis, and transformations into the temporal and Fourier domains. However, these traditional approaches often exhibit limitations, as they may not be universally applicable to all types of spoof attacks, including print, replay, and mask-based attempts, and tend to fail in generalizing across disparate datasets due to the specialized nature of the learned features, which are heavily influenced by factors such as background, lighting conditions, camera resolution, and spoof materials.

In our experimental framework, each dataset constitutes a distinct domain, and our model is trained on domain-agnostic representations derived from three out of four datasets, following the approach outlined in [52]. Specifically, three datasets are randomly chosen as source domains for model training, while the fourth, unseen domain is reserved for evaluation purposes only, remaining inaccessible during the training phase. The performance of our model is assessed using Half Total Error Rate (HTER) and Area Under Curve (AUC) as key evaluation metrics.

This section outlines the detailed architecture of our proposed network, utilizing ResNet-50 as the foundation and implemented in PyTorch. The design specifications for the Class-conditional Domain Discriminator (CCDD) and the live/spoof classifier (LSC) are presented in Table 5, highlighting the layer-wise configuration. The ResNet-50 backbone processes a 224x224 image, generating a 2048-dimensional feature vector that serves as input for both the CCDD and LSC. The CCDD's live and spoof heads produce three softmax probability scores corresponding to the three source domains, while the LSC's final layer yields two softmax probability scores for the "live" and "spoof" class labels.

This section presents a visual examination of class activation maps, leveraging the Grad-CAM technique, to garner insight into the network's decision-making process for specific predictions. The visualization of class activation maps for live, print, and replay attack test samples, as depicted in Figure 6, yields notable observations. The network prioritizes facial regions when identifying "live" classes, which is consistent with the fact that facial characteristics, such as skin texture and eye movement, are primary indicators of a live face. In contrast, for print attacks, the network focuses on the paper surface, while for video replay attacks, it relies on distinctive features like hands or screens in the background, and in their absence, considers both facial and background areas to inform its decision.

Our framework utilizes ResNet-50 as the backbone network, with input images resized to 224x224 pixels. The training process employs the SGD optimizer and an alternative training approach, as illustrated in Figure 3, for both the IB and VB networks. Key hyperparameters include a constant learning rate of 0.0003, momentum of 0.9, and weight decay of 0.00001. The IB network is trained with a mini-batch size of 48, comprising 16 images from each of three domains, while the VB network uses a mini-batch size of 6, consisting of 2 video sequences from each domain, with an LSTM sequence length of 8. Additionally, the LSTM's input and hidden layer dimensions are set to 2048 and 256, respectively, with a constant GRL adaptation factor of -0.2 and lambda values of 1 for both IB and VB. Further experimental details can be found in Appendix A.2.

We utilize four domain generalization datasets derived from publicly available face anti-spoofing datasets, including Oulu-NPU, CASIA-MFSD, Idiap Replay-Attack, and MSU-MFSD, as described in reference [52]. The composition of the training, validation, and test sets for these datasets is outlined in Table 6, with CASIA-MFSD and MSU-MFSD utilizing a subset of their training sets as validation sets due to the absence of dedicated validation sets. During inference, predictions are generated from both image- and video-based live/spoof classifiers, and the output yielding the best performance on the validation set is selected as the final result. The ResNet-50 backbone is initialized with pre-trained weights from ImageNet.

This study tackles the domain shift issue in face anti-spoofing (FAS) under a domain generalization framework, where a network is trained on multiple source datasets and evaluated on an unseen target dataset. The objective is to develop domain-agnostic feature representations from source domain samples that can be generalized to target domain samples, enabling effective classification of live or spoof samples regardless of their domain origin. To achieve this, a novel approach is proposed, utilizing class-conditional domain discriminator modules in conjunction with a gradient reversal layer, which encourages the backbone network to generate domain-invariant live and spoof feature representations. This method is applicable to both image-based and video-based inputs, and intentionally avoids incorporating additional components, such as depth or remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) signals, which would require costly ground truth labels for network training.

The input tensor for the LSTM network is configured with a shape of $[T\times B\times 2048]$, where $T$ represents the sequence length and $B$ denotes the SGD mini-batch size, set to 8 and 2, respectively. At each time step, the LSTM generates a 256-dimensional feature vector, resulting in a total of 8 feature vectors that are subsequently concatenated to form a single 2048-dimensional feature vector, which is then fed into the LSC.

The efficacy of our approach is assessed through its application to a quartet of openly accessible face anti-spoofing datasets, namely the Oulu-NPU, CASIA-MFSD, Idiap Replay-Attack, and MSU-MFSD collections, which are respectively abbreviated as O, C, I, and M for the purposes of this evaluation.

To validate the efficacy of our proposed model's constituent components, we establish a foundational baseline for the ablation study, comprising a ResNet-50 backbone paired with a live/spoof classifier trained on diverse domain generalization datasets. Notably, this baseline demonstrates commendable performance, with supplementary experiments showcasing the viability of employing a more lightweight ResNet backbone. The introduction of DIB atop the ResNet backbone yields consistent enhancements across all four test sets, while the subsequent incorporation of LSTM leads to substantial further improvements. Ultimately, our comprehensive model achieves the most significant boosts in performance. Interestingly, the combination of ResNet and LSTM yields marginally superior results in three test configurations compared to the model leveraging ResNet and DIB. However, the addition of DVB to the ResNet and LSTM model does not yield additional enhancements, whereas joint training of DVB alongside ResNet, DIB, and LSTM - as embodied in our full model - does indeed surpass the ResNet baseline. This observation underscores the importance of leveraging both spatial and temporal domain-agnostic features, as our model, by integrating image-based (DIB) and video-based (DVB) components, achieves optimal results on the most demanding domain generalization test sets, specifically O&M&I $\to$ C and I&C&M $\to$ O.

The efficacy of our approach is assessed through its application to a quartet of publicly accessible face anti-spoofing datasets, namely the Oulu-NPU, referred to herein as O, the CASIA-MFSD, denoted as C, the Idiap Replay-Attack, abbreviated as I, and the MSU-MFSD, designated as M, each of which serves as a benchmark in the field of face authentication security.

This research yields several notable contributions, including the development of a class-conditional domain discriminator module that, when combined with a gradient reversal layer, facilitates the extraction of domain-agnostic features, as well as the utilization of a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to capture complementary temporal features that are similarly domain-agnostic. Furthermore, our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on four rigorous domain generalization test sets, complemented by a visual examination of feature embeddings and class activation maps that provide insight into the model's operation.

The rising tide of mobile and computing devices has created a pressing need for more secure methods of user verification, prompting the development of advanced biometric authentication systems, particularly those utilizing facial recognition. To counteract spoofing attempts, face anti-spoofing measures have been implemented, which rely on sophisticated deep learning algorithms to identify and distinguish between genuine and fake facial images or videos. However, these cutting-edge techniques often struggle to adapt to new, unseen scenarios due to the vast array of variables at play, such as diverse backgrounds, lighting conditions, and camera qualities. This limitation is addressed through the introduction of a novel domain discriminator module, designed to produce robust and distinctive features that can effectively withstand these variations, thereby enhancing the overall resilience of face anti-spoofing systems. Comprehensive testing has demonstrated the superiority of this approach over existing methods, yielding notable improvements in both quantitative results and visual feature analysis.

Upon examination of sub-figure (b), it becomes evident that our model possesses a superior capacity for learning discriminative features that effectively differentiate between live and spoof images.
