To evaluate the model's capacity for generalization, we expanded the number of source domains, thereby exposing the network to a diverse range of live and spoof examples that vary significantly in terms of subjects, environmental conditions, and other factors. This approach enables us to assess whether the observed improvements are solely attributable to the increased dataset size. A comparison with the ResNet baseline on the same data reveals that our image-based network consistently outperforms the baseline across three distinct domain generalization test settings, as evidenced in Table 8. Notably, the training process utilized two additional datasets, SiW and Idiap replay-mobile, which were only employed for training purposes. The results presented in Table 8, which report the average ACER metric over four test protocols for the Oulu-NPU dataset, demonstrate the effectiveness of our image-based approach, with further enhancements achievable through the incorporation of video-based networks.

The goal of this research is to develop a robust face anti-spoofing system that can effectively distinguish between genuine and fake faces, even when the data is sourced from diverse environments and devices. A significant challenge in achieving this goal is the domain shift problem, which occurs when the characteristics of the data differ between the training and testing datasets. To overcome this issue, it is essential to learn feature representations that are generalizable across multiple domains, rather than being specific to a particular dataset or environment. This can be achieved by identifying common attributes that are shared across different domains, such as the inherent properties of print and replay attacks, which can be used to develop more robust and domain-agnostic feature representations. By leveraging these common attributes, it is possible to learn feature representations that are not only effective for live/spoofing classification but also generalize well to unseen domains, thereby improving the overall performance and reliability of face anti-spoofing systems.

This study tackles a fundamental challenge in face anti-spoofing, wherein the significant variability in environmental factors, such as background, lighting, and camera resolution, as well as spoofing materials, renders the feature representations learned by convolutional neural networks overly specialized to specific domains, resulting in diminished performance on unseen domains. To address this issue, we propose a generalizable feature learning approach that effectively mitigates the domain shift problem in both image- and video-based face anti-spoofing. Our comprehensive experimental evaluation demonstrates superior performance across various test sets, showcases the enhanced generalizability of the learned feature representations, and provides insight into the network's decision-making process through class activation maps. Future research directions include exploring the use of multi-modal inputs and domain-agnostic multi-modal feature learning to further enhance classification accuracy.

A comparative analysis with state-of-the-art face anti-spoofing methods is presented in Table 1, demonstrating the superior performance of our proposed model across four domain generalization test sets, largely attributable to its capacity to extract robust and adaptable features that effectively generalize to unseen target domains, as illustrated in Figure 4. This notable advantage stems from our model's explicit addressing of the domain shift issue, allowing it to learn a domain-invariant representation by utilizing ground truth labels from multiple source domains, and effectively mapping live and spoof samples to a shared feature space where they are distinguishable yet domain-agnostic.

To investigate the impact of various domain discriminators on Face Anti-Spoofing (FAS) performance, a comprehensive experimental analysis was conducted. Two distinct domain discriminator architectures were evaluated: the novel Domain-Invariant Bottleneck (DIB) structure, illustrated in Figure 3, and the conventional domain classifier, originally introduced by [20] for unsupervised domain adaptation, as depicted in Figure 5 and denoted as "Dis" in Table 4. Notably, the experimental protocol in this section incorporated two additional datasets, specifically SiW (hereafter referred to as S) [42] and Idiap replay-mobile (henceforth R) [14], which were solely utilized for training purposes. In accordance with the methodology outlined in [42], the ACER metric was employed when assessing performance on the Oulu-NPU dataset. The results presented in Table 4 reveal that the ResNet-DIB configuration yields the optimal performance, whereas the ResNet-Dis setup experiences a marginal decline in performance. Moreover, combining Dis with DIB results in a significant degradation of performance on the Oulu-NPU dataset. These findings suggest that learning feature representations from multiple source domains, conditioned on both class labels (i.e., live and spoof) and domain labels, enables the acquisition of discriminative and domain-agnostic features. In contrast, conditioning solely on domain labels may fail to align live and spoof features effectively, leading to compromised classification accuracy. The proposed DIB architecture, which has access to both class and domain labels, unlike the Dis approach, is capable of learning more effective representations by correctly clustering live features from multiple source domains into one group and spoof features into another, as visualized in Figure 4.

This section presents a comparative evaluation of our proposed face anti-spoofing approach against existing domain adaptation-based methods, utilizing a standard unsupervised domain adaptation training framework. The results, summarized in Table 9, indicate that our approach surpasses the performance of existing methods in three out of four domain adaptation test sets, and achieves comparable results in the remaining set. These findings suggest that our model is effective in both domain adaptation and domain generalization scenarios, with potential for significant improvement in the former setting with minimal modifications, despite our primary focus on the latter.

This discussion outlines both conventional feature-based methods and contemporary convolutional neural network (CNN)-based techniques for Face Anti-Spoofing (FAS), followed by an examination of existing research on domain generalization as it applies to FAS.

To evaluate the model's capacity for generalization, we expanded the number of source domains, thereby exposing the network to a diverse range of subjects, environmental conditions, and other variables through live and spoof examples. This approach enables us to assess whether the observed improvements are solely attributable to the increased dataset size. A comparison with the ResNet baseline using the same data reveals that our image-based network consistently outperforms the baseline across three distinct domain generalization test scenarios, as illustrated in Table 8. Notably, we utilized two additional datasets, SiW and Idiap replay-mobile, exclusively for training purposes in this section. While incorporating video-based networks could yield further enhancements, we focus on demonstrating the improvements achievable with image-based face anti-spoofing, excluding the video-based case. Consistent with established protocols, we employed the ACER metric when testing on the Oulu-NPU dataset and reported average ACERs across four test protocols in Table 8.

Notwithstanding the enhanced performance exhibited by preceding studies, which can be partly ascribed to the utilization of convolutional neural networks, a significant hurdle persists in terms of achieving seamless generalizability across disparate datasets. The existence of an inherent disparity between various face anti-spoofing datasets, such as Replay Attack and CASIA-FASD, is a well-documented phenomenon, commonly referred to as domain shift, which consequently yields suboptimal results in cross-dataset evaluations. This manuscript seeks to transcend the limitations of existing CNN-based methodologies by directly addressing the domain shift conundrum in face anti-spoofing, thereby eliminating the need for supplementary supervisory signals, including depth information or remote photoplethysmography signals, which would necessitate a substantial effort to annotate and acquire.

To validate the efficacy of the proposed model components, a baseline was established for the ablation study, consisting of a ResNet-50 backbone and a live/spoof classifier trained on four distinct domain generalization datasets, yielding notable performance. The addition of DIB to the ResNet backbone resulted in consistent improvements across all test sets, with further significant enhancements achieved by incorporating LSTM. The full model demonstrated additional boosts in performance, while combining ResNet and LSTM yielded marginally better results in three test configurations compared to the ResNet and DIB model. Notably, the integration of DVB with ResNet, DIB, and LSTM in the full model led to improved outcomes over the ResNet baseline, confirming that leveraging both spatial and temporal domain-agnostic features enables the model to achieve optimal results on the most challenging domain generalization test sets.

A potential method under consideration involves utilizing spectroscopic data to compute the virial mass, which can be expressed as:

$M_{\rm BH}=fR_{\rm BLR}\sigma_{\rm line}^{2}/G$

where $f$ is a factor influenced by the BLR structure, dynamics, and orientation; $R_{\rm BLR}$ represents the size of the broad line region (BLR); $\sigma_{\rm line}$ refers to the line dispersion; and $G$ denotes the gravitational constant. As suggested by Peterson et al. (2004), the factor $f$ equals 5.5, and $\sigma_{\rm line}$ is equivalent to FWHM/2.355. In the absence of reverberation mapping measurements, the size of the BLR can be estimated through the scaling relationship with its luminosity, as discussed by Kaspi et al. (2005). Our data indicates a broad H $\alpha$ line luminosity of roughly $4\times 10^{41}\,\rm erg\,s^{-1}$; assuming a H $\alpha$ /H $\beta$ flux ratio of 3 (supported by Vermeulen et al.'s (1995) measurements), this implies $R_{\rm BLR}\sim 5\,\rm lt\,day$. Using the FWHM value of 4600 km s$^{-1}$ from Table 2, we calculate $M_{\rm BH}\sim 2\times 10^{7}M_{\sun}$. In light of Marconi et al.'s (2008) prescription to account for the potential impact of radiation pressure on the BLR, we determine a minor correction term for the black hole mass of approximately $0.6\times 10^{7}M_{\sun}$. The estimated virial mass of $M_{\rm BH}$ is approximately 20–30 times lower compared to the previously derived values.

The study indicates a significant enhancement of approximately 50% in the broad H$\alpha$ flux over the past ten years, potentially attributable to an influx of gas in the broad line region or a boost in disc luminosity. However, such flux fluctuations are common in Broad Lined active nuclei. The black hole mass of BL Lacertae was calculated using its correlation with bulge luminosity, yielding a range of 4–$6\times 10^{8}\,M_{\sun}$. Conversely, the virial mass derived from spectroscopic data suggests a value 20–30 times lower. The analysis of disc and broad line region properties in various Active Galactic Nuclei (AGNs) suggests this discrepancy is due to a sub-luminous broad line region in BL Lacertae.

In terms of the BL Lacertae parent population, the isotropic quantities were compared with other AGN classes. Based on the narrow emission line spectrum, the source appears close to low-excitation radio galaxies. However, the lower radio luminosity (for a given line luminosity) of BL Lacertae raises doubts about an association with FR I radio galaxies. Instead, the narrow line and radio luminosities of BL Lacertae align with those of a miniature radio galaxy sample, although these do not exhibit a broad line region. If misaligned BL Lacertae objects exist, they should be included in this sample. The possibility of exclusion due to a QSO optical appearance is also ruled out.

It is plausible that the distinctive features exhibited by BL Lacertae are the outcome of a fleeting, transitory phase of brief duration. To conceptualize this hypothesis, one may consider the following hypothetical sequence of events, reminiscent of the scenario proposed by Corbett et al. (1996). Initially, BL Lacertae shares attributes analogous to those of sources within the SDSS/NVSS sample, which encompass a significant number of massive, early-type galaxies. Notably, these galaxies possess narrow emission lines and radio luminosities akin to those of BL Lacertae. In terms of their optical spectra, they are characterized as low-excitation galaxies (LEG) and lack the broad lines typically observed in active galactic nuclei (AGN). Approximately two decades ago (or thereabouts), an enhancement in luminosity occurred within the broad line region (BLR) due to an influx of cold gas in the nuclear regions and/or a surge in the ionizing continuum's intensity. These two factors may be interconnected, resulting from an infusion of gas undergoing accretion. The BLR's structure may not have stabilized yet, which could explain the contrasting characteristics observed when compared to other AGN. Additionally, the luminosity of the narrow line region (NLR) will gradually increase over time and will eventually undergo a change in ionization state, albeit on a significantly slower timescale in comparison to the BLR.

To validate the precise calibration of the spectral data, synthetic aperture photometry was implemented on the Cousins’ $R$-band images captured immediately prior to and subsequent to the spectral acquisitions. An aperture of 2 arcseconds in diameter was designated, yielding the flux densities at an effective wavelength of 6410 Angstroms, which are tabulated in the first section. The comparison of the spectral data with the continuum around 6400 Angstroms unveiled a commendable level of consistency between the spectral intensities and those inferred from the photometry, with the sole discrepancy observed in the VHR-R spectra from July 2007. The adjustment factors required for the LR-B spectra were 0.96, 0.95, and 1.03 for July 2007, September 2007, and January 2008 respectively, while for the VHR-R spectra, the factors were 1.27, 0.99, and 1.02. To ensure the accuracy of the LR-B spectra, which span nearly the entire wavelength range encompassed by the broad-band $R$ filter, the calibration factors were cross-verified by superimposing the BL Lacertae spectra with the transmission curve of the TNG $R$-band filter, multiplied by the CCD quantum efficiency curve. It was ascertained that the margin of error in the calibration factors is about 2%.

In analyzing emission lines, we've obtained a broad H $\alpha$ luminosity of approximately $4\times 10^{41}$ erg s$^{-1}$, while the narrow emission lines suggest a [O III] luminosity of around $4\times 10^{40}$ erg s$^{-1}$. Despite the narrow emission line ratios providing insights into Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) properties, our data yielded accurate measurements only for [O III] and [N II]. The narrow emission lines appear to remain stable over the past 20 years, allowing us to supplement our data with literature findings. However, previous studies such as Vermeulen et al. (1995) and Corbett et al. (1996) have limitations in providing a clear understanding of the narrow emission lines. Nevertheless, we've managed to locate BL Lacertae in a non-standard diagnostic plane defined by [O I]/[N II] and [O III]/[N II] ratios, and it seems to lean towards a Low Excitation Galaxy classification based on its narrow emission line spectrum, albeit not definitively.

For a professional audience, the paragraph discusses the analysis of emission lines from the BL Lacertae object, specifically the H$\alpha$ and [O III] luminosities. The narrow emission lines, specifically [O III] and [N II], are found to be relatively stable over the past 20 years, allowing for the object to be located in a non-standard diagnostic plane based on the ratios [O I]/[N II] and [O III]/[N II]. Despite some challenges in obtaining accurate measurements, the object is found to fall in a region not well populated by 3CR sources, but closer to Low Excitation Galaxies, suggesting a tentative identification as such based on its narrow emission line spectrum.

To determine emission line intensities, we utilized the specfit package within IRAF, as outlined by Kriss (1994). By constraining the relative wavelength distances between lines and enforcing a consistent FWHM for all narrow lines, we reduced the number of free parameters. From our low-resolution spectra, we derived the [O III] $\lambda\lambda$ 4959,5007 flux, with an upper limit for the H $\beta$ flux. In our high-resolution analysis, we modeled the 6700–7350 Å spectral region employing seven components: individual Gaussian profiles for H $\alpha$ (both broad and narrow), [N II] $\lambda\lambda$ 6548,84, and [S II] $\lambda\lambda$ 6716,31, along with a linear continuum component. Figure 3 presents the outcomes of this spectral fitting. The [S II] $\lambda\lambda$ 6716,6731 doublet fit requires careful consideration due to the line's sensitivity to atmospheric absorption and the telluric correction (as depicted in Fig. 2 ). The FWHM of the narrow lines varies between approximately 500 and 600 $\rm km\,s^{-1}$ , while the broad H $\alpha$ component exhibits a FWHM of 4200–5000 $\rm km\,s^{-1}$ . Attempting to incorporate an additional component to fit the [O I] $\lambda\lambda$ 6300,64 line resulted in a marginal detection only.

Using spectroscopic data, we can determine the black hole mass (M_BH) via the virial equation: M_BH = (5.5 * R_BLR * (FWHM / 2.355)^2) / G, where R_BLR is the size of the broad line region (BLR), FWHM is the full width at half maximum of the broad H_alpha line, and G is the gravitational constant. The size of the BLR can be approximated using the scaling relationship with luminosity as discussed by Kaspi et al. (2005). With a broad H_alpha line luminosity of ~ 4 x 10^41 erg s^-1 and an H_alpha/H_beta flux ratio of ~ 3 (supported by measurements by Vermeulen et al. (1995)), we find R_BLR ~ 5 light days. Using the measured FWHM = 4600 km s^-1 from Table 2, we calculate M_BH ~ 2 x 10^7 M_sun. Accounting for the possible role of radiation pressure on the BLR as prescribed by Marconi et al. (2008), we derive a correction term of ~ 0.6 x 10^7 M_sun, resulting in a virial estimate of M_BH that is about 20–30 times lower than the initial calculation.

In conclusion, we examined properties independent of orientation and beaming to identify the parent population of BL Lacertae objects. We established diagnostic indices utilizing reliable narrow emission lines and determined that their values may tentatively classify BL Lacertae as a Low-Emission Gamma-Ray (LEG) source. Broad lines are only present in High-Emission Gamma-Ray sources, but the diffuse radio luminosity of BL Lacertae is significantly lower than these powerful radio emitters. On the contrary, miniature radio galaxies exhibit LEG-like properties in terms of narrow line and radio power, yet lack a Broad Line Region (BLR). Given the selection criteria for the miniature radio galaxy sample, it is plausible that it includes "misaligned BL Lacertae" objects, unless they were excluded due to a Quasar-like appearance. An examination of the galaxy morphology, spectral features, and radio power of the Quasar sources initially excluded from the miniature radio-galaxy sample revealed no candidate that fits the BL Lacertae parent population. However, considering typical Lorentz factor values, we anticipate the presence of 10 to 10 \textsuperscript{3} "misaligned BL Lacertae" objects.

Comparing their results with the original definition by Stickel et al. (1991), BL Lac objects typically have weak emission lines with equivalent widths up to 5 Å in their rest frame. This lack of strong emission lines is not due to low line fluxes, but rather the dominance of a high continuum flux (Scarpa & Falomo, 1997). However, strong emission lines, even broad ones, have been spotted occasionally in the spectra of BL Lac objects during periods of low activity. Examples include BL Lacertae, the namesake of the blazar subclass (Vermeulen et al., 1995), and the distant source AO 0235+164 (Cohen et al., 1987).

In order to delve into this subject, high- and low-resolution spectra of BL Lacertae were obtained using the TNG during four nights spanning 2007–2008, when the source's optical brightness was approximately R=14–14.5. Our analysis verified the presence of a substantial H $\alpha$ broad line with a luminosity of approximately $4\times 10^{41}\,\rm erg\,s^{-1}$ and a Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of around 4600 km/s, in addition to several narrow emission lines. A comprehensive comparison of our findings with those of Corbett et al. (2000) indicated a significant increase of about 50% in the BLR luminosity over a ten-year period. Such variability in Broad Lined Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) is not uncommon and does not necessarily point towards an evolutionary progression.

The question at hand is determining which category of objects display characteristics akin to BL Lacertae, as outlined in the provided description. A promising starting point includes examining radio-galaxies within the 3CR sample, as depicted in Figure 5. The radio luminosity of BL Lacertae falls at the lower extremity of the FR I subset within the 3CR, with only nearby source 3C 272.1 (M 84) being less luminous. Conversely, in terms of narrow emission lines, the [O III] luminosity of BL Lacertae resides at the upper echelons of the 3CR/FR I. This places BL Lacertae as a significant outlier (by a factor of approximately 200-400, depending on the adopted radio luminosity threshold) from the established relationship between line and radio luminosities observed in 3CR radio-galaxies. This observation applies not only to FR I, but also when comparing LEG and HEG classes, leading to significant skepticism regarding the association of BL Lacertae with FR I, and more broadly with radio-galaxies within the 3CR sample. It's worth noting that other conventional radio galaxy samples, such as the B2 and the 2 Jy, also adhere to relationships between radio and line emission similar to the 3CR.

It appears that the disparate measurements of supermassive black hole (SMBH) masses could be explained by a plausible hypothesis. The relationship between SMBH mass and broad line region (BLR) luminosity is articulated by the proportionality $M_{\rm BH}\propto L_{\rm BLR}^{0.6-0.7}$. To reconcile an estimated error in SMBH mass of up to 30 times, the BLR should exhibit a corresponding under-luminosity to the tune of 70–300 times. This alignment with our deduced ratio between BLR and ultraviolet fluxes lends credence to the argument.

However, recent studies have shown that the mass of supermassive black holes (SMBH) is correlated with the luminosity of the Broad Line Region (BLR), with an exponent between 0.6 and 0.7. This implies that a significant underestimation of the SMBH mass could occur if the BLR is underexposed by a factor of 20 to 30, which in turn could result in a BLR that is 70 to 300 times less luminous than expected. This finding aligns with our previous assumptions based on the ratio between the BLR and UV fluxes.

Corbett et al. (2000) continued their spectroscopic observation of BL Lacertae, capturing eight spectra from June 1995 to December 1997, with the Hα broad emission line identified in seven of these observations. They depict their findings in Figure 1, where the upper left panel illustrates the line flux (accounting for extinction and host galaxy contamination) over time, and the lower left panel overlays their continuum estimates with the light curve data by the WEBT collaboration (Villata et al., 2004). They failed to detect the Hα line during the significant optical outburst in June 1997. By combining their estimates of the Hα broad emission line flux with those of ours, we establish an average flux of $30.6\times 10^{-15}\rm\,erg\,cm^{-2}\,s^{-1}$ with a standard deviation of $8.8\times 10^{-15}\rm\,erg\,cm^{-2}\,s^{-1}$ . This suggests that, factoring in the errors, all Hα fluxes are within one standard deviation of the mean value, except for the December 1997 value. Corbett et al. (2000) concluded that there was no "convincing evidence" of "any significant variation" in the Hα flux between 1995 and 1997, and now we can confirm that, approximately ten years later, the Hα flux remains around the same intensity.

Bentz et al. (2009b) propose a revised Super Massive Black Hole (SMBH) mass versus bulge luminosity relation for Active Galactic Nuclei (AGNs) using a sample of 26 objects observed by the Hubble Space Telescope. The SMBH masses were estimated through the reverberation-mapping technique. The SMBH mass for BL Lacertae was derived as $M_{\rm BH}=3.76^{+1.28}_{-0.95}\times 10^{8}M_{\sun}$ when we adopt a bulge luminosity of $L_{V}=5.55\times 10^{10}L_{\sun}$. This estimate is in substantial agreement with the previous estimate by Woo & Urry (2002), who calculated the SMBH mass of BL Lacertae through the correlation between the SMBH mass and the stellar velocity dispersion, finding $M_{\rm BH}=1.70\times 10^{8}M_{\sun}$.

In the study of BL Lacertae's properties, data from the correlation of the observed broad line region (BLR) with the accretion disc provide valuable insights. The XMM-Newton Optical Monitor observations of BL Lacertae, as presented in Raiteri et al. (2009), demonstrate a significant increase in the spectral energy distribution (SED) in the ultraviolet region, which is indicative of the Big Blue Bump, a characteristic emission feature of the accretion disc. The disc luminosity at 2500 Å, estimated from Figs 6 and 7 in Raiteri et al. (2009), is approximately $\log L_{\nu}(2500\AA)\sim 29.4\rm\,erg\,s^{-1}\,Hz^{-1}$, a value comparable to those measured in type 1 Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN), including both radio-loud and radio-quiet types like Fairall 9, PG 1229+204, 3C 390.3, 3C 120, Mkn 509, PG 2130+099, PG 0844+349, and Akn 120, as reported by Vasudevan & Fabian (2009). The broad Hβ line luminosity of these sources ranges from 0.2 to $0.6\times 10^{43}\rm\,erg\,s^{-1}$, according to Kaspi et al. (2005). For BL Lacertae, the broad Hα line luminosity and the Hα/Hβ flux ratio (previously discussed) suggest a luminosity that is approximately 15 to 40 times lower.

Bentz et al. (2009b) have suggested a revised SMBH mass-to-bulge luminosity relation for active galactic nuclei (AGNs), using a sample of 26 objects observed by the Hubble Space Telescope, and for which SMBH masses were estimated via the reverberation-mapping technique. Utilizing a luminosity value of $L_{V}=5.55\times 10^{10}L_{\sun}$ for BL Lacertae, they determined an SMBH mass of $M_{\rm BH}=3.76^{+1.28}_{-0.95}\times 10^{8}M_{\sun}$, which aligns well with a previous estimate by Woo & Urry (2002). Woo & Urry (2002) calculated the SMBH mass of BL Lacertae through the correlation between SMBH mass and stellar velocity dispersion, with the value of $\sigma$ derived from the host galaxy's morphological parameters, as defined by Jorgensen et al. (1996). Their estimate resulted in an SMBH mass of $M_{\rm BH}=1.70\times 10^{8}M_{\sun}$.

For this study, we retrieved the spectra of the target objects from the SDSS public database. Initially classified as quasars, only 18 displayed substantial broad emission lines. The remaining 17 were assigned upper limits for their broad H $\alpha$ luminosities following the methodology of Buttiglione et al. (2010) , with values ranging from approximately $10^{40}$ to $10^{41}$ erg/s. Notably, these galaxies exhibit significantly higher [O III] luminosities compared to BL Lacertae, with values falling within the range $L\rm[{O\textsc{III}}]\sim 2\times 10^{41}-4\times 10^{42}$ erg/s (median of $\sim 5\times 10^{41}$ erg/s). This is a factor of 5 to 100 higher than in BL Lacertae, and these sources also exhibit a HEG spectrum. These objects possess line and radio luminosities as well as line ratios typical of Seyfert galaxies, such as those described by Whittle (1985) . One notable exception is SDSS J122358.97+404409.3, with [O III] luminosity only 20% smaller than BL Lacertae. However, this object demonstrates notable contamination from its active nucleus; the equivalent width (EW) of the NaD feature is EW(NaD) = 1.04 Å, significantly lower than the typical value for early-type galaxies of $\sim 4$ Å, suggesting a nuclear contribution of approximately 80 %. After accounting for the nuclear contribution, the corrected $r$ band magnitude of this object is $M_{R}=-20.7$ magnitudes, which is nearly 2 magnitudes below the host of BL Lacertae.

A professional analysis of BL Lacertae was conducted using the DOLORES spectrograph at the Telescopio Nazionale Galileo (TNG) in 2007–2008, during periods when the source was in a less intense state. On three occasions, we successfully fitted the intricate H $\alpha$ spectral range with multiple line components, subsequently measuring the broad H $\alpha$ and multiple narrow emission line fluxes.

In our analysis, we have not identified any object within the SDSS/NVSS sample that exhibits isotropic characteristics akin to BL Lacertae. Notably, only a limited number of sources display broad emission lines concurrently with a host galaxy luminosity and radio power equivalent to or greater than BL Lacertae. However, these sources typically have a higher narrow line luminosity, approximately 12 times greater than BL Lacertae. Furthermore, the HEG spectrum of these sources contrasts with the proposed identification of BL Lacertae as LEG. Given that "misaligned BL Lacertae" objects should be present in this extensive catalog, we can infer that we have not located the parent population of BL Lacertae, which seems to be an "orphan" AGN.

We retrieved the spectra of the objects from the SDSS public data repository. Although classified as QSO, only 18 of them exhibited prominent broad emission lines. For the remaining 17 objects, we established upper limits for their broad H $\alpha$ luminosity by fitting multiple emission lines to the spectra that were corrected for the stellar component, following the methodology of Buttiglione et al. (2010). These upper limits ranged between $\sim 10^{40}$ and $10^{41}$ erg s\textsuperscript{-1}.

The galaxies, however, had significantly higher [O III] luminosity than BL Lacertae, within the range $L\rm[{O\textsc{III}}]\sim 2\times 10^{41}-4\times 10^{42}$ erg s\textsuperscript{-1} (with a median of $\sim 5\times 10^{41}$ erg s\textsuperscript{-1}). This was a factor of 5 to 100 higher than in BL Lacertae, and the galaxies were also characterized by a HEG spectrum. These sources displayed line and radio luminosities, as well as line ratios, typical of Seyfert galaxies, such as those described by Whittle (1985).

One notable exception was SDSS J122358.97+404409.3, which had a lower [O III] luminosity than BL Lacertae, but was contaminated by its active nucleus. The equivalent width (EW) of the NaD feature was EW(NaD) = 1.04 Å, significantly lower than typical values for early type galaxies of $\sim 4$ Å, suggesting a nuclear contribution of approximately 80 %. After correcting for the nuclear contribution, the $r$ band magnitude of this object was $M_{R}=-20.7$, which was nearly 2 magnitudes lower than the host of BL Lacertae.

In the context of our quantum network, a fundamental component is the high-fidelity phase gate, which facilitates interaction between an external photon and a node comprising a multi-level quantum system embedded within a cavity, possessing qubit degrees of freedom. The dynamics of this gate can be elucidated through the input-output formalism, which provides a framework for understanding the photon-matter interface. By adopting the interaction picture, wherein oscillations at the cavity frequency are eliminated, the non-Hermitian Hamiltonian governing the photon-matter interface can be expressed as a comprehensive integral formulation. This formulation encompasses the energy of the photon, the coupling between the photon and the cavity mode, as well as the interaction between the cavity mode and the qubit. The qubit, in turn, is characterized by its ground state and excited state, with the former being decoupled from the cavity. The incorporation of non-Hermitian evolution terms enables the modeling of atomic decay and mirror absorption loss rates, thereby accounting for the effects of environmental baths on the system. Notably, the Hamiltonian does not induce transitions between the qubit's ground and excited states, allowing for the separate treatment of the wave function for each qubit state. The wave function of the system, comprising the external field modes and the cavity mode, can be solved using the Schrödinger equation, yielding a set of amplitude differential equations that govern the evolution of the system.

A novel paradigm for quantum networking is presented, wherein a straightforward interaction protocol is devised for implementation on a scalable architecture, comprising qubit systems encapsulated within cavities. These nodal points are interconnected through the deliberate manipulation of a lone photon, which facilitates deterministic coupling via transmission and reflection, subsequently disentangling from the qubits upon completion of the operational cycle. This photon-mediated mechanism enables the generation of a controlled-phase gate, denoted as C-PHASE, which can entangle an arbitrary number of qubits throughout the network. Notably, our multiquantum gate architecture yields substantially enhanced fidelity in comparison to conventional approaches reliant on concatenated one- and two-qubit gates, thereby providing a robust foundation for distributed quantum computation across multiple processing units. Furthermore, while our analytical framework focuses on atomic qubits interfaced with optical photons, the proposed scheme exhibits versatility and can be readily adapted to alternative architectures, including superconducting qubit nodes interconnected by microwave photons.

In this setup, two photon baths are introduced, denoting the left and right aspects of the cavity, which are characterized by the operators $a_{L}(\omega)$ and $a_{R}(\omega)$, along with their conjugates. The Hamiltonian, which is non-Hermitian in nature, can be expressed as a summation over the left and right baths, incorporating the frequency-dependent energies of the photonic modes, as well as their interactions with a cavity mode $b$ and its conjugate, mediated by the coupling functions $g_{j}(\omega)$. Additionally, the Hamiltonian accounts for the coherent coupling between the cavity mode and a two-level system, represented by the states $|e\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$, with a coupling strength $g$, while also considering the effects of cavity absorption losses, characterized by the rates $\kappa_{L}^{\prime}$ and $\kappa_{R}^{\prime}$. By extending the reasoning presented earlier, the wave function describing the two-qubit states can be expanded in terms of the basis states $|0\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$, where the external field modes are in their respective vacuum states, denoted by $0_{a_{L}}$ and $0_{a_{R}}$. This formulation yields a set of differential equations that govern the time-evolution of the amplitudes associated with each state.

The creation of an entangled state can be facilitated through a multi-step process. Initially, two qubits, situated in distinct cavity nodes, are prepared in the $|1\rangle$ state, after which they are subjected to the action of Hadamard gates, resulting in a superposition of states. This transformation yields an equal weighting of the $|00\rangle$, $|01\rangle$, $|10\rangle$, and $|11\rangle$ states, effectively creating a uniform distribution across these possibilities. Subsequently, a solitary photon is transmitted through the network, allowing for the introduction of a C-PHASE gate, albeit with imperfect fidelity, which contributes to the overall entanglement generation process.
We initialize the sequence with $g_{1}$ set to $g$ and $g_{0}$ set to $0$.

Within the Markov approximation framework, the input and output fields associated with the $j^{th}$ mode are defined by the time-dependent functions $b^{j}_{{in}}(t)$ and $b^{j}_{{out}}(t)$, which are expressed in terms of the spectral functions ${\Phi_{j}}(\omega,0)$ and ${\Phi_{j}}(\omega,t_{f})$. The input-output relationship for the left and right modes is governed by the equation $b^{j}_{out}(t)=b^{j}_{in}(t)+\sqrt{\kappa_{j}}C_{b}(t)$. By incorporating an external field, as described by Eq. S.23, into Eq. S.19, we derive the temporal evolution of $C_{b}(t)$, yielding a differential equation that accounts for the interplay between the system's dynamics and external inputs. Upon transforming this equation to the frequency domain, we obtain a linear set of equations that describes the system's behavior in a more tractable form.

In the context of a three-cavity setup, a sequence of operations is applied, comprising the controlled-phase gate $U_{CP}$, which entails the exponential of a Hamiltonian that includes terms proportional to the product of projection operators for the first, second, and third cavities, as well as terms involving phases $\phi_3 + \phi_4$ and $\phi_1 + \phi_2$, effectively introducing conditional phase shifts. Additionally, the operations $U_{B_3}$ and $U_{B_{3,1}}$ are implemented, representing exponential functions of Hamiltonians that include phase-dependent terms and projection operators for specific cavity states. The sequence also incorporates the application of $\Pi_i$, a rotation operator acting on the $i$th cavity, generated by the Pauli $x$ matrix $\sigma_{x,i}$, with the overall aim of achieving dynamical decoupling through a carefully designed sequence of these operations.

In the context of a three-cavity scenario, a set of operations is applied, which can be mathematically represented as a series of exponential functions involving the Pauli matrices and phase factors $\phi_{1}$ through $\phi_{4}$. Specifically, these operations include $U_{CP}$, $U_{B_{3}}$, $U_{B_{3,1}}$, and $\Pi_{i}$, where the latter denotes a rotation around the x-axis by $\pi$ for the $i^{th}$ cavity. The dynamical decoupling sequence is then constructed by concatenating these operations in a particular order, resulting in a complex expression that can be simplified through a series of algebraic manipulations. By leveraging properties of exponential functions and the Pauli matrices, the expression can be transformed into a more compact form, ultimately yielding a product of exponential terms that depend on the phase factors and the state of the cavities. This final expression reveals a clear phase dependence on the combined phases $\phi_{1}+\phi_{2}$ and $\phi_{3}+\phi_{4}$, alongside a conditional phase gate that depends on the state of all three cavities. The net effect of this sequence is thus a conditional phase shift that is contingent upon the specific configuration of the cavity states, underscored by the appearance of terms like $e^{i\pi|1\rangle_{1}\langle 1|\otimes|1\rangle_{2}\langle 1|\otimes|1\rangle_{3}\langle 1|}$, which implies a phase shift of $-\pi$ when all cavities are in the $|1\rangle$ state.

The supplementary figure S1 elucidates the numerical outcomes for fidelity in multi-cavity configurations, comprising two, three, and four cavities, as a function of various physical parameters. Notably, these simulations presume a Gaussian incident wave packet characterized by a spectral distribution of $\left|\Phi(\omega)\right|^{2}=\exp\left(-\omega^{2}/2\Delta\Omega^{2}\right)/\Delta\Omega\sqrt{2\pi}$, where the bandwidth $\Delta\Omega$ is inversely proportional to the time duration $\Delta T$. Furthermore, the analysis assumes uniform propagation delays $\tau$ between adjacent cavities, as well as phase fluctuations denoted by $\delta$. Additionally, the cavities are modeled with identical transmission and absorption loss rates, denoted by $\kappa$ and $\kappa^{\prime}$ respectively, and equal photon loss rates $\eta$ between cavities. Fig. S1 also presents the fidelity and success probability of a heralded gate scheme contingent upon the detection of the transmitted photon. A critical observation emerges in Fig. S1, highlighting the paramount importance of the cooperativity parameter $C$, defined as the ratio of the coupling strength $g^{2}$ to the product of the cavity loss rate $\kappa$ and the decay rate $\gamma$. This underlines the necessity for strong coupling to achieve optimal performance, with approximate analytical expressions provided in Eq. [6] of the main text offering further insight into this relationship.

In this configuration, two photonic field baths are introduced, corresponding to the left and right sides of the cavity, denoted by $a_{L}(\omega),a_{L}^{\dagger}(\omega)$ and $a_{R}(\omega),a_{R}^{\dagger}(\omega)$. The non-Hermitian Hamiltonian is given, incorporating cavity absorption loss rates associated with both cavity mirrors. The wave function of the two-qubit states is described, and a set of differential equations for the amplitudes of the state is obtained. These equations are solved, yielding input-output relations for the left and right modes, which are then used to derive a linear set of equations in the frequency domain. By solving these equations in the symmetric case, where the transmission and absorption loss rates are equal for both mirrors, the reflection and transmission coefficients are obtained, which are applied in the main text. The overcoupled cavity regime is employed, where the absorption loss rate is significantly less than the transmission loss rate, to ensure the validity of the results.

The solution to this set of linear equations yields the reflection coefficient $R(\omega,g_{q})$, which is expressed as the ratio of output to input, $b_{out}(\omega)$ to $b_{in}(\omega)$. To achieve near-unit reflectance values, the overcoupled cavity regime is required, where transmission losses surpass absorption losses, specifically $\kappa\gg\kappa^{\prime}$. Notably, although the frequency-domain solution lacks an explicit time dependence, the input $b_{in}(\omega)$ represents the Fourier transform of a time-dependent pulse, enabling a return to the time domain via inverse Fourier transform to examine the dynamics of cavity excitation amplitude build-up and decay.

The differential equations governing the external field amplitudes, as expressed in equations S.17 and S.18, are resolved through integration over both retrospective and prospective time domains, consistent with previous methods.

The application of a C-PHASE gate to a superposition state, followed by a Hadamard gate on the second qubit, yields an entangled state that approximates $|\Psi_{ent}\rangle=\left[|01\rangle-|10\rangle\right]/\sqrt{2}$. The fidelity of this entangled state, denoted as $F_{ent}$, can be expressed as the squared absolute value of the inner product of the entangled state with the result of applying the C-PHASE gate and Hadamard gates to the initial state $|00\rangle$. By assuming that errors in initialization and single-qubit operations are negligible, the fidelity simplifies to the expression $F_{ent}=\left|\frac{1}{4}\left(\langle 00|U_{C-PHASE}|00\rangle+\langle 01|U_{C-PHASE}|01\rangle+\langle 10|U_{C-PHASE}|10\rangle-\langle 11|U_{C-PHASE}|11\rangle\right)\right|^{2}$, which is equivalent to the fidelity of the two-node C-PHASE operation, $F_{2}$, as presented in the main paper.

By applying the Fourier transform to the equations governing state amplitudes and input-output relations, we derive a set of linear equations in the frequency domain, which can be solved to yield the reflection coefficient $R(\omega, g_q)$. This coefficient, given by equation (S.14), describes the relationship between input and output fields, and its magnitude can approach unity in the overcoupled cavity regime, where transmission loss dominates absorption loss. To achieve near-unit reflection coefficients, the condition $\kappa \gg \kappa'$ must be satisfied, as discussed in previous work. Although the frequency-domain solution does not explicitly depend on time, the input field $b_{in}(\omega)$ represents the Fourier transform of a time-dependent pulse, and a subsequent inverse Fourier transform allows for the examination of the temporal dynamics of cavity excitation amplitude buildup and decay.

Within the framework of the Markov approximation, the spectral density $g_{c}(\omega)$ is treated as a quasi-constant, with its value approximately equal to $\sqrt{\kappa/2\pi}$, where $\kappa$ denotes the rate at which the cavity loses energy. To facilitate a more nuanced understanding, we define time-dependent wave packets for single-photon input and output, denoted by $b_{in}(t)$ and $b_{out}(t)$, respectively, which are expressed as Fourier transforms of the frequency-domain wave function $\Phi(\omega,t)$. Specifically, $b_{in}(t)$ and $b_{out}(t)$ are obtained by integrating $\Phi(\omega,0)$ and $\Phi(\omega,t_{f})$ over all frequencies, weighted by a phase factor and normalized by $\sqrt{2\pi}$. By directly comparing the solutions for the field amplitude, we derive an input-output relationship, which reveals that the output wave packet $b_{out}(t)$ is a superposition of the input wave packet $b_{in}(t)$ and the intracavity field $C_{b}(t)$, scaled by the damping rate $\kappa$. Furthermore, upon substituting the external driving field into the equation of motion, we obtain a differential equation governing the evolution of $C_{b}(t)$, which explicitly shows how the cavity field builds up in response to the incident photon wave packet, with the interplay between the coupling strength $g_{q}$, the damping rates $\kappa$ and $\kappa^{\prime}$, and the input field $b_{in}(t)$.

Figure S1 presents a comprehensive analysis of the fidelity for two, three, and four cavity scenarios, exploring the impact of various physical parameters on the system's performance. The simulations assume a Gaussian incident wave packet with a specified bandwidth, as well as uniform propagation delays, phase fluctuations, and loss rates across all cavities. The results highlight the critical role of the cooperativity parameter, underscoring the necessity of strong coupling for optimal performance, and are complemented by approximate analytical expressions and an examination of the fidelity and success probability of a heralded gate scheme relying on transmitted photon detection.

Our quantum network's fundamental component is a high-fidelity phase gate that interfaces a single photon with a multi-level quantum system, comprising qubit degrees of freedom within a cavity. The dynamics of this gate can be described using the input-output formalism, where the photon-matter interaction is represented by a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian that accounts for the coupling between the external photon, cavity mode, and qubit state. This Hamiltonian incorporates the effects of atomic decay and mirror absorption loss, which are modeled using non-Hermitian evolution terms. By solving the Schrödinger equation, we obtain a set of amplitude differential equations that describe the time-evolution of the system's wave function, which can be expressed in terms of the external photon, cavity mode, and qubit states. These equations form the basis for understanding the behavior of our quantum network, enabling the analysis of photon-qubit interactions and the characterization of the phase gate's performance.

Through the application of the Fourier transform to the state amplitude equations and the input-output relationship, a transition to the frequency domain is facilitated, yielding the following result:

The solution to these equations yields the reflection and transmission coefficients utilized in the primary analysis, specifically under symmetric conditions where $\kappa_{L}=\kappa_{R}=\kappa$ and $\kappa_{L}^{\prime}=\kappa_{R}^{\prime}=\kappa^{\prime}$. The resulting expression for $b_{out}^{R,L}(\omega)$ is derived as a function of input coefficients and system parameters, assuming an overcoupled cavity regime characterized by negligible absorption loss rate relative to transmission loss rate, i.e., $\kappa^{\prime}\ll\kappa$.

The creation of an entangled state can be achieved through a multi-step process, commencing with the initialization of two qubits in distinct cavity nodes to the $|1\rangle$ state, followed by the application of Hadamard gates to both, resulting in a superposition of states: $|11\rangle{\rightarrow}\frac{|00\rangle+|01\rangle+|10\rangle+|11\rangle}{2}$. Subsequently, a single photon is transmitted through the network, introducing the effect of an imperfect C-PHASE gate.

By leveraging qubit rotation and selective cavity exemption from multi-qubit C-PHASE operations, coupled with the transmission of multiple single photon wave packets, our protocol can be made more robust against phase fluctuations, analogous to the mitigation of low-frequency perturbations achieved through dynamical decoupling techniques.

In our theoretical framework, we assume a uniform spacing between consecutive components, denoted as $\delta L$, which is equivalent to a constant time delay of $c\tau$ (as illustrated in Fig. S2). This delay is mathematically represented by a phase shift of $\exp\left(i\tau\omega\right)$, which is applied to the frequency-domain wave packet as it propagates between adjacent components. To estimate the fidelity, we adopt a conservative approach by evaluating the overlap between the transmitted wave packets for all possible qubit configurations and a reference wave packet, allowing only a phase deviation of $\exp\left(-i\xi\omega\right)$ from the incident wave. Here, $\xi$ signifies a median delay of the wave packets, and we determine its optimal values for $N=2, 3,$ and $4$ qubits, yielding the expressions: 
$\begin{array}{cc}\xi_{2}=-1.4/\kappa-1.0\tau\\
\xi_{3}=-1.1/\kappa-1.7\tau\\
\xi_{4}=-0.8/\kappa-2.2\tau\\
\end{array}$ 
(Eq. S.31), which provide insight into the relationship between the delay and the number of qubits.

The solution to the differential equation governing the external field amplitude is obtained through integration over a specified time interval, yielding two equivalent expressions that describe the field amplitude at an arbitrary time. By integrating these expressions over the frequency domain, we derive equations that relate the field amplitude to the input and output photon wave packets, assuming a Markov approximation where the cavity-field coupling is treated as a slowly varying function of frequency. This approximation enables the introduction of time-dependent single-photon input and output wave packets, which in turn provide an input-output relation that describes the interaction between the cavity field and the external field. Ultimately, this relation is used to derive an equation that governs the dynamics of the cavity field, illustrating how the one-photon amplitude builds up in response to the incident photon wave packet.

To further assess the performance of the refocused gates, we analyzed their fidelity in relation to bandwidth and cooperativity, assuming negligible values for certain parameters. The resulting expressions for the fidelity of gates $F_2$, $F_3$, and $F_4$ reveal a decrease in fidelity as the number of photons required for the multi-C-PHASE gate increases, even in the absence of phase fluctuations, ultimately leading to lower fidelity compared to single-photon gates, as illustrated in the numerical results presented in Figure S1 D.

By leveraging qubit rotation capabilities and selectively excluding specific cavities from the multi-qubit C-PHASE operation, combined with the transmission of multiple single photon wave packets, our protocol's robustness against phase fluctuations can be enhanced, akin to the mitigation of low-frequency perturbations achieved through dynamical decoupling techniques.

When the effects of finite cooperativity and bandwidth limitations are minimal, the required operations comprise the initial, albeit imperfect, multi-qubit C-PHASE gate $U_{CP_{N}}$, a series of $\pi$-pulses $\Pi_{i}$ applied to individual qubits, and the $U_{B_{\left\{j\right\}}}$ operation that results from transmitting photons while selectively blocking one or more cavities $\left\{j\right\}$.

In the realm of large-dimensional random matrices, a fascinating phenomenon emerges, wherein the eigenvalue distributions of matrix products and sums, such as ${\bf A}{\bf B}$ and ${\bf A}+{\bf B}$, can be deterministically computed from the individual eigenvalue distributions of ${\bf A}$ and ${\bf B}$, provided that the matrices are independent and sufficiently large. This computation is facilitated by the process of convolution, which can be inverted through de-convolution. Notably, matrices with Gaussian-like properties are amenable to this framework, and the concept of freeness, a fundamental idea in free probability theory, enables the derivation of eigenvalue distributions from those of constituent matrices. Free probability theory, pioneered by Voiculescu in the 1980s, has evolved into a distinct field of research, leveraging the notion of freeness to establish an analogy to independence in classical probability, applicable to non-commutative random variables, such as matrices, which inhabit noncommutative probability spaces. Various techniques, including analytic methods utilizing $R$ and $S$ transforms, moment-based approaches, and recent statistical eigen-inference methods employing large Wishart matrices or random matrix theory, have been developed for convolution and de-convolution. Although these techniques have been successfully applied, their limitations become apparent when dealing with complex models, as they are generally restricted to cases where one of the matrices exhibits unitary invariance, a concept imbued with specific significance in wireless networks, implying a degree of symmetry in the problem under analysis. The moment technique, which will be the primary focus of this work, offers a powerful framework for deriving the exact asymptotic moments of non-free matrices, for which a general framework remains elusive. While this approach requires combinatorial expertise and can be applied to a broad class of random matrices, its primary limitation, compared to alternative tools like the Stieltjes transform method, lies in its rare ability to provide the exact eigenvalue distribution. Nevertheless, in numerous applications, only a subset of moments is necessary, depending on the number of parameters to be estimated, rendering the moment technique a valuable and appealing tool.

This study's findings have significant implications for inferring model parameters from observed output, particularly in scenarios where one or more parameters are unknown. The research enables a form of "deconvolution" that allows for the estimation of parameters in various models, such as those involving Vandermonde matrices. For instance, given observations of specific matrix forms, it is possible to infer the spectrum or phase distribution of certain components, provided that only one of these is unknown. The complexity of this inference process is determined by the number of moments considered, and the approach can be applied to various types of deconvolutions. A simple example demonstrates the estimation of lower-order moments of a component matrix, which can provide valuable information, especially in cases with few distinct eigenvalues and known multiplicities. Further development of a second-order theory for Vandermonde matrices has the potential to enhance this deconvolution technique.

This paper elucidates the specific matrix operations that are solely dependent on spectral properties, provides explicit formulas for the corresponding convolutions, and outlines the algorithmic derivation of these expressions, including cases where spectral representation is not feasible. Furthermore, it presents a software implementation of the underlying algorithms and expands upon the preliminary analysis in [20] by establishing a comprehensive criterion for determining when the mixed moments of Vandermonde matrices and deterministic matrices are solely dependent on the input spectra.

This manuscript is structured as follows: Section II presents foundational concepts from random matrix theory that underpin the primary findings, which are formally stated in Section III. These findings encompass a detailed criterion for dependence on matrix spectra, results pertaining to convolution operations, and extensions to specialized matrices, including Toeplitz and Hankel matrices, as well as a generalized framework for almost sure convergence of matrices. The derivation of the presented formulas is rooted in the implementation, with key components such as partition iteration and Fourier-Motzkin elimination [28] elucidated in Section IV. Section V reports on a simulation that validates the theoretical results.

Notational conventions will be employed wherein uppercase bold (lowercase bold) characters denote matrices (column vectors), with lowercase letters reserved for scalar quantities. The transpose of a matrix will be indicated by the superscript $T$, conjugation by $\star$, and the Hermitian transpose by $H$, which is defined as the conjugate of the transpose. The identity matrix of dimension $L \times L$ will be represented by ${\bf I}_{L}$. For square matrices, the trace operator, denoted by $\mathrm{Tr}$, is defined as the sum of the diagonal elements, given by $\sum_{i=1}^{L}a_{ii}$ for an $L \times L$ matrix ${\bf A}$ with diagonal elements $a_{ii}$. A normalized version of the trace, denoted by $\mathrm{tr}$, is also defined, which scales the trace by the inverse of the matrix dimension, yielding $\mathrm{tr}({\bf A}) = \frac{1}{L}\mathrm{Tr}({\bf A})$.
In subsequent discussions, the result of multiplying different types of matrices, as illustrated in expression 4, will be referred to as mixed moments.

The forthcoming expressions for convolutions 1)-4) are formulated in accordance with equation (10), with their derivations implicitly contained within the proof of Theorem 1 and explicitly outlined in Appendix B. Notably, the aspect ratio $c$ is treated as an external factor, decoupled from the algorithmic process itself. These results are deliberately constructed to facilitate inversion, thereby enabling the retrieval of original parameters through a process of deconvolution; for example, the moments of ${\bf D}(N)$ can be deduced from those of ${\bf D}(N){\bf V}^{H}{\bf V}$. The utility of these theorems in deconvolution applications is equally significant as their limiting cases, as it permits the estimation of underlying model parameters, represented here by ${\bf D}(N)$ and ${\bf V}$. Furthermore, the accompanying implementation provided in this paper also incorporates support for deconvolution procedures.

In the context of random matrix theory, consider a sequence of independent Vandermonde matrices ${\bf V}_{i}$, each of size $N_{i} \times L$, with aspect ratios $c_{i}$ defined as the limit of the ratio of the number of columns to the number of rows as $N_{i}$ approaches infinity, and phase distributions $\omega_{i}$ characterized by continuous densities over the interval $[0, 2\pi)$. The existence of the large $N$ limit of the mixed moment, expressed as the trace of a product of matrices ${\bf D}_{i}(N)$ and ${\bf V}_{i}$, is guaranteed when the matrices ${\bf D}_{i}(N)$ converge jointly in distribution. Specifically, in the absence of terms involving the product of the conjugate transpose of one Vandermonde matrix with another having a different phase distribution, the mixed moment depends solely on the asymptotic moments $V_{n}^{(i)}$ of the matrix product ${\bf V}_{i}^{H}{\bf V}_{i}$, the limiting trace of the product of the matrices ${\bf D}_{i}(N)$, denoted as $D_{i_{1},...,i_{s}}$, the aspect ratios $c_{i}$, and the permutation $\sigma$. This dependence takes the form of a finite sum of products, where each term is a rational coefficient $a_{i_{1},...,i_{s},j_{1},...,j_{r},k_{1},...,k_{r}}$ multiplied by the product of $D_{i_{1},...,i_{s}}$ and $V_{j_{t}}^{(k_{t})}$ for various indices $i$, $j$, $k$, and $t$.
Two partitions are considered equivalent if one can be obtained from the other through a cyclic permutation involving a fixed number of elements.

A detailed exposition of the proof underlying Theorem 9 is provided in Appendix D, which underscores the convergence properties of specific matrix constructs that emerge from our convolutional operations. Notably, the matrices in question, including the products ${\bf V}_{1}^{H}{\bf V}_{1}{\bf V}_{2}^{H}{\bf V}_{2}$ and the sum ${\bf V}_{1}^{H}{\bf V}_{1}+{\bf V}_{2}^{H}{\bf V}_{2}$, exhibit almost sure convergence in distribution, a phenomenon that can be attributed to the inherent characteristics of these matrix products and sums.

The rank of matrix $A$ is determined to be $k-1$ when the join of $\rho(\pi)$ and the interval $[0,1]_{n}$ yields $1_{2n}$. In the more general case, where this join results in a partition $\{\rho_{1},...,\rho_{r}\}$ with each $\rho_{i}$ satisfying $\rho_{i}\geq[0,1]_{\|\rho_{i}\|/2}$, the rank of the system is given by $|\rho(\pi)|-r$. This is because the equations corresponding to each $\rho_{i}$ are independent of those corresponding to $\rho_{j}$ and the sum of the equations for $\rho_{i}$ equals zero, implying that the coefficient matrix for $\rho_{i}$ has a rank one less than full. Furthermore, for any $2s+1,2s+2,2t+1,2t+2$ belonging to the same $\rho_{i}$, $N_{i_{s+2}}=N_{i_{t+2}}$, denoted as $N_{\rho_{i}}$, indicating a common upper limit for all variables $i_{l}$ associated with the same block $\rho_{i}$. Consequently, the number of solutions to equation (31) is of order $O\left(\prod_{i=1}^{r}N_{\rho_{i}}^{\|\rho_{i}\|/2-|\rho_{i}|+1}\right)$, which simplifies to $O\left(L^{n-|\rho|+r}\right)$. As $r$ depends solely on $\pi$, it is also denoted as $r(\pi)$. Given that the number of solutions to (31) is expressed by (32), the limit in (34) exists, and its value is denoted by $K_{\rho(\pi),u}$. Notably, $K_{\rho(\pi),u}$ can be factored into $\prod_{i=1}^{r}K_{\rho_{i},u}$ due to the independence of the equation sets. This extends the definition of Vandermonde mixed moment expansion coefficients to cases with unequal numbers of variables on each side of the equations, with the coefficients now defined in terms of $\rho(\pi)$, capturing any $\sigma_{1}$, a novel aspect of this analysis. In line with previous work, $K_{\rho(\pi),u,L}$ represents the quantity within the limit of (34), making the number of solutions to (31) equal to $\prod_{i=1}^{r}N_{\rho_{i}}^{\|\rho_{i}\|/2-|\rho_{i}|+1}K_{\rho(\pi),u,L}$.

By definition, each block in $\pi$ corresponds to at least one block in $\rho(\pi)$, implying that the number of blocks in $\pi$ is less than or equal to the number of blocks in $\rho(\pi)$, i.e., $|\pi|\leq|\rho(\pi)|$. Furthermore, when two blocks in $\rho(\pi)$ are adjacent, they share a common $j$-value at their boundary, which leads to a tighter bound of $|\pi|\leq|\rho(\pi)|-1$. Extending this logic to multiple adjacent blocks, $\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}, ..., \rho_{r}$, yields the inequality $|\pi|\leq|\rho(\pi)|-r(\pi)+1$. This construction also reveals that both equality and strict inequality are possible, depending on the specific boundary configurations of the blocks in $\rho(\pi)$.

The trace of the matrix $({\bf D}+{\bf V}^{H}{\bf V})^{n}$ can be expressed as a double summation over $k$ and $s$, where the inner sum iterates over all possible combinations of indices $(r_{1},...,r_{k})$ satisfying $\sum r_{i}=n-k-s$, and later refined to $\sum r_{i}=n-k$ with $r_{1}\geq s$. This formulation enables the computation of each summand by leveraging equation (41) and facilitates the traversal of multi-indices $(r_{1},...,r_{k})$. Furthermore, this approach can be generalized to calculate individual summands for Theorem 3 by expanding the vector $K_{n}$ to accommodate mixed moments, thus providing a framework for the implementation of the theorem's formulas.

The expectation of the fourth power of the trace of a matrix product can be expressed as a sum of terms involving the trace of the matrix raised to various powers, which can be further simplified using interval partitions. By defining classes of partitions based on which intervals are joined, the expression can be rewritten as a sum over these classes, and it is found that only one class, denoted as ${\cal P}_{1,2,3,4}$, contributes to the final result. For a uniform phase distribution, the coefficient matrix of the equation system corresponding to this class has a rank of $|\pi|-1$, resulting in a solution of order $N^{4r-|\pi|+1}$, and the final expression is of order $O(L^{-3})$. This result can be extended to non-uniform phase distributions using Lemma 2 and techniques from Appendix A, with a modification of the constant $C_{r}$ to account for the maximum of all Vandermonde mixed moment expansion coefficients of order $4r$.

As evident from equation (40), we can express the relationship between the matrices $M$ and $V$ using the equation $\left(\begin{array}[]{c}M_{1}\\ M_{2}\\ \vdots\\ M_{n}\end{array}\right)=K_{n}\left(\begin{array}[]{c}V_{1}\\ V_{2}\\ \vdots\\ V_{2n}\end{array}\right)$, where $K_{n}$ is an $n\times 2n$ matrix whose elements are determined solely by the results of Fourier-Motzkin elimination.

Recent studies have highlighted the significance of Vandermonde matrices in wireless communications, with applications in signal reconstruction, cognitive radio, physical layer security, and MIMO channel modeling. A Vandermonde matrix is defined as a matrix with entries on the unit circle, taking the form of ${\bf V}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\left(\begin{array}[]{lll}1&\cdots&1\\ e^{-j\omega_{1}}&\cdots&e^{-j\omega_{L}}\\ \vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\ e^{-j(N-1)\omega_{1}}&\cdots&e^{-j(N-1)\omega_{L}}\end{array}\right)$, where $\omega_{1}$, …, $\omega_{L}$ are independent and identically distributed phase distributions. Research has shown that the limit eigenvalue distributions of combinations of ${\bf V}^{H}{\bf V}$ and diagonal matrices ${\bf D}(N)$ depend only on the limit eigenvalue distributions of the individual matrices. However, the computational methods for these operations, particularly for multiplicative and additive models, remain unclear. Furthermore, the dependence of the results on the input spectra for other operations, such as $\lim_{N\rightarrow\infty}{\bf D}(N){\bf V}{\bf V}^{H}$ and $\lim_{N\rightarrow\infty}({\bf D}(N)+{\bf V}{\bf V}^{H})$, is still unknown, and is crucial for practical applications in cognitive radio and secure transmissions. Additionally, the behavior of convolution operations involving independent Vandermonde matrices, such as $\lim_{N\rightarrow\infty}{\bf V}_{1}^{H}{\bf V}_{1}{\bf V}_{2}^{H}{\bf V}_{2}$ and $\lim_{N\rightarrow\infty}({\bf V}_{1}^{H}{\bf V}_{1}+{\bf V}_{2}^{H}{\bf V}_{2})$, is also unknown and requires further investigation for applications in sensor recovery and MIMO multi-fold scattering.

This research examines viable policy solutions to address disparities in the provision of local public goods, with a specific focus on the interconnected issues of high property taxes, geographic income inequality, and unequal access to quality education in the United States. By incorporating variations in income and housing quality into a dynamic general equilibrium model, this study provides new insights into the competitive dynamics between local districts and proposes innovative policy interventions. The findings indicate that strategic competition among districts can lead to excessive taxation in pursuit of affluent residents, but implementing policies that limit or tax excessive spending in wealthier districts can be politically feasible and beneficial, reducing educational inequality while increasing funding for under-resourced schools. The analysis also explores the impact of homeownership rates on policy outcomes and validates the model's assumptions using empirical data from property tax referenda in Massachusetts.

An examination of the data reveals both direct and indirect indications of income sorting, with a notable positive correlation between tax rates and per capita income, as illustrated in the model outlined in equation 12 and summarized in Figure 3. However, a more nuanced interpretation of these findings is warranted, as the significant effect of taxation on income is mitigated when controlling for initial condition disparities between treatment groups through the estimation of percentage changes rather than absolute levels.

The district government's primary goal is influenced by various factors, including expenditures, taxation, pricing, and the distribution of wealth among existing and incoming residents, with a primary focus on enhancing the well-being of both groups. Specifically, the objective of the district can be represented by a function that prioritizes school quality and the welfare of existing residents, which is formalized as a weighted sum of school quality and the aggregate welfare of existing residents, where the weight reflects the importance assigned to educational quality. Notably, the district's decision-making process is likely driven by the interests of existing residents, particularly when these decisions are made during the intergenerational transition period, and it can be demonstrated that the district has an incentive to impose excessive taxation even in the absence of a direct benefit to new residents.

Consider a situation where a subset of districts, denoted as $Z$, is selected from the set of districts $\{2, \dots, n\}$. The goal is to analyze how district $1$ optimizes its expenditure and tax policy when the difference in expenditure between itself and the selected districts in $Z$ is maintained at a constant level, while the expenditure of districts not in $Z$ remains at their equilibrium levels. This specific scenario is termed the "fixed gap" problem. In this context, district $1$ aims to maximize its objective function, denoted as $\tilde{\Pi}(e_{1},\tau_{1})$, where $e_{1}$ represents the expenditure level and $\tau_{1}$ represents the tax schedule. The optimal tax schedule for collecting a given amount of revenue $e_{1}$ is represented by $\tilde{\tau}(e_{1})$, and the optimal expenditure level for district $1$ is denoted as $\tilde{e}^{1}_{1}$.

The derivation of the weak comparative statics outcome hinges on the satisfaction of a specific weak increasing differences condition, wherein for any $e^{\prime}$ exceeding the threshold $e^{A}_{1}$, the difference in profits $\Pi$ between the scenarios $(e^{\prime},\tau^{A}_{1}(e^{\prime}))$ and $(e^{A}_{1},\tau^{A}_{1}(e^{A}_{1}))$ is greater than or equal to the difference in modified profits $\tilde{\Pi}$ between the scenarios $(e^{\prime},\tilde{\tau}^{A}_{1}(e^{\prime})|e_{1}^{A})$ and $(e^{A}_{1},\tilde{\tau}^{A}_{1}(e^{A}_{1})|e^{A}_{1})$, thus establishing a fundamental relationship between the profit functions and the choice of $e$.

In a model featuring overlapping generations, individuals are assumed to live for two periods, with each period characterized by distinct economic activities. Each individual is defined by their per-period income, denoted as their type, which falls within a specified range. During the first period, individuals invest in a residence, utilize local educational resources, and allocate funds for consumption, while also having the ability to borrow or save at a specified interest rate. In the subsequent period, they liquidate their residential asset and engage in further consumption. The economic outcomes for an individual of a given type are determined by their decisions regarding housing quality, district of residence, and savings, which in turn influence their overall payoff.

The stability of the equilibrium depends on the single-crossing property of individual preferences regarding location quality and present discounted value, where a lower-type individual's preference for a high-quality location with low present discounted value implies the same preference for a higher-type individual. To prove this property holds between two variables, it's enough to demonstrate a specific inequality involving the value function. Given that the value function is differentiable almost everywhere due to the envelope theorem, its derivative can be expressed in terms of utility functions. This leads to the condition that for the single-crossing property to hold, the derivative of the value function at a higher location quality must be greater than or equal to the derivative at a lower location quality for all relevant values. This condition is met if the derivative of the value function increases with location quality, which can be shown using the first-order condition for optimal savings and the concavity of the utility function, ultimately confirming the single-crossing property and thus the monotonicity of the equilibrium.

The district's objective function is assumed to exhibit specific properties in relation to its variables: it increases as $e^{j}$ rises, decreases as $\tau^{j}$ increases, and increases with $p^{j}$ under the uniform dominance partial order, while it weakly increases with $\Gamma^{j}$ under the first-order stochastic dominance partial order, and it is also continuous in $\tau^{j}$ with respect to the sup-norm.
