To establish the desired properties, we begin by verifying that the Hodge structure $V^{(k)}$ exhibits weights confined to $\{(-1,0),(0,-1)\}$. The condition imposed on $V^{(1)}$ ensures that the dimensions of the $\sigma$-components of ${V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{-1,0}$, where $\sigma:L\rightarrow\mathbb{C}$ varies over all embeddings, are as follows:

\[
\operatorname{dim}_{\mathbb{C}}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{-1,0} = \begin{cases}
n & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(0)}| \cap |\Phi^{(n)}|, \\
n-1 & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(0)}| \setminus |\Phi^{(n)}|, \\
1 & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(n)}| \setminus |\Phi^{(0)}|, \\
0 & \text{if } \sigma \notin |\Phi^{(0)}| \cup |\Phi^{(n)}|,
\end{cases}
\]

with analogous expressions governing $\operatorname{dim}_{\mathbb{C}}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{0,-1}$. From this, we deduce the Hodge decomposition for the $\sigma$-eigenspace of $\bigwedge_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}^{k}V^{(1)}$:

\[
\bigwedge_{\mathbb{C}}^{k}V_{\sigma}^{(1)} = \begin{cases}
\bigwedge_{\mathbb{C}}^{k}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{-1,0} & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(0)}| \cap |\Phi^{(n)}|, \\
\bigwedge_{\mathbb{C}}^{k}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{-1,0} \oplus \bigwedge_{\mathbb{C}}^{k-1}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{-1,0} \otimes_{\mathbb{C}}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{0,-1} & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(0)}| \setminus |\Phi^{(n)}|, \\
{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{-1,0} \otimes_{\mathbb{C}} \bigwedge_{\mathbb{C}}^{k-1}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{0,-1} \oplus \bigwedge_{\mathbb{C}}^{k}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{0,-1} & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(n)}| \setminus |\Phi^{(0)}|, \\
\bigwedge_{\mathbb{C}}^{k}{V_{\sigma}^{(1)}}^{0,-1} & \text{if } \sigma \notin |\Phi^{(0)}| \cup |\Phi^{(n)}|.
\end{cases}
\]

This decomposition reveals the Hodge weights (including multiplicities) for the $\sigma$-eigenspace as:

\[
(p,q) = \begin{cases}
\binom{n}{k} \times (-k,0) & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(0)}| \cap |\Phi^{(n)}|, \\
\binom{n-1}{k} \times (-k,0), \binom{n-1}{k-1} \times (1-k,-1) & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(0)}| \setminus |\Phi^{(n)}|, \\
\binom{n-1}{k-1} \times (-1,1-k), \binom{n-1}{k} \times (0,-k) & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(n)}| \setminus |\Phi^{(0)}|, \\
\binom{n}{k} \times (0,-k) & \text{if } \sigma \notin |\Phi^{(0)}| \cup |\Phi^{(n)}|.
\end{cases}
\]

Comparing these weights with those of ${V_{\sigma}^{(0)}}^{\otimes_{\mathbb{C}}1-k}$, which are:

\[
(p,q) = \begin{cases}
(k-1,0) & \text{if } \sigma \in |\Phi^{(0)}|, \\
(0,k-1) & \text{if } \sigma \notin |\Phi^{(0)}|,
\end{cases}
\]

confirms that the only weights present in $V^{(k)}$ are $\{(-1,0),(0,-1)\}$ and validates the expression for formula (8).

Finally, to ensure the positivity of the pairings $\psi^{(k)}(x,h^{(k)}(i)y)$, we note that each embedding $\sigma:L\rightarrow\mathbb{C}$ induces sesquilinear pairings $\Psi_{\sigma}^{(k)}$ on $V_{\sigma}^{(k)}$. Defining $E_{\sigma}^{(k)} = \Psi_{\sigma}^{(k)}(x,h^{(k)}(i)y)$, and leveraging the given positive definiteness of $\psi^{(1)}$ and $\psi^{(0)}$ on $V_{\mathbb{R}}^{(1)}$ and $V_{\mathbb{R}}^{(0)}$, respectively, we infer that $E_{\sigma}^{(k)}$ inherits positive definiteness. Specifically,

\[
E_{\sigma}^{(k)}(x_{0}^{1-k}x_{1}\wedge\dots\wedge x_{k},y_{0}^{1-k}y_{1}\wedge\dots\wedge y_{k}) = E_{\sigma}^{(0)}(x_{0},y_{0})^{1-k}\det(E_{\sigma}^{(1)}(x_{i},y_{j})_{i,j}),
\]

thereby establishing the positive definiteness of $E_{\sigma}^{(k)}$. Consequently, the pairing $\psi^{(k)}(x,h^{(k)}(i)y)$, being the sum of these definite forms across all $\sigma$, is itself positive definite. ∎

Upon performing a base change via some $\sigma:L\rightarrow\mathbb{C}$, we can apply [16] to $\mathcal{MT}\times_{L,\sigma}\mathbb{C}$. Without loss of generality, we may suppose that $F$ is a finite extension of $E^{(0\times 1)}$. We select an embedding of $F$ into $\mathbb{C}$. The construction of the map $g^{(k)}:{{}_{K^{(0\times 1)}}M}(G^{(0\times 1)},X^{(0\times 1)})\rightarrow{{}_{K^{(k)}}M}(G^{(k)},X^{(k)})$ gives rise to isomorphisms of Hodge structures equipped with $\mathcal{O}_{L}$-actions:

$$
\begin{CD}
H_{1}(Y_{\xi}^{(0)}(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Z})^{\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}1-k}\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}\bigwedge_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}^{k}H_{1}(Y_{\xi}^{(1)}(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Z}) \\
@V{t^{(k)}}V{}V \\
H_{1}(Y_{\xi}^{(k)}(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Z})
\end{CD}
$$

Let us denote by $t_{dR}^{(k)}$ and $t_{et}^{(k)}$ the de Rham and étale realizations, respectively, obtained from $t^{(k)}$ by composing with the comparison isomorphisms:

$$
H_{1}^{dR}(Y_{\xi}^{(k)}/\mathbb{C})\cong H_{1}(Y_{\xi}^{(k)}(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{C})
$$

and

$$
H_{1}(Y_{\xi}^{(k)}\times_{F}F^{ac},\mathaccent 94{\mathbb{Z}})\cong H_{1}(Y_{\xi}^{(k)}(\mathbb{C}),\mathaccent 94{\mathbb{Z}}).
$$

The pairs $(t_{dR}^{(k)},t_{et}^{(k)})$ are Hodge cycles (on the abelian variety ${Y_{\xi}^{(0)}}^{\times_{F}k-1}\times_{F}Y_{\xi}^{(1)}\times_{F}Y_{\xi}^{(k)}$) in the sense of [3]. By [3], these are absolute Hodge cycles (on the aforementioned product of abelian varieties over $F^{ac}$). By [3], this implies that the de Rham components $t_{dR}^{(k)}$ are horizontal with respect to the Gauss-Manin connection, hence they descend to isomorphisms over $F^{ac}$:

$$
\begin{CD}
H_{1}^{dR}(Y_{\xi}^{(0)}/F^{ac})^{\otimes_{F^{ac}\otimes L}1-k}\otimes_{F^{ac}\otimes L}\bigwedge_{F^{ac}\otimes L}^{k}H_{1}^{dR}(Y_{\xi}^{(1)}/F^{ac}) \\
@V{t_{dR}^{(k)}}V{}V \\
H_{1}^{dR}(Y_{\xi}^{(k)}/F^{ac}),
\end{CD}
$$

as per [3]. Furthermore, the subgroup of $\operatorname{Gal}(F^{ac}/F)$ fixing $t_{dR}^{(k)}$ coincides with the subgroup fixing $t_{et}^{(k)}$, essentially because Galois conjugates of absolute Hodge cycles remain absolute Hodge cycles, as shown in [3]. Lemma 2.2 completes the argument since $t_{et}^{(k)}$ is rational over $F$, ensuring that our horizontal map $t_{dR}^{(k)}$ descends to $F$:

$$
\begin{CD}
H_{1}^{dR}(Y_{\xi}^{(0)}/F)^{\otimes_{F\otimes L}1-k}\otimes_{F\otimes L}\bigwedge_{F\otimes L}^{k}H_{1}^{dR}(Y_{\xi}^{(1)}/F) \\
@V{t_{dR}^{(k)}}V{}V \\
H_{1}^{dR}(Y_{\xi}^{(k)}/F)
\end{CD}
$$

too. ∎

In the following discussion, we will often utilize the equivalence between the category of Barsotti-Tate groups over $R$ and the category of projective systems of Barsotti-Tate groups over $R/M$, where $M$ ranges over all $\mathfrak{m}$-primary ideals [9]. This allows us to consider the generic fibre of a deformation, particularly in the ordinary case, which can be constructed as follows:

The data $\psi^{(k)}$ induces a $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$-valued perfect pairing on the $\mathcal{O}_{L}\otimes\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$-module $V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(k)}$ if and only if the map

$\Psi^{(k)}:V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(k)}\times V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(k)}\rightarrow\mathcal{D}_{L}^{-1}\otimes\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$

exhibits this property, where $\mathcal{D}_{L}$ denotes the different of $\mathcal{O}_{L}$. If this condition holds for $k\in\{0,1\}$, it extends to the exterior pairing

$\bigwedge_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}^{k}V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(1)}\times\bigwedge_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}^{k}V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(1)}\rightarrow\mathcal{D}_{L}^{-k}\otimes\mathbb{Z}_{(p)},$

induced by $\Psi^{(1)}$, and to the $1-k$-fold self-product of $\Psi^{(0)}$:

${V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(0)}}^{\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}1-k}\times{V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(0)}}^{\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}1-k}\rightarrow\mathcal{D}_{L}^{k-1}\otimes\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}.$

By taking the product once more, it follows that

$\Psi^{(k)}:V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(k)}\times V_{\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}}^{(k)}\rightarrow\mathcal{D}_{L}^{-1}\otimes\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$

is indeed perfect, as desired. ∎

Let $\mathcal{G}_{0}$ be an ordinary $\mathcal{O}_{L}$-Barsotti-Tate group over $k/\mathbb{F}$ with dimension $a$ and $\mathcal{O}_{L}$-height $a+b$. Let $\mathcal{G}_{i,j}$ be the $\mathcal{O}_{L}$-Barsotti-Tate group over $R$ defined by the pushout:

$$
\setcounter{MaxMatrixCols}{11}\begin{CD}0@>{}>{}>\Sigma\times_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}R@>{}>{}>\mathcal{G}_{i,j}@>{}>{}>L/\mathcal{O}_{L}@>{}>{}>0\\
@A{}A{}A@A{\phi_{i,j}}A{}A@A{}A{}A@A{=}A{}A@A{}A{}A\\
0@>{}>{}>{\mathcal{O}_{L}}@>{}>{}>L@>{}>{}>L/\mathcal{O}_{L}@>{}>{}>0\\
\end{CD}
$$

Let $c_{1}:\operatorname{Gal}(L^{ac}/L)\rightarrow\mathcal{O}_{L}^{\times}$ be the character arising from the Tate module $\mathcal{O}_{L}(1)$ of $\Sigma\times_{\mathcal{O}_{L}}L^{ac}$. Then, the action of $\operatorname{Gal}(K^{ac}/K)$ on the Tate module of $\mathcal{G}\times_{R}K^{ac}$ is given by:

$$
\left(\begin{matrix}c_{1}E_{a}&C\\
0&E_{b}\end{matrix}\right)
$$

where $C=(c_{i,j})$ is an $a\times b$ matrix such that each $c_{i,j}:\operatorname{Gal}(K^{ac}/K)\rightarrow\mathcal{O}_{L}(1)$ is a $1$-cocycle representing the continuous cohomology class in $H_{cont}^{1}(\operatorname{Gal}(K^{ac}/K),\mathcal{O}_{L}(1))$ obtained from the extension (12).

We present a theoretical framework to establish the interdependencies among magnetic moment, heat, and magnon propagation fluctuations in a ferromagnetic insulating junction, focusing on noise and the bosonic Wiedemann-Franz (WF) law. By employing the Schwinger-Keldysh formalism, we compute all transport coefficients of the noise spectrum for both magnonic spin and heat currents, thereby deriving Onsager relations that link thermomagnetic currents to zero-frequency noise. Leveraging the magnonic WF law and the Seebeck coefficient in the low-temperature regime, we uncover universal noise relations—free from material-specific parameters—for both nonequilibrium and equilibrium states, demonstrating that each noise is solely described by thermal conductance. Lastly, we introduce a magnonic spin-analog of the Fano factor, defined as the noise-to-current ratio, and show that the magnonic spin-Fano factor attains a universal value at low temperatures, remaining valid even beyond the linear response regime.

The magnon operators are subjected to the commutation relations of bosonic operators. This approach allows us to compute the current and noise as functions of the exchange interaction, where the current and noise are proportional to the square of the exchange interaction. By introducing a modified exchange interaction parameter, which accounts for an additional exchange interaction that does not conserve magnetic momentum, we can compute the current and noise in this modified scenario. The Onsager coefficients, which describe the transport properties, are then expressed in terms of this modified parameter. While the ratios between the Onsager coefficients and the noise or the zero-frequency noise term are altered due to the presence of the additional exchange interaction, other ratios, such as those between different Onsager coefficients or between different noise terms, remain unchanged. This indicates that the relative scaling of these quantities is preserved even when the exchange interaction is modified, providing a deeper understanding of the system's behavior under varying exchange interactions.

Using the Schwinger-Keldysh formalism, which corresponds to the nonequilibrium Green’s function approach, and treating the tunneling Hamiltonian ${\cal{H}}_{\rm{ex}}$ perturbatively, the magnonic spin (heat) current ${\cal{I}}_{{\rm{m}}(Q)}$ and the noise ${\cal{S}}_{{\rm{m}}(Q)}$ can be calculated up to ${\cal{O}}(J_{\rm{ex}}^{2})$. The expressions for these quantities are given by:

${\cal{I}}_{\rm{m}}=(J_{\rm{ex}}S)^{2}\sum_{{\mathbf{k}},k_{x}^{\prime}}\int({d\omega}/{2\pi})g\mu_{\rm{B}}(G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{<}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{>}}-G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{>}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{<}})$,

${\cal{S}}_{\rm{m}}=-(J_{\rm{ex}}S)^{2}\sum_{{\mathbf{k}},k_{x}^{\prime}}\int({d\omega}/{2\pi})(g\mu_{\rm{B}})^{2}(G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{<}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{>}}+G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{>}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{<}})$,

${\cal{I}}_{Q}=(J_{\rm{ex}}S)^{2}\sum_{{\mathbf{k}},k_{x}^{\prime}}\int({d\omega}/{2\pi})\hbar\omega(G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{<}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{>}}-G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{>}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{<}})$,

${\cal{S}}_{Q}=-(J_{\rm{ex}}S)^{2}\sum_{{\mathbf{k}},k_{x}^{\prime}}\int({d\omega}/{2\pi})(\hbar\omega)^{2}(G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{<}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{>}}+G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{>}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{<}})$,

where $G_{{\rm{L}}}^{<(>)}\equiv G_{{\rm{L}},{\mathbf{k}},\omega}^{<(>)}$ and $G_{{\rm{R}}}^{<(>)}\equiv G_{{\rm{R}},{\mathbf{k}}^{\prime},\omega}^{<(>)}$ represent the bosonic lesser (greater) Green’s functions of magnons for the left and right FIs, respectively. These Green’s functions can be expressed as $G^{<}=2in(\omega){\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}$ and $G^{>}=2i[1+n(\omega)]{\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}$, where $G^{\rm{r}}$ is the retarded Green’s function and $n(\omega)=({\rm{e}}^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1)^{-1}$ is the Bose-distribution function. By defining $\Delta n(\omega)\equiv n_{\rm{R}}(\omega)-n_{\rm{L}}(\omega)$, the Green’s functions simplify to:

$G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{<}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{>}}-G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{>}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{<}}=4{\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{L}}}{\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{R}}}\cdot\Delta n(\omega)$,

$G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{<}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{>}}+G_{{\rm{L}}}^{\rm{>}}G_{{\rm{R}}}^{\rm{<}}=-4{\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{L}}}{\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{R}}}[2{\cal{F}}_{0}(\omega)+{\rm{coth}}(\beta\hbar\omega/2)\cdot\Delta n(\omega)]$.

Substituting these results leads to Eqs. (1a)-(1d) in the main text.

In the presence of an applied strong magnetic field \( B_{\rm{L(R)}} \), under the assumption of the Zeeman term \( g\mu_{\rm{B}}B_{\rm{L(R)}} \), and expanding \( \Delta n(\omega) \) within the linear response regime with respect to \( \Delta B \) and \( \Delta T \), i.e., \( \Delta n(\omega) = -\beta g\mu_{\rm{B}}{\cal{F}}_{0}(\omega)\Delta B + (\beta/T){\cal{F}}_{1}(\omega)\Delta T \), we derive the Onsager coefficients Eqs. (3a)-(3e) in the main text using Eqs. (1a)-(1d).

While ${\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{L}}}{\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{R}}}$ is temperature-independent, it depends on $B_{\rm{L(R)}}$. Expanding ${\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{L}}}{\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{R}}}$ in powers of $\Delta B$ using Eqs. (1b) and (1d) yields the Onsager coefficients ${\cal{S}}_{11}$ and ${\cal{S}}_{21}$ as given in Eq. (2):

$$
{\cal{S}}_{11} = \beta(g\mu_{\rm{B}})^3 \int \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} {\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_0 {\rm{coth}}(\beta\hbar\omega/2) - 2(g\mu_{\rm{B}})^2 \int \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} {\cal{G}}^{\prime}_{\rm{R}}{\cal{F}}_0,
$$
$$
{\cal{S}}_{21} = \beta g\mu_{\rm{B}} \int \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} {\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_2 {\rm{coth}}(\beta\hbar\omega/2) - 2 \int \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} {\cal{G}}^{\prime}_{\rm{R}}{\cal{F}}_2.
$$

Here, ${\cal{G}}^{\prime}_{\rm{R}} = {\cal{G}}^{\prime}_{\rm{R}}(B) \equiv 4(J_{\rm{ex}}S)^2 \sum_{{\mathbf{k}},k_{x}^{\prime}} {\rm{Im}}G_{{\rm{L}},{\mathbf{k}},\omega}^{\rm{r}} (\partial/\partial B) {\rm{Im}}G_{{\rm{R}},{\mathbf{k}}^{\prime},\omega}^{\rm{r}} \big|_{B_{\rm{R}}=B}$. Due to the ${\cal{G}}^{\prime}_{\rm{R}}$-term, which originates from the ${\cal{F}}_0$-term in Eqs. (1b) and (1d), the ratios ${\cal{S}}_{11}/L_{11}$ and ${\cal{S}}_{21}/(TL_{22})$ do not reduce to $g\mu_{\rm{B}}$ even at low temperatures. However, in the magnonic shot noise regime where $2{\cal{F}}_0(\omega) \ll {\rm{coth}}(\beta\hbar\omega/2) |\Delta n(\omega)|$, the ${\cal{G}}^{\prime}_{\rm{R}}$-term vanishes, leading to:

$$
{\cal{S}}_{11} = \beta(g\mu_{\rm{B}})^3 \int \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} {\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_0 {\rm{coth}}(\beta\hbar\omega/2),
$$
$$
{\cal{S}}_{21} = \beta g\mu_{\rm{B}} \int \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} {\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_2 {\rm{coth}}(\beta\hbar\omega/2),
$$

and the ratios reduce to ${\cal{S}}_{11}/L_{11} \to {\cal{S}}_{21}/(TL_{22}) \to g\mu_{\rm{B}}$ at low temperatures $0 \neq k_{\rm{B}}T \ll g\mu_{\rm{B}}B$. Notably, even if ${\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{L}}}{\rm{Im}}G^{\rm{r}}_{{\rm{R}}}$ is temperature-dependent as a function of $T_{{\rm{L(R)}}}$ (e.g., temperature-dependent lifetime), the results in the main text remain valid qualitatively in the magnonic shot noise regime. The Onsager coefficients ${\cal{S}}_{ij}$ within the linear response region, including ${\cal{S}}_{{\rm{m}}(Q)}^{0}$, remain unchanged in this regime. Additionally, in the low-temperature limit of the magnonic shot noise regime, we find ${\cal{S}}_{\rm{m}}/{\cal{I}}_{\rm{m}} \to g\mu_{\rm{B}}$ regardless of the order of $\Delta T$ and $\Delta B$.

By considering a scenario where the temperature difference $\Delta T$ is significantly large (i.e., $g\mu_{\rm{B}}\mid\Delta B\mid\ll k_{\rm{B}}\mid\Delta T\mid$), we expand $\Delta n(\omega)$ to second order in $\Delta T$, yielding:

$\Delta n(\omega) = -\beta g\mu_{\rm{B}}{\cal{F}}_{0}(\omega)\Delta B + (\beta/T){\cal{F}}_{1}(\omega)\Delta T + \left[-(\beta/T^{2}){\cal{F}}_{1}(\omega) + (\beta^{2}/2T^{2}){\cal{F}}_{2}(\omega)\coth(\beta\hbar\omega/2)\right](\Delta T)^{2}$.

From Eqs. (1a)-(1d), we derive the Onsager coefficients for nonlinear responses with respect to $\Delta T$ [Eq. (16)]:

$\displaystyle L_{13} = -\frac{\beta g\mu_{\rm{B}}}{T^{2}}\int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}{\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_{1} + \frac{\beta^{2}g\mu_{\rm{B}}}{2T^{2}}\int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}{\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_{2}\coth(\beta\hbar\omega/2), \quad (19a)$

$\displaystyle {\cal{S}}_{13} = -\frac{\beta(g\mu_{\rm{B}})^{2}}{T^{2}}\int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}{\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_{1}\coth(\beta\hbar\omega/2) + \frac{(\beta g\mu_{\rm{B}})^{2}}{2T^{2}}\int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}{\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_{2}\coth^{2}(\beta\hbar\omega/2), \quad (19b)$

$\displaystyle L_{23} = -\frac{\beta}{T^{2}}\int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}{\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_{2} + \frac{\beta^{2}}{2T^{2}}\int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}{\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_{3}\coth(\beta\hbar\omega/2), \quad (19c)$

$\displaystyle {\cal{S}}_{23} = -\frac{\beta}{T^{2}}\int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}{\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_{3}\coth(\beta\hbar\omega/2) + \frac{\beta^{2}}{2T^{2}}\int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}{\cal{G}}{\cal{F}}_{4}\coth^{2}(\beta\hbar\omega/2). \quad (19d)$

In the low-temperature regime $0 \neq k_{\rm{B}}T \ll g\mu_{\rm{B}}B$, the coefficients governing nonlinear responses, with the exception of ${\cal{S}}_{23}$, align with those of linear responses:

$\displaystyle L_{13} \stackrel{{\scriptstyle\rightarrow}}{{=}} \frac{\beta^{2}g\mu_{\rm{B}}}{4T^{2}}{\cal{S}}_{Q}^{0} = \frac{\beta^{2}g\mu_{\rm{B}}}{2}k_{\rm{B}}L_{22}, \quad (20a)$

$\displaystyle {\cal{S}}_{13} \stackrel{{\scriptstyle\rightarrow}}{{=}} \frac{(\beta g\mu_{\rm{B}})^{2}}{4T^{2}}{\cal{S}}_{Q}^{0} = \frac{(\beta g\mu_{\rm{B}})^{2}}{2}k_{\rm{B}}L_{22}, \quad (20b)$

$\displaystyle L_{23} = -\frac{1}{T}L_{22} + \frac{1}{2k_{\rm{B}}T^{2}}{\cal{S}}_{22} \stackrel{{\scriptstyle\rightarrow}}{{=}} \frac{1}{2k_{\rm{B}}T^{2}}{\cal{S}}_{22}. \quad (20c)$

Here, $L_{13}$ and ${\cal{S}}_{13}$ are characterized by the thermal noise ${\cal{S}}_{Q}^{0}$, and these coefficients can be expressed in terms of the thermal conductance $K$ via Eq. (11).

We establish a theoretical framework to explore the interdependent relationships between magnetic moment, thermal energy, and the propagation fluctuations of magnons in a ferromagnetic insulating junction. This analysis is conducted through the lens of noise phenomena and the bosonic Wiedemann-Franz (WF) law. By employing the Schwinger-Keldysh formalism, we compute all transport coefficients associated with the noise spectrum for both magnonic spin and heat currents, thereby uncovering Onsager reciprocal relations between thermomagnetic currents and zero-frequency noise. Leveraging the magnonic WF law and the Seebeck coefficient in the low-temperature regime, we derive universal relationships for both nonequilibrium and equilibrium noise that are independent of material-specific parameters. These findings demonstrate that each noise component can be characterized exclusively by thermal conductance. Furthermore, we introduce a magnonic spin analog of the Fano factor, defined as the noise-to-current ratio, and reveal that the magnonic spin-Fano factor attains a universal value in the low-temperature limit, maintaining its validity even beyond the linear response regime.

We present a theoretical analysis of the interconnections between magnetic moment, heat, and magnon propagation fluctuations in a ferromagnetic insulating junction, framed within the context of noise and the bosonic Wiedemann-Franz (WF) law. By employing the Schwinger-Keldysh formalism, we compute all transport coefficients of the noise spectrum for both magnonic spin and heat currents and derive Onsager relations linking thermomagnetic currents to zero-frequency noise. Utilizing the magnonic WF law and the Seebeck coefficient at low temperatures, we identify universal relations—material-parameter-independent—for both nonequilibrium and equilibrium noise, demonstrating that each noise is solely determined by thermal conductance. Lastly, we introduce a magnonic spin analog of the Fano factor, defined as the noise-to-current ratio, and show that the magnonic spin-Fano factor attains a universal value at low temperatures, remaining valid even beyond the linear response regime.

In this work, we establish the interdependent relationships between magnetic moment, thermal energy, and magnon propagation fluctuations within a ferromagnetic insulating junction, utilizing the concepts of noise and the bosonic Wiedemann-Franz (WF) law. Through the Schwinger-Keldysh framework, we compute the full suite of transport coefficients within the noise spectrum for both magnonic spin and heat currents, thereby deriving Onsager relations that connect thermomagnetic currents to zero-frequency noise. By leveraging the magnonic WF law and the Seebeck coefficient under low-temperature conditions, we uncover universal noise relations—those independent of material-specific parameters—for both equilibrium and non-equilibrium states, demonstrating that each noise component can be solely expressed through thermal conductance. Additionally, we introduce a magnonic spin analog of the Fano factor, defined as the noise-to-current ratio, and reveal that this magnonic spin-Fano factor attains a universal value in the low-temperature regime, maintaining its validity even beyond the linear response domain.

The bath Hamiltonian $H_{\rm B}$ can be diagonalized using the Jordan-Wigner transformation, which maps the spin operators to fermionic creation and annihilation operators. This transformation enables the description of the system as a central spin interacting with a bath of spinless fermions, governed by the Hamiltonian $H$. The Jordan-Wigner string and the effective magnetic field experienced by the central spin are key components of this formulation. The parity of the eigenvalue $N_{f}$ of the total fermion number operator $\mathcal{N}_{f}$ leads to the identification of two subspaces with distinct boundary conditions for the fermions. Applying Fourier transformations to these subspaces yields a diagonalized form of $H_{\rm B}$, which can be expressed in terms of the single-particle spectrum $\varepsilon_{k}$. To study the dynamics of the system, we work in the interaction picture and derive the interaction Hamiltonian $H_{I}(t)$, assuming an initial decoupling of the central spin and the bath. The initial state of the system is given by a superposition of the central spin states and a bath state with fixed magnetization, allowing for the consideration of time evolution in distinct subspaces.

The system's behavior is characterized by the Bessel function of the n-th order, denoted as $J_{n}(x)$, and the Chebyshev polynomials $T_{n}(z)$, which are defined recursively as $T_{n+1}(z) = 2zT_{n}(z) - T_{n-1}(z)$ with initial conditions $T_{0}(z) = 1$ and $T_{1}(z) = z$. Applying the time-evolution operator expansion to the initial state $|\psi_{0}\rangle$ yields a set of states $|\phi_{n}\rangle = T_{n}(H^{\prime})|\psi_{0}\rangle$, which satisfy a recursive relationship and initial conditions $|\phi_{0}\rangle = |\psi_{0}\rangle$ and $|\phi_{1}\rangle = H^{\prime}|\psi_{0}\rangle$, encapsulating the dynamics of the system.

We are examining the polarization $\langle S_{z}(t)\rangle$ and the decoherence factor $|r(t)|$, which can be readily computed by transforming equation (II.2) into the Schrödinger picture, yielding expressions for $\langle S_{z}(t)\rangle$ and $|r(t)|$ in terms of the coefficients $a$, $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$. Notably, $\langle S_{z}(t)\rangle$ is found to be twice the autocorrelation function $S(t)$, as given by equation (25). Additionally, a derivation from the equations of motion (16)-(19) reveals that the initial decay of $|r(t)|$ is at least quadratic in time, as stated in equation (26), implying that the short-time decay of $|r(t)|$ exhibits a Gaussian behavior, consistent with observations in previous studies.

We initiated our analysis by investigating the central spin polarization dynamics with the bath in a fully polarized initial state, followed by an examination of the decoherence dynamics with an antiferromagnetic initial condition. Although the high-dimensional Hilbert spaces limited our ability to simulate large baths with 16 or more spins using the equation of motion method, the recently developed Chebyshev expansion technique enabled us to apply the Chebyshev expansion technique approach to the model, facilitating the treatment of larger baths and ultrastrong intrabath interactions. Our results were consistent with previous studies in the absence of intrabath coupling, but revealed novel decoherence behaviors for finite intrabath coupling strengths, characterized by an initial abrupt drop in the decoherence factor and a subsequent approach to a steady value. Notably, the long-time mean value of the decoherence factor exhibited a non-monotonic dependence on the intrabath coupling strength, with an optimal value of the coupling that maximized decoherence, beyond which coherence was restored and approached a fixed value dependent on the number of bath spins, displaying an oscillatory behavior with changing parity of N/2 that can be understood through degeneracy analysis of states in different magnetization sectors.

We proceed to investigate the dynamics of central spin decoherence, where the bath is initially in a zero-polarization antiferromagnetic state, characterized by the wave function $|\phi_{b}\rangle = |\bar{1}1\bar{1}...\bar{1}1\bar{1}\rangle$, which can be expanded as a sum of states $|\vec{k}_{\sigma,\frac{N}{2}}\rangle$ with coefficients $S^{*}_{\vec{k}_{\frac{N}{2}};(2,4,...,N)}$. This initial state yields specific values for the $\chi$ parameters, given by $\chi_{\vec{k}_{\frac{N}{2}}} = S^{*}_{\vec{k}_{\frac{N}{2}};(2,4,...,N)}$. For this scenario, we adopt an exponential distribution of coupling constants $g_{j} = \frac{g}{N}e^{-\frac{j-1}{N}}$, where $g$ sets the overall energy scale. The fluctuation frequency $\omega_{\rm{fluc}}$ is then determined to be $\frac{2g}{N}e^{\frac{1}{N}-1}\sqrt{\frac{e^{2}-1}{e^{\frac{2}{N}}-1}}$. The non-perturbative crossover and strong magnetic field regimes are defined by the conditions $\omega_{\rm{fluc}} \lesssim \omega \lesssim 2g$ and $\omega > 2g$, respectively.

This appendix delves into the decoherence dynamics of a spin bath consisting of four spins, where the interactions between spins are not uniform. The system's behavior is governed by a Hamiltonian that accounts for both the interactions among the bath spins and the coupling between the central spin and each bath spin. To analyze the dynamics, the initial state is set as a superposition of states, and the spin bath is diagonalized within specific subsectors. This diagonalization yields eigenvalues that depend on the coupling strengths and the spin configurations. Notably, when the couplings are uniform, there are multiple zero-energy eigenstates, but introducing variations in the couplings lifts these degeneracies. Despite this, if the energy differences are comparable to the coupling strengths, the decay of coherence can still occur rapidly, influenced by the interplay of these factors in the system's dynamics.

As the ratio of interaction strength to fluctuation frequency increases from zero, the loss of quantum coherence, known as decoherence, becomes more pronounced. However, in systems of finite size, coherence can be partially regained when the interaction strength becomes very large, due to the entanglement of states with the same energy. This suggests that there exists an optimal balance between interaction strength and fluctuation frequency at which the decoherence is minimized over long periods of time. To better understand this phenomenon, a time-averaged measure of coherence, denoted as $R(t)$, has been introduced to quantify the point at which the decoherence factor reaches its minimum value.

In the asymptotic limit of exceedingly large $J$, our model approaches an extreme regime, wherein the initial bath state $|\phi_{b}\rangle$ can be expressed as a superposition of eigenstates of the $XX$ ring, denoted by $|\vec{k}_{\sigma,m}\rangle$, with a fixed magnetization quantum number $m = N/2$. Here, the hyperfine interaction with the central spin is treated as a perturbation of negligible strength. For finite $N$, the energy spectrum is characterized by discrete levels, with the spacing between adjacent eigenenergies increasing monotonically with $J$. As $J$ exceeds a certain threshold, even the smallest excitation energy surpasses the maximum hyperfine coupling energy scale, $g_{1}$, thereby suppressing spin-flip processes that involve the creation of particle or hole excitations. This, in turn, leads to an enhancement of the long-time limit of $|r(t)|$. In the absence of degeneracies, the system would exhibit complete coherence, i.e., $|r(t)| = 1$, at all times, thus preserving quantum coherence. Nevertheless, spin-flip processes can induce coupling between energetically degenerate eigenstates with magnetization quantum numbers $m$ and $m \pm 1$. Specifically, it is possible to identify combinations of $\vec{k}_{m}$ and $\vec{k}_{m\pm 1}$ that yield identical occupation energies, albeit only at a coincident energy of $\mathcal{E}_{\vec{k}_{m}} = \mathcal{E}_{\vec{k}_{m\pm 1}} = 0$. Upon introducing the hyperfine coupling, the degeneracy is lifted, regardless of the perturbation strength $g_{j}/J$, resulting in maximally entangled states. The concomitant spin-flip processes generate a mixture of local spin up and down states, causing the mean value of $|r(t)|$ to plateau at a value less than unity as $J$ approaches infinity. This plateau value exhibits a dependence on $N$, but counterintuitively, our numerical simulations reveal that it does not decrease with increasing $N$, despite the growing degree of degeneracy in the coupled subsectors, as will be demonstrated subsequently.

By invoking equation II.2, one can verify that the expectation value of $S_{z}(t)$ is equivalent to twice the value of the autocorrelation function, as defined in expression 3, thereby establishing a direct relationship between these two quantities.

The presence of a non-zero $J$ has a substantial impact, as evidenced by the rapid decline of $|r(t)|$ from its initial value of $|r(0)|=1$ to a stable oscillation around a fixed average value, governed by the characteristic time scale $1/\omega_{\rm fluc}$ associated with the Overhauser field fluctuation. This behavior is consistent with previous findings, which reported an average value of approximately 0.55 for $|r(t)|$ at short time scales when $J=0$ and $N=12$. As $N$ increases, the mean value of $|r(t)|$ decreases, as illustrated in Fig. 3, and is expected to approach 1/2 in the limit of $N\to\infty$, assuming the long-time averages of the coefficients $|A|$ and $|B|$ remain finite and the initial spectral weight is evenly distributed among $A, B, C,$ and $D$.

This study examines the real-time quantum dynamics of an extended central spin model, which builds upon the traditional Gaudin model by incorporating a circular arrangement of bath spins and homogeneous nearest-neighbor interactions. By leveraging the conserved total magnetization and translational symmetry of the spin bath, we formulated the time-dependent wavefunctions in momentum space and derived exact equations of motion for the probability amplitudes. Numerical integration of these equations yielded the temporal evolution of the central spin's polarization and decoherence factor, revealing that the initial decay of the decoherence factor commences at a rate of at least quadratic order in time. Furthermore, our framework provides a clear physical interpretation of the dynamics in the case of uniform system-bath coupling, rooted in momentum-conserving scattering processes between adjacent subsectors of the bath.

The time-evolved state in the interaction picture is expressed as a linear combination of states with conserved total magnetization, where the probability amplitudes A, B, C, and D are functions of time and momentum. The initial values of these amplitudes can be determined from the given equations, and the time-evolution of these amplitudes is governed by a set of equations of motion (EOMs) derived from the Schrödinger equation. The EOMs describe the dynamics of the system in terms of the auxiliary functions f and f', which are defined in terms of the system's parameters and the Slater determinants of the plane waves. These equations provide a framework for understanding the behavior of the system over time, taking into account the interactions and correlations between the particles. The derivation of the EOMs is an extension of previous work, and the resulting equations provide a comprehensive description of the system's dynamics.

This study focuses on baths with a relatively small number of spins, specifically $N\leq 16$, but it is generally accepted that this size is sufficient to mitigate finite-size effects and yield qualitatively similar results for larger $N$ in the context of the central spin model. Consequently, the key findings, including the existence of an optimal $J$ value that maximizes decoherence and the increase in $|r(t)|$ as $J$ approaches this optimal value, are expected to persist for larger baths. The interplay between intrabath and spin-bath coupling leads to a minimum in $R(J)$ at the optimal $J$, which occurs when $J$ is comparable to the spin-bath coupling strengths $\{g_{j}\}$. Notably, as $N$ increases, the dimensionless spin-bath coupling strength decreases, causing a shift in the optimal $J$ value relative to the fluctuation frequency.

The coefficients $a_{\bar{1}}$ and $a_{1}$ are constrained by the condition $|a_{\bar{1}}|^{2}+|a_{1}|^{2}=1$, while the initial state of the bath, denoted by $|\phi_{b}\rangle$, is characterized by a fixed magnetization of $L_{z}=m-\frac{N}{2}$, where $m$ represents the number of up spins in the bath. Notably, if $|\phi_{b}\rangle$ exhibits nonzero overlap across multiple $L_{z}$ subspaces, the resulting time evolution in each subspace can be superposed to yield the overall dynamics.

By initializing $E_{M} (0)$ and $H_{M} (0)$ within a small-scale magnetic eddy, the expression for a modified $EMF$ was derived to account for simulation outcomes.

The dependence of $E_{M,L}(t)$ and $H_{M,L}(t)$ on large-scale initial conditions was demonstrated in Ref. (9). The strong Alfv $\acute{e}$ n effect at large scales ensures that the evolution of the small-scale ${\bf b}$ field is governed by the large-scale magnetic field. Additionally, the growth of the ${\bf\overline{B}}$ field is influenced by the integrals $\int_{0}^{t}(\alpha-\beta-\eta)\,d\tau$ and $\int_{0}^{t}(\alpha+\beta+\eta)\,d\tau$, which originate from small-scale velocity and magnetic field contributions. The influence of the initial small-scale magnetic field ${\bf b}_{i}(0)$ becomes significant only when $E_{M,L0}$ and $H_{M,L0}$ are small. Given that $\alpha$ is negative while the other terms are positive, the second terms in Eq. (12) and Eq. (13) predominantly dictate the evolution of $E_{M,L}$ and $H_{M,L}$. Furthermore, the negative value of $H_{M,L0}-2E_{M,L0}$ implies that $E_{M}$ evolves positively, whereas $H_{M,L}$ becomes negative.

It is important to note that $E_{M}$ in Eq. (13) pertains to helical magnetic energy. In a general, non-isotropic system, the assumption that $\langle{\bf v}\times{\bf b}\rangle$ is a linear function of ${\bf\overline{B}}$ becomes challenging to apply. Since $E_{M}$ can be both helical and nonhelical, several significant terms remain in the analysis. However, given that the small-scale field is globally isotropic (locally anisotropic in MHD) and the system is driven by fully helical kinetic energy, this assumption remains valid and reasonable.

The evolution of astrophysical objects often involves the interaction between magnetic fields and conducting fluids. Understanding the fundamental processes of magnetic field generation, amplification (dynamo), and annihilation (reconnection) is essential for explaining celestial phenomena, though the mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Helical kinetic and magnetic fields, which lack reflection symmetry, are believed to play a significant role in magnetic field evolution. The physical significance of helical kinetic turbulence, characterized by kinetic helicity $\langle{ f u}\cdot{ f\omega}angle$ (${ f\omega} = 
abla 	imes { f u}$), is relatively well understood: it transfers energy to magnetic eddies and cascades magnetic energy to larger scales. In contrast, the role of helical magnetic fields, specifically magnetic helicity $H_{M} \equiv \langle{ f A} \cdot { f B}angle$ (${ f B} = 
abla 	imes { f A}$), is only partially known. Magnetic helicity quantifies the topological twist and linkage of magnetic field lines ($2\Phi_{1}\Phi_{2}$, where $\Phi = \int_{A}{ f B} \cdot d{ f S}$), and corresponds to a force-free field (${ f J} 	imes { f B} = 0$, $
abla 	imes { f B} = \lambda{ f B}$) in a minimum energy equilibrium state. It is known that large-scale $|H_{M}|$ enhances the generation of the mean magnetic field $\overline{B}$, while small-scale $|H_{M}|$, more precisely $k^{2}|H_{M}|$, suppresses $\overline{B}$ growth. Additionally, $H_{M}$ contributes to the magnetic energy $E_{M}$, with its magnitude constrained to not exceed $E_{M}/2k$. This realizability condition implies that $E_{M}$ can accommodate arbitrary magnetic helicity under certain conditions.

Fig. 2 illustrates the detailed evolution of $E_{M}$ at $k=1, 2, 5$ for $fhm=0,\,\pm 1.0$. The results demonstrate that $E_{M}$ associated with positive $H_{M}$ (0) at $k=5$ decreases more rapidly compared to negative $H_{M}$ (0) when positive $\langle{\bf v}\cdot{\bf\omega}\rangle$ (which corresponds to the generation of negative $H_{M}$) is introduced into the system. This accelerated decline of $E_{M}$ at $k=5$ facilitates a greater increase in $E_{M}$ at $k=2$, subsequently resulting in a slower yet more pronounced growth rate of $E_{M,L}$.

The effects of initial small-scale magnetic energy ($E_M(0)$) and helicity ($H_M(0)$) on magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) dynamo behavior were analyzed. Equations governing $E_M(t)$, $H_M(t)$, and the electromotive force ($\langle{\bf v}\times{\bf b}\rangle$, $EMF$) were derived and solved. The results reveal that the small-scale magnetic field (${\bf b}_i$) induced by $E_M(0)$ modifies the $EMF$ and introduces additional terms, with their influence dependent on magnetic diffusivity ($\eta$), the wavenumber associated with initial conditions ($k_f$), and time ($\sim e^{-\eta k_f^2 t}$). This field (${\bf b}_i$) enhances the inverse cascade of energy, promoting the growth of the large-scale magnetic field ($\overline{{\bf B}}$). Simulation results indicate that $E_M(0)$ at small scales increases the growth rate, which also scales proportionally with $H_M(0)$. For a fixed $E_M(0)$, positive $H_M(0)$ is more effective in driving the MHD dynamo compared to negative $H_M(0)$. The discussion also addresses why the large-scale magnetic helicity should possess the opposite sign of the injected kinetic helicity.

The electromotive force (EMF), originally expressed as $\langle{\bf u}\times{\bf b}\rangle$, is replaced with $\alpha{\bf\overline{B}}-\beta\nabla\times{\bf\overline{B}}$, where $\alpha=1/3\int^{t}(\langle{\bf j}\cdot{\bf b}\rangle-\langle{\bf u}\cdot{\bf\omega}\rangle)d\tau$ and $\beta=1/3\int^{t}\langle u^{2}\rangle d\tau$ (see Eq. (8)). The functions $E_{M}(t)$ and $H_{M}(t)$ can be derived from the exact momentum and magnetic induction equations using the EDQNM approach (Eq. (13)). However, the same $H_{M}(t)$ and $E_{M}(t)$ can also be obtained through the mean field method (Eqs. (10) and (11)). From Eq. (7), we derive the expression for $\partial H_{M}/\partial t$ (refer to Eqs. (3) and (6)).

When variables are decomposed into mean values and fluctuations, such as ${\bf U} = {\bf\overline{U}} + {\bf u} \rightarrow {\bf u}$ (where ${\bf\overline{U}} \equiv 0$) and ${\bf B} = {\bf\overline{B}} + {\bf b}$, the magnetic induction equation for the ${\bf\overline{B}}$ field takes the form given by (6).

To date, we have leveraged the generation of left-handed magnetic helicity ( $\langle{\bf a}_{2}\cdot{\bf b}_{2}\rangle<0$ ) when driven by right-handed kinetic helicity ( $\langle{\bf u}_{2}\cdot{\bf\omega}_{2}\rangle>0$ ), with limited scrutiny. The evolution of the large-scale magnetic field ( ${\bf B}_{1}$ ) or magnetic helicity ( ${\bf H}_{1}$ ) is mathematically captured by differential equations such as Eq.(7) or Eq.(9). However, since these equations alone cannot account for the reversal of variable signs, a more fundamental and physically grounded approach is essential.

The magnetic field $B_{1}\hat{y}$, as depicted in Fig. 3 (section 6), is hypothesized to interact with a plasma motion characterized by a right-handed helical structure. This motion can be decomposed into two velocity components: a toroidal component $u_{2,t}\hat{\phi}$ and a poloidal component $u_{2,p}\hat{z}$. The interaction between the toroidal velocity and the magnetic field ${\bf B}_{1}$ induces a current density ${\bf j}_{2}$, approximated by the cross product ${\bf u}_{2,t}\times{\bf B_{1}}$. In the front region, this current density ${\bf j}_{2,f}$ flows in the positive $\hat{z}$ direction, whereas in the rear region, the current density ${\bf j}_{2,r}$ flows oppositely, along the negative $\hat{z}$ direction. These current densities act as sources for a magnetic field $-b_{2}\hat{x}$, following the relation $\nabla\times{\bf b} \sim {\bf j}$. This newly induced magnetic field interacts with the poloidal velocity component $u_{2,p}\hat{z}$, generating a current density $-J_{in}\hat{y}$, proportional to ${\bf u}_{2,p}\times{\bf b_{2}}$. Consequently, the current density ${\bf J}_{in}$ gives rise to a magnetic field ${\bf B}_{in}$, forming a closed loop in conjunction with ${\bf B}_{1}$ (as shown in the upper panel of Fig. 3). Extending this analysis further, ${\bf B}_{in}$ can be reinterpreted as a toroidal magnetic field ${\bf B}_{tor}$, while ${\bf B}_{1}$ corresponds to a poloidal magnetic field ${\bf B}_{pol}$. This configuration results in a helical magnetic field structure with left-handed chirality, indicated by $\langle{\bf a}\cdot{\bf b}\rangle < 0$ (lower panel of Fig. 3). The toroidal magnetic field ${\bf B}_{tor}$ interacts with the positive $\langle{\bf u}_{2}\cdot{\bf\omega}_{2}\rangle$ to induce a current density ${\bf J}$ that opposes the direction of ${\bf B}_{tor}$. This induced current reinforces the poloidal magnetic field ${\bf B}_{pol}$, thereby establishing an $\alpha^{2}$ dynamo mechanism involving the interchange between $B_{pol}$ and $B_{tor}$, driven externally.

The behavior of small-scale $E_{M}$ (0) and $H_{M}$ (0) has been observed to influence the evolution of large-scale $B$ fields. The initial magnetic energy contributes additional terms, such as ${\bf b}_{i} \sim e^{-\eta k^{2}_{f}t}$, within the $EMF$ framework. A roughly linear relationship exists between the growth rate of $E_{M,L}$ and $H_{M}$ (0) or $E_{M}$ (0). However, further investigation is required before drawing definitive conclusions, as $E_{M}$ can accommodate arbitrary $H_{M}$ ( $\leq 2E_{M}/k$ ), a nearly conserved quantity with variable sign and magnitude. The initial $E_{M}$ (0) modifies the $EMF$ term, generating ${\bf b}_{i}$ and associated additional terms. While the explicit factors influencing the growth rate include $\eta$, $k_{f}$, and $t$, the interaction between ${\bf\overline{B}}$ or ${\bf\overline{A}}$ and ${\bf b}_{i}$ remains to be fully explored for a comprehensive understanding of the effects of $H_{M}$ (0) and $E_{M}$ (0). The derivation of the modified $EMF$ assumes an isotropic system; however, this assumption becomes challenging when a mean or large-scale magnetic field is present. At minimum, local anisotropy caused by the mean magnetic field must be considered, even if the system driven by isotropic random forces is globally isotropic. This topic will be addressed in future research.

The growth rate of $|H_{M,L}|$ is illustrated in Fig. 2, comparing scenarios with different initial magnetic helicity values. The growth rate is proportional to the initial magnetic helicity, $H_{M}(0)$. In Fig. 2, thick lines plotting $H_{M,L}$ over the time range $0.3 < t < 8$ reveal a cusp, which marks the moment when $H_{M}$ transitions from positive to negative. The positive $H_{M}$ observed earlier is attributed to the system's tendency to conserve total magnetic helicity, $H_{M,tot}$, despite the injection of negative $H_{M}$. However, when the ratio of negative $H_{M}(0)$ to $E_{M}(0)$ is sufficiently large (e.g., $fhm = -1$) or when $E_{M}$ is not dominant (as referenced in $HKF$), $H_{M,L}$ undergoes a sign change.

In the context of the $\alpha$ $\Omega$ dynamo mechanism, an attempt was made to account for the handedness of twist and writhe observed in coronal ejections by invoking the principle of magnetic helicity conservation (2). Notably, even in the absence of differential rotation, the $\alpha^2$ dynamo generates magnetic helicity ($H_{M}$) with a sign that is opposite to that of the injected $\langle{\bf u}\cdot{\bf\omega}\rangle$.

The profiles of $H_{M}$ at $k=1$, $k=2$, and $k=5$ are shown in Fig. 2. While $H_{M}$ at $k=1$ and $k=2$ becomes negative, $H_{M}$ at $k=5$ becomes positive, independent of the initial value $H_{M}(0)$. This behavior represents a reactive interaction with the negative larger scale, consistent with the conservation of $H_{M,tot}$. When $H_{M}(0)$ at $k=5$ is positive, it decreases faster than when it is negative. This rapid decrease accelerates the growth of $|H_{M}|$ at $k=1$ and $k=2$. Conversely, when $H_{M}(0)$ at $k=5$ is negative, the injected negative $H_{M}$ reduces the rate of decrease of $|H_{M}|$ at $k=5$ and slows the growth of $|H_{M}|$ at $k=1$ and $k=2$. If $H_{M}(0)$ at $k=5$ is zero, $H_{M}$ at $k=5$ initially decreases due to the inflow of negative $H_{M}$. However, as the magnitude of the $\overline{B}$ field increases, the diffusion of positive $H_{M}$ from the large scale, acting as a counteraction based on $H_{M,tot}$ conservation, causes $H_{M}$ at $k=5$ to become positive.

Under the assumption of isotropy without mirror symmetry, the electromotive force (EMF) $\xi$ (expressed as $\xi_{1} + \xi_{2}$, where $\xi_{1}$ is associated with ${\bf\overline{B}}$ and $\xi_{2}$ with ${\bf b}_{i}$) can be expressed in a simplified form. Equation (16) demonstrates that ${\bf b}$ is a linear combination of ${\bf B}$ and ${\bf b}_{i}$, characteristic of a kinetic dynamo mechanism. Consequently, the components of the EMF are constrained to ${\bf B}$, $\nabla\times{\bf B}$, ${\bf b}_{i}$, and $\nabla\times{\bf b}_{i}$, with the divergence of the magnetic field being disregarded. This establishes the fundamental structure of the EMF, as outlined in equations (1) and (6).

We present a systematic approach to incorporating $su(2)$ invariance into matrix product states (MPS), utilizing a diagrammatic framework for computational clarity. As a practical application, we conduct a variational MPS analysis of a spin-$1/2$ quantum chain. To ensure computational efficiency, we systematically integrate $su(2)$ symmetry throughout the calculation process. Specifically, (i) the matrix space is decomposed into a direct sum of irreducible representations, (ii) local matrices with state-dependent entries are constructed as superpositions of $su(2)$ singlet operators, and (iii) operator products are evaluated algebraically using identities involving $3j$ and $6j$ symbols. Numerical computations, including the diagonalization of the transfer matrix and the minimization of the energy expectation value, are performed in symmetry-degenerate-free spaces. The energy expectation value serves as an upper bound for the true ground-state energy, providing a reliable assessment of the accuracy of previously reported DMRG results. Additionally, we provide explicit results with precision exceeding $10^{-4}$ for nearest- and next-nearest-neighbor spin correlators, as well as general dimer-dimer correlators, in the thermodynamic limit of the frustrated spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ Heisenberg chain.

The structure of this paper is as follows. Section 2 provides a comprehensive derivation of equilibrium states expressed as matrix product states (MPS), along with a concise overview of MPS tensor calculus, particularly focusing on symmetry implementations. Section 3 introduces $su(2)$-invariant local operators constructed from Wigner’s $3j$ symbols. This section also defines the transfer matrices and demonstrates how operator products can be evaluated using identities that involve both $3j$ and $6j$ symbols. Section 4 presents detailed numerical results obtained from evaluating the fundamental equations derived in the preceding section. These results are benchmarked against existing DMRG calculations for the frustrated spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ Heisenberg chain, as reported in reference [32], and provide insights into the precision of the methods employed.

The singlet \( G \) can be expressed as a superposition of elementary singlets, given by:

\[
G = \sum_{(j_{1},i_{1}),(j_{3},i_{3})} A^{j_{1},j_{3}}_{i_{1},i_{3}} |(j_{1},i_{1}),j_{2},(j_{3},i_{3})\rangle \quad (11)
\]

where the coefficients \( A^{j_{1},j_{3}}_{i_{1},i_{3}} \) are appropriately defined. Notably, \( j_{2} \) is omitted as an index argument of \( A \) since it is fixed at \( \frac{1}{2} \) and unique, hence no \( i_{2} \) is introduced. The symmetry of the \( 3j \) symbols under the exchange of two columns:

\[
\left(\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\( j_{3} \) & \( j_{2} \) & \( j_{1} \) \\
\( m_{3} \) & \( m_{2} \) & \( m_{1} \)
\end{tabular}\right) = (-1)^{j_{1}+j_{2}+j_{3}} \left(\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\( j_{1} \) & \( j_{2} \) & \( j_{3} \) \\
\( m_{1} \) & \( m_{2} \) & \( m_{3} \)
\end{tabular}\right) \quad (12)
\]

yields the condition:

\[
A^{j_{3},j_{1}}_{i_{3},i_{1}} = (-1)^{j_{1}+j_{2}+j_{3}} A^{j_{1},j_{3}}_{i_{1},i_{3}} \quad (13)
\]

as a sufficient requirement for parity invariance. It is important to note that \( j_{1} + j_{2} + j_{3} \) is always an integer.

To systematically utilize $su(2)$ invariance in matrix product states, we demonstrated a method for numerically efficient variational computation of ground state energy. The algebraic computations for $su(2)$-invariant building blocks were formulated diagrammatically. As an example, we considered the (frustrated) spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ Heisenberg chain with interactions between nearest and next-nearest neighbors. Our algebraic constructions resulted in key expressions: the transfer matrix (18), and modified transfer matrices (24, 25) and (28, 29), where the coefficient matrix must satisfy condition (13) for parity invariance.

The action of the transfer matrix \( T \) on a singlet \( \sigma(j; i, \tilde{i}) \) generates analogous singlets with \( j \) modified by \( \pm\frac{1}{2} \). The matrix element \( \langle j_l; i_l, \tilde{i}_l | T | j_r; i_r, \tilde{i}_r \rangle \) is given by

\[
\langle j_l; i_l, \tilde{i}_l | T | j_r; i_r, \tilde{i}_r \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2j_l + 1)(2j_r + 1)}} A^{j_l, j_r}_{i_l, i_r} \left( A^{j_l, j_r}_{\tilde{i}_l, \tilde{i}_r} \right)^*, \quad (18)
\]

where \( j_l = j_r \pm \frac{1}{2} \). This expression is analogous to equation (6) in [2], without imposing condition (10) from [2]. The matrix \( T \) exhibits a block structure with zero diagonal blocks and non-zero secondary diagonal blocks. From (13), it follows that \( T \) is symmetric. The defining blocks are

\[
\langle j; i_l, \tilde{i}_l | T | j + \nicefrac{1}{2}; i_r, \tilde{i}_r \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2j + 1)(2j + 2)}} A^{j, j + \nicefrac{1}{2}}_{i_l, i_r} \left( A^{j, j + \nicefrac{1}{2}}_{\tilde{i}_l, \tilde{i}_r} \right)^*. \quad (19)
\]

In expression (18), the coefficients \( A \) and their complex conjugates \( A^* \) arise explicitly from (11), while the prefactor is derived from the identity of \( 3j \) symbols, which holds when \( j_1 \) is coupled with \( j_2 \) and \( j_3 \):

\[
\sum_{m_2, m_3} \begin{pmatrix} j_1' & j_2 & j_3 \\ m_1' & m_2 & m_3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} j_1 & j_2 & j_3 \\ m_1 & m_2 & m_3 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2j_1 + 1} \delta_{j_1, j_1'} \delta_{m_1, m_1'}. \quad (20)
\]

This identity is illustrated in Figure 4.

For the nearest-neighbor spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ Heisenberg chain, we observed that even a small number of low-dimensional multiplets in the matrix space $V$ yield highly accurate results. For instance, the ground-state energy deviates from the exact value $e_{0}=1/4-\ln 2$ by approximately $5\cdot 10^{-5}$, and dimer correlations differ from the exact results by about $10^{-4}$. This level of precision is achieved with $n_{0}=4$, $n_{\nicefrac{{1}}{{2}}}=4$, $n_{1}=3$, $n_{\nicefrac{{3}}{{2}}}=2$, and $n_{2}=1$ (with $n_{s}=0$ for $s>2$). Notably, attempts to increase higher spin multiplets at the cost of reducing low-spin ones were unsuccessful; for example, setting $n_{s}=1$ for all $s$ leads to the simple dimer (Majumdar-Ghosh) state.

As an illustration of spin-spin correlation functions, we present results for the nearest- and next-nearest-neighbour cases in Table 1. For $\alpha=0$, the exact values are known: $C_{2}^{\rm exact}=e_{0}^{\rm exact}$, as given in (64), and $C_{3}^{\rm exact}=0.182\ 039...$ from [35]. Even for $C_{3}$, our numerical value deviates from the exact value by less than $10^{-4}$. Due to the variational nature of our calculations, the expectation values of the energy are strict upper bounds for the ground-state energy. Notably, for cases $\alpha=0.1,0.2411,0.3,0.4$, our results are lower than those reported in [32], with differences ranging from $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$. Hence, the deviation of these DMRG results from the true ground-state energy must be of the same order or even larger. In DMRG calculations, errors arise from two sources: (i) truncation of the Hilbert space and (ii) finite-size effects due to the finite length of the considered chains. In our approach, we consider the strictly infinitely long chain.

Our analysis closely mirrors the approach outlined in [1], which utilized a similar methodology for studying gapped spin-1 chains and spin ladders. However, the variational matrix product state (MPS) calculations for the spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ Heisenberg chain present notable challenges: the model exhibits algebraically decaying correlation functions, and it remains uncertain whether such decay patterns can be reliably captured using variational MPS methods with limited multiplets in the matrix space. A key distinction from [1] lies in the composition of our matrix space, which encompasses all integer and half-odd integer spin multiplets. For our specific computations, we employed a matrix space comprising 4 singlets, 4 doublets, 3 triplets, 2 quadruplets, and 1 quintuplet. This configuration enabled us to compute the ground-state energy with a precision exceeding $10^{-4}$. Additionally, the correlation functions were determined with an accuracy on the order of $10^{-4}$, facilitating the determination of the scaling dimension in scenarios of critical frustration, as evidenced by $\alpha = 0.2411$.

A more comprehensive analysis of the correlation functions and the physics of frustrated systems with $\alpha > 0.5$ will be presented in a separate publication. In the meantime, we would like to emphasize that for $\alpha > 0.5$, the matrices that replace equation (63) inherently include complex numbers.

The numerical computations, such as matrix diagonalizations, were significantly reduced in complexity through the algebraic $su(2)$ implementation, decreasing the space from 1156-dimensional to 46-dimensional. The methodical integration of additional and higher spin multiplets is evident. Extending these calculations to more complex systems, such as spin-$S$ Heisenberg chains with competing interactions, is straightforward. We are confident that the systematic utilization of symmetries within the MPS analysis of quantum spin chains will yield high-quality data with minimal truncation errors.

We employ a variational computational framework akin to [1], utilizing $su(2)$-invariant matrix product states (MPS). To streamline the methodology, we minimize the required constructions without incorporating any reference to density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) algorithms. Additionally, we adopt a diagrammatic representation to illustrate key components and relationships pertinent to the evaluation of the norm and energy expectation value of the MPS. Our goal is to enhance the accessibility of the subject matter. The primary focus of this study is on the investigation of ground-state energies, spin-spin, and dimer-dimer correlations within the context of a frustrated antiferromagnetic spin-1/2 Heisenberg chain characterized by nearest- and next-nearest neighbor interactions. For moderate frustration levels, this system exhibits critical behavior. Notably, the variational $su(2)$-invariant MPS approach yields highly accurate results, even for correlation functions. Contrary to the assertion made in [2], our findings suggest that this method is extendable to odd-legged spin-1/2 ladders that are not finitely correlated.

The graphical computation of the matrix elements of \( T_{3} \) is illustrated in Figs. 8 and 9. Unlike the modified transfer matrix \( T_{2} \), but similar to \( T \), the matrix \( T_{3} \) features zero diagonal blocks and non-zero secondary diagonal blocks. The matrix element of \( T_{3} \) corresponding to \( \sigma(j_{l};i_{l},\tilde{i}_{l})-\sigma(j_{r};i_{r},\tilde{i}_{r}) \) is expressed as:

\]
where \( j_{l}=j_{r}\pm\frac{1}{2} \) and \( C^{j_{l},j_{r}} \) is defined by:

C^{j_{l},j_{r}}=\sum_{j_{1},j_{3}}(-1)^{\nicefrac{1}{2}+j_{1}+j_{3}}\left\{\hbox{\begin{tabular}[]{ccc}$j_{l}$&$\nicefrac{1}{2}$&$j_{r}$\\
$j_{3}$&$\nicefrac{1}{2}$&$j_{1}$\end{tabular}}\right\}A^{j_{l},j_{1}}A^{j_{1},j_{3}}A^{j_{3},j_{r}}.

Using this and the parity invariance condition (13), the expression simplifies to:
