v_{c}=\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon}{M_{0}-50\varepsilon\frac{\cosh^{2}\left(\sqrt{5}X_{0}\right)}{\left(5+\sinh^{2}\left(\sqrt{5}X_{0}\right)\right)^{2}}}}\frac{5\left(3-\sinh^{2}\left(\sqrt{5}X_{0}\right)\right)}{2\left(5+\sinh^{2}\left(\sqrt{5}X_{0}\right)\right)}. \tag{13}
$$

Conversely, if the initial velocity exceeds $v_{c}$, the soliton traverses the barrier, resulting in energy exceeding $E_{max}$. Figure 2 illustrates these trajectories for $\varepsilon=0.2$, derived from numerical solutions of equation (III). The dashed line represents a soliton with a sub-critical velocity, climbing but failing to surpass the barrier and reflecting. The solid line depicts a soliton with a velocity above $v_{c}$, successfully passing through the barrier. The minor fluctuations on the barrier's crest indicate a distinct physical phenomenon, further explored in subsequent sections.

The interaction of DSG solitons with external defects has been analyzed in (41) utilizing a collective coordinate approach, employing a distinct method for incorporating the potential into the equations of motion. While no direct comparison between the model's predictions and numerical simulations was conducted in (41), the predicted general behavior of the soliton aligns with the findings presented here. Both models successfully capture the essential characteristics of soliton-potential interactions. However, notable disparities emerge in the finer details of the interaction dynamics, attributed to the differing methodologies for adding the potential. In our model, solitons exhibit a dynamically space-dependent mass, whereas the soliton mass remains constant in the model presented in (41). Our approach preserves the symmetries and topological properties of the theory in the presence of an external potential, whereas the model in (41) fails to accurately retain all field properties. It is important to note that solitonic solutions in the sine-Gordon model fundamentally arise due to topological boundary conditions.

Equation (13) indicates that the soliton's critical velocity depends on its initial position. It is anticipated that the numerical results can be effectively fitted to this equation using the computed $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eff}}$. Figure 5 illustrates the critical velocity as a function of the soliton's initial position, where the dashed line represents the analytical prediction from equation (13) and the solid line corresponds to the numerical findings. The figure clearly shows a strong agreement between the analytical and numerical results.

Consider a soliton approaching the center of a potential well from an initial position \( X_0 \) with an initial velocity \( \dot{X}_0 \). The soliton will escape to infinity if its initial velocity exceeds the escape velocity

\[
v_{\mathrm{escape}} = \frac{\varepsilon}{M_0} \frac{10 \cosh\left(\sqrt{5} X_0\right)}{\sqrt{\frac{50 \varepsilon}{M_0} \cosh^2\left(\sqrt{5} X_0\right) + \left(5 + \sinh^2\left(\sqrt{5} X_0\right)\right)^2}},
\]

but it will be captured by the well and oscillate within it if its initial velocity is below \( v_{\mathrm{escape}} \). The maximum distance between the soliton and the center of the potential well, \( X_{\mathrm{max}} \), is determined using equations (III.2) and (18):

\[
100 \varepsilon \frac{\cosh^2\left(\sqrt{5} X_{\mathrm{max}}\right)}{\left(5 + \sinh^2\left(\sqrt{5} X_{\mathrm{max}}\right)\right)^2} = \left(2 - \dot{X}_0^2\right) \left(M_0 + 50 \varepsilon \frac{\cosh^2\left(\sqrt{5} X_0\right)}{\left(5 + \sinh^2\left(\sqrt{5} X_0\right)\right)^2}\right) - 2 M_0.
\]

The interaction between solitons and potentials can be analyzed theoretically based on the results presented earlier. Equation 7 was numerically solved using the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method for temporal derivatives, while spatial derivatives were calculated using the finite difference method. The Hamiltonian density was computed using the finite difference method at each time step. A Gaussian function, expressed as \( V(x) = \sqrt{\frac{b}{\pi}} \varepsilon e^{-bx^{2}} \), was employed to approximate the delta function. The numerical methods' stability and the accuracy of the results were verified by performing calculations with various grid spacing values (0.01, 0.05, and 0.001). The time step was selected to be less than \( \frac{1}{4} \) of the grid spacing to ensure numerical stability.

The sine-Gordon equation has garnered significant attention within the physics community. As a non-linear partial differential equation, it emerges spontaneously across diverse physical domains, such as atomic physics, electromagnetism, superconductivity, quantum field theory, biophysics, and statistical mechanics. Furthermore, its applications extend to condensed matter systems and nonlinear optics. Notably, the solitons and kinks associated with the $\mathrm{SU(3)}$ generalized sine-Gordon model (GSG) have been instrumental in elucidating the baryonic spectrum of two-dimensional quantum chromodynamics (QCD2).

The agreement between numerical and analytical results is not consistent across all predictions. Figure 6 illustrates the oscillation period in a soliton-barrier system, where the dashed line represents the analytical prediction using equation (16), and the solid line shows numerical results with corresponding $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eff}}$. Both curves indicate that the oscillation period decreases as $\varepsilon$ increases. However, the analytical model's predicted period significantly differs from the numerical results. In contrast, the situation improves for small oscillations in a soliton-well system, as shown in Figure 7. Numerical simulation results are plotted as the solid line, while the dashed line corresponds to equation (26).

The integration of automated components in emerging vehicular systems offers potential for optimized control, reducing congestion and enhancing efficiency. Recent research has explored the application of deep reinforcement learning to design effective control strategies for these complex systems. However, existing studies often rely on customized training setups tailored to specific vehicular systems, hindering efficient analysis of diverse systems. This article addresses this challenge by introducing a streamlined methodology for vehicular microsimulation, enabling the discovery of high-performance control strategies with minimal manual intervention. A novel approach is presented, utilizing variable agents and multi-task optimization to improve control of Partially Observed Markov Decision Processes in vehicular systems. Experimental validation on mixed autonomy traffic systems demonstrates significant improvements, ranging from 15-60%, over human-driven baselines across various traffic scenarios. The study also reveals emergent behaviors that resemble established traffic management strategies, which are subsequently analyzed to produce interpretable control strategies that align with the learned optimal controls.

The contemporary landscape of mobility systems is witnessing a pronounced shift towards the integration of autonomous control mechanisms in vehicles that have historically been operated by humans. This phenomenon is evident across a diverse array of applications, including urban transportation networks and logistics facilities that utilize autonomous mobile robots for tasks such as inventory management, categorization, and distribution. As the level of autonomy in these systems continues to escalate, it enables the implementation of sophisticated algorithmic control strategies, thereby facilitating the coordination of autonomous vehicles in a manner that enhances overall system efficiency, mitigates congestion, and optimizes key performance metrics, including fuel consumption.

This research builds upon our prior studies on applying Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to mixed autonomy traffic systems, offering a streamlined and unified methodology that can be easily applied to a broad range of open and closed traffic systems. Unlike previous approaches that often require significant system-specific customization and tuning, our approach simplifies the DRL framework, reducing the need for extensive hyperparameter tuning and design choices. By revising the Flow Framework, we provide a more accessible and flexible tool for researchers and practitioners to design and analyze traffic systems. Furthermore, our analysis of DRL-controlled autonomous vehicles reveals behaviors that mirror those designed by traffic engineering experts, informing the development of simple, DRL-inspired controllers. This work prioritizes the ease of applicability of model-free DRL to various vehicular systems, complementing our previous research, which focused on the performance of DRL in specific traffic systems.

The US Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency's (DARPA) autonomous driving challenges have driven significant research into the key components required for the real-world deployment of autonomous vehicles, with a primary focus on enhancing the safety, comfort, and performance of individual vehicles, as well as their ability to mimic human-like driving behaviors, rather than optimizing the overall traffic system.

This study presents several key contributions, including the development of a versatile and effective deep reinforcement learning framework for optimizing mixed autonomy traffic in simulated vehicular systems, demonstrating its broad applicability and ease of use. Additionally, we derive and evaluate simple control strategies informed by the learned behaviors, assess the robustness of trained policies across varying vehicle densities, and provide an accessible codebase designed to facilitate adoption by researchers and practitioners, with accompanying resources available on Github.

Future research avenues in vehicular and traffic systems encompass several key areas, including the extension of existing methodologies to broader vehicular systems, incorporation of complex traffic scenarios with varied maneuvers and control mechanisms, optimization for diverse objectives such as fuel efficiency and passenger comfort, analysis of heterogeneous vehicle behavior, examination of systems under non-stationary conditions like fluctuating traffic volume, and the application of decomposition techniques to scale up to larger, more complex systems. Notably, the current methodology's adaptability to non-stationary traffic conditions is a promising aspect, whereas the remaining research directions necessitate additional exploration and development.

We modified existing Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) algorithms from previous studies to develop policies for the Single Ring, Highway Bottleneck, and Intersection systems, adapting them to our specific traffic scenarios while maintaining consistency with the original frameworks. To ensure a fair comparison, we utilized the same training and evaluation protocols as our in-house methodology, where applicable, for these adapted algorithms.

To successfully integrate Sim2Real technologies into physical systems, additional research and development are necessary, focusing on introducing increased randomization and stochasticity into simulations to enhance policy robustness. Furthermore, real-world system design should prioritize minimizing modeling errors. The application of Sim2Real technologies is anticipated to be viable in the near future for automated systems that utilize high-fidelity simulators and safety protocols, particularly in industrial robotics where human intent simulation is not required. Future research directions may also explore the development of automated methods to distill complex deep reinforcement learning policies into interpretable, modular components that can be shared across various systems, thereby enhancing the transparency and applicability of these policies in real-world deployments.

Model-free reinforcement learning, a methodology rooted in optimal control and machine learning, enables the optimization of sequential decision-making processes without relying on explicit system modeling. By utilizing Markov Decision Processes as an interface, this approach effectively decouples control optimization from system dynamics, eliminating the need for comprehensive knowledge of the underlying system or optimal behavior. The integration of deep neural networks with model-free reinforcement learning, known as model-free deep reinforcement learning, has yielded notable performance enhancements in various applications, including traffic management, robotics, and manufacturing. Nevertheless, the simulation-based nature of model-free deep reinforcement learning algorithms poses significant challenges when transitioning to real-world deployment, highlighting a substantial gap between simulated and real-world environments.

Contrary to previous research, such as [15], which achieved similar results in Figure Eight systems but relied on manual tuning of the reward function to steer the system towards a predetermined optimal speed, our approach eliminates the need for human intervention and instead relies solely on optimizing the ultimate goal, allowing the system to converge naturally without manual guidance.

This research utilizes a Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework to model sequential decision-making processes, which is formally defined as a finite-horizon discounted MDP comprising a state space, action space, transition function, reward function, initial state distribution, discount factor, and horizon. The objective of reinforcement learning and optimal control within this MDP framework is to maximize the expected cumulative reward by identifying the optimal sequence of actions, which is achieved by solving the optimization problem that selects actions to maximize the expected sum of discounted rewards over a specified horizon, given the defined MDP components and constraints.

The REINFORCE policy gradient algorithm is designed to maximize the expected cumulative reward of a given policy, denoted as $\pi_{\theta}$, by iteratively sampling trajectories and optimizing the objective function through gradient descent with respect to the policy parameters $\theta$. This optimization process involves updating $\theta$ at each step based on the gradient of the log probability of the policy, weighted by the cumulative reward and discounted by a factor of $\gamma$, with the learning rate $\alpha$ controlling the step size of each update.

In our approach, we utilize the TRPO algorithm to train policies for each system, limiting the training process to a maximum of 200 gradient updates. To perform each update, we gather data from between 40 and 45 trajectories, evenly distributed across different configurations, and utilize this batched data to inform our gradient steps. Each trajectory begins with a warmup phase, consisting of a random number of steps between $H_{0}$ and $\frac{100}{\delta}$, followed by a horizon of $\frac{1000}{\delta}$ steps. Notably, our training process is relatively streamlined, with the discount factor $\gamma$ being the only hyperparameter subject to tuning, within a range of 0.9 to 0.9999. This focused approach enables efficient training, with each policy requiring less than three hours to train on a high-performance 48-core CPU. The training process, although stochastic, yields remarkably consistent results across different runs, with minimal variation in learned behaviors and performance. However, for significantly larger systems, the TRPO algorithm may become impractical due to slow training times and high memory demands, potentially necessitating the use of alternative methods like REINFORCE.

This study utilizes a finite-horizon discounted Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework to model sequential decision-making processes, characterized by a set of parameters including state and action spaces, a stochastic transition function, a reward function, an initial state distribution, a discount factor, and a horizon. The primary objective of reinforcement learning and optimal control within this MDP framework is to optimize the selection of actions in order to maximize the expected cumulative reward over a specified horizon, as expressed by the maximization of the expected value of a discounted sum of rewards accrued through a sequence of states and actions.

In policy-based model-free deep reinforcement learning algorithms, a policy function $\pi_{\theta}(a|s)$ is defined, which determines the probability of selecting action $a$ in state $s$, with the policy being parameterized by $\theta$, such as the weights of a linear function or neural network. The primary objective is to optimize $\theta$ in order to maximize the expected cumulative reward, which is formulated as maximizing the expectation of the sum of discounted rewards over a trajectory, where the expectation is taken with respect to the initial state distribution, the policy, and the environment's transition dynamics.

This study seeks to substantiate the efficacy of our approach within the context of traffic systems, acknowledging that real-world applications often encompass a broad spectrum of vehicular concentrations, thus necessitating the development of adaptable policies that can effectively transcend disparate vehicle density scenarios.

The fidelity of derived policies in simulated traffic systems is contingent upon the parameters of the Intelligent Driver Model (IDM), which encapsulates human driving behavior through a suite of parameters. Given that uncontrolled vehicles in these simulations adhere to the IDM, variations in its parameters can yield disparate simulation outcomes. To assess the robustness of our derived policies across a range of car following parameters, we examine the Highway Bottleneck scenario with a total target inflow of 2600 vehicles per hour. Specifically, we consider the default IDM parameters, comprising maximum acceleration, comfortable deceleration, desired velocity, minimum spacing, desired time headway, and exponent, which are set at 2.6 m/s², 4.5 m/s², 30 m/s, 2.5 m, 1 s, and 4, respectively. By introducing reasonable perturbations to these parameters and maintaining the derived policy parameters constant, we evaluate the performance of both the derived and baseline policies, as outlined in Section VII-D, and present the results in Table I, thereby providing insight into the policies' sensitivity to variations in IDM parameters.

In a Double Ring scenario, the Baseline policy exhibits lane behavior akin to the Single Ring. However, when an autonomous vehicle (AV) is enabled to change lanes and optimized using Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for either Greedy or Global objectives, distinct behavioral patterns emerge. The Greedy policy adopts a strategy of maintaining a constant speed within its designated lane, disregarding the movements of vehicles in adjacent lanes. In contrast, the Global policy seeks to mitigate stop-and-go waves across both lanes by maintaining a constant speed in its own lane and utilizing turn signals to regulate the speed of the adjacent lane without actually changing lanes. Notably, the Greedy policy also exhibits frequent turn signal flickering, which warrants further analysis to discern signal patterns and their impact on performance. Although the Global policy's behavior may exploit a limitation in the SUMO simulation, it is reminiscent of human driving behaviors where a driver may slow down in response to a leading vehicle attempting to change lanes into their path. The Derived policy, constructed similarly to the Single Ring without lane change capability, lacks the strategic turn signaling observed in the Global policy.

The remainder of this article is structured as follows: an overview of pertinent literature is presented in Section II, key concepts related to Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) are introduced in Section III, a description of the relevant vehicular systems is provided in Section IV, the methodology employed for DRL is outlined in Section V, the experimental configuration is detailed in Section VI, and the outcomes of the experiments are reported in Section VII.

In order to mitigate the complexity associated with training individual policies for a myriad of vehicular system configurations, each characterized by distinct vehicle densities, a more pragmatic approach is adopted. By partitioning the density configuration space into a finite set of discrete, evenly spaced configurations, it becomes feasible to develop a unified, multi-task policy that can effectively operate across this spectrum. The training process involves the concurrent initialization of multiple environments, each corresponding to a specific configuration within the predefined set. At each iteration of the training cycle, the policy gradient algorithm aggregates trajectory data from all environments, subsequently batching the gradient updates derived from these trajectories. This multi-task learning paradigm enables a single, trained policy to exhibit a high degree of generalizability, thereby obviating the need for configuration-specific policies and the attendant costs associated with their development.

The present study proposes a streamlined and integrated approach to optimizing vehicular systems that exhibit either partial or complete autonomy, with the methodology's versatility and efficacy being exemplified through its application to various mixed autonomy traffic scenarios. Notably, this framework can be readily extended to other robotic systems that involve vehicular components. A key distinction between this work and preceding research, which often relied heavily on deep reinforcement learning (DRL) and necessitated substantial hyperparameter calibration and reward function engineering, lies in the minimal manual configuration and tuning required by the proposed methodology. To assess the performance and resilience of the trained policies, a comparative analysis is conducted with optimized rule-based policies. Ultimately, this research provides a compact and adaptable framework that can be effortlessly tailored to accommodate diverse systems and domains, thereby facilitating future investigations. By developing a novel, lightweight framework, the limitations imposed by earlier frameworks, such as the Flow framework, which relied on the versatile yet cumbersome RLLib library, have been overcome, enabling a more nuanced exploration of the constituent elements of the methodology and their respective contributions to its overall effectiveness.

Channel-independent modeling approaches process each time series variable in isolation, disregarding potential interdependencies between channels, which may limit their efficacy in certain scenarios due to neglected complex relationships between variables. However, these models have demonstrated notable performance gains, as evidenced by architectures such as DLinear, which leverages a single linear model across the time dimension to outperform state-of-the-art Transformer-based models, and PatchTST, which divides time series data into patches and applies a Transformer model to extract temporal insights.

This study highlights the promise of the Transformer model in multivariate time series forecasting, particularly when enhanced sequence and channel data extraction methods are employed. To address this, we propose the CSformer model, which effectively captures and integrates sequence and channel information without requiring modifications to the Transformer's attention mechanism. Our approach involves a novel dimensionality-augmented embedding technique that increases sequence dimensionality while preserving original data integrity, as depicted in Figure 1. We then apply a shared attention mechanism, separately attending to sequence and channel dimensions, with shared parameters to facilitate cross-dimensional influence. Additionally, an adapter is introduced after each attention operation to enable distinct feature extraction across the two attention stages, ultimately contributing to improved forecasting performance.

The realm of time series forecasting is broadly bifurcated into two primary categories: traditional statistical approaches and deep learning methodologies. The former, encompassing models such as ARIMA and Exponential Smoothing, has garnered significant traction owing to its efficacy in capturing temporal dynamics and inherent simplicity. Nonetheless, these models are constrained by their univariate nature, neglecting the potential impact of exogenous variables. In contrast, deep learning paradigms have emerged as a formidable force in recent years, leveraging their robust modeling capabilities to accommodate complex patterns. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), along with their variants, have become ubiquitous in time series forecasting applications. However, the autoregressive propensity of RNNs renders them ill-suited for multi-step time series forecasting (MSTF) tasks. To mitigate this limitation, a plethora of innovative models has been proposed, prompting a taxonomy that dichotomizes them into channel-independent and channel-dependent architectures, as inspired by the PatchTST framework.

Channel-dependent modeling approaches acknowledge the interdependencies between multiple time series variables, enabling more nuanced and accurate representations of underlying data patterns and trends. Unlike earlier methods, which relied on simplistic embedding operations to capture inter-channel relationships and often resulted in suboptimal information extraction and potential noise introduction, recent advancements have addressed these limitations. For instance, models like Crossformer and TSMixer have introduced innovative attention mechanisms and layer architectures that effectively leverage cross-dimension and cross-time dependencies, while others, such as iTransformer, have improved performance by sequentially applying embedding operations and attention mechanisms along the time dimension and between variables.

In the realm of multivariate time series forecasting, a historical input sequence is represented as a matrix $\mathbf{X}$, comprising $N$ variables and $L$ time points, where each time point is associated with a vector of $N$ variable values. The sequence of values for a specific variable $k$ is denoted as $\mathbf{X}^{(k)}$. Given a prediction horizon of $T$ time steps, the forecasting objective is to generate a predicted sequence $\mathbf{\hat{X}}$ spanning $T$ future time points. This process can be formalized using a model $\mathbf{f_{\theta}}$, parameterized by $\theta$, which takes the historical input sequence $\mathbf{X}$ and yields the predicted sequence $\mathbf{\hat{X}}$, thus encapsulating the multivariate time series forecasting process as a mapping from $\mathbf{X}$ to $\mathbf{\hat{X}}$ via the model $\mathbf{f_{\theta}}$.

In practical applications, time-series data often exhibits dynamic shifts in response to exogenous factors, thereby exacerbating the issue of data distribution bias. To mitigate this challenge, researchers have introduced Reversible Instance Normalization (ReVIN), a methodology devised by Kim et al. in 2021. By transforming the distribution of each input variable's sequence, as delineated in Equation 1, ReVIN effectively diminishes the discrepancies in distributional characteristics among distinct univariate variables inherent in the original distribution, ultimately yielding a normalized output that restores the original mean, variance, and adaptable parameters, thus refining the predictive results.

Recent advancements in multivariate time series analysis have seen predictive models achieve notable success by leveraging the assumption of channel independence, but this approach overlooks the complex relationships between channels that significantly impact prediction outcomes. As a result, the limitations of channel independence become apparent, leading to diminished performance. To address this issue, we propose CSformer, a novel framework featuring a sophisticated two-stage self-attention mechanism that separately extracts sequence-specific and channel-specific information while fostering synergy between them through shared parameters. Additionally, our model incorporates sequence and channel adapters to identify key features across multiple dimensions. Extensive experiments on diverse real-world datasets demonstrate the efficacy of our approach, consistently yielding superior predictive performance and enhancing the model's ability to extract valuable insights from multivariate time series data.

By tackling this obstacle, our approach introduces an innovative methodology wherein parameters gleaned from Multi-Scale Analysis (MSA) along the channel axis are repurposed for sequence modeling purposes. Notably, the output tensor $\mathbf{A}_{c}$, resulting from the channel-oriented MSA and characterized by dimensions $L \times N \times D$, is subjected to a deliberate rearrangement, thereby facilitating a smooth transformation into the input tensor $\mathbf{H}_{s}$, defined by dimensions $N \times L \times D$, which serves as the foundation for the ensuing sequence-based MSA. This carefully crafted architecture seeks to foster a symbiotic relationship between the channel and sequence-based representations, ultimately yielding a unified and efficient feature extraction process.

The PatchTST approach, as proposed by Nie et al. (2023), employs a channel-independent method that overlooks inter-channel interactions, which can lead to a loss of meaningful information. To maximize the utility of available data, it is essential to consider both sequential and channel-based information. Although various techniques, including TSMixer (Ekambaram et al., 2023) and Crossformer (Zhang & Yan, 2023), have been developed to extract these types of information, they often fail to effectively capture the interdependence between sequence and channel data. Moreover, these methods can introduce distortions during processing, ultimately resulting in subpar predictive outcomes due to incomplete information capture.

The output of the sequence adapter, represented as $\mathbf{A}_{s}$, is obtained by applying a normalization function to $\mathbf{Z}_{s}$ and then passing the result through an adapter, with the final result being added to $\mathbf{H}_{s}$. This technique effectively allows the model to imbue both sequence and channel features with unique properties, enabling a high degree of differentiation between them. By leveraging a lightweight adapter, the model is able to achieve this advanced level of functionality without substantially increasing its overall complexity, striking a balance between performance and efficiency.

The initial stage's channel attention mechanism shares similarities with the iTransformer, as both utilize Multi-Head Self-Attention (MSA) across the channel dimension, but differs in its approach to time series data, applying channel-wise attention at each time step to capture inter-channel relationships, whereas the iTransformer treats the entire time series as a single token, and this process is mathematically represented by transforming the input $\mathbf{H}_{c}$ into the output $\mathbf{Z}_{c}$ through the channel-wise attention mechanism.

A comprehensive evaluation of the results presented in Table 2 reveals that our CSformer model consistently outperforms its counterparts, yielding the highest overall performance across the entirety of the datasets examined. Notably, it claims the top spot in a substantial majority of the metrics, securing 26 and 18 first-place rankings in the MSE and MAE categories, respectively. This dominant display of superior rankings serves as a testament to the efficacy and excellence of our proposed approach, surpassing the state-of-the-art iTransformer model and further unlocking the potential of Transformer-based architectures to achieve enhanced performance. Furthermore, a comparative analysis with the Crossformer model, which shares a similar methodological framework, highlights the considerable margin by which CSformer excels, potentially attributable to the detrimental effects of premature channel mixing inherent in Crossformer's embedding process, which may introduce extraneous variability or interference. Moreover, the significant performance gains demonstrated by CSformer over the channel-independent PatchTST model underscore the importance of effectively capturing and representing inter-variable dependencies, thereby emphasizing the need for a more nuanced and sophisticated approach to modeling complex relationships.

Recent approaches, such as PatchTST, proposed by Nie et al. in 2023, involve partitioning the input sequence into discrete temporal intervals, which are then processed as individual tokens by the Transformer architecture to facilitate learning. In contrast, the Timesnet methodology, introduced by Wu et al. in 2023, leverages the Fast Fourier transform to decipher distinct periodic patterns embedded within the sequence, subsequently reconfiguring the data into a two-dimensional vector that is then inputted into a Convolutional Neural Network for additional processing. Although these techniques effectively enhance the dimensional complexity of the sequences, they also introduce a potential caveat: the risk of information degradation inherent in the sequential data, which can compromise the fidelity of the results.

Our proposed CSformer model is designed to simultaneously capture channel and sequence information, with its architecture outlined in Figure 2. The process begins with the Dimension-augmented Embedding operation, which enriches multivariate time-series data. A two-stage attention mechanism is then employed to extract both channel and sequence features, leveraging shared parameters to facilitate cross-information interaction during training. To maintain the unique contributions of each attention mechanism, we utilize channel and sequence adapters to refine the output of the dual multi-head self-attention mechanisms, thereby enhancing their ability to learn across disparate dimensions.

The shared parameterization between the channel and sequence multi-head self-attention mechanisms poses a challenge for simultaneous learning across two disparate dimensions, prompting the adoption of Adapter technology, a technique borrowed from large-scale model fine-tuning in Natural Language Processing. This approach utilizes a modular architecture consisting of two fully connected layers and an activation function, which collectively enable the model to iteratively refine its channel representations through a process of dimensionality reduction and restoration. The resulting output, denoted as $\mathbf{A}_{c}$, is obtained by applying the Adapter to the normalized output of the multi-head self-attention mechanism, $\mathbf{Z}_{c}$, and additively combining it with the original $\mathbf{H}_{c}$, thereby mitigating potential gradient-related issues and ensuring the stability of the overall system.

Upon completing the dual-phase Multiscale Attention mechanism, a dimensionality reduction process is initiated, wherein the sequential output is scaled by the embedding dimension, yielding a matrix $\mathbf{Z}$ with dimensions $N\times(L*D)$, inhabiting the realm of real numbers. Leveraging the exhaustive feature extraction facilitated by the preceding two-stage paradigm, a simplistic linear transformation is subsequently applied to yield the predicted outcome, denoted as $\mathbf{\hat{X}}$, which resides in the $\mathbb{R}^{N\times T}$ space. By harnessing the potent representations distilled through the two-stage MSA, this linear predictive model efficiently generates the ultimate forecast, underscoring the efficacy of this methodological synergy.

In conformity with the methodological framework established by iTransformer, our experimental design featured a consistent look-back window length of 96 time steps, while the predictive horizon was varied across four distinct intervals: 96, 192, 336, and 720 time steps. Computational resources were provided by an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 graphics processing unit, with the PyTorch library serving as the foundational framework for implementing our models. To optimize model performance, the mean squared error (MSE) was selected as the primary loss function, and a dual-metric evaluation approach was adopted, incorporating both MSE and mean absolute error (MAE) to comprehensively assess the accuracy and reliability of the predictive outcomes.

The ReVIN operation is defined as a set of transformations applied to the input data $\mathbf{X}$, where each transformation is parameterized by $\gamma_k$ and $\beta_k$, and involves standardizing the $k^{th}$ component of $\mathbf{X}$ by subtracting its mean and dividing by the square root of its variance plus a small positive constant $\varepsilon$ to prevent division by zero, followed by an affine transformation.

The initiation of the process involves introducing the input into a multiple sequence alignment, which is formally defined by a precise conceptual framework that outlines the specific parameters and governing principles.

The CSformer architecture comprises $M$ blocks, each of which features a two-stage Multi-Head Self-Attention (MSA) mechanism. This design enables the model to separately capture channel and sequence information from the input data within each block. To facilitate the discriminatory learning of these extracted features, an adapter module is applied after each MSA stage, allowing for a more refined representation of the data. A comprehensive overview of the MSA and adapter components will be presented in the following sections.
The centralized covariance matrix, denoted as $\mathbf{Z}_{c}$, is obtained by applying the MSA operator to the matrix $\mathbf{H}_{c}$.

This study highlights the importance of leveraging both sequential and channel information in multivariate time series data to enhance predictive accuracy, and introduces a new framework, CSformer, which utilizes a two-stage multi-self-attention mechanism with shared parameters and adapters to effectively extract insights from both information modalities, yielding state-of-the-art performance on several real-world datasets, although the approach is not without its limitations, including increased computational complexity with large numbers of variables, which will be addressed in future optimization efforts.

The sequence MSA's output is represented by $\mathbf{Z}_{s}$, a three-dimensional tensor of size $N \times L \times D$, which is obtained by applying the multi-scale attention mechanism to $\mathbf{H}_{s}$. Notably, the MSA layer is shared across different input dimensions, allowing for a unified and efficient processing framework. By leveraging this shared layer, the model is able to simultaneously enhance its sequencing and channel modeling capacities, thereby achieving a synergistic improvement in performance while maintaining a parsimonious parameterization.

Our study's subsequent experiments substantiate the underlying rationale of the proposed architecture through a thorough examination of key design elements, including the sequence and channel MSA arrangement, parameter sharing efficacy, and Adapter integration effects. Ablation experiments were conducted to systematically assess the individual and combined impacts of these components, confirming their logical consistency and importance within the broader model structure, thereby validating the design decisions and providing a detailed understanding of the architecture's performance attributes.

We introduce CSformer, a novel two-stage attention Transformer model designed to optimize the extraction of sequential and channel information in multivariate time series, achieving a balance between capturing complex patterns and minimizing parameter growth. By leveraging this architecture, CSformer attains state-of-the-art results across diverse real-world datasets, including those related to electricity and weather forecasting. Through a comprehensive set of ablation experiments, we provide a detailed understanding of CSformer's performance drivers, offering valuable insights for future research directions in the application of Transformer models to multivariate time series forecasting.

In a similar manner to the channel MSA, we propose the incorporation of an additional Adapter module following the reused MSA layer, specifically designed to capture sequence-level features, with its architectural design analogous to that of the channel adapter, as defined by the subsequent mathematical expression.

In real-world applications like managing traffic flow, monitoring power grid operations, and analyzing healthcare trends, predicting future events based on past patterns is crucial, but the complexity of long-term relationships and interactions between various factors over time poses significant hurdles, prompting the development of more sophisticated approaches, notably multivariate time series forecasting, which has become a key area of focus due to its potential to effectively handle intricate data sets.

Traditionally, dominant approaches have focused on attentional modulation along a single axis, whether it be the sequential or channel domain, as epitomized by paradigmatic models like Transformer and its variants. Conversely, the Crossformer paradigm pioneers a innovative strategy, wherein sequential attention is first applied, followed by channel-wise attention, effectively creating a two-pronged approach. Nevertheless, this sequential deployment of attention mechanisms, characterized by their autonomous operation, precludes the direct interchange of information, which may lead to a dearth of synergistic feature extraction, ultimately hindering the model's ability to capture complex interdependencies between channels and sequences.

To effectively implement the multi-head self-attention mechanism across both channel and sequence dimensions, a novel dimension-enhanced embedding approach is proposed, enabling the embedding of time-series data following dimensionalization, with the embedding process outlined in Figure 3 and described in further detail subsequently.

The application of deep learning models, specifically Transformer-based architectures, to multivariate time series forecasting (MTSF) has gained significant attention, with notable models such as Informer, Autoformer, and Fedformer demonstrating enhanced predictive capabilities through refined attention mechanisms. However, recent studies have cast doubt on the suitability of Transformers for MTSF, with a simple linear model, DLinear, outperforming traditional Transformer-based approaches. Despite the success of Transformers in other fields, researchers have begun exploring alternative strategies, including modifying input data rather than attention structures. Novel approaches, such as iTransformer and PatchTST, have revitalized the use of Transformers in MTSF by introducing innovative techniques like sequence embedding and dimensionality expansion, ultimately reestablishing their prominence in the field.

The assessment of CSformer's efficacy was undertaken through the utilization of a diverse array of benchmark datasets, each tailored to the task of multivariate long-term forecasting, encompassing a range of domains such as energy consumption patterns, as exemplified by the ETT dataset variants (ETTh1, ETTh2, ETTm1, ETTm2), as well as datasets pertaining to meteorological conditions, electrical demand, and solar energy production, all of which are comprehensively outlined in Table 1.

In recent years, several state-of-the-art deep learning models have emerged as top performers in multivariate time series forecasting, and we have carefully selected a range of these models for comparison. Our selection includes a variety of Transformer-based models, such as Informer, Autoformer, and Stationary, as well as more recent models like FEDformer, Crossformer, PatchTST, and iTransformer, which currently holds the top spot among Transformer models. Notably, the Crossformer model bears some resemblance to our own proposed model, CSformer. We have also chosen to include Multilayer Perceptron-based models, such as DLinear and TiDE, which have shown promising results in predictive performance. Additionally, our evaluation will include Temporal Convolutional Network-based models, including SCINet and Timesnet, in order to provide a comprehensive overview of the current deep learning landscape for multivariate time series forecasting.

A comprehensive dataset on weather patterns in the United States has been compiled, featuring 11 key weather indicators and a focal point of "wet bulb" temperature, with data collected every 10 minutes across approximately 1,600 locations nationwide over a four-year period from 2010 to 2013.

Accurate forecasting of solar energy output is crucial for optimizing energy consumption, and the solar-energy dataset provides a comprehensive foundation for this endeavor, comprising 137 variables and 52,179 data points collected at 10-minute intervals.

Accurate forecasting of electricity demand is crucial for effective grid management, as it enables utilities to plan and schedule maintenance downtime in advance, minimizing disruptions to the power supply. To achieve this, a comprehensive dataset was compiled, comprising 321 key indicators and three years' worth of historical electricity usage patterns, recorded at hourly intervals, to facilitate informed predictions of future energy consumption trends.

The Electricity Transformer Temperature (ETT) serves as a crucial indicator for identifying transformer irregularities and averting potential hazards. Our study utilized a two-year dataset, which was categorized into two groups based on distinct sampling frequencies. The first group consists of data sampled at 15-minute intervals, denoted as ETTm1 and ETTm2, while the second group comprises data sampled at 1-hour intervals, referred to as ETTh1 and ETTh2.

A rigorous and exhaustive experimental framework was devised to subject the CSformer model to a thorough examination, with a particular emphasis on deconstructing the underlying architectural paradigm. Through a multifaceted evaluation protocol, the investigation sought to provide a nuanced understanding of the model's operational dynamics, casting a critical eye on its performance metrics while concurrently elucidating the theoretical underpinnings that informed its developmental trajectory. As the research continues to evolve, forthcoming iterations will incorporate an expanded array of empirical findings, further enriching the understanding of this complex model.

The importance of properly extracting cross-dependency to avoid introducing noise, as highlighted in PatchTST by Nie et al. (2023), motivates the development of CSformer, which aims to effectively leverage cross-variable information while simultaneously extracting temporal information. Additionally, given the limited efficiency gains achieved by enhanced attention mechanisms in practical applications, as noted by Zeng et al. (2023), this paper utilizes a traditional attention mechanism architecture.

We present a novel sequence embedding approach, drawing inspiration from word embedding concepts introduced by Mikolov et al. in 2013. Our method involves an initial dimensionality augmentation step, where the input data $\mathbf{X}$ is transformed from $\mathbb{R}^{N\times L}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{N\times L\times 1}$, thereby preserving the original data. The augmented sequence is then element-wise multiplied by a learnable vector $\mathbf{\nu}$, resulting in an embedded representation $\mathbf{H}$ of dimensions $\mathbb{R}^{N\times L\times D}$. This embedding process effectively augments the dimensionality of the input sequence without compromising its inherent information, providing a foundation for the subsequent two-stage multi-step approach outlined in the following sections.

We present a sequence embedding method, drawing inspiration from word embedding concepts introduced by Mikolov et al. in 2013. Our approach involves an initial dimensionality augmentation of the input data, transforming $\mathbf{X}$ from $\mathbb{R}^{N\times L}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{N\times L\times 1}$, which preserves the original data. The augmented sequence is then element-wise multiplied by a learnable vector $\mathbf{\nu}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{1\times D}$, resulting in the embedded representation $\mathbf{H}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{N\times L\times D}$. This technique facilitates dimensionality expansion and embedding while maintaining the integrity of the original input, providing a foundational framework for the subsequent two-stage Multi-Head Self-Attention mechanism.

A notable limitation of PatchTST, as proposed by Nie et al. in 2023, lies in its channel-agnostic methodology, which overlooks the intricacies of inter-channel relationships and veers away from physically interpretable outcomes, thereby incurring a loss of valuable data insights. To maximize the utility of available data, it is essential to consider the interplay between sequential and channel-based information. In response to this challenge, various scholars have investigated alternative approaches, including TSMixer, introduced by Ekambaram et al. in 2023, and Crossformer, developed by Zhang and Yan in 2023, among others. Although these methods attempt to harness both types of information, they fail to effectively capture the synergistic relationships between sequential and channel data, potentially leading to information degradation during processing and ultimately compromising predictive efficacy.

Building on the principles of word embedding introduced by Mikolov et al. in 2013, our approach involves directly embedding sequences while preserving their original structure. To achieve this, we first augment the input data's dimensions, transforming it from a two-dimensional representation to a three-dimensional one, denoted as $\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times L}\to\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times L\times 1}$. The augmented sequence is then element-wise multiplied by a learnable vector $\mathbf{\nu}\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times D}$, resulting in an embedded representation $\mathbf{H}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times L\times D}$. This method allows for the augmentation of dimensions and embedding without modifying the intrinsic characteristics of the input data, providing a foundation for the two-stage multi-scale analysis that follows.

Accurate forecasting of Electricity Consuming Load (ECL) is crucial for informed decision-making and proactive scheduling of grid maintenance, enabling utilities to optimize their operations. Our study utilizes a comprehensive dataset comprising 321 variables and three years of historical data, sampled at hourly intervals, to predict future electrical consumption patterns.

Our approach draws on the concept of word embedding, as introduced by Mikolov et al. in 2013, and adapts it for sequence embedding through a direct method. To preserve the integrity of the original input data, we first apply a dimension-augmentation transformation, which converts the input from $\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times L}$ to $\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times L\times 1}$. The augmented sequence is then element-wise multiplied by a learnable vector $\mathbf{\nu}\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times D}$, resulting in the embedded output $\mathbf{H}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times L\times D}$, where $\mathbf{H}$ is the product of $\mathbf{X}$ and $\mathbf{\nu}$. This method allows for dimensionality augmentation and embedding without compromising the original information, providing a basis for the two-stage multi-self-attention mechanism described in subsequent sections.

We estimate certain mixed $L^{p}\left(L^{2}\right)$ norms of the discrepancy between the volume and the number of integer points in $r\Omega - x$, where $r\Omega - x$ is a dilated by a factor $r$ and translated by a vector $x$ of a convex body $\Omega$ in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$. These norms are expressed as
\[
\left\{{\displaystyle\int_{\mathbb{T}^{d}}}\left(\dfrac{1}{H}{\displaystyle\int_{R}^{R+H}}\left|\sum_{k\in\mathbb{Z}^{d}}\chi_{r\Omega-x}(k)-r^{d}\left|\Omega\right|\right|^{2}dr\right)^{p/2}dx\right\}^{1/p}.
\]
We derive estimates for fixed values of $H$ as $R\to\infty$, as well as asymptotic estimates when $H\to\infty$.

The difference between the volume and the number of integer points in the set \( r\Omega - x \), which is a dilation of a bounded domain \( \Omega \) in \( \mathbb{R}^{d} \) scaled by a factor \( r \) and then translated by a vector \( x \), is expressed as  
\[
\mathcal{D}\left(r\Omega - x\right) = \sum_{k \in \mathbb{Z}^{d}} \chi_{r\Omega - x}(k) - r^{d} \left|\Omega\right|.
\]

Let $\Omega$ be a convex body in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with a smooth boundary and positive Gaussian curvature everywhere. The support function is defined as $g(x) = \sup_{y \in \Omega} \{x \cdot y\}$. For large $|\xi|$, the Fourier transform of the characteristic function of $\Omega$ admits the asymptotic expansion

\[
\widehat{\chi}_{\Omega}(\xi) = \int_{\Omega} \exp(-2\pi i \xi \cdot x) dx = \exp(-2\pi i g(\xi)) |\xi|^{-(d+1)/2} \sum_{j=0}^{h} a_{j}(\xi) |\xi|^{-j} + \exp(2\pi i g(-\xi)) |\xi|^{-(d+1)/2} \sum_{j=0}^{h} b_{j}(\xi) |\xi|^{-j} + \mathcal{O}(|\xi|^{-(d+2h+3)/2}),
\]

where $\{a_j(\xi)\}$ and $\{b_j(\xi)\}$ are smooth, homogeneous functions of degree $0$ in $\mathbb{R}^{d} \setminus \{0\}$. These functions depend on a finite number of derivatives of a parametrization of $\partial\Omega$ at points with outward unit normals $\pm \xi / |\xi|$. Notably, $a_0(\xi)$ and $b_0(\xi)$ are proportional to $K(\pm \xi)^{-1/2}$, where $K(\pm \xi)$ denotes the Gaussian curvature of $\partial\Omega$ at the corresponding points.

If \( g(x) \) is the support function of \( \Omega \), then for every \( \frac{d+1}{2} \leq \alpha < d \) and \( \beta \geq 0 \), there exists a constant \( C \) such that for every \( y \in \mathbb{R}^d \setminus \{0\} \),

\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} |x|^{-\alpha} |x - y|^{-\alpha} \left(1 + |g(x) - g(x - y)|\right)^{-\beta} dx
\]

satisfies the bound

\[
\begin{cases}
C |y|^{d - 2\alpha - \beta} & \text{if } 0 \leq \beta < 1, \\
C |y|^{d - 2\alpha - 1} \log(2 + |y|) & \text{if } \beta = 1, \\
C |y|^{d - 2\alpha - 1} & \text{if } \beta > 1.
\end{cases}
\]

The range \( p > \frac{2d}{d-3} \) in this theorem should be contrasted with the range \( p < \frac{2d-4}{d-3} \) in the preceding theorem. The behavior of the system within this intermediate range remains an open question. Notably, both indices \( \frac{2d-4}{d-3} \) and \( \frac{2d}{d-3} \) approach 2 as \( d \to +\infty \). The proof of this theorem hinges primarily on estimating the \( L^{p/2}(\mathbb{T}^d) \) norm of the function \( \mathcal{G}(x) \), which emerges as the discrepancy limit in Theorem 1.1. While the limit function associated with the unit ball \( \{ |x| \leq 1 \} \) is unbounded, for a general ellipsoid, the limit function is constant. It remains uncertain whether the theorem's statement for the ball extends to all convex sets.

This result is established using part (1) of the theorem in conjunction with the extrapolation Lemma 3.12. For the case $p<+\infty$, it suffices to note that the discrepancy satisfies the trivial bound $\left|\mathcal{D}\left(r\Omega-x\right)\right|\leq Cr^{d}$ for all $r\geq 1$. \]

\[
\leq\begin{cases}

C\left\{\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}^{2}}\left(1+\left|\delta n\right|\right)^{-\gamma}\left(1+\left|k\right|\right)^{-2}\log\left(2+\left|k\right|\right)\right\}^{1/2} & \text{if }\beta=1,\\

\[

\[

\]

Furthermore, if $\beta > 0$ and $p < A$, the family of functions indexed by $R$ and $H$,

\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}}\left|r^{-\left(d-1\right)/2}\mathcal{D}\left(r\Omega-x\right)\right|^{2}d\mu_{H,R}\left(r\right),

converges to a limit $\mathcal{G}(x)$ in the $L^{p/2}(\mathbb{T}^{d})$ norm as $H$ approaches infinity. This convergence of the functions implies the convergence of their norms, and this convergence is uniform for all $R \geq 1$:

\[
\lim_{H\rightarrow+\infty}\left\{\int_{\mathbb{T}^{d}}\left({\displaystyle\int_{\mathbb{R}}}\left|r^{-\left(d-1\right)/2}\mathcal{D}\left(r\Omega-x\right)\right|^{2}d\mu_{H,R}\left(r\right)\right)^{p/2}dx\right\}^{1/p} = \left\{\int_{\mathbb{T}^{d}}\left|\mathcal{G}\left(x\right)\right|^{p/2}dx\right\}^{1/p}.
\]

Let us first revisit the concept of the Fourier dimension of a measure, which is defined as the supremum of all δ for which there exists a constant C such that the magnitude of the Fourier transform of the measure, |μ̂(ζ)|, is bounded above by C|ζ|^{-δ/2}. For further details, consult references [6] and [26]. A classical result by D. Kendall establishes that the L² norm of the discrepancy associated with an oval D(rΩ - x) scales proportionally to r^{(d-1)/2}. This can be found in [22] and subsequent discussions. Motivated by this scaling behavior, we introduce the normalized discrepancy as r^{-(d-1)/2}D(rΩ - x). The primary focus of our investigation is to estimate the Fourier dimension of the set where this normalized discrepancy attains large values.

If $d\mu\left(r\right)$ is the uniformly distributed measure in $\left\{0<r<1\right\}$, then the $L^{p}\left(L^{2}\right)$ mixed norm in Theorem 1.1 is 

$\left\{\int_{\mathbb{T}^{d}}\left(\dfrac{1}{H}{\displaystyle\int_{R}^{R+H}}\left|r^{-\left(d-1\right)/2}\mathcal{D}\left(r\Omega-x\right)\right|^{2}dr\right)^{p/2}dx\right\}^{1/p}.$ 

The Fourier transform of the uniformly distributed measure in $\left\{0<r<1\right\}$ has decay $\beta=1$, 

$\widehat{\mu}\left(\zeta\right)={\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}}\exp\left(-2\pi i\zeta r\right)dr=\exp\left(-\pi i\zeta\right)\dfrac{\sin\left(\pi\zeta\right)}{\pi\zeta}.$ 

On the other hand, if $\psi\left(r\right)$ is a non-negative smooth function with integral one and support in $0\leq r\leq 1$, one can consider a smoothed average 

$\left\{\int_{\mathbb{T}^{d}}\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}}\left|r^{-\left(d-1\right)/2}\mathcal{D}\left(r\Omega-x\right)\right|^{2}H^{-1}\psi\left(H^{-1}\left(r-R\right)\right)dr\right)^{p/2}dx\right\}^{1/p}.$ 

This smoothed average is equivalent to the uniform average over $\left\{R<r<R+H\right\}$, but the decay of the Fourier transform $\widehat{\psi}\left(\zeta\right)$ is faster than any power $\beta$. Hence, for the uniformly distributed measure in $\left\{0<r<1\right\}$, the theorem applies with the indexes corresponding to $\beta>1$: 

$\displaystyle\left\{\int_{\mathbb{T}^{d}}\left(\dfrac{1}{H}{\displaystyle\int_{R}^{R+H}}\left|r^{-\left(d-1\right)/2}\mathcal{D}\left(r\Omega-x\right)\right|^{2}dr\right)^{p/2}dx\right\}^{1/p}$ 

$\displaystyle\leq\left\{\begin{array}[c]{ll}Cp^{1/2}&\text{if }d=2\text{ and }p<+\infty\text{,}\\ 
C\log^{1/2}\left(1+R\right)&\text{if }d=2\text{ and }p=+\infty\text{,}\\ 
C\left(6-p\right)^{-1/3}&\text{if }d=3\text{ and }p<6\text{,}\\

Observe that the range of indexes in the above theorem and corollaries for which the mixed $L^{p}\left(L^{2}\right)$ norm remains uniformly bounded is larger than the range of indexes in [16] and [4] quoted in the previous example.
