Balancing the aggressiveness of proactively spawning new containers and queuing requests at existing ones is crucial for achieving high container utilization and low SLOs, hence this act hinges solely on the prediction model adopted. 

Key takeaway: Request batching can minimize containers spawned while avoiding SLO violations, but cannot hide cold-starts. SLO violations caused by cold starts can be avoided by provisioning containers in advance by predicting the future load, but reaping the benefits is contingent upon the accuracy of the prediction model used.

In Section 3, we highlighted the importance of identifying available slack and execution time at each stage to precisely calculate the number of requests that can be processed in a single container. To achieve this, we perform offline profiling to measure the runtimes of all microservices utilized in six widely adopted ML-based applications sourced from the Djinn&Tonic Hauswald et al. (2015a) benchmark suite, as described in Section 2.2.2. These measurements are then employed to construct an estimation model using linear regression, yielding an accurate Mean Execution Time (MET) for each service based on input size (as shown in Table 3). Notably, we exclude larger inputs that would breach our Service Level Objectives (SLOs). This model is integrated into Fifer as an offline component.

For slack estimation, we establish the SLO (response latency) at 1000ms, representing the maximum value of 5 × execution_time as per Gujarati et al. (2017) across all applications in our workloads. The slack for each application is derived by subtracting the application's total execution time from the fixed response latency. To allocate slack across individual stages, we consider two approaches: equal distribution (ED), where slack is uniformly apportioned, or proportionate allocation, where slack is distributed according to the execution time ratio of each stage. In Fifer, we adopt the proportionate method, as it has been demonstrated to enhance per-stage utilization compared to ED, as noted in Kannan et al. (2019).
This subsection presents the outcomes of our real-system implementation.

Function hosting containers are deployed on servers, which may be virtual machines (VMs) or physical, bare-metal servers. In Fifer, we adopt a scheduling strategy similar to that used for function scheduling, assigning containers to the server with the least available resources. Servers are indexed sequentially from 1 to n, where n represents the total number of available servers. The cluster scheduler is configured to allocate containers to the lowest-numbered server with the fewest available CPU cores that can meet the container's CPU and memory requirements. This approach ensures that unused cores consume power idly, and servers with all cores idle can be powered down after a period of inactivity. Consequently, this strategy may lead to energy savings in the cluster by effectively packing active containers onto fewer servers.

The consolidation process enhances cluster efficiency. Figure 13 illustrates the average cluster-wide energy consumption across all nodes, measured in 10-second intervals throughout the workload period. Fifer demonstrates a 30.75% improvement in energy efficiency compared to Bline under heavy workload conditions, attributed to its accurate container count estimation, enabling consolidation into fewer nodes. Furthermore, Fifer outperforms RScale by 17% in energy efficiency, as proactive provisioning minimizes reactively created containers and reduces idle CPU cores. While Fifer's energy efficiency is nearly equivalent to Sbatch (with a 4% difference), it offers the advantage of scaling dynamically to meet demand, thereby reducing SLO violations compared to Sbatch.
This subsection presents the outcomes of our real-system implementation.

To better understand the benefits of proactive provisioning, Figure 12(b) illustrates the total number of containers launched, measured over 10-second intervals, across all four resource managers (RMs). The results demonstrate that both RScale and Fifer effectively adapt to the request rate distribution, spawning up to 60% and 82% fewer containers on average compared to Bline RM due to their batching capabilities. Notably, Fifer outperforms RScale by 22% because it can more accurately estimate the required number of containers at each stage using LSTM predictions.

As demonstrated in Algorithm 1, the system employs a forecasting mechanism to estimate the number of incoming requests during each monitoring interval, leveraging historical arrival patterns. At each stage, if the existing container capacity is insufficient to manage the projected request volume, Fifer initiates the creation of additional containers in an anticipatory manner. This proactive approach complements the reactive scaling mechanism that operates at each stage. In scenarios where the prediction model successfully anticipates future load conditions, container provisioning occurs in advance, thereby preventing service-level objective (SLO) breaches. However, if the prediction model fails to accurately forecast the load, the reactive policy detects delays at the respective stages and triggers the instantiation of additional containers with cold starts to address the shortfall. The following section provides an in-depth explanation of the prediction model utilized in Fifer.
The number of cold-starts experienced by three distinct RMs during a 2-hour snapshot of both traces is depicted in Figure 16.

In Figure 8(a) and Figure 8(b), the percentage of SLO violations and the average number of containers spawned for five different RMs across three workload mixes are presented. Fifer exhibits the lowest average container spawns, excluding SBatch. SBatch's fixed container count leads to 15% more SLO violations compared to Fifer. Bline and BPred, being non-batching RMs, over-provision containers to minimize SLO violations, with BPred using 20% fewer containers than Bline. Fifer and RScale employ batching to reduce container spawns. While RScale's reactive scaling causes 10% more SLO violations than Bline, Fifer's proactive container provisioning avoids SLO violations. Thus, Fifer effectively combines batching (from RScale) and proactive scaling (from BPred) for optimal performance.

Figure 15 illustrates the percentage of service-level objective (SLO) violations and the normalized average number of containers spawned relative to Bline for the WITS trace across all three workloads. The WITS trace is characterized by abrupt peaks in arrival rates, as evidenced by a peak-to-median ratio of 5 (with a peak of 1200 req/s and a median of 240 req/s). These sudden surges result in significantly elevated tail latencies, as detailed in Table 6. Notably, Fifer achieves a reduction of up to 66% in tail latencies compared to Sbatch and RScale policies. The SLO violation rates, as depicted in Figure 15(a), are notably lower for all policies when compared to the Wiki trace, attributed to the reduced dynamism in arrival patterns. Despite this, Fifer consistently spawns fewer containers on average—7.7× and 2.7× less than Bpred and RScale, respectively, as shown in Figure 15(b). The comparatively smaller savings of Fifer over RScale in this context are due to the diminished necessity for reactive scaling when variations in arrival rates are less frequent. Importantly, Fifer maintains SLO compliance to the same extent (up to 98%) as Bline and Bpred RMs, mirroring performance observed in real-world systems.

The containers that host functions are deployed on servers, which can be either virtual machines (VMs) or bare-metal servers. In Fifer, following a similar approach to function scheduling, we employ a greedy strategy to schedule containers on the server with the least available resources. These servers are sequentially numbered from 1 to n, where n represents the total number of available servers. The cluster scheduler is configured to allocate containers to the lowest-numbered server that has the fewest available cores and can meet the CPU and memory requirements of the container. This approach ensures that unused cores only consume idle power, and servers whose cores are entirely idle can be powered down after a period of inactivity. Consequently, this strategy can lead to energy savings in the cluster by efficiently packing active containers onto fewer servers.

The key enhancements in Fifer stem from two main factors: (i) the reduction of queuing delays achieved through proactive container provisioning and (ii) the improvement in container utilization and energy efficiency resulting from stage-aware container batching. These advancements are discussed in detail below. The stage-specific results are presented for three stages of IPA application under a heavy workload mix, with similar outcomes observed for other applications as well.

Impact of Queuing: Figure 10(b) illustrates the queuing time distribution for a heavy workload mix. It is evident that Fifer experiences a high median queuing latency (50-400ms), indicating that more requests are being queued due to the exploitation of stage-specific slack. For the RScale scheme, the median queuing latency is slightly higher than Fifer (500ms), attributed to increased waiting times caused by reactive container spawning with cold starts. However, for both Bline and BPred RM, the latency distribution is inconsistent, as queuing latency varies depending on the number of over-provisioned containers.

Stage-Aware Batching and Scaleout: Figure 11 displays the stage-wise container distribution across all three stages. The execution time distribution for the stages was previously presented in Figure 3(a).

To analyze the latency characteristics during cold and warm starts, we conducted experiments using an ML image inference application implemented with the MxNet framework developed by Chen et al. (2015) on AWS Lambda (2020). Our study employed seven pre-trained ML models of varying sizes, each with distinct execution durations proportional to their model complexity. Figure 2 illustrates the breakdown of total execution time for both cold and warm starts, focusing on two key metrics: (i) the execution time reported by AWS Lambda for the inference task (exec_time), and (ii) the round-trip time (RTT) from query submission at the client to response receipt from AWS Lambda. Cold start latency refers specifically to the first invocation of a request, as no existing containers are available to process it. Warm start latency, on the other hand, is determined by averaging the latencies of 100 subsequent requests over a five-minute period. As shown in Figure 2(a), cold start overhead frequently surpasses the actual query execution time, particularly for larger models such as ResNet-200. In contrast, Figure 2(b) indicates that warm start latencies generally remain under 1500 ms, except for the largest models. These findings reveal that cold starts introduce an additional latency of approximately 2000 to 7500 ms beyond the inherent execution time of the function.

Fifer prioritizes minimizing response latency, which underscores the necessity of analyzing the system-level overheads resulting from its design decisions. The MongoDB database, operating as a centralized server on the head node, exhibits an average latency of 1.25ms for all read/write operations. The LSF scheduling policy demonstrates an average runtime of 0.35ms per scheduling decision. Meanwhile, LSTM request prediction, which operates outside the critical scheduling path as a background process, averages 2.5ms. Container instantiation times, inclusive of image retrieval, range between 2s and 9s, contingent on the image size.

Our prototype was evaluated on a Kubernetes cluster comprising 80 compute cores. A designated node served as the head node, with each node equipped as a Dell PowerEdge R740 server, utilizing Intel Cascade Lake Xeon CPUs. The hardware and software specifications for a single node are detailed in Table 2. Kubernetes was employed as the resource management platform. The MongoDB database (Chodorow, 2013) and a Python daemon were both hosted on the head node. Energy consumption was measured using an open-source version of Intel Power Gadget (pow, 2020), capturing energy usage across all sockets within a node.

The load generator was tested using various traces. First, a synthetic Poisson-based request arrival rate was applied, with an average arrival rate of λ=50. Second, real-world traces from Wiki (Urdaneta et al., 2009) and WITS (wit, 2013) were utilized, as illustrated in Figure 7. As shown in Figure 7(a), the WITS trace exhibited significant peak variations (average=300 req/s, peak=1200 req/s) compared to the Wiki trace. The Wiki trace (average=1500 req/s) displayed characteristics typical of machine learning inference workloads, such as recurring patterns tied to time of day and day of the week, whereas the WITS trace featured unpredictable load spikes, akin to those observed during peak shopping events like Black Friday. The simulation was scaled to accommodate a peak request rate equivalent to a 2500-core cluster, representing a 30-fold increase over the prototype cluster's capacity.

Figure 8(a) and 8(b) depict the percentage of SLO violations and the average number of containers deployed across five RMs under three workload scenarios. Notably, Fifer exhibits the lowest average container count, surpassing all schemes except SBatch, which avoids scaling containers with varying request loads. This approach, however, leads to a 15% higher SLO violation rate in SBatch compared to Fifer. The Bline and BPred RMs, due to their non-batching nature, over-provision containers, thereby minimizing SLO violations. BPred outperforms Bline by using 20% fewer containers on average through proactive provisioning. Conversely, both Fifer and RScale employ batching to reduce container usage. While RScale's reactive-scaling strategy results in a 10% higher SLO violation rate than Bline, Fifer's proactive container provisioning avoids SLO violations. In summary, Fifer effectively combines the strengths of batching (as utilized by RScale) and proactive scaling (as employed by BPred), achieving optimal performance.

The rise of public cloud platforms over the past decade has significantly increased the adoption of microservice-based applications, as highlighted by Gan et al. (2019). Major cloud-based enterprises, including Amazon Cloud (2011), Facebook (Sriraman et al., 2019), Twitter (Khaleq and Ra, 2018), and Netflix (Mauro, 2015), have leveraged the scalability and development ease offered by microservices, establishing them as a primary application model (Villamizar et al., 2016). For example, various Machine Learning (ML) applications, such as facial recognition (Bartlett et al., 2005), virtual systems (Hauswald et al., 2015b), and content recommendation (Hazelwood et al., 2018), are implemented as interconnected microservices, also referred to as microservice-chains (Zhou et al., 2018). These applications are directly user-facing (Wu et al., 2019) and require stringent service-level objectives (SLOs), typically under 1000 ms (Gujarati et al., 2017). Therefore, minimizing the end-to-end latency of a microservice-chain is crucial to ensuring a positive user experience. The SLOs for these microservices are influenced by two key factors: (i) resource provisioning latency and (ii) application execution time. Given that most microservices execute within a few milliseconds (Hauswald et al., 2015a), serverless functions (Lam, 2020) have emerged as a superior alternative to virtual machines (VMs), offering not only reduced resource-provisioning latencies but also eliminating the need for users to manage resources explicitly.

We have integrated Fifer into the Brigade serverless workflow framework (bri, 2020) and deployed it within a Kubernetes cluster. To demonstrate its superiority over existing frameworks, we conducted a thorough evaluation using diverse request arrival patterns, leveraging both synthetic and comprehensive real-world traces. Our experimental results, obtained from an 80-core cluster and large-scale simulations, reveal that Fifer reduces the average number of spawned containers by up to 80%. This leads to a fourfold improvement in container utilization and a 31% reduction in cluster-wide energy consumption compared to state-of-the-art non-batching reactive schedulers used in serverless platforms.

To effectively capture the temporal nature of request arrival scenarios in data centers, we utilize a Long Short Term Memory Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM) model, as introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997). LSTMs are recognized for their state-of-the-art performance across various application domains, such as stock market forecasting and language processing. For a periodic monitoring interval (T) of 10 seconds, Fifer samples the arrival rate in adjacent windows of size W_s (5 seconds) over the past 100 seconds. It tracks the maximum arrival rate within each window and computes the global maximum arrival rate. Based on this global arrival rate, the model predicts the number of containers as a maximum within a future window of size W_p. The interval (T) is set to 10 seconds, aligning with the average container start-up latency, which typically ranges from 1 to 10 seconds. The prediction window (W_p) is set to 10 minutes, as this duration provides sufficient insight into long-term trade-offs, while short-term load fluctuations are still captured within the 10-second interval.

Fifer reduces cold-starts by up to 7× and 3.5× compared to Bpred for the Wiki and Wits traces, respectively. While RScale also significantly reduces cold-starts compared to Bline and BPred, Fifer remains 3× better than RScale. This improvement is due to Fifer's ability to accurately spawn containers in advance, thereby avoiding many reactive cold-starts. It is also worth noting that the Wiki trace has a higher number of cold-starts because its average request rate is 5× higher than that of the Wits trace.

Based on PQ length, spawning a new container is considered unnecessary if the time required to service requests by queuing on existing containers is less than the cold-start delay. The function evaluates the trade-off between queuing delay (D_f) and cold start delay (C_d). For Stage S, the queuing delay threshold D_f is calculated using the total number of requests (L) that can be processed without violating SLOs and the total time (T_d) needed to satisfy all pending requests, as follows:

L = Σ Bsize_i, T_d = PQ_len × S_r, D_f = T_d / L

Here, S_r represents the per-stage response latency, and N is the number of containers in S. S_r is defined as the sum of the stage's allocated slack and execution time. If D_f exceeds C_d, the load balancer (LB) spawns additional containers (N_c) for each stage, which we refer to as the dynamic reactive scaling (RScale) policy.

Stage-aware Container Scaleout: Each stage of an application has asymmetrical running times (as shown in Figure 3(a)), meaning the number of containers required at each stage varies. The baseline resource manager (RM) is unaware of this asymmetry. In contrast, Fifer is inherently stage-aware due to its proportionate slack allocation policy, resulting in similar batch sizes for containers across stages despite differing execution times. Additionally, Fifer's load monitor (LM) estimates queuing delays for individual stages by continuously monitoring the load, enabling better stage-wise container scaling compared to uniform scaling.
Figure 16 illustrates the count of cold-start instances experienced by three distinct resource managers during a two-hour window derived from the collected data traces.

In our experiments, each container is configured with 4 vCPUs and 8 GB of memory, and all containers are launched with a burst capacity of 80% of the base CPU capacity for the first 10 seconds. We evaluate the performance of Fifer under varying loads, including a regular load with 150 concurrent requests and a bursty load of 750 concurrent requests. The results demonstrate Fifer's ability to effectively utilize container resources, reduce latency, and scale resources efficiently under different workloads.

In the IPA application, both Bline and BPred allocate more containers to Stage-1 (ASR), the longest-running stage and bottleneck. The RScale scheme allocates slightly more containers to Stage-1 (44%) and Stage-3 (35%) than Baseline due to its proportionate slack allocation policy, which distributes the load evenly across stages. Ideally, this distribution should align closely with Sbatch, but reactive scaling results in numerous idle containers. Conversely, Fifer allocates nearly equal percentages of containers to Stage-2 (38%) and Stage-3 (36%) because its proactive container scaling policy, combined with stage-aware batching, reduces aggressive reactive scaling. Fewer containers are allocated to Stage-2 (21%) since it is a short-running stage (less than 2% of total execution time), leading to early scale-in of idle containers. While aggressive batching can cause SLO violations (15% and 12% more than Bline for SBatch and RScale, respectively), Fifer maintains similar SLO violation rates as Bline, as the LSTM model adapts well to variations in arrival rates.

Our system employs a MongoDB database (as outlined by Chodorow, 2013) to store job-specific statistics, such as creation and completion times, and container-specific metrics, including last usage timestamps and batch sizes. These records are accessible to worker pods and load balancers for periodic querying. During an offline phase, for each function chain, the database is populated with (a) response latency, (b) the sequence of stages, (c) estimated execution durations, and (d) slack per stage—computed as detailed in Section 4.1. These values enable each stage’s containers to calculate their available free slots.

**Pod Container Selection:** At runtime, worker pods query the database to select a pod with the fewest available free slots for task scheduling. Upon selection, the task is added to the pod’s local queue, and its free slots are updated in the database. This process is repeated for each subsequent task within the job.

**Load Balancer:** We developed a Python daemon (1K lines of code) comprising a load monitor (LM) and a load predictor (LP). The LM periodically measures queuing delays at each stage's global queue and initiates additional container instances as needed (as described in Section 4.2). The LP employs an LSTM model for request load prediction, trained using Keras (Chollet, 2018) and TensorFlow (Abadi et al., 2016) over 100 epochs with a two-layer configuration, 32 neurons, and a batch size of 1. The daemon retrieves job arrival data from the database for model input, with prediction specifics outlined in Section 4.5.

**Node/Server Selection:** To optimize container distribution and minimize node usage, we modified Kubernetes’ MostRequestedPriority scheduling policy to prioritize nodes with the least available resources. In our experiments, each container was allocated 0.5 CPU-core and up to 1GB of memory, with all containers set to a CPU limit of 0.5. Node idle cores are determined by subtracting the sum of CPU shares for all allocated pods from the total cores in the node.

The benefits of proactive provisioning are illustrated in Figure 12(b), which shows the total number of containers spawned over 10-second intervals for all four resource managers (RMs). The results demonstrate that both RScale and Fifer effectively adapt to the request rate distribution, spawning on average 60% and 82% fewer containers, respectively, compared to Bline RM due to their batching mechanisms. Fifer outperforms RScale by an additional 22% because it leverages LSTM predictions to precisely estimate the required number of containers at each stage.

Many frameworks are designed solely to satisfy the individual service-level objectives (SLOs) of each function within a system. However, this approach often overlooks the overall end-to-end SLO of the entire function chain, leading to inefficient uniform scaling of containers across all stages. This results in over-provisioning of containers, requiring more machines to host idle ones and consequently increasing operational costs for service providers.

To enhance resource utilization, it is imperative to minimize the number of idle containers at any given time. This objective is contingent upon the scheduling policy implemented, specifically as outlined in reference 4. In the Fifer framework, we have devised a scheduling policy that directs each stage to submit requests to the container with the least remaining free slots, where the number of free slots is determined by the container's batch size. Additionally, we incorporate a 10-minute timeout mechanism to remove containers that have remained idle without servicing any requests during this period. By adopting a greedy strategy that selects containers with the fewest remaining free slots (as demonstrated in Algorithm 1d) for scheduling, we facilitate the timely reduction of lightly loaded containers, thereby optimizing resource allocation.

The advancements in Fifer are primarily attributed to two key factors: (i) the mitigation of queuing delays through the implementation of proactive container provisioning and (ii) the enhancement of container utilization and energy efficiency achieved by employing stage-aware container batching. These improvements are elaborated upon in the subsequent analysis. The results are presented in a stage-wise manner for three stages of IPA application under a heavy workload mix, with similar outcomes observed for other applications as well.  

**Impact of Queuing:** Figure 10(b) illustrates the queuing time distribution under a heavy workload mix. The median queuing latency for Fifer is notably high (ranging from 50-400 ms), reflecting an increased number of queued requests due to the exploitation of stage-specific slack. In contrast, the RScale scheme exhibits a slightly higher median queuing latency (500 ms) compared to Fifer, attributed to the prolonged waiting times caused by reactive container spawning with cold starts. Meanwhile, the queuing latency distribution for both Bline and BPred RM is inconsistent, as it varies depending on the number of over-provisioned containers.  

**Stage-Aware Batching and Scaleout:** Figure 11 provides a detailed visualization of the stage-wise container distribution across all three stages, complementing the previously demonstrated execution time distribution in Figure 3(a).

The integration of serverless functions presents unique challenges concerning scheduling and resource management (RM) for cloud providers like Simon and Joel (2020), particularly when handling extensive deployments of millisecond-scale function chains \footnote{3 Throughout this paper, we use the terms "microservice-chains" and "function chains" interchangeably. Each individual function within a chain is referred to as a "stage."}. While prior research, such as the work conducted by Sreekanti et al. (2020), has focused on developing RM frameworks that capitalize on the inherent asynchronicity, event-driven architecture, and scalability of serverless applications, significant inefficiencies persist in managing thousands of short-lived function chains. These inefficiencies manifest in suboptimal resource utilization and a failure to consistently meet service-level objectives (SLOs). Treating each function within a chain as an entirely independent entity exacerbates these issues. Through an analysis of current RM frameworks, we have identified three prima

factors contributing to these inefficiencies.

We present Aquatope, a QoS-and-uncertainty-aware resource scheduler for end-to-end serverless workflows that takes into account the inherent uncertainty present in FaaS platforms, and improves performance predictability and resource efficiency. Aquatope uses a set of scalable and validated Bayesian models to create pre-warmed containers ahead of function invocations, and to allocate appropriate resources at function granularity to meet a complex workflow’s end-to-end QoS, while minimizing resource cost. Across a diverse set of analytics and interactive multi-stage serverless workloads, Aquatope significantly outperforms prior systems, reducing QoS violations by $5\times$ , and cost by 34% on average and up to 52% compared to other QoS-meeting methods.

1. Introduction

Serverless computing is becoming increasingly popular, due to its ease of programming and maintenance, fast elasticity, and fine-grained billing. Serverless simplifies management for users, since its interface removes the need for users to explicitly configure virtual machines (VMs) or containers. Serverless also avoids overprovisioning, as users only pay for the resources they use during execution. For applications with high data-level parallelism and intermittent activity, serverless can achieve much higher performance for the same or lower cost.

Despite these benefits, serverless introduces several challenges, especially when a service has to meet quality of service (QoS) requirements in terms of execution time or tail latency (27) . A lot of prior work has focused on reducing the cold start overheads

, overheads associated with instantiating new containers or VMs, and installing necessary dependencies (68) . While impactful, cold starts are not the sole reason behind degraded performance in serverless. Another crucial issue the system has to tackle is appropriate function-level resource management. Without a proper resource configuration, the function can suffer from performance degradation and increased execution cost (70) . More importantly, these two problems are closely correlated with each other, as cold and warm starts lead to different function performance, and require significantly different resources. For the system to minimize cost, while satisfying QoS, we n

d to tackle both challenges jointly.

Data centers are experiencing a significant increase in the use of serverless functions for microservices-based applications. These microservices generally execute in under a second, have stringent service-level objective (SLO) requirements, and are often linked together to meet the needs of an application. These characteristics present new challenges, particularly concerning container provisioning and management. Current resource management frameworks in serverless platforms typically treat microservice-based applications similarly to traditional monolithic applications, leadin

to microservice-agnostic scheduling and excessive container over-provisioning, especially during workload variations. This results in suboptimal resource utilization.

The inefficiencies in function chains collectively create opportunities for stage-aware resource management by utilizing the "leftover slack" within these chains. By tapping into this slack, individual functions can be batched and queued within existing containers without compromising application-level Service Level Objectives (SLOs). This paper introduces Fifer, which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first work to implement stage-aware container provisioning and management for function chains in serverless environments. Fifer employs innovative slack estimation techniques for individual functions, enabling a significant reduction in the number of containers used, thereby enhancing resource utilization and cluster energy efficiency. While slack-based request batching can substantially decrease container usage, it may still result in SLO violations due to cold starts, particularly under dynamic load conditions. To address this, Fifer utilizes proactive container provisioning through load prediction models to mitigate SLO violations caused by cold starts. The key contributions of this paper are as follows:

We implement Aquatope on OpenWhisk (3) and evaluate it across a wide set of

nalytics and interactive multi-stage serverless applications, including ML pipelines, video processing frameworks, and social networks. In all cases, Aquatope outperforms prior empirical and ML-driven approaches in performance and efficiency, reducing QoS violations by $5\times$ compared to prior work, and execution cost by 34% on average and up to 52% compared to other QoS-meeting methods.

2. Background and Motivation

Many real-world serverless applications are implemented as multi-stage serverless workflow in which incoming user requests invoke sets of serverless functions that coordinate with each other to execute a serverless workflow. Existing platforms provide various composition mechanisms to control a workflow and transfer intermediate state across functions (4) . By splitting a complex application into dependent but loosely-coupled functions, the application benefits from fine-grained scalability, parallel execution, and modular development (70) . At the same time, decoupling a serverless application into multiple stages also introduces challenges in resource management, including additional function instantiation overheads, data transfer overheads, and varied

source needs across execution stages. Without an appropriate framework in place, multi-stage serverless workflows can experience QoS violations and resource inefficiency.

Aquatope specifically targets such serverless workflows that must meet pre-defined QoS constraints. Aquatope tackles two correlated aspects of serverless resource management: ensuring that function instantiation overheads are minimal so that tasks do not suffer from cold starts, and optimizing the resource configuration of each stage to minimize cost while satisfying QoS. While Aquatope is geared towards multi-stage serverless workloads, it can also be applied to simpler applications with a single stage.

Resource management for multi-stage serverless workflows faces the following challenges.

Cold starts: Cold starts are one of the most studied overheads associated with serverless (68) . A cold start invocation occurs when a serverless application is triggered, but its function instances are not

aaS platform, a cold start involves launching a new container (and/or a new VM), setting up its runtime environment, and fetching and loading necessary libraries and dependencies. This process can take a long time relative to the short-lived function execution (68) .

The paragraph begins with a concise introduction to serverless function-chains, followed by an in-depth examination of their operational efficiency, which highlights the rationale behind the development of Fifer.

Correlation of cold start and resource allocation: Cold starts not only affect the function startup latency but also exacerbate runtime performance degradation, as they can prevent a function invocation from reusing its execution context (9) , which caches global variables (e.g., SDK clients, database connections, ML models, etc.). In this case, the function is forced to execute the user-provided initialization code to download data dependencies and initialize runtime packages, etc. (35) . This leads to different runtime performance and resource requirements for warm and cold starts, with cold start function invocations requiring more resources to meet the same performance target than warm start invocations. Without eliminating cold starts, the resource manager is forced to strike a balance between the performance behaviors of cold and warm starts, leading to degraded performance and excess resources.

In function-chains composed of multiple serverless functions (as outlined in Section 2.1), individual containers are initiated for each stage. Current se

erless platforms employ the RM framework to consistently spawn containers at each stage, based on the request arrival rate. However, the execution durations of the functions at each stage vary.

All decisions pertaining to container scaling, scheduling, and load prediction hinge on a centralized database, which may act as a bottleneck in large-scale systems comprising thousands of nodes. This issue can be alleviated through database distribution across a subset of nodes or by employing advanced solutions such as Zookeeper, as outlined by Hunt et al. (2010). The LSTM model within Fifer is pre-trained using 60% of the arrival trace. While both Wiki and WITS accurately represent typical arrival rate scenarios in a datacenter, the model may not perform well for entirely unexpected arrival patterns. In such instances, continu

accuracy. These discussions are reserved for future research.

Cloud Provider Support: Fifer's cold-start measurements and characterizations are primarily based on AWS. However, its core design could theoretically be adapted to other major cloud platforms. The system assumes that serverless platform providers offer APIs enabling tenants to specify their application SLO requirements, which are essential for estimating resource slack. Such APIs would facilitate automatic configuration of tenants' execution environments, significantly enhancing resource efficiency, as highlighted by Dukic and Singla (2019).

Mitigating cold starts: Cold start overheads have been studied extensively, including pre-crafting virtual network interfaces (52) , restoring a function from a well-formed checkpoint image to skip initialization (33) , and prefetching a function’s working set of memory pages (67) . Most FaaS pro

ders keep container instances loaded in memory for a fixed amount of time after a function terminates (68) . AWS Lambda offers a provisioned concurrency (15) configuration to pre-load a fixed number of containers to accelerate function startup. FaaSCache (35) uses a caching-inspired container eviction policy to terminate containers when the server is saturated, but does not pre-warm containers. Shahrad et al. (62) proposed a histogram-based policy to adjust a container’s keep-alive time. Similarly, IceBreaker (59) uses a Fourier-transformation-based model to predict future invocation patterns, and pre-warms function containers accordingly. These techniques can mitigate cold starts, but are often not robust to fluctuating work

ads, and are designed for single-stage serverless applications, which are not prone to cascading cold starts across dependent functions. Aquatope is complementary to these proposals but focuses on reducing cold starts for realistic, multi-stage serverless applications.

Scheduling and Resource Management:  
$\bullet$ Private Cloud: A significant body of research by Ousterhout et al. (2013) has focused on ensuring QoS guarantees for latency-critical applications by developing sub-millisecond scheduling strategies using both distributed and hybrid schedulers. Certain studies by Thinakaran et al. (2019) utilize prediction-based scheduling in resource managers (RMs) to execute latency-critical tasks alongside batch tasks. However, these approaches do not address the efficient execution of applications involving microservice chains, as they treat microservices similarly to traditional monolithic applications.  
$\bullet$ Public Cloud: Several research efforts aim to optimize resource provisioning costs in public cloud environments. These initiatives generally fall into two categories: (i) adjusting auto-scaling policies based on dynamic d

ands (e.g., Spot, On-Demand), as explored by Gujarati et al. (2017), and (ii) predicting peak loads and implementing proactive auto-scaling policies, as in Gujarati et al. (2017). Fifer employs comparable load prediction models for auto-scaling containers, specifically for serverless function chains. Swayam, developed by Gujarati et al. (2017), is akin to our work in that it manages container provisioning alongside load balancing. However, unlike Fifer, which targets microservice chains, Swayam is tailored for single-function machine learning inference services.  

Exploiting Slack: Leveraging slack between tasks is a well-established technique appl

d across various scheduling domains, such as SSD controllers (Cui et al., 2018), memory controllers (Ghosh and Lee, 2007), and network-on-chip (Das et al., 2010). The novelty in our approach lies in identifying slack relevant to the problem domain and designing policies to exploit it effectively.  

Mitigating Cold-Starts: Recent studies by Boucher et al. (2018) propose optimizations to minimize container setup overheads. For instance, SOCK by Oakes et al. (2018) and SAND by Akkus et al. (2018) address language framework-level overheads and step function transition overheads, respectively. Some works advocate replacing containers with alternative virtualization techniques, such as Firecracker by Brooker et al. (2020) and uni-kernels by Zhang et al. (2018). Complementing these methods, Fifer decouples conta

er cold-starts from request execution time.

The widespread adoption of serverless functions for executing microservice-based applications in the cloud has introduced significant inefficiencies in scheduling and resource management for cloud providers, particularly when deploying a large volume of millisecond-scale, latency-critical functions. In this paper, we introduce and evaluate Fifer, an adaptive resource management framework designed to optimize the execution of function-chains on serverless platforms. Fifer ensures high container utilization and cluster efficiency while maintaining service-level objectives (SLOs). The framework leverages a novel combination of stage-wise slack awareness and proactive container allocation, powered by an LSTM-based load prediction model. It enables intelligent scaling and balancing of containers for each individual stage. Experimental results on an 80-compute-core cluster and large-scale simulations demonstrate that Fifer reduces container usage by up to 80% on average, achieving a 4× improvement in container utilization and a 31% boost in cluster efficiency.

2.1. Problem Statement

Many real-world serverless applications are implemented as multi-stage serverless workflow in which incoming user requests invoke sets of serverless functions that coordinate with each other to execute a serverless workflow. Existing platforms provide various composition mechanisms to control a workflow and transfer intermediate state across func

ons (4) . By splitting a complex application into dependent but loosely-coupled functions, the application benefits from fine-grained scalability, parallel execution, and modular development (70) . At the same time, decoupling a serverless application into multiple stages also introduces challenges in resource management, including additional function instantiation overheads,

ta transfer overheads, and varied resource needs across execution stages. Without an appropriate framework in place, multi-stage serverless workflows can experience QoS violations and resource inefficiency.

In addition to dynamically adjusting the number of containers required to accommodate requests at each stage, it is essential to develop a scheduling policy capable of selecting the most suitable request from each stage's queue. A critical consideration arises when a single developer hosts multiple application types, some of which may utilize shared functionalities (stages). While it is important to note that serverless platforms do not enable cross-tenant microservice sharing due to security and isolation concern

tages may still contain requests from various applications. Each application's available slack varies depending on its total execution time, and processing these requests in strict FIFO order could result in SLO breaches. To mitigate this, we implement the Least Slack First (LSF) scheduling policy, as demonstrated in Algorithm 1b. This approach ensures that Fifer prioritizes requests with the least available slack, thereby preventing SLO violations and avoiding request starvation. Although shared microservices are not th

primary focus of this work, it is worth mentioning that alternative scheduling policies tailored for sharing may present distinct trade-offs, which are not addressed here.

2.2. Challenges

Resource management for multi-stage serverless workflows faces the following challenges.

In serverless environments, function invocations during tenant application deployment occur within newly created containers, resulting in cold-start latency. While modern virtualization techniques, such as microVMs (Brooker et al., 2020), improve container start-up times, the majority of cold-start

prioritize executing functions in pre-existing warm containers (Wang et al., 2018). However, when all warm containers are in use, a new container must be created, typically taking several seconds. For applications with execution times measured in milliseconds, this delay is substantial, particularly in user-facing scenarios where maintaining responsive performance is essential.

Diverse resource requirements: Serverless functions vary in functionality and are implemented with different libraries and runtimes. Their resource requirements also vary a lot (61) , and without proper resource management, both performance and cost can suffer. Existing FaaS platforms, including AWS Lambda (5) , Google Cloud Functions (10) , and IBM Cloud Functions (12) require users to specify a memory limit for serverless functions, and allocate CPU resources proportional to the amount of provisioned memory, which can le

to CPU or memory overprovisioning.

Fifer demonstrates a significant reduction in cold-starts, achieving up to 7× and 3.5× improvements over Bpred for the Wiki and Wits traces, respectively. While RScale also notably reduces cold-starts compared to Bline and BPred, Fifer outperforms RScale by a factor of 3×. This superior performance is attributed to Fifer's ability to avoid reactive cold-starts by proactively spawning containers in advance. It is also worth noting that the Wiki trace exhibits a higher number of cold-starts due to its average request rate being 5× greater than that of the Wits trace.

We introduce the concept of slack, defined as the difference between execution time and overall response latency. Our approach, Fifer, levera

s this slack to compute the optimal batch size, determining the ideal number of requests to group at each stage. Fifer is inherently stage-aware, allocating slack proportionally to each function stage based on its execution time and independently setting scale-out thresholds for every stage.

Based on the ascending scale of total available slack across workloads (calculated as the average slack for both applications), we classify them into Heavy, Medium, and Light categories. By analyzing these three workload mixes, we thoroughly examine the extent of benefits Fifer provides under varying proportions of slack availability. The individual slack values for each application are detailed in Table 4. 

**Container Configuration:** All microservices listed in Table 3 are containerized as "pods" within Kubernetes. Each pod is configured with an imagePullPolicy that defaults to pulling container images from Docker Hub upon container initialization, mimicking the behavior of serverless functions where new instances are loaded from external storage for each container launch.

**Metrics and Resource Management Policies:** Our evaluation employs the following metrics: (i) frequency of SLO violations, (ii) mean number of containers instantiated, (iii) median and tail latencies (with tail latency defined

s the 99th percentile of request completion times across the workload), (iv) container utilization rates, and (v) energy savings across the cluster. These metrics are compared for Fifer against three resource managers (RMs): Bline, Sbatch, and BPred. In Sbatch, batch size is determined using an equal-slack-division approach, with container counts fixed according to average arrival rates derived from workload traces. BPred, built on Bline, incorporates LSF scheduling and EWMA prediction policies. This setup mirrors the scheduling and prediction policies used in Archipelago Singhvi et al. (2019), which do

not support request batching. To isolate the benefits of Fifer's components, we conduct a comparative analysis of Fifer in two configurations: (a) with dynamic scaling (RScale) alone, and (b) integrated with both RScale and proactive provisioning. Both variants utilize LSF job scheduling and a greedy container/node selection policy. Notably, Fifer with RScale aligns with the dynamic batching strate

n et al. (2019).

2.3. Related Work

To mitigate cold-start issues, certain frameworks employ a pre-warmed pool of idle containers, which, while effective, result in excessive memory consumption and energy inefficiency. For millisecond-scale applications, such as Squeezenet (Iandola et al., 2016) as illustrated in Figure 2, this inefficiency can be circumvented by routing requests through existing containers rather than launching new ones. This approach is viable when the delay from queuing is less than the delay caused by cold-starts. Consequently, the choice between queuing and initiating a new container hinges on the service-level objective (SLO), the application's execution time, and the container's cold-start latency. In contrast, Azure's RM frameworks typically queue incoming requests on a fixed number of containers

as outlined by Wang et al. (2018). However, this static approach, which does not account for application-specific characteristics, can lead to SLO violations, particularly for functions requiring stringent response times. 

Key takeaway: By considering SLOs, cold-start latencies, and application execution times, queuing functions can reduce the number of containers spawned while maintaining SLO compliance.

This research evaluates the aforementioned limitations by employing diverse workloads on a Kubernetes-managed multi-node cluster integrated with the Brigade serverless framework. To overcome these challenges, we introduce Fifer, an adaptive resource management framework designed to optimize the handling of function-chains on serverless platforms. The framework operates on two core principles: (i) enhancing utilization through efficient job allocation via function-aware container scaling and intelligent request batching, thereby minimizing the number of containers required, and (ii) ensuring compliance with service-level objectives by preemptively launching containers to mitigate cold-starts,

nsequently reducing response latencies. As a result, Fifer achieves a 4-fold improvement in container utilization and a 31% reduction in cluster-wide energy consumption compared to existing state-of-the-art schedulers, all while maintaining SLO adherence.

The response latency is decomposed into three components: execution time, cold-start delay, and batching delay, with the latter being specific to RScale and Fifer policies. Notably, the P99 latency for RScale and SBatch is observed to be up to three times higher than that of Bline and Bpred. This discrepancy arises due to the inability of aggressive batching and reactive scaling to effectively manage load fluctuations, resulting in request queue congestion. While Bpred exhibits a lower P99 latency than

hing 60% more containers. Conversely, the combination of aggressive batching and proactive provisioning in Fifer only results in a P99 latency that is twice as high as that of Bline and Bpred. Furthermore, Figure 9 illustrates that Fifer experiences significantly less cold-start delay compared to RScale. This is attributed to the reduced number of reactively scaled containers, which is a consequence of Fifer's accurate load forecasting capability.

3. Aquatope Design Overview

Aquatope is a QoS-and-uncertainty-aware resource scheduler for end-to-end, multi-stage serverless workflows. The design objective of Aquatope is to meet the user-defined QoS of a multi-stage serverless application, while using the minimum amount of resources. To this end, Aquatope j

ntly tackles two correlated challenges in serverless: (1) it maintains a dynamic pre-warmed container pool to minimize cold starts and ensure most of the function invocations are handled by warm containers, and (2) it employs a container resource manager that allocates appropriate resources to each function, based on its warm-start performance behavior.

Both components are implemented with Bayesian approaches to overcome the uncertainties present in FaaS platforms, including workload fluctuations, performance unpredictability, and interference from colocated applications. The dynamic pre-warmed container pool uses a hybrid Bayesian neural network (71) , which provides accurate and high-confidence predictions of function invocation rates, allowing Aquatope to

just the number of pre-warmed containers ahead of function invocations. The container resource manager then builds surrogate models to approximate the relationship between resource configurations and end-to-end performance and cost. The engine uses customized Bayesian optimization to efficiently explore the resource allocation space to find a configuration that meets the end-to-end QoS with minimal overprovisioning. It arrives at a suitable configuration by balancing exploration and exploitation, while adapting to noise in the cloud.

Our observations have motivated the development of a new RM framework, Fifer, which is designed to efficiently manage function-chains on serverless platforms. Figure 5 provides an overview of Fifer's architecture, with its key components detailed in the subsequent subsections, as indicated by the circled annotations.

4. Eliminating Cold Starts

Fifer employs a request queue to manage all incoming tasks for each stage. A load balancer (LB) and a load monitor (LM) are integrated into each stage to facilitate efficient container scaling for the application

xecution time and available slack, the LB calculates the batch size (B_size) for each stage using Equation 1. To accurately determine the required number of containers at each stage, which depends on B_size and queue length, we periodically measure the queuing delay caused by request batching. As outlined in Algorithm 1, at each stage, the LM monitors scheduled requests from the last 10 seconds to identify any SLO violations due to queuing delays. Such delays indicate insufficient container capacity to handle queued requests. In response, the Estimate_Containers function is used to determine the additional containers needed. This function estimates the number of containers (N_c) based on the batch size (B_size) and the number of pending requests in the queue (PQ_len), calculated as N_c = PQ_len / B_size.

Since multi-stage serverless applications are built with diverse runtimes and topologies, the optimal number of pre-warmed containers is application-specific. Aquatope uses a set of machine learning (ML) models to infer the total number of required containers for each active serverless application over the next time interval, and adjusts the number

different types of containers accordingly. While several models can be applied towards this purpose, Aquatope uses a hybrid Bayesian neural network to infer future invocation rates, which achieves high accuracy, fast inference, and agility to load fluctuations.

4.1. Time Series Prediction

The problem of predicting function invocation patterns can be formalized as follows: given a number of different types of active function containers for a serverless workflow in the past $t$ time windows $\{{x_{1}},{x_{2}},...,{x_{t}}\}$ , we nee

tern for the next time window $\{x_{t+1}\}$ . The time window size is configurable, and is set to 1 minute by default, which is the typical timescale for container keep-alive times in FaaS platforms (62) . External features, which are the time of day, time of week, and function trigger types (HTTP, object storage, event hub, etc.), also need to be integrated into the prediction model to improve accuracy. Aquatope also a

s in a multi-stage workflow, by predicting the invocation pattern of downstream containers in $x_{t+1}$ , when it sees their upstream containers invoked in $\{{x_{1}},{x_{2}},...,{x_{t}}\}$ . This captures both probabilistic and deterministic dependencies between execution stages, by predicting the expected and exact number of containers respectively. Since load fluctuates and invocation patterns may change, it is also important to incorporate uncertainty estimation to improve the robustness of the model, and to ensure that the scheduler makes reliable decisions and can recover from anomalies.

4.2. Hybrid Bayesian Neural Network Model

Classic timeseries prediction models (e.g., exponential smoothing, ARIMA models, Theta method) usually require manual tuning to configure the model and uncertainty parameters (46) . Moreover, it is difficult to incorporate external features into these models, which can be impactful to accuracy. Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) models (45) have also gained popularity in timeseries prediction. LSTM can capture long-term sequential dependencies in the data and outper

rm traditional methods (48) . However, conventional LSTM models cannot easily embed non-temporal external features or incorporate noise and uncertainty into their predictions, which is important for handling fluctuating workloads.

To overcome these problems and achieve generalizable and scalable prediction, we build a hybrid Bayesian neural network model. The novelty of our Bayesian model is twofold. First, it can utilize external features, such as time of day, to forecast function invocations. Second, it takes system noise into account when making predictions, allowing it to provide reliable uncertainty estimation, which is critical for fluctuating workloads. As shown in Fig. 2 , the model consists of two parts: (i) the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) encoder-decoder, which serves as a feature-detection blackbox that extracts a latent variable from the input timeseries; and (ii) the prediction network, which infers the invocation pattern in the next time window using the latent variable and external features. We use Monte Carlo (MC) dropout (36) to

pproximate Bayesian inference and quantify the prediction uncertainty.

We quantitatively validate the use of LSTM through a comparative analysis of non-ML and ML-based models, focusing on their performance on a specific input arrival trace. For non-ML approaches, we evaluate Moving Window Average (MWA), Exponential Weighted Moving Average (EWMA), Linear Regression (Linear R.), and Logistic Regression (Logistic R.), each fitted incrementally over the past t-100 seconds for every T. The ML models include a Simple Feed-Forward Network, WeaveNet, DeepAREstimator, and LSTM, all pre-trained on 60% of the WITS (2013) arrival trace. ML predictions are performed using a time-step approach as detailed in the preceding subsection. Figure 6(a) illustrates the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) and latency for the eight models, showing LSTM achieves the lowest RMSE. Figure 6(b) demonstrates LSTM's accuracy on the WITS trace, achieving 85% pr

iction accuracy over the 800-second test period.

Prediction network: After training the LSTM encoder-decoder, we use the LSTM encoder as an automatic feature-extraction blackbox. The last hidden state of the encoder is the latent variable $Z$ . Then, we train a prediction network to forecast the number of active containers ( $Y$ ) in the next time window, using $Z$ as features. To further increase the prediction accuracy, we concatenate the external feature vector ( $L$ ) with $Z$ , then feed it into the prediction network. We build the prediction network using a multi-layer perceptron, which consists of tanh activation functions and three fully connected layers. The model parameters of the LSTM and prediction network are selected based o

the validation accuracy.

Bayesian inference: Incorporating noise and uncertainty into the model is essential for accurate timeseries forecasting under fluctuating load. To enable this, we leverage approximate Bayesian inference. Due to its simplicity, generality and scalability, we use MC dropout (36) to approximate Bayesian neural networks and achieve epistemic uncertainty estimates, rather than training a deterministic model. We apply variational dropout to the encoder (37) , and regular dropout to the prediction network. By applying stochastic dropouts to each hidden layer of the encoder and prediction network, we can obtain the predictive mean and variance through $T$ forward passes using different samples of model weights ( $\{W_{t}\}^{T}_{t=1}$ ).

4.

s important to note that queuing and batching still cannot help in hiding the cold-start latencies encountered (shown in Figure 4) when spawning new containers. These are exacerbated especially when there is dynamism in request rate. While cold-start latencies can be reduced by OS-level optimizations Mohan et al. (2019), the only way to hide them entirely is by proactively spawning containers. Balancing the aggressiveness of proactively spawning new containers and queuing requests at existing ones is crucial for achievi

Os, hence this act hinges solely on the prediction model adopted. 

Key takeaway: Request batching can minimize containers spawned while avoiding SLO violations, but cannot hide cold-starts. SLO violations caused by cold starts can be avoided by provisioning containers in advance by predicting the future load, but reaping the benefits is contingent upon the accuracy of the prediction model used.

5. Optimizing Per-Function Resources

Cold starts are not the sole reason for performance degradation in FaaS platforms. It is also critical to ensure that the resources allocated to each function are appropriate. The pre-warmed container pool manager ensures that the majority of function invocations are handled by warm containers, which simplifies function-level resource

location, narrowing it down to only considering the warm-start performance behavior of serverless workflows.

In Section 3, we highlighted the importance of identifying available slack and execution time at each stage to precisely calculate the number of requests that can be processed in a single container. To achieve this, we perform offline profiling to measure the runtimes of all microservices utilized in six widely adopted ML-based applications sourced from the Djinn&Tonic Hauswald et al. (2015a) benchmark suite, as described in Section 2.2.2.

ese measurements are then employed to construct an estimation model using linear regression, yielding an accurate Mean Execution Time (MET) for each service based on input size (as shown in Table 3). Notably, we exclude larger inputs that would breach our Service Level Objectives (SLOs). This model is integrated into Fifer as an offline comp

ent.

For slack estimation, we establish the SLO (response latency) at 1000ms, representing the maximum value of 5 × execution_time as per Gujarati et

ur workloads. The slack for each application is derived by subtracting the application's total execution time from the fixed response latency. To allocate slack across individual stages, we consider two approaches: equal distribution (ED), where slack is uniformly apportioned, or proportionate allocation, where slack is distributed according to the execution time ratio of each stage. In Fifer, we adopt the proportionate method, as it has been demonstrated to enhance per-stage utilization compared to ED, as noted in Kannan et al. (2019).

Rather than relying on manually-derived analytical models

a-driven approach, to learn the mapping from resource configurations to performance and cost. BO has been effective in black-box resource optimization for long-running cloud workloads (54) , where application behaviors are not known to the cloud provider in advance. However, previous BO-based resource managers did not take noise and uncertainty into account, leading to increased search time and cost, and degraded performance. Aquatope’s container resource manager leverages an improved Bayesian Optimization (BO) approach that considers noise and uncertainty and is robust to biased ob

rvations and data outliers, resulting in fast convergence and lower search overheads. Aquatope also exploits the scalability of serverless workloads to accelerate exploration by enabling batch sampling, rather than using individual samples as in previous work.

This subsection presents the outcomes of our real-system implementation.

5.1. Bayesian Optimization Workflow

Function hosting containers are deployed on servers, which may be virtual machines (VMs) or physical, bare-metal servers. In Fifer, we adopt a scheduling strategy similar to that used for function scheduling, assigning containers to the server with the least available resources. Servers are indexed sequentially from 1 to n, where n represents the total

umber of available servers. The cluster scheduler is configured to allocate containers to the lowest-numbered server with the fewest available CPU cores that can meet the container's CPU and memory requirements. This approach ensures that unused cores consume power idly, and servers with all cores idle can be powered down after a period of inactivity. Consequently, this strategy may lead to energy savings in the cluster by effectively packing active containers onto fewer servers.

The consolidation process enhances cluster efficiency. Figure 13 illustrates the a

across all nodes, measured in 10-second intervals throughout the workload period. Fifer demonstrates a 30.75% improvement in energy efficiency compared to Bline under heavy workload conditions, attributed to its accurate container count estimation, enabling consolidation into fewer nodes. Furthermore, Fifer outperforms RScale by 17% in energy efficiency, as proactive provisioning minimizes reactively created containers and reduces idle CPU cores. While Fifer's energy efficiency is nearly equivalent to Sbatch (with a 4% difference), it offers the advantage of scaling

ynamically to meet demand, thereby reducing SLO violations compared to Sbatch.

This subsection presents the outcomes of our real-system implementation.

Bayesian optimization: BO relies on two key components. First, BO relies on a model that captures the relationship between input and objective function to drive the optimization process, a model commonly referred to as surrogate model in BO literature (63) . Second, BO leverages acquisition functions that d

d based on the predictions of the surrogate model. As shown in Fig. 3 , the algorithm proceeds iteratively and in each epoch, the surrogate model is updated with the data (resource configuration and corresponding performance metrics) sampled in the previous epoch, and the acquisition function leverages the updated surrogate model to determine the next data point (candidate resource configuration) to be sampled in the current epoch.

5.2. Challenges for Conventional BO

Conventional BO-based resource managers can suffer from increased search time and cost, and degraded performance due to the following cha

enges:

• Cloud noise: Previous BO-based resource managers assume a noiseless setting (54) (58) (63) . However, the cloud is a noisy environment. For example, resource interference and workload fluctuation, can exacerbate performance unpredictability, and result in biased observations of workload performance. Serverless applications can also suffer from interference, leading to misleading observations (outliers) in BO’s sampling process. In this case, the na

e BO workflow would suffer from model misspecifications caused by outliers, and the GP models would fail to characterize the performance of the workflow. • QoS constraint: Adding black-box inequality constraints like QoS constraints to BO is challenging (63) . Prior BO-based resource managers (54) rely on manually crafted objective functions with a penalty term that is triggered upon QoS violation, to guide the sampling process. However, manually crafted objective functions lack the flexibility to capture the behavior of complex serverless workflows and can lead to slow convergence and performance degradation. • Batch sampling: Conventional BO samples and evaluates one configuration at a time (63) , limiting the speed of convergence. For serverless applications, if we take advantage of the scalability of serverless by sampling multiple configurations at a time, the exploration can be greatly accelerated, improving the resource s

ings.

5.3. Customized Bayesian Optimization

To better understand the benefits of proactive provisioning, Figure 12(b) illustrates the total number of containers launched, measured over 10-second intervals, across all four resource managers (RMs). The results demonstrate that both RScale and Fifer effectively adapt to the request rate distribution, spawning up to 60% and 82% fewer containers on average compared to Bline RM due to their batching capabilities. Notably, Fifer outperforms RScale by 22% because it can more accurately estimate the required number of containers at each stage using LSTM predictions.

Customized surrogate models: Aquatope uses

ussian process (GP) (56) as the surrogate model. GP is a suitable surrogate model for resource exploration for several reasons. GP is non-parametric and does not make any assumptions over the target black-box function and is thus flexible enough to capture the relationship between resources and performance. GP is also computationally tractable and can be evaluated and updated cheaply and often (24) . Finally, GP can provide a measure of uncertainty for the predictions of unsampled data points and naturally captures Gaussian noise. Specifically, Aquatope uses fixed-noise GP models with Matérn(5/2) as the covariance kernel (56) to model the Gaussian noise.

More importantly, instead of combining t

cost and performance targets with a manually crafted objective function (54) and building a single GP model for it, Aquatope builds independent GP models for the cost target $f$ and the QoS constraint $\ell$ . The intuition for separating the two is to allow the GP models to converge faster and more accurately. The cost GP model captures the cost reduction for an unsampled resource configuration, and the performance GP model narrows down the search space, by discerning the regions more likely to be feasible (i.e., satisfy QoS).

As demonstrated in Algorithm 1, the system employs a forecasting mechanism to estimate the nu

er of incoming requests during each monitoring interval, leveraging historical arrival patterns. At each stage, if the existing container capacity is insufficient to manage the projected request volume, Fifer initiates the creation of additional containers in an anticipatory manner. This proactive approach complements the reactive scaling mechanism that operates at each stage. In scenarios where the prediction model successfully anticipates future load conditions, container provisioning occurs in advance, thereby preventing service-level objective (SLO) breaches. However, if the prediction model fails to accurately forecast the load, the reactive policy detects delays at the respective stages and triggers the instanti

ion of additional containers with cold starts to address the shortfall. The following section provides an in-depth explanation of the prediction model utilized in Fifer.

We use the method in (41) to multiply NEI of reducing cost with the probability of satisfying QoS, which is derived from the performance GP model, to obtain the constrained NEI. The constrained NEI helps Aquatope to focus on the feasible configuration space, where QoS can be met. QMC provides an approximation of constrained NEI and its gradient, which do not have analytic expressions, and enables batch optimization by iteratively maximizing NEI integrated over pending unobserved samples. We use a batch size of 3, which speeds up the search without sacrificing quality.

The number of cold-starts experienced by three distinct RMs during a 2-hour snapshot of both traces is depicted in Figure 16.

In Figure 8(a) and Figure 8(b), the percentage of SLO violations and the average number of containers spawned for five different

s across three workload mixes are presented. Fifer exhibits the lowest average container spawns, excluding SBatch. SBatch's fixed container count leads to 15% more SLO violations compared to Fifer. Bline and BPred, being non-batching RMs, over-provision containers to minimize SLO violations, with BPred using 20% fewer containers than Bline. Fifer and RScale employ batching to reduce container spawns. While RScale's reactive scaling causes 10% more SLO violations than Bline, Fifer's proactive container provisioning avoids SLO violations. Thus, Fifer effectively combines batching (from RScale) and proactive scaling (from BPred) for optimal performance

of 5 (with a peak of 1200 req/s and a median of 240 req/s). These sudden surges result in significantly elevated tail latencies, as detailed in Table 6. Notably, Fifer achieves a reduction of up to 66% in tail latencies compared to Sbatch and RScale policies. Despite this, Fifer consistently spawns fewer containers on average—7.7× and 2.7× less than Bpred and RScale, respectively, as shown in Figure 15(b).
