Incremental retraining: The ano

widely-used open-source FaaS platform that powers IBM’s Cloud Functions (12) . Fig. 5 shows Aquatope’s implementation.

OpenWhisk architecture: The API gateway of OpenWhisk is implemented with NGINX (17) . The backend of OpenWhisk consists of controllers and invokers that scale horizontally, with one invoker deployed per worker server. Function invocations are forwarded to a controller, which chooses an invoker to execute the invocation by considering invoker capacity and execution history. The invocation is sent to the invoker through a message channel implemented with Kafka (2) . Function implementations, invocation histories, execution results, and statistics are stored in CouchDB (1) .

The containers that host functions are deployed on servers, which can be either virtual machines (VMs) or bare-metal

ervers. In Fifer, following a similar approach to function scheduling, we employ a greedy strategy to schedule containers on the server with the least available resources. These servers are sequentially numbered from 1 to n, where n represents the total number of available servers. The cluster scheduler is configured to allocate containers to the lowest-numbered server that has the fewest available cores and can meet the CPU and memory requirements of the container. This approach ensures that unused cores only consume idle power, and servers whose cores are entirely idle can be powered down after a period of inactivity. Consequently, this strategy can lead to energy savings in the cluster by efficiently packing active containers onto fewer servers.

Dynamic pre-warmed container pool: Similar to AWS Lambda’s provisioned concurrency (15) , OpenWhisk’s invoker maintains a pool of pre-warmed containers ( stem cell ) for heavily-used functions. By default, the configuration of the pre-warmed container pool is static and pre-defined, and all worker servers share the same configuration. W

modify the controller and invoker to support dynamic adjustment of the pre-warmed container pool, making it worker-server specific, and configured via the controller for all managed invokers (or via the invoker directly). The load balancer in the controller is aware of the pre-warmed containers and routes function invocation requests to the supporting invokers accordingly.

The key enhancements in Fifer stem from two main factors: (i) the reduction of queuing delays achieved through proactive container provisioning and (ii) the improvement in container utilization and energy efficiency resulting from stage-aware container batching. These advancements are discussed in detail below. The stage-specific results are presented for three stages of IPA application under a heavy workload mix, with similar outcomes observed for other applications as well.

Impact of Queuing: Figure 10(b) illustrates the queuing time distribution for a heavy workload mix. It is evident that Fifer experiences a high median queuing latency (50-400ms), indicating that more requests are being

exploitation of stage-specific slack. For the RScale scheme, the median queuing latency is slightly higher than Fifer (500ms), attributed to increased waiting times caused by reactive container spawning with cold starts. However, for both Bline and BPred RM, the latency distribution is inconsistent, as queuing latency varies depending on the number of over-provisioned cont

ners.

Stage-Aware Batching and Scaleout: Figure 11 displays the stage-wise container distribution across all three stages. The execution time distribution for the stages was previously presented in Figure 3(a).

Container resource manager: Aquatope aims to find a near-optimal configuration for a serverless application. When a new application is registered, Aquatope obtains its metadata and QoS from CouchDB, and starts the optimization process. The GP models are implemented using GPyTorch (42) and the optimization workflow is implemented in BoTorch (23) . The engine samples the candidate resource configurations on the worker servers. The execution results and performance metrics are fetched from CouchDB. After selecting a near-optimal configuration, the engine sends messages to the controller to update the configuration of the application.

7. Me

odology

Generic function workflows: We first implement several generic function workflows using the Apache OpenWhisk Composer (4) , to combine multiple synthetic serverless functions into m

ti-stage workflows. We create a function generator to synthesize configurable resource-intensive functions that emulate varying CPU and memory workloads. We generate two workflows which are often present in multi-stage workflows: Chain and Fan-out/Fan-in. In Chain, a sequence of functions executes in a specific order. The output of one function is fed to its downstream function. In Fan-in/Fan-out, the workflow executes multiple functions in parallel, and only returns when the last child completes after some aggregation.

To analyze the latency characteristics during cold and warm starts, we conducted experiments using an ML image inference application implemented with the MxNet framework developed by Chen et al. (2015) on AWS Lambda (2020). Our study emp

yed seven pre-trained ML models of varying sizes, each with distinct execution durations proportional to their model complexity. Figure 2 illustrates the breakdown of total execution time for both cold and warm starts, focusing on two key metrics: (i) the execution time reported by AWS Lambda for the inference task (exec_time), and (ii) the round-trip time (RTT) from query submission at the client to response receipt from AWS Lambda. Cold start latency refers specifically to the first invocation of a re

est, as no existing containers are available to process it. Warm start latency, on the other hand, is determined by averaging the latencies of 100 subsequent requests over a five-minute period. As shown in Figure 2(a), cold start overhead frequently surpasses the actual query execution time, particularly for larger models such as ResNet-200. In contrast, Figure 2(b) indicates that warm start latencies generally remain under 1500 ms, except for the largest models. These findings reveal that cold starts introduce an additional latency of

pproximately 2000 to 7500 ms beyond the inherent execution time of the function.

Fifer prioritizes minimizing response latency, which underscores the necessity of analyzing the system-level overheads resulting from its design decisions. The MongoDB database, operating as a centralized server on the head node, exhibits an

erage latency of 1.25ms for all read/write operations. The LSF scheduling policy demonstrates an average runtime of 0.35ms per scheduling decision. Meanwhile, LSTM request prediction, which operates outside the critical scheduling path as a background process, averages 2.5ms. Container instantiation times, inclusive of image retrieval, range between 2s and 9s, contingent on the image size.

Social network: We use a serverless implementation of the broadcast-style Social Network in DeathStarBench (39) . Fig. 8 shows the architecture of the serverless implementation of the service. Users can create posts embedded with text, media, links, and tags, which are then broadcast to all their followers. The texts and images uploaded by users go through the text-filter and image-filter functions. Contents violating the service’s ethical guidelines are rejected. Users can also rea

lines. The backend uses Memcached and Redis for caching, and MongoDB for persistent storage. We use the socfb-Reed98 Facebook network dataset (57) as the social graph, with 962 users and 18.8K follow relationships.

We use the Locust (14) load generator to emulate real user traffic. We generate custom-shaped loads based on scaled-down invocation pattern traces from the Azure Function Dataset (62) . Since the Azure dataset does not contain traces of Azure Durable Functi

s (the workflow engine for composing function logic) (7) , we use the function invocation traces to emulate workflow invocation patterns. Within each one-minute interval provided in the trace, we use a Poisson process to generate workflow invocation traffic with an exponential distribution of inter-arrival times. We scale the invocation rate proportionally so that the maximum CPU utilization in the cluster does not exceed 70%, which is in accordance with the CPU utilization in the Google and Alibaba production clusters (64) , and the Azure Function cluster (72) . Thi

workload generation method is consistent with the methodology in (62) . The load generators and functions are never physically co-located on a server.

Our prototype was evaluated on a Kubernetes cluster comprising 80 compute cores. A designated node served as the head node, with each node equipped as a Dell PowerEdge R740 server, utilizing Intel Cascade Lake Xeon CPUs. The hardware and software specifications for a single node are detailed in Table 2. Kubernetes was employed as the resource management platform. The MongoDB database (Chodorow, 2013) and a Python daemon were both hosted on the head node. Energy consumption was measured using an open-source version of Intel Power Gadget (pow, 2020), capturing energy usage across all sockets within a node.

The load generator was tested using various traces. First, a synthetic Poisson-based request arrival rate was applied, with an average arrival rate of λ=50. Second, real-world traces from Wiki (Urdaneta et al., 2009) and WITS (wit, 2013) were utilized, a

illustrated in Figure 7. As shown in Figure 7(a), the WITS trace exhibited significant peak variations (average=300 req/s, peak=1200 req/s) compared to the Wiki trace. The Wiki trace (average=1500 req/s) displayed characteristics typical of machine learning inference workloads, such as recurring patterns tied to time of day and day of the week, whereas the WITS trace featured unpredictable load spikes, akin to those observed during peak shopping events like Black Friday. The simulation was scaled to accommodate a peak request rate equivalent to a 2500-core cluster, representing a 30-fold increase over the prototype cluster's capacity.

yed across five RMs under three workload scenarios. Notably, Fifer exhibits the lowest average container count, surpassing all schemes except SBatch, which avoids scaling containers with varying request loads. s SLO violations. In summary, Fifer effectively combines the strengths of batching (as utilized by RScale) and proactive scaling (as employed by BPred), achieving optimal performance.

7.1. Applications

Generic function workflows: We first implement several generic function workflows using the Apache OpenWhisk Composer (4) , to combine multiple synthetic

erverless functions into multi-stage workflows. We create a function generator to synthesize configurable resource-intensive functions that emulate varying CPU and memory workloads. We generate two workflows which are often present in multi-stage workflows: Chain and Fan-out/Fan-in. In Chain, a sequence of functi

specific order. The output of one function is fed to its downstream function. In Fan-in/Fan-out, the workflow executes multiple functions in parallel, and only returns when the last child completes after some aggregation.

ML pipeline: We implement an ML pipeline which serves as the backend of a parking lot security system, trying to recognize humans and vehicles. Fig. 6 shows the architecture of the ML pipeline. The image recorded by the parking lot security camera is uploaded to the object store and triggers the ML pipeline. The pipeline performs object detection (43) and the labeled images are uploaded to the object store, with vehicle and human recognition being invoked in parallel.

Video processing framework: We implement a serverless video processing framework similar to Sprocket (20) . The input video URLs are fetched and decoded into fixed-length frames. Then different function pipelines

ssed in parallel. Fig. 7 shows the framework’s architecture. We use MinIO (16) as the ephemeral storage for the video frames for each stage.

The rise of public cloud platforms over the past decade has significantly increased the adoption of microservice-based applications, as highlighted by Gan et al. (2019). Major cloud-based ent
prises, including Amazon Cloud (2011), Facebook (Sriraman et al., 2019), Twitter (Khaleq and Ra, 2018), and Netflix (Mauro, 2015), have leveraged the scalability and

velopment ease offered by microservices, establishing them as a primary application model (Villamizar et al., 2016). For example, various Machine Learning (ML) applications, such as facial recognition (Bartlett et al.,

05), virtual systems (Hauswald et al., 2015b), and content recommendation (Hazelwood et al., 2018), are implemented as interconnected microservices, also referred to as microservice-chains (Zhou et al., 2018). These applications are direct

user-facing (Wu et al., 2019) and require stringent service-level objectives (SLOs), typically under 1000 ms (Gujarati et al., 2017). Therefore, minimizing the end-to-end latency of a microservice-chain is crucial to ensuring a positive user experience. The SLOs for these microservices are influenced by two key factors: (i) resource provisioning latency and (ii) application execution time. Given that most microservices execute within a few milliseconds (Hauswald et al., 2015a), serverless functions (Lam, 2020) have emerged as a superior alternative to virtual machines (VMs), offering not only reduced resource-provisioning latencies but also eliminating the need for users to manage resources explicitly.

7.2. Workload Generation

We use the Locust (14) load generator to emulate real user traffic. We generate custom-shaped loads based on scaled-down invocation pattern traces from the Azure Function Dataset (62) . Since the Azure dataset does not contain traces of Azure Durable Functions (the workflow engine for composing function logic) (7) , we use the function invocation traces to emulate workflow invocation patterns. Within each one-minute interval provided in the trace, we use a Poisson process to generate workflow invocation traffic with an exponential distribution of inter-arrival times. We scale the invocation rate proportionally so that the maximum CPU utilization in the cluster does not exceed 70%, which is in accordance with the CPU utilization in the Google and Alibaba production clusters (64) , and the Azure Function cluster (72) . This workload generation method is consistent with the methodology in (62) . The load generators and functions are never physically co-located on a server.

7.3. Server Cluster

We have integrated Fifer into the Brigade serverless workflow framework (bri, 2020) and deployed it within a Kubernetes cluster. To demonstrate its superiority over existing frameworks, we conducted a thorough evalu

ion using diverse request arrival patterns, leveraging both synthetic and comprehensive real-world traces. Our experimental results, obtained from an 80-core cluster and large-scale simulations, reveal that Fifer reduces the average number of spawned containers by up to 80%. This leads to a fourfold improvement in container utilization and a 31% reduction in cluster-wide energy consumption compared to state-o

the-art non-batching reactive schedulers used in serverless platforms.

7.4. Comparison Baselines

We compare Aquatope with multiple strategies that mitigate cold starts and optimize resource allocations. In terms of reducing cold starts, we compare against (1) the fixed keep-alive policy used by most FaaS providers (5) ; (2) Apache OpenWhisk’s reactive-stem-cell policy (11) , which enables autoscaling for pre-warmed containers; (3) FaaSCache’s container eviction and dynamic auto-scaling policy (35) ; (4) histogram-based container keep-alive policy in (62) , which uses historical function inter-arrival time to dynamically adjust the keep-alive time; (5) Icebreaker (59) , which uses Fourier Transformation to predict and pre-warm function containers based on historical invocation patterns.

For resource management

we compare Aquatope with (a) autoscaling techniques (60) , that dynamically adjust a container’s CPU and memory to match the function’s latency requirements; (b) a random-search-based tuning system (44) ; and (c) CLITE (54) , which uses a BO-driven approach to search for a near-optimal resource configuration that minimizes the cost while satisfying QoS. We modify CLITE’s score function to make it applicable to multi-stage serverless applications. Details are provided in Section 8.2 .

8. Evaluation

We first evaluate Aquatope’s two key components ( dynamic pre-warmed container pool and container resource manager) separately, and then perform an end-to-end evaluation that includes both components.

8.1.

ynamic Pre-warmed Container Pool

To effectively capture the temporal nature of request arrival scenarios in data centers, we utilize a Long Short Term Memory Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM) model, as introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997). LSTMs are recognized for their state-of-the-art performance across v

ious application domains, such as stock market forecasting and language processing. For a periodic monitoring interval (T) of 10 seconds, Fifer samples the arrival rate in adjacent windows of size W_s (5 seconds) over the past 100 seconds. It tracks the maximum arrival rate within each window and computes the global maximum arrival rate. Based on this global arrival rate, the model predicts the number of containers as a maximum within a future window of size W_p. The interval (T) is set to 10 seconds, aligning with the average container start-up latency, which typically ranges from 1 to 10 seconds. The prediction window (W_p) is set to 10 minutes, as this duration provides sufficient insight into long-term trade-offs, while short-term load fluctuations are still captured within the 10-second interval.

Table 1 shows the Symmetric Mean Absolute Percentage Error (SMAPE) of the four models across all workflows in terms o

s, a widely used metric in time series prediction (74) . Aquatope’s hybrid model significantly outperforms all other alternatives, with a 40% reduction in prediction error compared to the second best model, the vanilla LSTM. Our proposed Bayesian NN outperforms fixed Keep-Alive and ARIMA because these simple analytical models do not fully capture the dynamic invocation pattern, and it outperforms the vanilla LSTM because it uses information-rich external features as input, and also takes into account cloud noise and uncertainty when making predictions.

Fifer reduces cold-starts by up to 7× and 3.5× compared to Bpred for the Wiki and Wits traces, respectively. While RScale also significantly reduces cold-starts c

pared to Bline and BPred, Fifer remains 3× better than RScale. This improvement is due to Fifer's ability to accurately spawn containers in advance, thereby avoiding many reactive cold-starts. It is also worth noting that the Wiki trace has a higher number of cold-starts because its average request rate is 5× higher than that of the Wits trace.

Based on PQ length, spawning a new container is considered unnecessary if the time required to service requests by queuing on existing containers is less than the cold-start delay. The function evaluates the trade-off between queuing delay (D_f) and cold start delay (C_d). For Stage S, the queuing delay threshold D_f is calculated using the total number of requests (L) that can be processed without violating SLOs and the total time (T_d) needed to satisfy all pending requests, as follows:

L = Σ Bsize_i, T_d = PQ_len × S_r, D_f = T_d / L

Here, S_r represents the per-stage response latency, and N is the number of containers in S. S_r is defined as the sum of the stage's allocated slack and execution time. If D_f exceeds C_d, the load balancer (LB) spawns additional containers (N_c) for each stage, which we refer to as the dynamic reactive scaling (RScale) policy.

Stage-aware Container Scaleout: Each stage of an application has asymmetrical running times (as shown in

igure 3(a)), meaning the number of containers required at each stage varies. The baseline resource manager (RM) is unaware of this asymmetry. In contrast, Fifer is inherently stage-aware due to its proportionate slack allocation policy, resulting in similar batch sizes for containers across stages despite differing execution times. Additionally, Fifer's load monitor (LM) estimates queuing delays for individual stages by continuously monitoring the load, enabling better stage-wise container scaling compared to uniform scaling.

Figure 16 illustrates the count of cold-start instances experienced by three distinct resource managers during a two-hour window derived from the collected data traces.

Reducing over-provisioned memory: Although pre-warming containers reduces cold starts, holding containers in memory for too long wastes resources. Fig. LABEL:subfig:eval_wasted_memory shows the relative aggregate provisioned memory time for each approach. We use the same resource configuration for serverless containers across all approaches for a fair comparison.

 In our experiments, each container is configured with 4 vCPUs and 8 GB of memory, and all containers are launched with a burst capacity of 80% of the base CPU capacity

or the first 10 seconds. We evaluate the performance of Fifer under varying loads, including a regular load with 150 concurrent requests and a bursty load of 750 concurrent requests. The results demonstrate Fifer's ability to effectively utilize container resources, reduce latency, and scale resources efficiently under different workloads.

Handling fluctuating load: Aquatope’s dynamic pre-warmed container pool is designed to be noise-aware, making it robust to fluctuating workloads. For the Azure dataset we use, we look at the benefits of Aquatope compared to the best-performing previous work, IceBreaker (59) , for loads with different coefficients of variation (CV) (standard deviation divided by the mean), as shown in Fig. 10 . CV greater than 1 indicates significant variability in inter-arrival time (62) . For traces with CVs close to 0, Aquatope yields marginal improvement over IceBreaker. For traces with CV=1 to 4, Aquatope reduces 13%–41% more cold

tarts than IceBreaker, demonstrating the effectiveness of Aquatope’s noise-aware approach. In the Azure dataset, more than 40% of invocation traces have CVs greater than 2, which highlights the high variability present in FaaS environments.

To further demonstrate the benefits of incorporating noise and uncertainty into the Bayesian prediction model, we also compare Aquatope with a simplified implementation without the uncertainty

stimation of Sec. 4.2 , referred to as AquaLite. The results are shown in Fig. 11 , which shows the aggregate container memory provisioned by AquaLite and Aquatope over time, under a fluctuating load. Thanks to the uncertainty estimation, Aquatope is robust to fluctuating workloads and adjusts the pre-warmed container pool more accurately than AquaLite, reducing 3% more cold starts and saving 8% more provisioned memory.

Overhead: Aquatope’s container pool scheduler makes adjustment to pre-warmed containers asynchronously, off the critical path, and does not impact the latency of function invocations. Training the hybrid model with a week’s trace from Azure Function Dataset (62) takes 50s, which can easily accommodate retraining if needed. The latency of the prediction is below 10ms, which is marginal compared to the adjustment interval of the container pool.

8.2. Container Resource Manager

In the IPA application, both Bline and BPred allocate more containers to Stage-1 (ASR), the longest-running stage a

bottleneck. The RScale scheme allocates slightly more containers to Stage-1 (44%) and Stage-3 (35%) than Baseline due to its proportionate slack allocation policy, which distributes the load evenly across stages. Ideally, this distribution should align closely with Sbatch, but reactive scaling results in numerous idle containers. Conversely, Fifer allocates nearly equal percentages of containers to Stage-2 (38%) and Stage-3 (36%) because its proactive container scaling policy, combined with stage-aware batching, reduces aggressive reactive scaling. Fewer containers are

located to Stage-2 (21%) since it is a short-running stage (less than 2% of total execution time), leading to early scale-in of idle containers. While aggressive batching can cause SLO violations (15% and 12% more than Bline for SBatch and RScale, respectively), Fifer maintains similar SLO violation rates as Bline, as the LSTM model adapts well to variations in arrival rates.

Fig. 13 shows the mean aggregated CPU and memory time of different serverless workflows, under different resource managers. Experi

nts are repeated 30 times, to account for system noise. For random search, we take the best of all 30 trials for evaluation, because each trial does not always find a QoS-satisfying configuration, consistent with how random search is used in prior work (19) , and all the other resource managers successfully meet QoS. Under the same time budget for resource exploration, Aquatope outperforms all other approaches across examined applications, and significantly reduces CPU and memory time. On average, Aquatope finds a near-optimal configuration with cost within 5% of the optimal configuration obtained by ORACLE, which exhaustively searches the entire allocation space. As shown in Fig. 13 , Aquatope is not only capable of managing resources for simple applications (e.g., Chain), but can also find near-optimal configurations for co

l Network), whose functions vary widely in resource needs. Aquatope outperforms the second best resource managers, using 25%–62% less CPUs and 18%–51% less memory.

Our system employs a MongoDB database (as outlined by Chodorow, 2013) to store job-specific statistics, such as creation and completion times, and container-specific metrics, including last usage timestamps and batch sizes. These records are accessible to worker pods and load balancers for periodic querying. During an offline phase, for each function chain, the database is populated with (a) response latency, (b) the sequence of stages, (c) estimated execution durations, and (d) slack per stage—computed as detailed in Section 4.1. These values enable each stage’s containers to calculate their available free slots.

**Pod Container Selection:** At runtime, worker pods query the database to select a pod with the fewest available free slots for task scheduling. Upon selection, the task is

dded to the pod’s local queue, and its free slots are updated in the database. This process is repeated for each subsequent task within the job.

**Load Balancer:** We developed a Python daemon (1K lines of code) comprising a load monitor (LM) and a load predictor (LP). The LM periodically measures queuing delays at each stage's global queue and initiates additional container instances as needed (as described in Section 4.2). The LP employs an LSTM model for request load prediction, trained using Keras (Chollet, 2018) and TensorFlow (Abadi et al., 2016) over 100 epochs with a two-layer configuration, 32 neurons, and a batch size of 1. The daemon retrieves job arrival data from the database for model input, with prediction specifics outlined in Section 4.5.

**Node/Server Selection:** To optimize container distribution and minimize node usage, we modified Kubernetes’ MostRequestedPriority scheduling policy to prioritize nodes with the least available resources. In our experiments, each container was allocated 0.5 CPU-core and up to 1GB of memory, with all containers set to a CPU limit of 0.5. Node idle cores are determined by subtracting the sum of CPU shares for all allocated pods from the total cores in the node.

The benefits of proactive provisioning are illustrated in Figure 12(b), which shows the total number of contai

rs spawned over 10-second intervals for all four resource managers (RMs). The results demonstrate that both RScale and Fifer effectively adapt to the request rate distribution, spawning on average 60% and 82% fewer containers, respectively, compared to Bline RM due to their batching mechanisms. Fifer outperforms RScale by an additional 22% because it leverages LSTM predictions to precisely estimate the required number of containers at each stage.

Many frameworks are designed solely to satisfy the individual service-level objectives (SLOs) of each function within a system. However, this approach often overlooks the overall end-to-end SLO of the entire function chain, leading to inefficient uniform scaling of containers across all stages. This results in over-provisioning of containers, requiring more machines to host idle ones and consequently increasing operational costs for service providers.

Resilience to cloud noise: A major challenge when applying Bayesian Optimization to FaaS is the noise in cloud environments, due to e.g., resource contention. If noise is not h

ely, resource managers can violate QoS and/or waste resources. While baseline BO can account for some noise, that is not sufficient to capture the variability of FaaS infrastructures. Aquatope’s resource manager uses customized noise-aware BO to find near-optimal resource configurations under noisy observations. We use a synthetic single function workflow with different degrees of execution time variability to evaluate the performance for Aquatope in a noisy environment. Fig. 14 shows that Aquatope outperforms CLITE in execution cost by 7%–45% as the inherent noise of the function increases.

As shown in Fig. 15 , we further evaluate the robustness of Aquatope to irregular system noise by introducing intermittent background jobs (53) on the same worker servers, causing noise and data outliers in the sampling process of the ML pipeline. The noise level represents the frequency and intensity of the background jobs. As the noise level increases, the number of dat

A key element of variational quantum algorithms (VQA) is the state-preparation circuit, often referred to as the ansatz or variational form. Typically, this circuit is constructed to align with the symmetries of the problem Hamiltonian, thereby restricting the variational search to a specific subspace. In this work, we demonstrate that this conventional approach is not universally beneficial by introducing ansätze that incorporate symmetry-breaking unitaries. These ansätze, termed Quantum-Optimal-Control-inspired Ansätze (QOCA), draw inspiration from the principles of quantum optimal control and enhance the convergence of VQAs for certain critical problems. Specifically, we evaluate QOCA against widely-used ansätze in the context of the Fermi-Hubbard model at half-filling, revealing that our variational circuits achieve a significantly higher accuracy in approximating the ground state for larger systems. Furthermore, we illustrate how QOCA can be employed to determine the ground state of the water molecule and compare its performance with other commonly utilized ansätze for chemistry-related problems. This research marks an initial step toward the creation of a broader class of symmetry-breaking ansätze with potential applications in both physics and chemistry domains.

In many quantum simulation studies of the Fermionic Hubbard Model (FHM) discussed in the literature, such as those by Wecker et al. (2015b), the initial state is chosen as the ground state of the non-interacting FHM. This is achieved by setting \( U = \mu = 0 \) in Equation 11, resulting in a Hamiltonian that is diagonal in Fourier space. This choice simplifies classical computation of the ground state, making it a practical starting point. However, preparing this state on a quantum computer typically requires extensive quantum circuits, particularly due to the fermionic Fourier transformation. Current implementations of this transformation, as outlined by Verstraete et al. (2009), are restricted to periodic systems, limiting the applicability of this initial state. To our knowledge, no implementation of an open-boundary-conditions fermionic Fourier transformation has been developed yet. Additionally, the non-interacting FHM's ground state can be degenerate, complicating the selection of the appropriate state or superposition for use. This issue is frequently highlighted in the literature, such as in Cade et al. (2019), as a significant challenge in Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs), where prior knowledge of the target state is often leveraged to initialize the state with maximum fidelity. The lack of a clear method for making this choice as system complexity grows remains an unresolved issue.

The HEA is particularly effective for a comprehensive exploration of Hilbert space, as it does not inherently prioritize a specific symmetry sector. This approach has been experimentally validated for preparing the ground state of small molecules (Kandala et al., 2017), simulating the folding of short amino acid chains (Robert et al., 2019), and solving classical optimization problems (Barkoutsos et al., 2020). While these demonstrations highlight its utility for small-scale problems, they do not confirm its scalability for larger systems. Notably, parametrized circuits generated by methods like HEA, which exhibit sufficient randomness, are prone to exponentially diminishing gradients as the number of qubits increases, thereby complicating convergence for larger systems (McClean et al., 2018).

Approaches that exploit the problem's structure can circumvent the previously mentioned scalability challenges by avoiding the exhaustive exploration of the exponentially large Hilbert space. In 2015, Wecker et al. developed the Variational Hamiltonian Ansatz (VHA), which involves a parameterized adaptation of the quantum circuit that implements time evolution under the problem Hamiltonian using Trotterization. Within the VHA framework, the state-preparation unitary is expressed as 

$\hat{U}_{\text{VHA}}(\bm{\theta})=\prod_{d}\prod_{j}e^{i\theta_{j,d}\hat{H}_{j}},$  (3) 

where $\bm{\theta}=\{\theta_{j,d}\}$ represents the variational parameters and $\hat{H}_{\text{prob}}=\sum_{j}\hat{H}_{j}$ is the problem Hamiltonian, decomposed into a sum of non-commuting term groups labeled $\hat{H}_{j}$. The depth $d$ corresponds to each time increment in the Trotterization of the time-evolution operator. If the terms are grouped efficiently, this method can be implemented with a limited number of variational parameters, thereby simplifying the classical optimization process. However, depending on the problem's complexity, the resulting circuits may be significantly longer compared to those typically employed with the HEA.

In the context of the Variational Quantum Annealing (VQA) paradigm, a quantum processor maintains a quantum state $|\psi(\bm{\theta})\rangle$, which is defined by a set of classical variational parameters $\bm{\theta}$. This state is constructed from an initial reference state $|\psi_{0}\rangle$, chosen for its simplicity and ease of preparation, through the application of a quantum circuit (referred to as the ansatz) $\hat{U}(\bm{\theta})$, yielding $|\psi(\bm{\theta})\rangle = \hat{U}(\bm{\theta})|\psi_{0}\rangle$. The classical co-processor systematically modifies the parameter vector $\bm{\theta}$ to minimize the energy function 

$E[\bm{\theta}] = \frac{\langle\psi(\bm{\theta})|\hat{H}_{\text{prob}}|\psi(\bm{\theta})\rangle}{\langle\psi(\bm{\theta})|\psi(\bm{\theta})\rangle},$ (1)

which represents the expected value of the problem Hamiltonian $\hat{H}_{\text{prob}}$. A wide range of ansatz structures $\hat{U}(\bm{\theta})$ have been investigated in prior studies, as demonstrated in Kandala et al. [2017]. Prior to presenting our methodology, this section provides a concise overview of two commonly employed ansätze, discussing their respective benefits and limitations.

Extending the framework of quantum optimal control, we enhance the Variational Hamiltonian Ansatz (VHA) by introducing a variational form that incorporates a set of drive terms $\{\hat{H}_{k}\}$ alongside the problem Hamiltonian $\hat{H}_{\text{prob}}$. This approach, termed QOCA, emulates time evolution governed by the composite Hamiltonian:

$\hat{H}_{\text{QOCA}}(t) = \hat{H}_{\text{prob}} + \sum_{k} c_{k}(t) \hat{H}_{k},$ (8)

where the design ensures that $[\hat{H}_{\text{prob}}, \hat{H}_{k}] \neq 0$ for all $k$. The QOCA state-preparation circuit is constructed by parameterizing the time-evolution-like operator:

$\hat{U}_{\text{QOCA}}(\bm{\theta}, \bm{\delta}) = \prod_{d} \left( \prod_{j} e^{i\theta_{j,d}\hat{H}_{j}} \prod_{k} e^{i\delta_{k,d}\hat{H}_{k}} \right),$ (9)

Here, $\hat{H}_{\text{prob}} = \sum_{j} \hat{H}_{j}$ represents the problem Hamiltonian, and $\bm{\theta} = \{\theta_{j,d}\}$ are its associated parameters. Similarly, $\bm{\delta} = \{\delta_{k,d}\}$ correspond to the discrete drive amplitudes of the control fields $c_{k}(t)$ from Eq. (8), serving as variational parameters. The index $d$ denotes the ansatz depth, akin to the time-evolution steps.

This approach involves treating all (or nearly all) gate angles as variational parameters, enabling the classical optimizer to explore the Hilbert space defined by the ansatz. However, this comes at the expense of increased optimization time. By default, the HEA adopts a fully parametrized configuration, as all single-qubit gates are variational. Similarly, for the VHA, each $\hat{a}^{\dagger}_{i\sigma}\hat{a}_{j\sigma}+\text{h.c.}$ hopping term is assigned a single parameter, which is duplicated to account for both spin orientations. This duplication is unnecessary at half-filling and zero total spin due to spin-inversion symmetry, which renders spins up and down indistinct. Furthermore, each term in the on-site interaction is also associated with a variational parameter. The asymptotic scaling of the number of variational parameters for all ansätze, under both parametrization strategies, is summarized in Table 3.

For the specific case of $L=4$ (or $2\times 2$ ), the fidelity of the 16 degenerate ground states from Eq.25 was calculated relative to the target ground state. Subsequently, the states with the highest fidelity values were selected. However, this approach is inherently limited in scalability, leaving unresolved how one would proceed in scenarios where the fidelity with the target ground state cannot be precomputed.

The Hardware-efficient Ansatz (HEA), as presented in Ref. Kandala et al. [2017], leverages gates inherent to quantum hardware to construct circuits characterized by high expressibility and low depth, as demonstrated in Simet et al. [2019]. Specifically, the HEA involves sequential blocks of parameterized single-qubit rotations, followed by an entangling unitary operator $\hat{U}_{\text{Ent.}}$. For a system of $N$ qubits, this structure can be expressed as:

$\hat{U}_{\text{HEA}}(\bm{\theta}) = \prod_{d} \hat{U}_{\text{Ent.}} \prod_{n=1}^{N} R_{Z}^{(n)}(\theta_{n,d}^{Z}) R_{Y}^{(n)}(\theta_{n,d}^{Y}),$  (2)

where $\bm{\theta} = \{\theta_{n,d}^{Z}, \theta_{n,d}^{Y}\}$ encompasses all variational parameters, and $R_{\sigma_{a}}^{(n)}(\theta) = \exp[-i\theta \sigma_{a}/2]$ represents a single-qubit rotation of angle $\theta$ about the $a \in \{x, y, z\}$ axis on qubit $n$. Here, $\sigma_{a}$ denotes the corresponding Pauli matrix. The parameter $d$ signifies the number of layers or depth of the ansatz. Throughout this paper, operator multiplication follows the convention $\prod_{i}^{N} \hat{U}_{i} = \hat{U}_{N} \cdots \hat{U}_{1}$.

The drive $D$ depicted in Fig. 2 and the kinetic component of the FHM Eq. 11 both exhibit block-diagonal characteristics in the spin degree of freedom. To simplify the ansatz and reduce the computational cost associated with two-qubit gates, we omitted the kinetic term, resulting in the simplified form:

\[
\hat{U}_{\text{sQOCA}}(\bm{\nu},\bm{\delta})=\prod_{d}\left(\prod_{j}e^{i\nu_{j,d}\hat{V}_{j}}\prod_{k}e^{i\delta_{k,d}\hat{H}_{k}}\right),
\]
where $\hat{V}=\sum_{j}\hat{V}_{j}$ represents the on-site interaction component of the Fermi-Hubbard Hamiltonian Eq. 11, and $\bm{\nu}=\{\nu_{j,d}\}$ denotes the corresponding variational parameters. This simplified version of the QOCA ansatz is referred to as short-QOCA, as illustrated in Fig. 1.

To reduce the number of variational parameters, we set $\alpha_{j}(t)$ to $1$ and $i$ in Eq.10, resulting in 

$\displaystyle\hat{H}_{1} = \sum_{j=1}^{L}(\hat{a}^{\dagger}_{j} + \hat{a}_{j})$ (13)

and 

$\displaystyle\hat{H}_{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{L}i(\hat{a}^{\dagger}_{j} - \hat{a}_{j})$ (14). 

For a spinless system, we derive the corresponding drive equations and apply the resulting circuit independently to the two subspaces associated with spin projections up and down across all sites. Applying the Jordan-Wigner transformation to Eqs.13 and 14 yields 

$\displaystyle\hat{H}_{1} \mapsto \sum_{j=1}^{L}\hat{X}_{j}\bigotimes_{l<j}\hat{Z}_{l}$ (15)

and 

$\displaystyle\hat{H}_{2} \mapsto \sum_{j=1}^{L}\hat{Y}_{j}\bigotimes_{l<j}\hat{Z}_{l}$ (16), 

where $\hat{X}$, $\hat{Y}$, and $\hat{Z}$ denote Pauli matrices. To integrate these expressions into the QOCA variational form in Eq.9, we perform a first-order Trotter-Suzuki decomposition, obtaining the circuit equation for the $d$th layer of the ansatz:

$\begin{split}&\prod_{k=1,2}e^{i\delta_{k,d}\hat{H}_{k}}\\
&\quad\approx\prod_{j=1}^{L}\exp\left[i\delta_{1,d}\,\hat{X}_{j}\,\bigotimes_{l<j}\,\hat{Z}_{l}\right]\,\exp\left[i\delta_{2,d}\,\hat{Y}_{j}\,\bigotimes_{l<j}\,\hat{Z}_{l}\right],\end{split}$ (17)

where $\{\delta_{k,d}\}$ are the variational parameters associated with the $k$th drive term of that layer. A schematic of the circuit implementing Eq.17 for 4 qubits is shown in Fig.2, along with a compiled version using CNOT gates.

The QOCA variational form is demonstrated to be effective under unstructured, simple initial conditions. Additionally, we highlight improved convergence by employing the ground state of the non-interacting FHM with $U=\mu=0$ as defined in Eq. 11 as the initial state. Further details on the preparation of this more intricate state are included in Appendix D.

The performance of VQAs is significantly influenced by the selection of the initial state and variational parameters. The initial state serves as an educated guess for the target state and is typically chosen to be computationally tractable classically. Following Hadfield et al. [2019], who suggest that the initialization stage of variational algorithms should be simple or treated independently, we focus on evaluating the QOCA variational form using the straightforward initial state:

\[
|\psi_{0}\rangle = H^{\otimes N}|0\rangle = |+\rangle^{\otimes N},
\]

where \( H \) represents the Hadamard gate. This initial state is not only easy to prepare but also corresponds to half-filling and zero total spin, aligning it with the symmetry sector of the target state.

This approach involves treating all (or nearly all) gate angles as variational parameters, allowing the classical optimizer to thoroughly explore the Hilbert space defined by the ansatz, albeit at the expense of increased optimization time. Notably, the HEA inherently adopts a fully parametrized setup, as all single-qubit gates are parameterized by default. Similarly, the VHA strategy assigns a single parameter to each $\hat{a}^{\dagger}_{i\sigma}\hat{a}_{j\sigma}+\text{h.c.}$ hopping term and duplicates this parameter to account for both spin orientations. This duplication is unnecessary at half-filling and zero total spin due to spin-inversion symmetry, which renders spins up and down indistinguishable. Furthermore, every term in the on-site interaction is paired with its own variational parameter. The asymptotic scaling of the number of variational parameters for all ansätze under both parametrization strategies is detailed in Table 3.

In a scalable parametrization approach, the number of variational parameters used remains constant regardless of the system size. While this reduced parameter count may lead to faster optimization, achieving the same level of accuracy as full parametrization could require deeper circuits.

When the drive angles are adjusted away from zero by the optimizer, both implementations of QOCA, which include particle-non-conserving drive terms, permit the average site occupancy to vary from $\langle\hat{N}\rangle/L=1$. As depicted in Figure 4, this variation can result in the prominent features observed during the initial $\sim10^{2}$ iterations, as the classical optimizer may initially overemphasize certain terms. Throughout the entire optimization process, the particle count deviates only modestly from the target value of $\langle\hat{N}\rangle/L=1$, with shifts in site occupancy of approximately $5\%$. This suggests that the symmetry-breaking terms in QOCA enable the ansatz to explore a slightly expanded Hilbert space beyond the fixed particle number manifold. However, despite these minor deviations from the target symmetry sector, the optimization process is notably facilitated. As shown by the vertical dashed lines in Figure 4, the return to the target symmetry sector often coincides with abrupt decreases in infidelity, potentially indicating the discovery of regions with steep gradients in the parameter space.

The use of the short-QOCA variational form reveals that QOCA circuits can be significantly shortened by eliminating over half of the two-qubit gates at each step, with minimal impact on performance for small systems. Specifically, for the $2\times 2$ Hubbard model, a fidelity of $0.9999$ is achieved using only $9$ layers of this ansatz.

The Fermi-Hubbard model's ground state is systematically attained by QOCA with notably superior accuracy compared to VHA across both system sizes, suggesting that the inclusion of symmetry-breaking terms enhances convergence. This performance remains robust even when variational parameters are drastically reduced—from 16 to 5 per layer in QOCA's scalable parametrization—where it achieved a fidelity of $0.9992$ at $d=10$ for the $2\times 2$ system. While the hardware-efficient approach outperforms VHA, it does so at the cost of utilizing substantially more parameters, as it typically requires additional layers to match the performance of other ansätze. The challenge of minimizing this parameter count to achieve a more favorable scaling remains unresolved.

To parameterize these circuits, two strategies are considered: one involves fully parameterizing all single- and two-qubit gates, while the other employs a parameter count that scales with the ansatz depth but not with the number of qubits. The latter strategy is explicitly labeled as 'scalable' when referenced. Both methods are thoroughly discussed in Appendix B, and details of the numerical simulations are provided in Appendix C.

The Fermi-Hubbard model is a seminal framework for investigating strongly correlated materials, as introduced by Hubbard in 1963. It represents interacting spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ fermions on a lattice, with each site accommodating up to two particles of opposite spins. The Hamiltonian for the Fermi-Hubbard model (FHM) with $L$ lattice sites is expressed as

$$
\hat{H}_{\text{FHM}} = \underbracket{-t \sum_{\langle i,j \rangle, \sigma} \hat{a}^{\dagger}_{i \sigma} \hat{a}_{j \sigma}}_{\equiv \, \hat{T}} + \underbracket{U \sum_{i=1}^{L} \hat{n}_{i \uparrow} \hat{n}_{i \downarrow} - \mu \sum_{i, \sigma} \hat{n}_{i \sigma}}_{\equiv \, \hat{V}},
$$

where $i, j$ represent lattice-site indices, and $\sigma = \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$ denotes the spin degree of freedom. The first term involves a summation over nearest-neighbor sites, with $\hat{n}_{i \sigma} = \hat{a}^{\dagger}_{i \sigma} \hat{a}_{i \sigma}$ being the occupation operator for the spin-orbital labeled $i \sigma$.

In this section, the fidelity of the variational state $|\psi(\bm{\theta})\rangle$ with respect to the target state $|\Omega\rangle$ (i.e., the ground state of the FHM or the water molecule) is employed to measure the quality of the variational state. The fidelity is defined as

\[
\text{Fidelity} = |\langle\psi(\bm{\theta})|\Omega\rangle|^{2}, \quad (19)
\]

where a higher value indicates a closer approximation to the target state.
