Let \([\xi] \in \widehat{G}\) and \(\xi \in [\xi]\). The entry functions \(\xi_{ij}\) of any unitary representation \(\xi = (\xi_{ij})_{i,j=1}^{d_{\xi}}\) span an eigenspace \(\mathcal{H}^{\xi} \subset L^{2}(G)\) of the Laplacian. The restriction of the Laplacian to this eigenspace, \(\mathcal{L}_{G}|_{\mathcal{H}^{\xi}} = \lambda_{[\xi]}I\), acts as a scalar multiple of the identity operator on \(\mathcal{H}^{\xi}\), as established in Theorem 10.3.13 of \([RT10]\). The eigenvalue \(\lambda_{[\xi]}\) is invariant under the choice of \(\xi \in [\xi]\). Consequently, for any \(\xi\), the entry functions \(\xi_{ij}\) are smooth (\(C^{\infty}\)-class) functions. They serve as eigenfunctions of the positive Laplace operator \(\mathcal{L}_{G}\), satisfying \(\mathcal{L}_{G}\xi_{ij} = \lambda_{[\xi]}\xi_{ij}\).

If we replace the sub-Laplacian $\mathcal{L}$ with the Laplace-Beltrami operator on the group $G$ in Corollary 3.5, it follows that $Q = n = \dim(G)$. Consequently, we arrive at the stated result for the standard Hörmander classes on $G$. This is grounded in the established equivalence between the classes $\Psi^{-n}_{\rho,0}(G\times\widehat{G})$ and $\Psi^{-n}_{\rho,0}(G;\textnormal{loc})$ for all $0 < \rho \leq 1$.
In the ensuing theorem, we consolidate several key characteristics of the subelliptic calculus as presented in [CR20], which will serve as foundational tools for our subsequent analytical endeavors.

Let $G$ be a compact Lie group, and let $0 \leq \delta, \rho \leq 1$. Consider a sub-Laplacian $\mathcal{L} = -(X_1^2 + \cdots + X_k^2)$ on $G$, where the vector field system $X = \{X_i\}_{i=1}^k$ satisfies the Hörmander condition of step $\kappa$. A symbol $\sigma$ belongs to the subelliptic Hörmander class $S^{m,\mathcal{L}}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G})$ if it satisfies the following symbol inequalities:

$$
p_{\alpha,\beta,\rho,\delta,m,\text{left}}(\sigma)' := \sup_{(x,[\xi]) \in G \times \widehat{G}} \left\| \widehat{\mathcal{M}}(\xi)^{(\rho|\alpha| - \delta|\beta| - m)} \partial_X^{(\beta)} \Delta_\xi^\alpha \sigma(x,\xi) \right\|_{\text{op}} < \infty, \quad \forall \alpha, \beta, \text{ (2.12)}
$$

and

$$
p_{\alpha,\beta,\rho,\delta,m,\text{right}}(\sigma)' := \sup_{(x,[\xi]) \in G \times \widehat{G}} \left\| (\partial_X^{(\beta)} \Delta_\xi^\alpha \sigma(x,\xi)) \widehat{\mathcal{M}}(\xi)^{(\rho|\alpha| - \delta|\beta| - m)} \right\|_{\text{op}} < \infty, \quad \forall \alpha, \beta. \text{ (2.13)}
$$

The family of seminorms

$$
p_{\alpha,\beta,\rho,\delta,m,\text{right}}(\sigma)', \, p_{\alpha,\beta,\rho,\delta,m,\text{right}}(\sigma)' \text{ (2.14)}
$$

endows the class $S^{m,\mathcal{L}}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G})$ with a natural structure of Fréchet space. By denoting

$$
\Psi^{m,\mathcal{L}}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G}) := \{ A : \sigma_A \in S^{m,\mathcal{L}}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G}) \}, \text{ (2.15)}
$$

and by defining

$$
p_{\alpha,\beta,\rho,\delta,m,\text{right}}(A)' := p_{\alpha,\beta,\rho,\delta,m,\text{right}}(\sigma_A)', \quad p_{\alpha,\beta,\rho,\delta,m,\text{left}}(A)' := p_{\alpha,\beta,\rho,\delta,m,\text{left}}(\sigma_A)', \text{ (2.16)}
$$

the operator class $\Psi^{m,\mathcal{L}}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G})$ also has a structure of Fréchet space.

In this section, we establish several relationships between the determinant of operators within the framework of invariance, as detailed in Section 2.4 and originally proposed in [DR18], and the computational aspects of the Dixmier trace for elliptic operators. To proceed, let $\Psi^{m}_{+e}(M)$ denote the class of positive elliptic classical pseudo-differential operators of order $m \in \mathbb{R}$.

In this section, we examine the Dixmier traceability of pseudo-differential operators on compact Lie groups. Our analysis will focus on the subelliptic pseudo-differential classes $\Psi^{-Q,\mathcal{L}}_{\rho,0}(G\times\widehat{G})$. Specifically, substituting the Laplace-Beltrami operator for the sub-Laplacian $\mathcal{L}$ allows our results to be extended to the standard Hörmander classes $\Psi^{-n}_{\rho,0}(G;\textnormal{loc})=\Psi^{-n}_{\rho,0}(G\times\widehat{G})$.

To obtain the result, we substitute the sub-Laplacian $\mathcal{L}$ with the Laplace-Beltrami operator on $G$ in Theorem 3.9. As previously established, this substitution yields $Q = n = \dim(G)$ and leads to the following conclusion for the standard Hörmander classes on $G$. This follows from the equivalence of the classes $\Psi^{-n}_{\rho,0}(G\times\widehat{G}) = \Psi^{-n}_{\rho,0}(G;\textnormal{loc})$ for all $0 < \rho \leq 1$, as demonstrated in [RTW14].

Let $A$ be a positive, elliptic pseudo-differential operator of class $\Psi^{-n}_{cl}(G,\textnormal{loc})$ defined on the group $G$. Suppose its symbol admits an asymptotic expansion of the form 

$\sigma_{A}(x,[\xi]) \sim \sum_{k=-\infty}^{-n} \sigma_{k}(x,[\xi]),$  (3.22)

where the terms are arranged in decreasing order of homogeneity. Specifically, for any natural number $N$, the difference

$\sigma_{A}(x,[\xi]) - \sum_{k=-N-n}^{-n} \sigma_{k}(x,[\xi])$ 

lies in the symbol class $S^{-(N+1)-n}_{1,0}(G \times \widehat{G})$.  (3.23)

Consequently, the Wodzicki residue of $A$ is determined by the formula

$\textnormal{res}(A) = \int_{G} \|\sigma_{-n}(x,[\xi])\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\widehat{G})} dx.$  (3.24)

In this section, we revisit fundamental concepts of pseudodifferential calculus on compact Lie groups, delve into the subelliptic Hörmander classes recently established in [CR20], explore determinants on Hilbert spaces, and examine the Dixmier ideal.

In general, if $1 \leq p < \infty$ and the sequence of singular values is $p$-summable, the operator $T$ is classified as an element of the Schatten-von Neumann class ${S}_{p}({\mathcal{H}})$. It is established that each ${S}_{p}({\mathcal{H}})$ constitutes an ideal within $\mathcal{L}({\mathcal{H}})$. For $1 \leq p < \infty$, the associated norm for ${S}_{p}({\mathcal{H}})$ is given by

$$
\|T\|_{S_{p}} = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} (s_{k}(T))^{p} \right)^{\frac{1}{p}}.
$$

When $1 \leq p < \infty$, the class $S_{p}({\mathcal{H}})$ is a Banach space equipped with the norm $\|T\|_{S_{p}}$. For $p = \infty$, $S_{\infty}({\mathcal{H}})$ represents the class of bounded linear operators on $H$, with the norm $\|T\|_{S_{\infty}} := \|T\|_{\textnormal{op}}$, corresponding to the operator norm.

Let $A \in \Psi^{-n}_{cl}(G,\textnormal{loc})$ be an elliptic pseudo-differential operator on $G$. Suppose its symbol admits an asymptotic expansion

$\sigma_{A}(x,[\xi])\sim\sum_{k=-\infty}^{-n}\sigma_{k}(x,[\xi]),\,$ (3.27)

in components of decreasing order. This implies that for any $N\in\mathbb{N}$,

$\sigma_{A}(x,[\xi])-\sum_{k=-N-n}^{-n}\sigma_{k}(x,[\xi])\in S^{-(N+1)-n}_{1,0}(G\times\widehat{G}).$ (3.28)

The Wodzicki residue of $A$ is then computed via the formula

$\displaystyle\textnormal{res}(A)=\int\limits_{G}\left(\|\textnormal{Re}(\sigma_{-Q}(x,[\xi]))^{+}\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\widehat{G})}-\|\textnormal{Re}(\sigma_{-Q}(x,[\xi]))^{-}\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\widehat{G})}\right)dx$

$\displaystyle+i\int\limits_{G}\left(\|\textnormal{Im}(\sigma_{-Q}(x,[\xi]))^{+}\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\widehat{G})}-\|\textnormal{Im}(\sigma_{-Q}(x,[\xi]))^{-}\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\widehat{G})}\right)dx.$

In this study, we determine the Wodzicki-Guillemin residue and the Dixmier trace of pseudo-differential operators on compact Lie groups by analyzing their global symbols or, equivalently, through the lens of the groups' representation theory. Our geometric and spectral formulas are established within the framework of pseudo-differential operators linked to Riemannian and sub-Riemannian structures on these groups, as detailed in [RT10]. The plausibility of these findings can be inferred from methodologies advanced in [LNP16], where the Wodzicki residue was investigated using a modified version of the global-symbolic calculus on the torus, originally formulated by the third author alongside Turunen in [RT10]. Additional insights can be drawn from [CDC20]. Notably, the theoretical framework in [RT10] can be viewed as a discrete adaptation of the algebraic formalism introduced by Connes and Moscovici in [CM95], tailored for the context of Lie groups. This adaptation facilitates the comprehensive analysis of residues, determinants, and traces in a global setting.

Let ${\mathcal{H}}$ be a complex Hilbert space and ${\mathcal{H}}^{\infty}$ be a dense linear subspace of ${\mathcal{H}}$. Consider a sequence $\{d_{j}\}_{j\in{{\mathbb{N}}}_{0}}\subset{{\mathbb{N}}}$ and an orthonormal basis $\{e_{j}^{k}\}_{j\in{{\mathbb{N}}}_{0},1\leq k\leq d_{j}}$ of ${\mathcal{H}}$, where each $e_{j}^{k}$ belongs to ${\mathcal{H}}^{\infty}$. For each $j$, define $H_{j}$ as the span of $\{e_{j}^{k}\}_{k=1}^{d_{j}}$, and let $P_{j}$ be the orthogonal projection from ${\mathcal{H}}$ onto $H_{j}$. For any $f\in{\mathcal{H}}$, denote $\widehat{f}(j,k):=(f,e_{j}^{k})_{{\mathcal{H}}}$, and let $\widehat{f}(j)\in\mathbb{C}^{d_{j}}$ be the column vector formed by $\widehat{f}(j,k)$ for $1\leq k\leq d_{j}$. Let $T:{\mathcal{H}}^{\infty}\to{\mathcal{H}}$ be a linear operator. The following conditions are equivalent: (A) For each $j\in\mathbb{N}_{0}$, $T$ maps $H_{j}$ into $H_{j}$. (B) For each $\ell\in\mathbb{N}_{0}$, there exists a matrix $\sigma_{T}(\ell)\in\mathbb{C}^{d_{\ell}\times d_{\ell}}$ such that for all $e_{j}^{k}$, 
$$
\widehat{Te_{j}^{k}}(\ell,m) = \sigma_{T}(\ell)_{mk}\delta_{j\ell}.
$$
(C) For each $\ell\in\mathbb{N}_{0}$, there exists a matrix $\sigma_{T}(\ell)\in\mathbb{C}^{d_{\ell}\times d_{\ell}}$ such that for all $f\in{\mathcal{H}}^{\infty}$,
$$
\widehat{Tf}(\ell) = \sigma_{T}(\ell)\widehat{f}(\ell).
$$

For $k\in\mathbb{N}$, the space $\mathcal{S}^{k}_{\Gamma}(\mathbb{R}^{n})$ consists of smooth functions $f$ such that for all multi-indices $\alpha$ with $[\alpha]\leq k$, the derivatives $X^{\alpha}f$ are bounded. The space $\mathcal{S}^{k}_{\Gamma}(\mathbb{R}^{n})$ is equipped with a family of norms defined by $\|f\|_{k}:=\sup_{[\alpha]\leq k}\sup_{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n}}|X^{\alpha}f(x)|$. This construction ensures that the space is well-suited for studying functions with anisotropic regularity properties.

If $A \in \Psi^{m}_{\rho,\delta}(G,\textnormal{loc})$ with $\rho \geqslant 1-\delta$, the matrix-valued symbol $\sigma_A$ of $A$ satisfies the following inequality (as shown in [RT10]):

$$\|{X}_{x}^{\beta}\Delta_{\xi}^{\gamma}\sigma_{A}(x,\xi)\|_{\textnormal{op}} \leqslant C_{\alpha,\beta}\langle\xi\rangle^{m-\rho|\gamma|+\delta|\beta|} \quad \text{(2.9)}$$

for all multi-indices $\beta$ and $\gamma$, and for all $(x,[\xi]) \in G \times \widehat{G}$. When $0 \leqslant \delta,\rho \leqslant 1$, we say that $\sigma_A \in {S}^{m}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G})$ if the global symbol inequalities (2.9) are satisfied. Consequently, for $\sigma_A \in {S}^{m}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G})$, we write $A \in \Psi^{m}_{\rho,\delta}(G) \equiv \textnormal{Op}({S}^{m}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G}))$. It is important to note that:

$$\textnormal{Op}({S}^{m}_{\rho,\delta}(G \times \widehat{G})) = \Psi^{m}_{\rho,\delta}(G,\textnormal{loc}), \quad 0 \leqslant \delta < \rho \leqslant 1, \quad \rho \geqslant 1-\delta.$$

According to the theory established by Hörmander [Hör85], every pseudo-differential operator \( A \) acting on \( C^{\infty}(M) \), where \( M \) is a closed manifold (i.e., compact and without boundary), can be associated with a symbol defined via local coordinate systems. This symbol is a function on the cotangent bundle \( T^{*}M \). However, from a geometric perspective, certain details about the operator, such as its lower-order terms, may not be fully captured. In contrast, as demonstrated by the third author and Turunen in [RT10], when \( M = G \) is a compact Lie group, a global symbol definition for the Hörmander classes can be provided. These symbols can be expressed as matrix-valued functions on the phase space \( G \times \widehat{G} \), where \( \widehat{G} \) represents the unitary dual of \( G \), i.e., the set of equivalence classes of continuous irreducible unitary representations of the group.

For a compact, orientable manifold $M$ of dimension $n$ without boundary, a pseudo-differential operator $A$ can be locally defined through the concept of a symbol. Specifically, within a local chart $U$, the operator acts as 

$$Au(x) = \int_{T^{*}_{x}U} e^{2\pi i x \cdot \xi} \sigma^{A}(x, \xi) \widehat{u}(\xi) \, d\xi.$$

An operator $A$ is termed classical if its symbol $\sigma^{A}$ exhibits an asymptotic expansion $\sigma^{A}(x, \xi) \sim \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \sigma^{A}_{m-j}(x, \xi)$, where each $\sigma_{m-j}(x, \xi)$ is homogeneous of degree $m-j$ in $\xi$ for $\xi \neq 0$. The collection of such operators of order $m$ is $\Psi^{m}_{cl}(M)$, while $\Psi^{m}_{+e}(M)$ denotes the subset of positive elliptic classical operators. For $A \in \Psi_{cl}^{m}(M)$, the integral $\int_{|\xi|=1} \sigma_{-n}(x, \xi) \, d\xi$ yields a local density that can be consistently extended across $M$. Consequently, the non-commutative residue of $A$ is given by

$$\textnormal{res}\,(A) = \frac{1}{n(2\pi)^{n}} \int_{M} \int_{|\xi|=1} \sigma^{A}_{-n}(x, \xi) \, d\xi \, dx.$$

Operators of order $-n$, where $n = \dim(M)$, belong to the Dixmier class $\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(L^{2}(M))$, and additionally:
We now present the family of subelliptic pseudo-differential operators that will be examined within the framework of the Dixmier trace.

For \( m \in \mathbb{R} \) and \( 0 \leq \delta < \rho \leq 1 \), consider a symbol \( a = a(x, \xi) \) belonging to the class \( S^{m, \mathcal{L}}_{\rho, \delta}(G \times \widehat{G}) \). Suppose that \( a(x, \xi) \) is invertible for all \( (x, [\xi]) \in G \times \widehat{G} \) and satisfies the condition
\[
\sup_{(x, [\xi]) \in G \times \widehat{G}} \|\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(\xi)^{m} a(x, \xi)^{-1}\|_{\textnormal{op}} < \infty. \quad (2.17)
\]
Under these circumstances, the operator \( A \) associated with \( a(\cdot, \cdot) \) is said to be \( \mathcal{L} \)-elliptic.

Let $F$ be a topological space. A (operator-valued) measure $\mu$ on $F$ is a map from the Borel subsets of $F$ to the space of bounded operators on a Hilbert space such that for each vector in the Hilbert space, the corresponding vector-valued measure is scalar-valued and countably additive. The operator-valued measure is called regular if, for every Borel set, the measure of that set can be approximated by measures of compact sets. The concept of operator-valued measures generalizes the notion of vector-valued measures and scalar measures, and it is particularly important in quantum mechanics and non-commutative integration theory. The regularity condition ensures that the measure behaves nicely with respect to the topology of the underlying space, which is crucial for many applications in analysis and mathematical physics.

Following the work of Connes [Con94], consider a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ (with particular interest in $\mathcal{H}=L^{2}(M)$, where $M$ is a closed manifold of dimension $n$). The class $\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\mathcal{H})$ consists of compact linear operators $A$ on $\mathcal{H}$ for which the sum of their singular values satisfies 

$\sum_{1\leq n\leq N}s_{n}(A)=O(\log(N)),\,\,\,N\rightarrow\infty,$ 

where $\{s_{n}(A)\}$ represents the singular values of $A$, defined as the square roots of the eigenvalues of the positive-definite self-adjoint operator $A^{\ast}A$. The space $\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\mathcal{H})$ is equipped with the norm 

$\|A\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\mathcal{H})}=\sup_{N\geq 2}\frac{1}{\log(N)}\sum_{1\leq n\leq N}s_{n}(A).$ 

For an operator $A\in\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\mathcal{H})$, the sequence 

$\alpha_{N}(A):=\frac{1}{\log(N)}\sum_{j=1}^{N}s_{j}(A),\,\,\,N\in\mathbb{N},$ 

is bounded. If the limit of this sequence as $N\rightarrow\infty$ exists, we define 

$\textnormal{\bf Tr}_{\textnormal{Dix}}(A):=\lim_{N\rightarrow\infty}\frac{1}{\log(N)}\sum_{j=1}^{N}s_{j}(A).$

The residue formula for classical operators arises as a specific instance of a broader framework established in our investigation of subelliptic operators on compact Lie groups, as detailed in [CR20]. In Corollary 3.6, we demonstrate the formula 

\[
\textnormal{\bf{Tr}}_{\omega}(A) = \int\limits_{G} \|\sigma_{-Q}(x,[\xi])\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\widehat{G})} dx = \lim_{N \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{\log(N)} \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{j}(A),
\]

which holds for a positive subelliptic operator \( A \), where \( s_j(A) \) represents the singular values of \( A \), accounting for multiplicities. The corresponding Dixmier trace formula without the positivity condition is established in Theorem 3.9. Here, \( Q \) signifies the Hausdorff dimension linked to a positive sub-Laplacian \( \mathcal{L} = -\sum_{j=1}^{k} X_j^2 \), associated with a Hörmander system of vector fields \( \{X_j\}_{j=1}^{k} \) (see Subsection 2.2 for further details), and \( A \) is a positive operator within the Hörmander classes tied to \( \mathcal{L} \) (see Subsection 2.2 or [CR20] for comprehensive information). Notably, this formula, akin to Lidskii's theorem, connects the Dixmier trace with the eigenvalues of the operator. This connection is explored in the context of Dixmier traces in recent studies by Sukochev, Zanin, and collaborators, including [GG17], [SSZ15], [SZ14b], [GS07], and [SZ14a].

Let \( A \in \Psi^{-Q,\mathcal{L}}_{\rho,0}(G \times \widehat{G}) \) be a positive \(\mathcal{L}\)-elliptic pseudo-differential operator with \( 0 < \rho \leq 1 \). Suppose that the symbol of \( A \) has an asymptotic expansion 

\[
\sigma_{A}(x,[\xi]) \sim \sum_{k=-\infty}^{-Q} \sigma_{k}(x,[\xi]),
\]

structured in components of decreasing order. This implies that for any \( N \in \mathbb{N} \),

\[
\sigma_{A}(x,[\xi]) - \sum_{k=-N-Q}^{-Q} \sigma_{k}(x,[\xi]) \in S^{-(N+1)\rho - Q}_{\rho,0}(G \times \widehat{G}),
\]

as given in equation (3.4), where the principal symbol \( \sigma_{-Q}(x,[\xi]) \geq 0 \) is positive. Consequently, \( A \in \mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(L^{2}(G)) \), and the trace is expressed as

\[
\textnormal{\bf{Tr}}_{\omega}(A) = \int\limits_{G} \|\sigma_{-Q}(x,[\xi])\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\widehat{G})} dx,
\]

as shown in equation (3.5). Furthermore, the determinant is defined as

\[
\textnormal{Det}_{\omega,A}(\lambda) = \exp\left( \textnormal{\bf{Tr}}_{\omega}(A) \lambda \right) = \exp\left( \int\limits_{G} \|\sigma_{-Q}(x,[\xi])\|_{\mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)}(\widehat{G})} dx \cdot \lambda \right),
\]

for \( \lambda \in \mathbb{C} \) with \( |\lambda| \) sufficiently small, and the derivative at \( \lambda = 0 \) is

\[
\textnormal{Det}_{\omega,A}^{\prime}(0) : = \frac{d}{d\lambda} \textnormal{Det}_{\omega,A}(\lambda) |_{\lambda=0} = \textnormal{\bf{Tr}}_{\omega}(A).
\]

Based on Theorem 2.16, we derive a determinant formula for \( E \)-invariant operators, which will subsequently be utilized to formulate a determinant expression using the Dixmier trace (see [CDR21] for the proof).

We conclude the introduction by examining two distinct aspects of Lie group analysis, traditionally considered disparate, both of which are connected to the validity of (1.3). The first aspect draws from the theories presented in [RT10], demonstrating that the analysis of pseudo-differential operators with global symbols can be effectively addressed on compact Lie groups. This is achieved through techniques rooted in the representation theory of compact Lie groups, where the unitary dual is a discrete set, enabling analogies to Fourier analysis on the torus or on $\textnormal{SU(2)}$. Within this framework, Lidskii-type formulae are employed to localize the spectrum of operators and establish trace formulae in terms of their matrix-valued symbols (cf. [Del10a]). The second aspect stems from the twisted versions of periodic analysis in [RT10] or, equivalently, from twisted structures in non-commutative geometry, such as deformations of the torus or $\textnormal{SU}(2)$. This perspective allows for the generalization of Wodzicki-type results, as initially observed in [LNP16] using the concept of a global symbol (building on earlier work in [FK13]). Additionally, extensions of the Dixmier trace to a broader setting of Banach spaces have been developed in [Pie12]. For a comprehensive overview of trace theory and its historical development, we refer the reader to [Pie14].

Following Hörmander [Hör85], a classical pseudo-differential operator \( A \) on a closed manifold \( M \) of dimension \( n \) is characterized by its symbol \( \sigma^{A}(x,\xi) \sim \sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\sigma^{A}_{m-j}(x,\xi) \), where \( (x,\xi)\in T^{*}M \), constructed through localizations. The Wodzicki residue, which captures the local properties of the operator, is defined as follows: By a key result from Connes [Con94], if \( A \) has order \( -n \), then \( \textnormal{res}\,(A) \) aligns with the Dixmier trace of \( A \), expressed as:

When \( M \) exhibits symmetries, such as when \( M \) is a torus, \( M = \textnormal{SU}(2) \), or \( M = G \) is an arbitrary compact Lie group, the research by the third author and Turunen [RT10] demonstrates that the Hörmander classes in [Hör85] can be effectively described using the representation theory of \( G \). This leads to a natural inquiry: Can the Wodzicki residue be computed through this algebraic framework, or equivalently:

In this study, we affirmatively address (1.2) by establishing the validity of the formula for a positive pseudo-differential operator \( A \) of order \( -n \), where the full symbol \( \sigma_{-n}(x,[\xi]) \), \( (x,[\xi])\in G\times\widehat{G} \), represents the component of order \( -n \) of the matrix-valued symbol \( \sigma_{A}(x,[\xi]) \) of \( A \). This symbol is a global mapping on the non-commutative phase space \( G\times\widehat{G} \). The \( \mathcal{L}^{(1,\infty)} \)-norm of the symbol is defined as:

where the sequence \( \langle\xi\rangle \), \( [\xi]\in\widehat{G} \), corresponds to the eigenvalues of the operator \( (1+\mathcal{L}_{G})^{\frac{1}{2}} \), with \( \mathcal{L}_{G} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_{j}^{2} \) denoting the positive Laplacian on \( G \); see Remark 2.2 for further details. Additionally, we compute the Wodzicki residue for classical pseudo-differential operators in Corollary 3.11 without requiring the positivity of the operator.

This introduction culminates in an examination of two distinct yet interconnected facets of Lie group analysis, which, despite their apparent divergence, are both pivotal to the context of (1.3). The first pertains to the theories outlined in [RT10], which underscore the efficacy of global symbol-based pseudo-differential operator analysis on compact Lie groups. Here, the discrete nature of the unitary dual in compact Lie groups facilitates the application of generalized Fourier techniques akin to those on the torus or $\textnormal{SU(2)}$. Notably, Lidskii-type formulae play a crucial role in localizing operator spectra and deriving trace expressions through matrix-valued symbols, as demonstrated in [Del10a]. The second aspect arises from the twisted periodic analysis framework in [RT10], mirroring non-commutative geometric structures, such as deformed tori or $\textnormal{SU}(2)$, where Wodzicki-type results are extended, as initially observed in [LNP16] utilizing global symbols (building on prior work in [FK13]). Additionally, the extension of Dixmier traces to broader Banach space settings is well-documented in Pietsch’s work [Pie12], with [Pie14] offering a comprehensive overview of trace theory and its historical development.

Let $u_{it}$ and $m_{jt}$ represent the latent features of user $i$ and movie $j$ at time $t$, respectively. The predicted rating for user $i$ on movie $j$ at time $t$ is denoted by $\hat{r}_{ij|t}$, while $r_{ij|t}$ represents the actual rating. The set of all ratings for movie $j$ is denoted as $\{r_{ij|t}\}_{j}$. The following model is defined for updates.

Explainability in machine learning models remains a focal point of research and discussion. This issue is particularly critical in recommendation systems (RS) due to their widespread integration into user interfaces. RS now underpin many daily activities, making transparency and explainability essential to enhance user experience and trust. For instance, Netflix justifies its recommendations by displaying similar movies derived from user social networks, while Amazon uses neighborhood-based Collaborative Filtering (CF) to explain its suggestions. Deep Learning (DL) models have gained traction in recommendation systems for their high prediction accuracy. However, their primary drawback—non-interpretability—has sparked recent efforts to address this limitation, as seen in works like Binder et al. [2016].

For recommendation systems (RS), most explanation-based methods can be categorized into either classical neighborhood-based Collaborative Filtering (CF) or rule-based approaches, as outlined by Pazzani and Billsus [2007]. CF-based recommendation methods, which harness collective user wisdom, are more widely adopted due to their scalability and reliability. Recent advancements, such as those by Abdollahi and Nasraoui [2016a] and Abdollahi and Nasraoui [2016b], have integrated explanations into Matrix Factorization (a latent factor model) and Restricted Boltzmann Machines. However, these approaches are not suitable for temporal recommendations, which aim to capture the evolution of user preferences over time. Modeling the temporal dynamics of user preferences and item states for effective recommendation systems remains a key research focus, with recent studies demonstrating the effectiveness of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) [Wuet al., 2017] in this domain.

Recurrent Recommender Networks, as introduced by Wü et al. [2017], represent a robust method for temporal recommendation systems. Our proposed model builds upon this framework, integrating a neighborhood-based explanation mechanism. The architecture employs LSTM-based RNNs, as developed by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber [1997], to capture user and item latent states. While the experimental focus is on movie recommendations, the methodology demonstrates broad applicability across various domains. We formalize the concept of explainability by constructing a dynamic bipartite graph connecting users and items, where edge weights reflect the explanatory value of an item for a user, derived from ratings of similar users. To balance both prediction accuracy and explainability, our optimization objective includes a term that minimizes the disparity between user and item latent features, weighted by their defined explainability scores.

We denote the latent features of user \(i\) and movie \(j\) at time \(t\) as \(u_{it}\) and \(m_{jt}\), respectively. The predicted rating for user \(i\) on movie \(j\) at time \(t\) is denoted by \(\hat{r}_{ij|t}\), while \(r_{ij|t}\) represents the actual rating. The set of all ratings for movies \(j\) is denoted by \(\{r_{ij|t}\}_{j}\). We define the following model for updates:

\[
\hat{r}_{ij|t} = f(u_{it}, m_{jt}),
\]
\[
u_{i,t+1} = g(u_{it}, \{r_{ij|t}\}_{j}),
\]
\[
m_{i,t+1} = h(m_{it}, \{r_{ij|t}\}_{j}).
\]

The functions \(f\), \(g\), and \(h\) are learned by the model to infer user and movie states. Section 3.1 of the RRN paper (Wu et al. [2017]) provides a detailed explanation of the user and movie state formulations, which we briefly summarize below. 

\[
y_{t} = V[x_{t}, 1, \tau_{t}, \tau_{t-1}], \quad u_{t} = \text{LSTM}(u_{t-1}, y_{t}),
\]

Here, \(\tau_{t}\) represents the wallclock time at \(t\), \(x_{t}\) is the rating vector for the user, and \(V\) represents the transformation. Following the approach in RRN (Wu et al. [2017]), we incorporate the profile identities of the user and the movie as stationary components of the rating, in addition to their time-varying state vectors. Thus,

\[
\hat{r}_{ij|t} = f(u_{it}, m_{jt}, u_{i}, m_{j}) = \langle \tilde{u}_{it}, \tilde{m}_{jt} \rangle + \langle u_{i}, m_{j} \rangle,
\]

where \(\tilde{u}_{it} = A u_{it} + c\) and \(\tilde{m}_{jt} = B m_{jt} + d\). This decomposition highlights how RRN essentially performs matrix factorization over time, as previously mentioned.

The temporal progression of user preferences and item characteristics has been extensively explored in prior research. Notably, impactful contributions include the works of Donkers et al. (2017) and RRN Wü et al. (2017). Donkers et al. introduced a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)-based RNN, leveraging the GRU architecture proposed by Cho et al. (2014), to model sequential user preference evolution. Their approach incorporated a rectified linear integration within the GRU cell to dynamically update user states for personalized recommendations. Conversely, RRN focused on movie recommendation by integrating explicit user feedback, employing customized LSTM cells to model the evolution of user and item latent states. Additionally, Devooght and Bersini (2017) utilized sequential data to address the sparsity of user-item interactions, distinguishing between items likely to be eventually preferred and those immediately desired. Since our framework builds upon RRN, further details are provided in Section 3.2.

We employ LSTM-based Recurrent Neural Networks to model the temporal progression of user and movie latent states. This methodology is akin to matrix factorization over time, where LSTM-based networks capture the temporal dynamics of latent factors. Our approach shares conceptual similarities with the work of Wuet al.[2017], with the key distinction being the integration of explainability, detailed in Section 3.3.

To address two fundamental questions—1) the effectiveness of our model in generating explanations and 2) the trade-off between prediction accuracy and explainability—we conducted a series of simulation experiments. All experiments were implemented using Tensorflow r1.4 (Abadi et al., 2016) in Python 3, employing the ADAM optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014) for training. The Netflix dataset used in Wu et al. (2017), originally introduced in Diao et al. (2014), served as our experimental basis. This dataset comprises 100 million movie ratings from 1999 to 2005, with each data point represented as a (user-id, item-id, time-stamp, rating) tuple, where timestamps are recorded at a daily granularity. Consistent with Wu et al. (2017), we maintained the same preprocessing and train-test split methodology.

In addition to our temporal interpretation, we propose a stationary representation of user and item latent features (as discussed in Section 3.2), which necessitates the construction of a stable bipartite graph connecting the user and item sets for interpretative purposes. This graph's edge weight matrix is formulated as:

$$
M_{um} = \frac{\sum_{z \in Q^{p}(u)} r_{zm}}{p \times \max\{r_{zm} : z \in Q^{p}(u)\}}
$$

(Equation 2)

Here, the variables retain their definitions from the prior equation, with the exclusion of the temporal index.

In addition, since we hypothesize that stationary components exist within the latent features of users and items (as discussed in Section 3.3), we also construct a stationary bipartite graph connecting the set of users to the set of items to aid in interpretation. This complements the earlier time-varying perspective. For this bipartite graph, the edge weight matrix is defined as $M_{um}=\frac{\sum_{z\in Q^{p}(u)}r_{zm}}{p\times max\{r_{zm}\forall z\in Q^{p}(u)\}}$. In this equation, all terms retain the same meaning as those in the previous formula, but without the time index.

We define the decoder as $\mathcal{D}(\cdot)=\text{MLP}(\cdot)$ with a hidden dimension of $256$, and two layers. The encoder $\mathcal{E}$ is a single-layer RNN with hidden dimension $256$. The prior and posterior networks are also single-layer RNNs with hidden dimension $256$. The decoder $\mathcal{D}$ uses the hidden state of the encoder to predict the next step of the state. The KL weight, which controls the trade-off between the KL term and the reconstruction term, is linearly increased from $0$ to $1$ over the course of training. The temperature parameter $\tau$ is set to $1$ as it does not significantly affect the performance. The model is trained using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of $0.001$ and a batch size of $128$.

The wall-clock time at time step $t$ is denoted by $\tau_{t}$, $x_{t}$ represents the user's rating vector, and $V$ is the transformation matrix. Following the methodology of RRN Wuet al.[2017], we incorporate the user and movie profile identifiers as static components of the rating, along with their time-varying state vectors. Consequently,

If movie $j$ is explainable to user $i$ at time step $t$, their latent representations $\tilde{m}_{jt}$ and $\tilde{u}_{it}$ must be close. This intuition informs the inclusion of the term $(\tilde{m}_{jt}-\tilde{u}_{it})^{2}M_{ijt}$ in our optimization objective. The overall objective is formulated to optimize both prediction accuracy and explainability.
