Table 1 and the bottom row of Figure 2 present the outcomes of our analysis. When $p=50$, it is evident that TempEx-Fluid exhibits superior RMSE and MRR values compared to the standard RRN model. This suggests that the integration of explainability enhances prediction accuracy by introducing an additional regularizing mechanism, thereby benefiting the model's performance. Furthermore, the bottom row of Figure 2 demonstrates that TempEx-Fluid consistently achieves higher Mean Average Precision and Mean Recall across all values of $p$, reinforcing the notion that explainability does not compromise predictive performance. Additionally, a comparative analysis between TempEx-Dry and TempEx-Fluid reveals that the latter consistently outperforms the former for all $p$ values. This is attributed to the more sophisticated temporal decay incorporated into TempEx-Fluid's optimization objective, which effectively diminishes the influence of past effects.

Recommendation systems are a critical component of Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly as AI commercialization expands. While deep learning (DL) techniques offer robust latent-feature models for recommendation systems (RS), they are often criticized for a lack of interpretability. This paper introduces a framework for explainable temporal recommendations within a DL model. The proposed approach utilizes an LSTM-based Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture for recommendation purposes and employs a neighborhood-based scheme to generate explanations. The effectiveness of this method is demonstrated through experiments on the Netflix dataset, with results showing improvements in both prediction accuracy and explainability.

We evaluate the performance of the proposed model using RMSE (Root Mean Square Error) and MAE (Mean Absolute Error), which are common metrics in rating prediction tasks. Additionally, we adopt the NDCG@10 (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at 10) metric to assess the ranking performance of our recommendation system.

Table 1 and the bottom row of Figure 2 present the results of our analysis. At $p=50$, the RMSE and MRR values for TempEx-Fluid exceed those of the standard RRN model, suggesting that integrating explainability enhances prediction accuracy by introducing an additional regularizer. The bottom row of Figure 2 further demonstrates that TempEx-Fluid consistently outperforms on Mean Average Precision and Mean Recall across all values of $p$, reinforcing that explainability does not compromise prediction accuracy. Additionally, TempEx-Fluid outperforms TempEx-Dry for all values of $p$, attributed to its more sophisticated temporal decay in the optimization objective, which effectively diminishes the influence of past effects.

While the standard approach for training Recurrent Neural Networks involves Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT), as noted in Wuet al.[2017], applying backpropagation across two sequences—where ratings depend on both user and item state RNNs—is computationally impractical. To address this challenge, we employ the subspace descent strategy described in Wuet al.[2017]. Empirical findings indicate that incorporating Dropout, as proposed by Srivastavaet al.[2014], effectively stabilizes gradients and mitigates over-fitting by accounting for additional terms in the optimization objective. The hyperparameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ were optimized via grid-search within the range of $0$ to $1$, with the optimal values set at $\alpha=0.4$ and $\beta=0.6$ for all experiments.

To address question 2), we assess the performance of our model by comparing it to benchmark models using standard metrics such as RMSE, Mean Average Precision (MAP), Mean Recall (MR), and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR). This evaluation aims to determine whether integrating explainability terms into the optimization objective results in significant improvements or declines in the actual prediction accuracy of ratings.

The concept of neighborhood-style explanations has been integrated into various graph-based models, including those proposed by He et al. [2015]. We define a time-dependent bipartite graph connecting the set of users and items, with an edge weight matrix given by $M_{umt} = \frac{\sum_{z \in Q^{p}_{t}(u)} r_{zm|t}}{p \times \max\{r_{zm|t} \forall z \in Q^{p}_{t}(u)\}}$. Here, $Q^{p}_{t}(u)$ represents the set of $p$ neighbors for user $u$, and $r_{zm|t}$ denotes the rating provided by user $z$ for movie $m$ at time step $t$. Notably, $M_{umt}$, which signifies the edge weight between user $u$ and movie $m$ at time step $t$, is a real number in the range [0, 1]. This value quantifies the explainability of movie $m$ to user $u$, where $r_{zm|t} = 0$ indicates that user $z$ did not rate movie $m$ at time step $t$. Consequently, a value of $M_{umt} = 0$ implies that none of the users in the neighborhood of user $u$ have rated movie $m$ at time step $t$, rendering it unexplainable to user $u$ at that time.

The dynamic progression of user preferences and item characteristics has been extensively explored in prior research. Among the most notable studies are Donkers et al. [2017] and RRN Wü et al. [2017], which have significantly influenced the field. Donkers et al. [2017] proposed a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) based RNN, utilizing a rectified linear integration of user states within their modified GRU cell to refine user state evolution for personalized recommendations. In contrast, RRN focused on movie recommendation by leveraging explicit user feedback, employing customized LSTM cells to model the evolution of user and item latent states. Devooght and Bersini [2017] addressed the scarcity of user-item interactions by incorporating sequence information to predict items likely to be preferred in the long term and those desired immediately. Given that our architecture builds upon RRN, a detailed discussion follows in Section 3.2.

We present an analysis of universal properties of the chiral phase transition in $N_{f}$ = 2+1 QCD, utilizing simulations with Highly Improved Staggered fermions on lattices of temporal extent $N_{\tau}$ = 6. Our investigation examines the dependence of chiral condensates and chiral susceptibilities on quark mass and volume in QCD, featuring two degenerate light quarks and a strange quark. The strange quark mass is fixed at its physical value ($m^{phy}_{s}$), while the light quark mass ($m_{l}$) is varied across five values within the range $1/20 \gtrsim m_{l}/m^{phy}_{s} \gtrsim 1/80$. These quark mass values correspond to pseudo-Goldstone pion masses spanning from approximately 160 MeV to 80 MeV. Additionally, the study explores the $O(N)$ scaling of chiral observables and the universal scaling's impact on physical observables in the vicinity of physical quark mass values.

In this presentation, we examine the latest advancements in the investigation of the chiral phase transition within Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) featuring $N_{f}$ =2+1 flavors, utilizing the framework of Highly Improved Staggered Quarks.

In Figure 2, we present the volume dependencies of the light quark chiral condensates $\langle\bar{\psi}\psi\rangle_{l}$ and the disconnected chiral susceptibilities $\chi_{disc}$ at the physical quark mass, shown in the left and right plots, respectively. The chiral condensate exhibits minimal volume dependence when $N_{s}\geq 20$. In conjunction with the volume dependence of the disconnected chiral susceptibilities, it is evident that for $N_{s}\geq 24$ at the physical quark mass, the system attains the thermodynamic limit.

The dependence of chiral condensates and disconnected chiral susceptibilities on the system's volume is illustrated in Fig. 3 for our smallest quark mass, which corresponds to a pion mass of approximately 80 MeV. The analysis does not reveal any signs of a first-order phase transition in the QCD medium at the current quark mass value.

To investigate the scaling window of the QCD system with the HISQ action, we begin by assuming that the two lowest quark masses lie within the scaling regime. The order parameter \( M \), defined as \( m_{s}\langle\bar{\psi}\psi\rangle_{l}N_{\tau}^{4} \), is fitted at these two lowest quark masses using Eq. (1) with an \( O(2) \) scaling function. As noted, a fit using a \( Z(2) \) scaling function with an additional parameter \( m_{c} \) was also conducted, but the analysis suggests a vanishing \( m_{c} \). This fitting process yields the non-universal critical exponents \( t_{0} \), \( h_{0} \), and \( T_{c} \). Subsequently, all datasets are plotted within the framework of \( M/h^{1/\delta} \) as a function of \( z \), as illustrated in the left panel of Fig. 5. A comparison reveals that the scaling window for the HISQ action is narrower than that of the p4fat3 action on \( N_{\tau}=4 \) lattices, as reported in Ref. [7]. The scaling violations observed in the left panel are hypothesized to originate from the regular component of the QCD partition function. To address this, an ansatz is proposed:

\[
M = h^{1/\delta}f_{G}(z) + f_{reg}, \quad f_{reg} = \left(a_{0} + a_{1}\frac{T-T_{c}}{T_{c}}\right)\frac{m_{l}}{m_{s}},
\]

and the results of this fit are presented in the right panel of Fig. 5. The inclusion of this ansatz, which incorporates a linear dependence on the quark mass in the regular term, appears to adequately describe the data. This approach enables a characterization of scaling violations at non-zero quark masses through an examination of the scaling behavior associated with the chiral phase transition.

A novel two-parameter discrete distribution, the PoiG distribution, has been developed through the convolution of a Poisson variate and an independently distributed geometric random variable. This distribution encompasses both the Poisson and geometric distributions and is suitable for modeling over-dispersed as well as equi-dispersed count data. Key statistical properties of the proposed count model are discussed, including the probability generating function, moment generating function, moments, survival function, and hazard rate function. Monotonic properties, such as log concavity and stochastic ordering, are also thoroughly investigated. The method of moments and maximum likelihood estimators for the parameters of the proposed model are presented. It is anticipated that the PoiG distribution may provide a valuable tool for practitioners in modeling over-dispersed count data, compared to its closest competitors.

In the academic literature, over-dispersion is a common occurrence in count data, where the variance exceeds the mean. This phenomenon is more prevalent than under-dispersion and equi-dispersion. A variety of count models have been proposed for over-dispersed data, with ongoing research focusing on developing simple yet effective models in this area (as indicated by [37], [25], [32], [35], [30], [29], [9], [19], [26], [34], [5], [2], and [36]). The Poisson distribution is a common count data model, but its equi-dispersion characteristic is a limitation. To address this, several alternatives have been developed, such as the hyper-Poisson (HP) of Bardwell and Crow [6], generalized Poisson distribution of Jain and Consul [20], double-Poisson of Efron [16], weighted Poisson of Castillo and Pérez-Casany [15], weighted generalized Poisson distribution of Chakraborty [10], Mittag-Leffler function distribution of Chakraborty and Ong [13], and the popular COM-Poisson distribution Shmueli et al. [31]. The COM-Poisson distribution generalizes the binomial and negative binomial distribution. The classical geometric and negative binomial models also possess the over-dispersion characteristic. Several extensions of the geometric distribution have been introduced for over-dispersed count data modelling (e.g., [11], [12], [18], [20], [22], [27], [28], and [33]). The negative binomial and COM-Poisson distributions are among the most widely used for over-dispersed data. There is still a need for developing new discrete distributions with a simple structure and explicit interpretation, suitable for over-dispersed data.

The dispersion index assesses the suitability of a distribution for modeling over, under, and equi-dispersed datasets. Denoted as $I_Y$, this index signifies over-dispersion when $I_Y > 1$ and under-dispersion when $I_Y < 1$. Equi-dispersion is indicated when $I_Y = 1$. The dispersion index formula for a random variable Y is as follows:

$I_Y = \dfrac{{\sigma}^2}{\mu} = 1 + \dfrac{(1-\theta)^2}{\theta(1+\lambda\theta-\theta)}$

From this equation, it is clear that the Poisson-Gamma (PoiG) distribution is equi-dispersed when $\theta = 1$ and over-dispersed for $0 < \theta < 1$. Figure 5 indicates that the dispersion index, $I_Y$, increases as both $\lambda$ and $\theta$ decrease.

In a recent development, Bourguignon and colleagues have presented the BerG distribution [8] through the combination of a Bernoulli and geometric random variable. In a subsequent publication, the same group has introduced the BerPoi distribution, derived from a similar rationale [7]. This is a product of a Bernoulli and Poisson random variable. The former exhibits the ability to model over-dispersed, under-dispersed, and equi-dispersed data, whereas the latter is particularly effective for under-dispersed data. This simple yet powerful methodology serves as the foundation for the creation of a novel over-dispersed count model in the present study.

In the aforementioned study, we introduce a novel discrete distribution, referred to as $PoiG$, derived from the convolution of two independent count random variables, specifically, Poisson and geometric. This two-parameter distribution offers several advantages, including structural simplicity, as it is more straightforward to understand compared to the COM-Poisson distribution, which requires a complex normalizing constant in its probability mass function. The ease of expression for the mean and variance in closed-form makes the proposed distribution particularly suitable for regression modeling. Unlike the COM-Poisson distribution, the mean and variance of the $PoiG$ distribution can be expressed in closed form. The $PoiG$ distribution represents an extension of both the Poisson and geometric distributions.

This scholarly discourse is structured into four essential sections. In the second part, we introduce the $PoiG$ distribution. In the third section, we delve into its significant statistical characteristics, which encompass the recurrence relationship, generating functions, moments, dispersion index, mode, reliability properties, monotonic properties, and stochastic ordering. The fourth section outlines the moment and maximum likelihood techniques for parameter estimation. The article concludes by highlighting the study's limitations and potential avenues for future research.

In this analysis, we propose a novel discrete distribution by combining two independent discrete random variables, denoted as $Y_{1}$ and $Y_{2}$. $Y_{1}$ and $Y_{2}$ follow the Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda > 0$ and the geometric distribution with mean $0 < 1/\theta < 1$, respectively. Both variables share the same support, the set of non-negative integers $\{0,1,2,...\}$. For simplicity, we express $Y_{1}$ as $P(\lambda)$ and $Y_{2}$ as $G(\theta)$. The sum of these two variables, denoted as $Y$, can be represented as the convolution of the Poisson and geometric distributions, which we term the $PoiG$ distribution. The probability mass function (pmf) of $PoiG(\lambda,\theta)$ can be expressed as:

$p_{Y}(y) = \frac{\theta{(1-\theta)^{y}}}{\Gamma(y+1)}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}{\Gamma\left(y+1,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)},\hskip 30.0pty=0,1,2,...\,\,.$

The cumulative distribution function (cdf) of $PoiG$ distribution is:

$F_{Y}(y) = \dfrac{\Gamma(y+1,\lambda)}{\Gamma(y+1)}-\dfrac{(1-\theta)^{y+1}}{\Gamma(y+1)}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(y+1,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right),\hskip 25.0pty=0,1,2,...\,\,.$

Figure 1 and 2 show the nature of the pmf and cdf for different choices of $(\lambda,\theta)$. The mean and variance of the $PoiG(\lambda,\theta)$ distribution are:

$E(Y)=\mu=\lambda+\dfrac{1-\theta}{\theta}\hskip 10.0pt\textrm{and}\hskip 10.0ptV(Y)=\sigma^{2}=\lambda+\dfrac{1-\theta}{\theta^{2}}$

As $\lambda \rightarrow 0$, the $PoiG(\lambda,\theta)$ distribution behaves like the geometric distribution $G(\theta)$. On the other hand, as $\theta \rightarrow 1$, it behaves like the Poisson distribution $P(\lambda)$. The incomplete gamma function used in the expression can be rewritten as:

$\Gamma\left(y+1,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)$ $= \Gamma(y+1){\sum_{i=0}^{y}}\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(i+1)}\exp{\left(-\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)^{i}},$

where $\Gamma(y+1)=y!$ and $\Gamma(i+1)=i!$. The proportion of zeros in the $PoiG$ distribution tends to $\theta$ as $\lambda \rightarrow 0$ and to zero as $\lambda \rightarrow \infty$.

In the context of probability theory, we designate the probability generating function (pgf) as $H$. Each random variable is assigned a corresponding subscript. For the case where $Y_1$ follows a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda$ and $Y_2$ adheres to a general distribution with parameter $\theta$, the respective pgfs are expressed as:

$$H_{Y_1}(s) = e^{\lambda(s - 1)}$$

and

$$H_{Y_2}(s) = \frac{\theta}{1 - (1 - \theta)s}.$$

Utilizing the convolution property of pgfs, we derive the pgf of the composite Poisson-General (PoiG) distribution as:

$$H_Y(s) = \frac{\theta e^{\lambda(s - 1)}}{1 - s + \theta s}.$$

(8) denotes this result. Analogous methods are employed to obtain the moment generating function (MGF) $M_Y(t)$, characteristic function $\phi_Y(t)$, and cumulant generating function (cgf) $K_Y(t)$. The corresponding expressions are provided below:

$$M_Y(t) = \frac{\theta e^{\lambda(t - 1)}}{1 - (1 - \theta)t}$$

(9)

$$\phi_Y(t) = \frac{\theta e^{\lambda(e^{it} - 1)}}{1 - (1 - \theta)e^{it}}$$

(10)

$$K_Y(t) = \lambda(e^{t} - 1) + \log\left\{\frac{\theta}{1 - (1 - \theta)e^{t}}\right\}$$

(11)

Here, we delve into significant statistical characteristics of the proposed $PoiG(\lambda,\theta)$ distribution. We examine various aspects, including a recurrence relation, probability generating function (pgf), moment generating function (mgf), characteristic function (cf), cumulant generating function (cgf), moments, coefficient of skewness, and kurtosis. Furthermore, we analyze the distribution's reliability through the survival function and the hazard rate function. Log-concavity and stochastic ordering of the suggested model are also scrutinized.

In Section 3.7, we establish that the distribution represented by the function $PoiG(\lambda,\theta)$ is unimodal. It is noteworthy that the condition $\displaystyle p_{Y}(1)\leq p_{Y}(0)$ leads to the inequality $\displaystyle\lambda\leq\theta$. This, in turn, implies the following chain of inequalities:

$\displaystyle(1+\lambda-\theta)\theta e^{-\lambda}\leq\theta e^{-\lambda}$

$\displaystyle\implies$ $\displaystyle\lambda\theta e^{-\lambda}-\theta^{2}e^{-\lambda}\leq 0$

$\displaystyle\implies$ $\displaystyle\lambda-\theta\leq 0$

$\displaystyle\implies$ $\displaystyle\lambda\leq\theta.$

For the case where $\lambda\leq\theta$, the distribution's mode resides at zero. Figure 1 demonstrates this for the values $\lambda=0,0.5$ and $\theta>0,0.5$. In the event that $\lambda=\theta$, the distribution's mass at zero and at unity are equal, as shown in Figure 6. However, when $\lambda>\theta$, the distribution exhibits a non-zero mode, although finding an explicit expression for this non-zero mode proves challenging, if not impossible.

For a professional audience, the given paragraph discusses the derivation of the raw and central moments for a Poisson-Gamma (PoiG) distribution, which is a combination of a Poisson and a Gamma distribution. The raw moments are obtained by combining the moments of the Poisson and Gamma distributions using binomial coefficients, Stirling numbers of the second kind, and the polylogarithm of negative integers. The explicit expressions for the first four raw moments are provided, and from these, the first four central moments are derived. The coefficients of skewness and kurtosis are also derived in closed form. As the Gamma parameter approaches 1, the distribution tends towards a normal distribution.

The probability distribution function, denoted by $Y$, follows a Poisson distribution with gamma-shaped parameters, $\lambda$ and $\theta$, and is log-concave if it satisfies the following inequality for all $y \geq 1$:

$\displaystyle p_{Y}^{2}(y) \geq p_{Y}(y-1)p_{Y}(y+1)$

Log-concave distributions, boasting a multitude of desirable characteristics, include the Bernoulli, binomial, Poisson, geometric, and negative binomial distributions. Notably, the convolution of two independent log-concave distributions is itself a log-concave distribution [21]. As the $PoiG$ distribution is the convolution of the Poisson and Geometric distributions, it inherits the log-concave property. As a result, several statements hold true for the $PoiG$ distribution [23] and [3]:

• The distribution is strongly unimodal.
• It has at most one exponential tail.
• All moments exist.
• The survival function is log-concave.
• The hazard rate function is monotonically increasing.
• The mean residual life function is monotonically decreasing (as depicted in Figure 7).

The probability recurrence relation is a useful tool for calculating subsequent terms based on the preceding term, particularly in the computation of mass values. The equation below, denoted as $p_{Y}(y)$, represents the relation:

$p_{Y}(y) = \theta{(1-\theta)^{y}}e^{-\lambda}s_{y}$

Where $s_{y}$ is defined as:

$s_{y} = \sum_{i=0}^{y}\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(i+1)}\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)^{i}$

And $s_{y+1}$ is given by:

$s_{y+1} = s_{y} + \dfrac{1}{\Gamma(y+2)}\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)^{y+1}$

Using these, we can derive the recurrence formula for the Poisson-Gamma distribution:

$p_{Y}(y+1) = (1-\theta)p_{Y}(y) + \theta e^{-\lambda}\dfrac{{\lambda}^{y+1}}{\Gamma(y+2)}$

It can be observed that as $y$ approaches infinity, the ratio of $s_{y+1}$ to $s_{y}$ and the ratio of $p_{Y}(y+1)$ to $p_{Y}(y)$ both approach 1. The behavior of the tail of the distribution is influenced by $\theta$. As $\theta$ approaches 0, the tail decays slowly, implying a long tail. Conversely, as $\theta$ approaches 1, the tail decays quickly, indicating a short tail. This can be confirmed through Figure 1.

For a professional audience, the following paragraph summarizes the distribution of the sum of n independent Poisson-Gamma random variables:

Result 1: The sum of n independent Poisson-Gamma random variables, each with parameter lambda_i and theta, follows a Poisson-Negative Binomial distribution. Mathematically, if Y_i ~ Poisson-Gamma(lambda_i, theta) for each i = 1, 2, ..., n, then

 ∑ₙₗₑYₑ ~ Poisson-Negative Binomial(∑ₙλₑ, n, theta)

The proof of Result 1 is based on the convolution property of the probability generating function (pgf). The pgf of each Y_i is derived as:

H_Y_i(s) = θe^(λ_i(s-1)) / (1-s + θs)

The pgf of the sum of n independent Poisson-Gamma random variables, Z = Y_1 + Y_2 + ... + Y_n, can be derived as:

H_Z(s) = (θ^n / (1-s + θs)^n) * e^(∑ₙλ_i(s-1))

The term θ^n / (1-s + θs)^n is the pgf of the Negative Binomial distribution, which is a generalization of the geometric distribution. The term e^(∑ₙλ_i(s-1)) is the pgf of the sum of the lambda_i parameters. Therefore, ∑ₙₗₑYₑ follows a Poisson-Negative Binomial distribution with parameters ∑ₙλₑ, n, and theta.

For a professional audience, the following paragraph presents the calculation of the mean and variance for a Poisson-Gamma distribution. The expressions for the mean and mean squared error are as follows:

μ1′ = λ + (1 - θ) / θ
μ2 = λ + (1 - θ) / θ^2

By subtracting μ2 from μ1', we get:

μ1' - μ2 = (1 - θ) / θ - (1 - θ) / θ^2

Simplifying, we find:

μ2 - μ1' = (1 - θ) / θ^2

From this, we can derive:

(1 - θ) / θ = √(μ2 - μ1')

And consequently:

θ = 1 / (1 + √(μ2 - μ1'))

By substituting this value of θ back into μ1', we obtain:

λ = μ1' - √(μ2 - μ1')

These calculations are based on equating sample moments with theoretical moments. By equating the first sample moment about the origin, m1' = (1/n) Σyi, with μ1', and the second sample moment about the mean, m2 = (1/n) Σ(yi - Ȳ)^2, with μ2, we obtain the following estimators for λ and θ:

hat(λ)MM = m1' - √(m2 - m1')

hat(θ)MM = 1 / (1 + √(m2 - m1'))

The notation MM signifies maximum likelihood estimation based on moments.

For a professional audience, the Coefficient of Variation (CV) is a metric used to measure data dispersion within a distribution. A higher CV value signifies a distribution's ability to model data with increased variability. The equation for calculating the CV of a random variable Y is as follows:

CV(Y) = [(√(λθ^2 - θ + 1)) / (λθ - θ + 1)] × 100%

This equation expresses the level of variability present in the data as a percentage.

This article introduces a novel two-parameter distribution, which has been thoroughly investigated, with equal emphasis on theoretical development and practical application. The proposed model is particularly useful for modeling over-dispersed data due to its user-friendly nature. The unique selling proposition of this model lies in the interpretability of its parameters, making it a viable alternative to existing over-dispersed count models. The parameter $\lambda$ controls the distribution's tail, while $\theta$ is responsible for adjusting for over-dispersion in a given dataset. The interplay between these parameters offers flexibility in shaping the distribution. When $\theta$ predominates $\lambda$, the distribution maintains a J-shaped mass distribution, while for larger $\lambda$ values, the distribution adopts a bell-shaped mass distribution, effectively accommodating the hump or concentration of observations. A simulation study to assess the performance of point and asymptotic interval estimators, along with a comparative analysis of real-life data, will be presented in the complete article.

The log-likelihood function for parameters $\lambda$ and $\theta$ of a Poisson distribution with gamma-inverse link, denoted as $Y\sim PoiG(\lambda,\theta)$, can be derived from the probability mass function in equation (2) as follows:

$l(\lambda,\theta;\bm{y}) = n\log\theta + n\overline{y}\log(1-\theta) + \frac{n\lambda\theta}{1-\theta} + \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n}\log\left(\frac{\Gamma\left(y_{i}+1,\frac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)}{\Gamma(y_{i}+1)}\right).$ (29)

For convenience, we define the following:

$\beta = \frac{\lambda}{1-\theta}$

$\alpha_{j}(y_{i}) = \frac{e^{-\beta}}{\Gamma\left(y_{i}+1,\beta\right)}\frac{1}{(1-\theta)^{j}}$ for $j=1,2,3,...$

The score functions for $\lambda$ and $\theta$ are then obtained by differentiating the log-likelihood function as follows:

$\frac{\partial}{\partial\lambda}l(\lambda,\theta;\bm{y}) = \frac{n\theta}{1-\theta} - \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_{1}(y_{i})\beta^{y_{i}}$ (30)

$\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}l(\lambda,\theta;\bm{y}) = \frac{n}{\theta} + \frac{n(\lambda-\bar{y})}{1-\theta} + \frac{n\lambda\theta}{(1-\theta)^{2}} - \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n}\lambda\alpha_{2}(y_{i})\beta^{y_{i}}$ (31)

In an ideal scenario, the maximum likelihood estimators would be derived by simultaneously solving the two equations obtained by setting the right-hand sides of (30) and (31) equal to zero. However, due to the intricate structure, explicit expressions for the maximum likelihood estimators could not be obtained. As a result, we resort to optimizing the log-likelihood function numerically with respect to the parameters, denoted as $\hat{\lambda}_{ML}$ and $\hat{\theta}_{ML}$.

This section aims to estimate the parameters $\lambda$ and $\theta$ from a random sample ${\bf Y}$ of size $n$ drawn from the $PoiG(\lambda,\theta)$ distribution. Given a realization ${\bf y}$ of ${\bf Y}$, the methods presented will be used for estimation. Additionally, we derive asymptotic confidence intervals for both parameters using maximum likelihood estimates.

For a large sample size $n$, under certain regularity assumptions, the distribution of $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\lambda}_{ML}-\lambda,\hat{\theta}_{ML}-\theta)$ is bivariate normal with a mean vector of $(0,0)$ and a dispersion matrix $\hat{I}^{-1}$. This matrix can be expressed as $\begin{pmatrix}J_{11}&-J_{12}\\-J_{21}&J_{22}\end{pmatrix}$, where $J_{ij}$ represents the elements of the inverse information matrix $I$. The asymptotic $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ confidence intervals for $\lambda$ and $\theta$ are, respectively, given by $(\hat{\lambda}_{ML}-Z_{\frac{\alpha}{2}}\sqrt{J_{11}},\hat{\lambda}_{ML}+Z_{\frac{\alpha}{2}}\sqrt{J_{11}})$ and $(\hat{\theta}_{ML}-Z_{\frac{\alpha}{2}}\sqrt{J_{22}},\hat{\theta}_{ML}+Z_{\frac{\alpha}{2}}\sqrt{J_{22}})$, where $Z_{\frac{\alpha}{2}}$ is the $\frac{\alpha}{2}$ quantile of the standard normal distribution.

In the realm of statistical analysis, the stochastic order serves as a critical tool for comparing the behavior of disparate random variables. Specifically, we delve into the likelihood ratio order, denoted as $\geq_{lr}$. Consider two random variables, X and Y, which follow a Poisson distribution with gamma-shaped parameters, $\lambda_{1}$ and $\lambda_{2}$, respectively, and a common shape parameter, θ. If Y exhibits a smaller value than X in the traditional likelihood ratio order, that is, $Y\leq_{lr}X$, it implies that the likelihood ratio, L(x), exhibits a monotonically increasing trend in the variable x, meaning that $L(x)\leq L(x+1)$ for all values of θ between 0 and 1, and $\lambda_{2}$ is less than $\lambda_{1}$.

The probability density functions of X and Y are respectively represented as:

$p_{X}(x) = \theta(1-\theta)^{x}e^{-\lambda_{1}}\sum_{i=0}^{x}\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(i+1)}\left(\dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{1-\theta}\right)^{i},\hskip 25.0ptx=0,1,2,...$

$p_{Y}(x) = \theta(1-\theta)^{x}e^{-\lambda_{2}}\sum_{i=0}^{x}\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(i+1)}\left(\dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{1-\theta}\right)^{i},\hskip 25.0ptx=0,1,2,...\,.$

The likelihood ratio, L(x), is defined as:

$L(x) = \exp{[-(\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2})]}\dfrac{\sum_{i=0}^{x}\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(i+1)}\left(\dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{1-\theta}\right)^{i}}{\sum_{i=0}^{x}\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(i+1)}\left(\dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{1-\theta}\right)^{i}},\hskip 12.0ptx=0,1,2,...$

It is straightforward to verify that $L(x)\leq L(x+1)$ for all $0<\theta<1$ and $\lambda_{2}<\lambda_{1}$.

The function that gauges the dependability of a discrete random variable, Y, at a particular value y, is defined as the likelihood of Y having values equal to or greater than y. This function is also known as the survival function. In the context of a Poisson-Gamma distribution, Y ~ PoiG(λ, θ), the survival function SY(y) is represented as:

$S_{Y}(y) = P(Y \geq y) = 1 - \frac{\Gamma(y, \lambda)}{\Gamma y} + \frac{(1 - \theta)^{y}}{\Gamma y} \exp{\left(\frac{\lambda\theta}{1 - \theta}\right)} \Gamma\left(y, \frac{\lambda}{1 - \theta}\right).$ (22)

The rate at which failures occur in a discrete random variable, T, at a specific time point, t, is termed the hazard rate or failure rate. The hazard rate function (hrf) of Y ~ PoiG(λ, θ) can be derived using equations 2 and 4 as follows:

$h_{Y}(y) = \frac{P(Y = y)}{P(Y \geq y)} = \frac{\frac{\theta(1 - \theta)^{y}}{\Gamma(y + 1)} \exp{\left(\frac{\lambda\theta}{1 - \theta}\right)} \Gamma\left(y + 1, \frac{\lambda}{1 - \theta}\right)}{1 - \frac{\Gamma(y, \lambda)}{\Gamma y} + \frac{(1 - \theta)^{y}}{\Gamma y} \exp{\left(\frac{\lambda\theta}{1 - \theta}\right)} \Gamma\left(y, \frac{\lambda}{1 - \theta}\right)}$

$= \frac{\theta(1 - \theta)^{y} \exp{\left(\frac{\lambda\theta}{1 - \theta}\right)} \Gamma\left(y + 1, \frac{\lambda}{1 - \theta}\right)}{\Gamma(y + 1) - y\Gamma(y, \lambda) + y(1 - \theta)^{y} \exp{\left(\frac{\lambda\theta}{1 - \theta}\right)} \Gamma\left(y, \frac{\lambda}{1 - \theta}\right)}.$ (23)

The hrf for various parameter selections is visually depicted in Figure 7. The Poisson-Gamma distribution demonstrates a constant failure rate when λ is extremely small and an increasing failure rate, up to a certain time period, as λ increases.

In the realm of reliability analysis, the mean residual life refers to the anticipated additional operational period of a component, given that it has endured beyond a predefined point in time. If we denote the lifetime of the component as a Poisson distribution with parameters $\lambda$ and $\theta$, the mean residual life can be mathematically represented as follows:

$\mu_{Y}(y)$ $\displaystyle=E(Y-y|Y\geq y)$

$\displaystyle=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}(y-k)P(Y=y)}{P(Y\geq y)}$

$\displaystyle=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}\bar{F}(y)}{\bar{F}(k-1)}$

$\displaystyle=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}\left(\ 1-\dfrac{\Gamma(y,\lambda)}{\Gamma y}+\dfrac{(1-\theta)^{y}}{\Gamma y}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(y,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)\right)}{1-\dfrac{\Gamma(k-1,\lambda)}{\Gamma(k-1)}+\dfrac{(1-\theta)^{k-1}}{\Gamma(k-1)}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(k-1,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)}.$

This expression (Equation 24) encapsulates the mean residual life of a component from a Poisson distribution with parameters $\lambda$ and $\theta$.

In the realm of probability theory, the symbol $Y\leq_{st}X$ signifies that the cumulative probability of $Y$ being greater than or equal to a certain value $x$, $P(Y\geq x)$, is always less than or equal to the corresponding probability for $X$, $P(X\geq x)$, for all values of $x$. This concept is known as stochastic ordering.

Analogously, $Y\leq_{hr}X$ indicates that the hazard rate of $Y$ is less than or equal to that of $X$, represented by the following inequality:

$\dfrac{p_{X}(x)}{P(X\geq x)}\leq\dfrac{p_{Y}(x)}{P(Y\geq x)}$

for all values of $x$. Conversely, $Y\leq_{rh}X$ signifies that the reversed hazard rate of $Y$ is less than or equal to that of $X$, which is equivalent to:

$\dfrac{p_{Y}(x)}{P(Y\leq x)}\leq\dfrac{p_{X}(x)}{P(X\leq x)}$

for all $x$.

From the perspective of the likelihood ratio order between $X$ and $Y$, several implications can be deduced [4]. These include:

• Stochastic order: $Y\leq_{st}X$
• Hazard rate order: $Y\leq_{hr}X$
• Reverse hazard rate order: $Y\leq_{rh}X$

In this discourse, we will delve into the definitions and symbols pertinent to Result 1. The symbol $G(\theta)$ has been previously introduced in Section 2. Let $R$ denote the number of failures preceding the initial success in a series of independent Bernoulli trials, assuming a success probability of $\theta\in(0,1)$ . In such a scenario, $R$ is said to adhere to the $G(\theta)$ distribution. If we await the $r^{th}$ success, the number of failures is a negative binomial random variable, characterized by index $r$ and parameter $\theta$ . Let's denote this distribution as $NB(r,\theta)$ . In a sequence where $R_{i}\sim G(\theta)$ holds for $i=1,2,...,r$ independently, and $S\sim NB(r,\theta)$ , we find that $S=R_{1}+R_{2}+...+R_{r}$ . This implies that $G(\theta)$ is a specific instance of $NB(r,\theta)$ when $r=1$ .

Similar to the derivation of the $PoiG$ model, the addition of a Poisson random variable and an independently distributed negative binomial random variable could result in a generalized version of the $PoiG$ model. A suitable notation for this distribution would be $PoiNB$ . Although our current study does not delve into the intricacies of the three-parameter distribution, Result 1 establishes that the transition from the geometric distribution to the negative binomial distribution mirrors the transition from the $PoiG$ distribution to a potential $PoiNB$ distribution. This finding may serve as a catalyst for future research aimed at generalizing the proposed model to the $PoiNB$ distribution.

In our current undertaking, we aim to derive asymptotic confidence intervals for the parameters under consideration. To achieve this objective, we require the information matrix, which is calculated from the second-order partial derivatives of the log-likelihood function. Below are the expressions for these derivatives:

1. $\displaystyle\dfrac{\partial^{2}l(\lambda,\theta;\bm{y})}{\partial\lambda^{2}}$:
   $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(\beta^{y_{i}}-y_{i}\beta^{y_{i}-1})\alpha_{2}(y_{i})-\beta^{2y_{i}}\alpha_{1}(y_{i})^{2}\right]$

2. $\displaystyle\dfrac{\partial^{2}l(\lambda,\theta;\bm{y})}{\partial\lambda\partial\theta}$:
   $\displaystyle\dfrac{n}{(1-\theta)^{2}}+\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\lambda(\beta^{y_{i}}-y_{i}\beta^{y_{i}-1})\alpha_{3}(y_{i})-\beta^{y_{i}}\alpha_{2}(y_{i})-\lambda(1-\theta)\beta^{2y_{i}}\alpha_{1}(y_{i})^{2}\right]$

3. $\displaystyle\dfrac{\partial^{2}l(\lambda,\theta;\bm{y})}{\partial\theta^{2}}$:
   $\displaystyle\dfrac{2n\lambda-n\bar{y}(1-\theta)}{(1-\theta)^{3}}-\dfrac{n}{\theta^{2}}+$
   $\displaystyle\hskip 12.0pt\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[((\lambda^{2}-2\lambda(1-\theta))\beta^{y_{i}}-\lambda^{2}y_{i}\beta^{y_{i}-1})\alpha_{4}(y_{i})-\lambda^{2}\beta^{2y_{i}}\alpha_{2}(y_{i})^{2}\right]$

The Fisher's information matrix for $(\lambda,\theta)$ is a 2x2 matrix consisting of these second-order derivatives' expectations. An approximation of this matrix can be obtained by plugging the maximum likelihood estimates, $\hat{\lambda}_{ML}$ and $\hat{\theta}_{ML}$, into the expressions for the second-order derivatives.

For a professional audience, the mean residual life in reliability studies refers to the anticipated additional operating time of a component beyond a specific point in time, given that the component has already endured past that point. If we denote the component's life by a Poisson distribution with rate $\lambda$ and shape parameter $\theta$, the mean residual life can be computed as:

$\mu_{Y}(y) = E(Y-y|Y\geq y)$

$=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}(y-k)P(Y=y)}{P(Y\geq y)}$

$=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}\bar{F}(y)}{\bar{F}(k-1)}$

$=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}\left(\ 1-\dfrac{\Gamma(y,\lambda)}{\Gamma y}+\dfrac{(1-\theta)^{y}}{\Gamma y}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(y,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)\right)}{1-\dfrac{\Gamma(k-1,\lambda)}{\Gamma(k-1)}+\dfrac{(1-\theta)^{k-1}}{\Gamma(k-1)}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(k-1,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)}.$ (24)

Here, $\Gamma(y,\lambda)$ represents the lower incomplete gamma function, $\Gamma y$ is the gamma function, and $\bar{F}(y)$ is the survival function.

For a professional audience, the mean residual life refers to the anticipated remaining operational duration of a component, given that the component has already survived for a specific period. In the context of reliability studies, if the lifespan of a component is modeled as a Poisson distribution with rate parameter $\lambda$ and shape parameter $\theta$ (denoted as $Y \sim PoiG(\lambda,\theta)$), the mean residual life can be calculated as follows:

$\mu_{Y}(y) = E(Y - y | Y \geq y)$
$\mu_{Y}(y) = \frac{\sum\limits_{y=k}^{\infty}{(y - k)P(Y=y)}}{P(Y \geq y)}$
$\mu_{Y}(y) = \frac{\sum\limits_{y=k}^{\infty}{\bar{F}(y)}}{\bar{F}(k-1)}$
$\mu_{Y}(y) = \frac{\sum\limits_{y=k}^{\infty}{\left(1 - \frac{\Gamma(y,\lambda)}{\Gamma y} + \frac{(1-\theta)^{y}}{\Gamma y}\exp{\left(\frac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(y,\frac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)\right)}}{1 - \frac{\Gamma(k-1,\lambda)}{\Gamma(k-1)} + \frac{(1-\theta)^{k-1}}{\Gamma(k-1)}\exp{\left(\frac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(k-1,\frac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)}.$

This formula (Equation 24) provides an analytical solution for calculating the mean residual life of a component in a Poisson distribution with given parameters.

In the context of reliability analysis, the mean residual life (MRL) refers to the estimated remaining lifespan of a component at a specific point in time, assuming the component has survived up to that point. If we denote the lifespan of a component as a Poisson distribution with rate parameter $\lambda$ and shape parameter $\theta$, the MRL can be calculated as follows:

  $\displaystyle\mu_{Y}(y)$ $\displaystyle=E(Y-y|Y\geq y)$  $\displaystyle=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}(y-k)P(Y=y)}{P(Y\geq y)}$  $\displaystyle=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}\bar{F}(y)}{\bar{F}(k-1)}$  $\displaystyle=\frac{\sum_{y=k}^{\infty}\left(\ 1-\dfrac{\Gamma(y,\lambda)}{\Gamma y}+\dfrac{(1-\theta)^{y}}{\Gamma y}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(y,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)\right)}{1-\dfrac{\Gamma(k-1,\lambda)}{\Gamma(k-1)}+\dfrac{(1-\theta)^{k-1}}{\Gamma(k-1)}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(k-1,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)}.$  (24)

Here, $\bar{F}(y)$ represents the survival function, $\Gamma(y,\lambda)$ is the incomplete gamma function, and $\Gamma y$ denotes the gamma function. The MRL equation (24) is particularly useful in predicting the remaining lifespan of components in various engineering and scientific applications.

In scholarly discourse, the occurrence of a count data's variance surpassing the mean is often referred to as over-dispersion. This phenomenon is significant in various modeling applications and is more prevalent than under-dispersion or equi-dispersion. A variety of count models are available for over-dispersed data in the literature. With ongoing research focus in this area, the development of a straightforward yet effective model is crucial.

The Poisson distribution, a basic count data model, is well-known for its equi-dispersion characteristic. This limitation can be addressed by employing alternative distributions such as the hyper-Poisson, generalized Poisson, double-Poisson, weighted Poisson, weighted generalized Poisson, Mittag-Leffler function distribution, and the COM-Poisson distribution. The COM-Poisson distribution generalizes the binomial and negative binomial distributions.

The negative binomial and COM-Poisson distributions are among the most commonly used for over-dispersed data. The gamma mixture of the Poisson distribution is responsible for generating the negative binomial distribution. Unlike the Poisson distribution, these two count models exhibit over-dispersion.

Extended versions of the geometric distribution have been proposed in literature for modeling over-dispersed count data. The negative binomial and COM-Poisson distributions remain the most popular choices for over-dispersed data. There is still a considerable scope for developing new discrete distributions with a simple structure and clear interpretation, suitable for over-dispersed data.

Over-dispersion, a term used in literature, refers to a situation where the variance of count data exceeds its mean. This occurrence is more prevalent than under-dispersion and equi-dispersion in various modeling applications. Researchers have developed numerous count models to address over-dispersed data. The Poisson distribution, a commonly used count data model, is characterized by equi-dispersion. However, this limitation can be overcome by using alternative distributions such as the hyper-Poisson, generalized Poisson, double-Poisson, weighted Poisson, weighted generalized Poisson, Mittag-Leffler function distribution, and the COM-Poisson distribution. The COM-Poisson generalizes the binomial and negative binomial distributions. The negative binomial and COM-Poisson are two popular distributions used for over-dispersed data. Additionally, the classical geometric and negative binomial models are used for over-dispersed count datasets. The gamma mixture of the Poisson distribution generates the negative binomial distribution. Unlike the Poisson distribution, these two count models possess the over-dispersion characteristic. Consequently, several extensions of the geometric distribution have been introduced in the literature for over-dispersed count data modeling. There is still a need for developing new discrete distributions with a simple structure and explicit interpretation, suitable for over-dispersed data.

In the literature, over-dispersion, where the variance of count data exceeds its mean, is a prevalent phenomenon that is more frequently observed than under-dispersion and equi-dispersion. This concept is significant in various modeling applications. A variety of count models for over-dispersed data are available, and further research in this area is ongoing (as cited in [37], [25], [32], [35], [30], [29], [9], [19], [26], [34], [5], [2], and [36]). The Poisson distribution is a common count data model, known for its equi-dispersion characteristic. However, this limitation can be addressed by utilizing alternative distributions such as the hyper-Poisson, generalized Poisson, double-Poisson, weighted Poisson, weighted generalized Poisson, Mittag-Leffler function distribution, and the COM-Poisson distribution, among others. The COM-Poisson distribution generalizes the binomial and negative binomial distribution. The negative binomial and COM-Poisson distributions are widely used for modeling over-dispersed count data. Additionally, extensions of the geometric distribution have been developed for over-dispersed count data modeling, such as those cited in [11], [12], [18], [20], [22], [27], [28], and [33]. There remains a need for developing new discrete distributions with a simple structure and explicit interpretation that are appropriate for over-dispersed data.

In the realm of reliability assessments, the mean residual life refers to the anticipated remaining lifespan of a component, provided that it has endured until a specified point in time. Let the random variable Y, which models the lifetime of a component, be distributed according to the Poisson gamma distribution with parameters λ and θ. The mean residual life is then derived as follows:

$\mu_{Y}(y) = E(Y-y|Y \geq y)$

This can be further expressed as:

$\mu_{Y}(y) = \frac{\sum\limits_{y=k}^{\infty}(y-k)P(Y = y)}{P(Y \geq y)}$

Or alternatively, in terms of the survival function:

$\mu_{Y}(y) = \frac{\sum\limits_{y=k}^{\infty}\bar{F}(y)}{\bar{F}(k-1)}$

Where $\bar{F}(y)$ is the survival function of Y.

Moreover, the expression can be simplified as:

$\mu_{Y}(y) = \frac{\sum\limits_{y=k}^{\infty}\left(1 - \dfrac{\Gamma(y, \lambda)}{\Gamma y} + \dfrac{(1-\theta)^{y}}{\Gamma y}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(y,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)\right)}{1-\dfrac{\Gamma(k-1,\lambda)}{\Gamma(k-1)}+\dfrac{(1-\theta)^{k-1}}{\Gamma(k-1)}\exp{\left(\dfrac{\lambda\theta}{1-\theta}\right)}\Gamma\left(k-1,\dfrac{\lambda}{1-\theta}\right)}$

This simplified equation (24) represents the mean residual life of a component when its lifetime is modeled by a Poisson gamma distribution.
